<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Categories of Software Estimation

Software estimation techniques are broadly classified into four categories, all testable in the KTU ESE:

### 2.1.1 Expert Judgment (Top-Down)

- Relies on the experience of senior developers/managers.
- Fast, cheap, and useful for proposals/bids.
- **Drawback**: Highly subjective, prone to bias, and not auditable.

### 2.1.2 Analogy-Based Estimation (Top-Down)

- Compares the new project to one or more **completed similar projects**.
- Uses *anchor points* from historical data.
- Formula:

$$
E_{\text{new}} = E_{\text{similar}} \times \frac{S_{\text{new}}}{S_{\text{similar}}} \times \frac{\text{Productivity factor adjustment}}{\text{Adjusted productivity}}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> Analogy-based estimation is the most practical technique used in **agile release planning** and is heavily weighted in KTU Part B questions.

### 2.1.3 Decomposition (Bottom-Up)

- The project is **decomposed** into smaller components (modules, functions, classes).
- Each component is estimated individually.
- Results are **aggregated** to produce a project total.
- **Work Breakdown Structure (WBS)** is the standard decomposition tool.
- **More accurate** than top-down but more **time-consuming**.

### 2.1.4 Empirical Models (Algorithmic)

- Use **mathematical formulas** derived from historical project data.
- Examples: **COCOMO**, **COCOMO II**, **Putnam's Model**, **Function Point Models**.

## 2.2 Size Estimation: Lines of Code (LOC)

**LOC** is the most traditional size measure. It counts source code lines (excluding comments and blank lines).

- **KLOC** = thousands of LOC.
- **DSI (Delivered Source Instructions)** is the modern term used in COCOMO II.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Pitfall**: LOC depends on the **programming language**. A program written in C may be 50 KLOC, but the *same functionality* in Java might be 30 KLOC, and in SQL might be 5 KLOC. This is why **Function Points** were invented — they are language-independent.

## 2.3 Size Estimation: Function Point Analysis (FPA)

Function Points (FP), invented by **Allan Albrecht at IBM in 1979**, measure software size based on **user-visible functionality**, independent of programming language.

### 2.3.1 The Five Function Point Components

| # | Component | Acronym | Description |
| :--- | :--- | :---: | :--- |
| 1 | External Inputs | EI | Data entering the system from outside |
| 2 | External Outputs | EO | Data leaving the system |
| 3 | External Inquiries | EQ | Interactive inputs that produce immediate outputs |
| 4 | External Interface Files | EIF | Files shared with other systems |
| 5 | Internal Logical Files | ILF | Logical data maintained inside the system |

### 2.3.2 The FP Calculation Formula (The Heart of the Topic)

$$
FP = \text{UFP} \times \big(0.65 + 0.01 \times \sum_{i=1}^{14} F_i\big)
$$

Where:
- $\text{UFP}$ = **Unadjusted Function Points** (raw sum from the 5 components above, weighted by complexity).
- $F_i$ = Complexity rating of each of the **14 General System Characteristics (GSC)**, on a scale 0 to 5.
- The expression $0.65 + 0.01 \times \sum F_i$ is called the **Value Adjustment Factor (VAF)**, which ranges from **0.65 to 1.35**.

The **14 General System Characteristics (GSC)** in FPA are:

1. Data communications
2. Distributed data processing
3. Performance
4. Heavily used configuration
5. Transaction rate
6. Online data entry
7. End-user efficiency
8. Online update
9. Complex processing
10. Reusability
11. Installation ease
12. Operational ease
13. Multiple sites
14. Facilitate change

### 2.3.3 Complexity Weighting Matrix

For each of the 5 FP components, count how many are **Simple**, **Average**, and **Complex**, then multiply by weights:

| Component | Simple | Average | Complex |
| :--- | :---: | :---: | :---: |
| EI | 3 | 4 | 6 |
| EO | 4 | 5 | 7 |
| EQ | 3 | 4 | 6 |
| ILF | 7 | 10 | 15 |
| EIF | 5 | 7 | 10 |

Then $\text{UFP} = \sum (\text{count} \times \text{weight})$ across all five rows.

## 2.4 The COCOMO Model (Constructive Cost Model)

**COCOMO** is the most important algorithmic estimation model for KTU. It was published by **Barry W. Boehm** in his 1981 book *Software Engineering Economics*. COCOMO exists in three forms:

### 2.4.1 COCOMO 81 — Three Sub-Models

| Sub-Model | When Used | Detail Level |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Basic** | Early feasibility / proposal stage | System-level only |
| **Intermediate** | Design stage | Subsystem-level with cost drivers |
| **Detailed** | Post-design stage | Module-level with detailed cost drivers |

### 2.4.2 The Basic COCOMO Effort Formula

$$
E = a \times (KLOC)^b
$$

$$
D = c \times (E)^d
$$

Where $E$ = effort in person-months, $D$ = duration in months, and $a, b, c, d$ are coefficients that depend on the **project mode**.

### 2.4.3 The Intermediate COCOMO Formula (with Cost Drivers)

$$
E = a \times (KLOC)^b \times \text{EAF}
$$

Where **EAF** = Effort Adjustment Factor = product of 15 cost driver multipliers ($EM_i$), each typically ranging from 0.70 (very low) to 1.66 (extra high):

$$
\text{EAF} = \prod_{i=1}^{15} \text{EM}_i
$$

### 2.4.4 The 15 COCOMO Cost Drivers (Intermediate Model)

1. Product attributes: Required reliability, Database size, Product complexity
2. Computer attributes: Execution time constraint, Main storage constraint, Virtual machine volatility, Computer turnaround time
3. Personnel attributes: Analyst capability, Applications experience, Programmer capability, Virtual machine experience, Programming language experience
4. Project attributes: Modern programming practices, Use of software tools, Required development schedule

### 2.4.5 COCOMO Project Modes and Coefficient Tables

| Project Mode | Description | $a$ | $b$ | $c$ | $d$ |
| :--- | :--- | :---: | :---: | :---: | :---: |
| **Organic** | Small teams, familiar environment, simple applications | 2.4 | 1.05 | 2.5 | 0.38 |
| **Semi-Detached** | Medium-size team, mixed experience, moderate complexity | 3.0 | 1.12 | 2.5 | 0.35 |
| **Embedded** | Tight constraints, complex hardware/software interaction | 3.6 | 1.20 | 2.5 | 0.32 |

## 2.5 The COCOMO II Model (1995+ Revision)

COCOMO II is the modern successor, designed for:
- Object-oriented systems
- Reuse-based development
- COTS (Commercial Off-The-Shelf) integration
- Web/spiral/agile processes

### 2.5.1 COCOMO II Effort Formula

$$
E = A \times \text{Size}^B \times \prod_{i=1}^{n} \text{EM}_i
$$

Where:
- $A$ = **calibration constant**, typically 2.94 (default).
- Size is measured in **SLOC (Source Lines of Code)** or equivalent FP (converted via backfiring tables).
- $B$ is an exponent that reflects project complexity and is calculated as:

$$
B = 1.01 + 0.01 \times \sum_{j=1}^{5} W_j
$$

The five scale factors $W_j$ (each 0 to 5) are: Precedentness, Development flexibility, Architecture/risk resolution, Team cohesion, Process maturity.

## 2.6 Use-Case Points (UCP) — Quick Reference

**Use-Case Points** adapt FPA to object-oriented / UML-based projects. The formula is:

$$
\text{UCP} = (\text{UUCW} + \text{UAW}) \times \text{TCF} \times \text{ECF}
$$

Where:
- $\text{UUCW}$ = Unadjusted Use-Case Weight (sum of unadjusted weights of all use cases).
- $\text{UAW}$ = Unadjusted Actor Weight.
- $\text{TCF}$ = Technical Complexity Factor.
- $\text{ECF}$ = Environmental Complexity Factor.

The **effort** is then: $E = \text{UCP} \times \text{CF}$, where CF (Conversion Factor) is typically 20 to 28 person-hours per UCP.

## 2.7 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet (Print-Ready)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The "Golden 6" Formulas Every KTU Student Must Memorize**

| # | Formula | What It Calculates | Constants / Notes |
| :---: | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| 1 | $E = a \times (KLOC)^b$ | COCOMO Basic effort (PM) | $a, b$ from mode table |
| 2 | $D = c \times (E)^d$ | COCOMO Basic duration (months) | $c, d$ from mode table |
| 3 | $E = a \times (KLOC)^b \times \text{EAF}$ | COCOMO Intermediate effort | EAF = product of 15 EMs |
| 4 | $FP = \text{UFP} \times (0.65 + 0.01 \times \sum F_i)$ | Function Points | VAF range: 0.65 to 1.35 |
| 5 | $E = A \times \text{Size}^B \times \prod \text{EM}_i$ | COCOMO II effort | $A = 2.94$ default |
| 6 | $\text{UCP} = (\text{UUCW} + \text{UAW}) \times \text{TCF} \times \text{ECF}$ | Use-Case Points | CF = 20-28 hrs/UCP |

## 2.8 Real-World Utility of These Models in Engineering

- **Bidding & Contracts**: COCOMO helps vendors quote realistic prices; clients verify fairness.
- **Resource Allocation**: Effort in person-months determines team size.
- **Risk Management**: Estimation models are run multiple times with different inputs to produce *probabilistic ranges* (Monte Carlo simulation).
- **Earned Value Management (EVM)**: Cost baseline = estimated cost. Earned value analysis compares actuals to estimates.
- **Project Portfolio Management**: Organizations use FP and COCOMO II to compare projects of different sizes and languages.

# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Worked Example 1: Basic COCOMO Calculation

**Problem Statement**: A software project is estimated to be **15 KLOC**. The project is to be developed by a small team in a familiar in-house domain, with no stringent constraints. Determine the **effort**, **duration**, and **average team size** using the **Basic COCOMO model**.

### Step 1 — Identify the Project Mode

The description matches the **Organic** mode:
- Small team ✓
- Familiar environment ✓
- Simple application ✓

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU examiner tip**: Words like "small team", "familiar", "in-house" → **Organic**. "Medium team, mixed experience" → **Semi-Detached**. "Real-time, tight hardware constraints" → **Embedded**.

From the coefficient table (Section 2.4.5):

$$
a = 2.4, \quad b = 1.05, \quad c = 2.5, \quad d = 0.38
$$

### Step 2 — Calculate the Effort (Person-Months)

$$
E = a \times (KLOC)^b
$$

$$
E = 2.4 \times (15)^{1.05}
$$

Compute $(15)^{1.05}$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
(15)^{1.05} &= (15)^1 \times (15)^{0.05} \\
&= 15 \times 1.0847 \\
&\approx 16.27
\end{aligned}
$$

Therefore:

$$
E = 2.4 \times 16.27 \approx 39.05 \text{ person-months}
$$

### Step 3 — Calculate the Duration (Months)

$$
D = c \times (E)^d
$$

$$
D = 2.5 \times (39.05)^{0.38}
$$

Compute $(39.05)^{0.38}$ using logarithms:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\ln(39.05) &\approx 3.665 \\
0.38 \times 3.665 &\approx 1.393 \\
e^{1.393} &\approx 4.025
\end{aligned}
$$

Therefore:

$$
D \approx 2.5 \times 4.025 \approx 10.06 \text{ months}
$$

### Step 4 — Calculate the Average Team Size

Average team size is effort divided by duration:

$$
\text{Team Size} = \frac{E}{D} = \frac{39.05}{10.06} \approx 3.88 \approx 4 \text{ persons}
$$

### Step 5 — Final Answer Summary

| Quantity | Value |
| :--- | :--- |
| Effort ($E$) | **39.05 PM** |
| Duration ($D$) | **10.06 months** |
| Team Size | **4 persons** |

---

## 3.2 Worked Example 2: Intermediate COCOMO with EAF

**Problem Statement**: The same 15 KLOC project is now being re-estimated using the **Intermediate COCOMO** model. The 15 cost driver multipliers have been rated and their product is found to be **EAF = 1.12**. Compute the new effort.

### Step 1 — Apply the Formula

$$
E = a \times (KLOC)^b \times \text{EAF}
$$

For the same Organic mode ($a = 2.4$, $b = 1.05$):

$$
E = 2.4 \times (15)^{1.05} \times 1.12
$$

### Step 2 — Substitute the Previously Computed Value

$$
E = 2.4 \times 16.27 \times 1.12
$$

### Step 3 — Multiply Step-by-Step

$$
\begin{aligned}
2.4 \times 16.27 &= 39.05 \\
39.05 \times 1.12 &= 43.74
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 4 — Final Answer

$$
\boxed{E \approx 43.74 \text{ person-months}}
$$

**Interpretation**: The 15 cost drivers collectively added ~12% to the basic effort estimate. This is realistic for an organic project.

---

## 3.3 Worked Example 3: Function Point Analysis

**Problem Statement**: A payroll system has been counted with the following component tally:

- **EI**: 3 simple, 2 average, 1 complex
- **EO**: 2 simple, 1 average, 1 complex
- **EQ**: 4 simple, 0 average, 0 complex
- **ILF**: 1 simple, 2 average, 0 complex
- **EIF**: 0 simple, 1 average, 0 complex

The sum of the 14 GSC ratings $\sum F_i = 35$. Compute the **adjusted Function Points**.

### Step 1 — Compute UFP (Unadjusted Function Points)

Using the weight table from Section 2.3.3:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{EI} &: (3 \times 3) + (2 \times 4) + (1 \times 6) = 9 + 8 + 6 = 23 \\
\text{EO} &: (2 \times 4) + (1 \times 5) + (1 \times 7) = 8 + 5 + 7 = 20 \\
\text{EQ} &: (4 \times 3) + (0 \times 4) + (0 \times 6) = 12 \\
\text{ILF} &: (1 \times 7) + (2 \times 10) + (0 \times 15) = 7 + 20 = 27 \\
\text{EIF} &: (0 \times 5) + (1 \times 7) + (0 \times 10) = 7
\end{aligned}
$$

Summing all five:

$$
\text{UFP} = 23 + 20 + 12 + 27 + 7 = 89
$$

### Step 2 — Compute the VAF (Value Adjustment Factor)

$$
\text{VAF} = 0.65 + 0.01 \times \sum_{i=1}^{14} F_i
$$

$$
\text{VAF} = 0.65 + 0.01 \times 35 = 0.65 + 0.35 = 1.00
$$

### Step 3 — Compute the Adjusted FP

$$
FP = \text{UFP} \times \text{VAF} = 89 \times 1.00 = 89
$$

### Step 4 — Final Answer

$$
\boxed{FP = 89}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> A VAF of 1.00 means the system is "average" in all 14 GSC dimensions. The minimum VAF is 0.65 (extremely simple) and the maximum is 1.35 (extremely complex).

---

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix: Comparing Estimation Techniques

> [!IMPORTANT]
> This is a **high-value KTU table** — examiners love asking "compare and contrast" questions.

| Criterion | Expert Judgment | Analogy | Decomposition (WBS) | COCOMO |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Speed** | Very fast | Fast | Slow | Moderate |
| **Accuracy** | Low–Moderate | Moderate | High | Moderate–High |
| **Cost** | Very low | Low | High | Moderate |
| **Bias Risk** | Very high | Moderate | Low | Low |
| **Data Required** | Domain experts | Past projects | Detailed WBS | KLOC + ratings |
| **Best Phase** | Concept / Bid | Early design | Detailed design | Feasibility |
| **Auditable** | No | Partially | Yes | Yes |
| **KTU Use Case** | Quick 3-mark theory | Theory + small calc | Theory + WBS diagram | Theory + numerical |

---

## 3.5 Quick Trick: How to Recognize a COCOMO Question in the Exam

Look for these **keywords** in the question paper:

- *"small/medium/large team"* + *"familiar/stiff environment"* → Mode
- *"compute effort / duration"* → $E$ and $D$ formulas
- *"cost drivers rated"* → EAF formula
- *"function points"* / *"UFP"* / *"GSC ratings"* → FPA formula

> [!TIP]
> If the question gives KLOC, the answer must end in **person-months**. If it gives FP/UCP, the answer ends in **hours or person-months** depending on the conversion factor given.

# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Diagram: The Estimation Workflow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    nodeA["Project Requirements & Scope"] --> nodeB["Step 1: Estimate Size LOC or FP"]
    nodeB --> nodeC["Step 2: Select Project Mode Organic, Semi, Embedded"]
    nodeC --> nodeD["Step 3: Apply COCOMO Formula E = a times KLOC to power b"]
    nodeD --> nodeE["Step 4: Adjust with EAF Product of 15 Cost Drivers"]
    nodeE --> nodeF["Step 5: Compute Duration D = c times E to power d"]
    nodeF --> nodeG["Step 6: Compute Cost Effort times Labor Rate"]
    nodeG --> nodeH["Step 7: Validate via Expert Judgment or Analogy"]
    nodeH --> nodeI{"Within Acceptable Range?"}
    nodeI -- Yes --> nodeJ["Final Estimate Released to PM Plan"]
    nodeI -- No --> nodeK["Refine Inputs and Re-iterate"]
    nodeK --> nodeB
```

## 4.2 Mermaid Diagram: COCOMO Model Hierarchy

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    cocomoRoot["COCOMO Constructive Cost Model"] --> basicSub["Basic COCOMO"]
    cocomoRoot --> interSub["Intermediate COCOMO"]
    cocomoRoot --> detailSub["Detailed COCOMO"]
    basicSub --> basicUse["Used at Feasibility and Proposal Stage Size only"]
    interSub --> interUse["Used at Design Stage Size plus 15 Cost Drivers"]
    detailSub --> detailUse["Used at Implementation Stage Module Level plus 17 Cost Drivers"]
    cocomoRoot --> cocomoII["COCOMO II Modern Version OO, Reuse, Agile"]
    cocomoII --> cocomoIIUse["Uses SLOC, Function Points, Scale Factors, 17 Effort Multipliers"]
```

## 4.3 Mermaid Diagram: Function Point Calculation Process

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    fpStart["Start FPA"] --> step1["Step 1: Count and Classify 5 Components EI, EO, EQ, ILF, EIF"]
    step1 --> step2["Step 2: Assign Complexity Simple, Average, Complex"]
    step2 --> step3["Step 3: Compute UFP Sum of count times weight"]
    step3 --> step4["Step 4: Rate 14 GSC Characteristics Scale 0 to 5"]
    step4 --> step5["Step 5: Compute VAF 0.65 plus 0.01 times sum F"]
    step5 --> step6["Step 6: Compute FP UFP times VAF"]
    step6 --> fpEnd["Adjusted Function Points Output"]
```

## 4.4 Mermaid Diagram: Estimation Technique Selection Decision Tree

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    decideStart["Need to Estimate a Software Project"] --> decideQ1{"What is the Project Phase?"}
    decideQ1 -- Concept or Feasibility --> pathA["Use Expert Judgment or Analogy"]
    decideQ1 -- Design Phase --> pathB["Use Decomposition WBS or COCOMO Basic"]
    decideQ1 -- Implementation Phase --> pathC["Use COCOMO Intermediate or Detailed"]
    decideQ1 -- Agile or OO Project --> pathD["Use COCOMO II or Use-Case Points"]
    pathA --> noteA["Quick, cheap, low accuracy"]
    pathB --> noteB["Moderate accuracy, needs historical data"]
    pathC --> noteC["High accuracy, needs detailed cost drivers"]
    pathD --> noteD["Modern, language-independent"]
```

## 4.5 Mermaid Diagram: The "4-Parameter Estimation" Block Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    sizeBlock["SIZE ESTIMATION\nLOC or FP or UCP"] --> effortBlock["EFFORT ESTIMATION\nE = a times KLOC to power b times EAF"]
    effortBlock --> durationBlock["DURATION ESTIMATION\nD = c times E to power d"]
    durationBlock --> costBlock["COST ESTIMATION\nC = E times Labor Rate"]
    costBlock --> feedbackLoop["FEEDBACK INTO PROJECT PLAN\nRisk Register, WBS, Resource Plan"]
    feedbackLoop -. refines .-> sizeBlock
```

> [!TIP]
> **KTU visual aid tip**: Always draw arrows *forward* (size → effort → duration → cost) and a dashed arrow *back* indicating iteration. Examiners award marks for showing the **iterative, refining nature** of estimation.

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A: Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1: **[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]**
**Define Software Estimation. List the four primary parameters that are estimated in a software project.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks)**:

> [!NOTE]
> **Definition (1 mark)**: Software estimation is the process of forecasting the size, effort, duration, and cost of a software project *before* development begins, using historical data, mathematical models, expert judgment, or decomposition techniques.

**Four Primary Parameters (2 marks — 0.5 each)**:
1. **Size** — measured in KLOC or Function Points.
2. **Effort** — measured in person-months (PM).
3. **Duration** — measured in calendar months.
4. **Cost** — measured in currency units (₹ / $).

---

### Question 2: **[KTU University Exam - June 2024]**
**Explain in brief the difference between Lines of Code (LOC) and Function Point (FP) as size estimation metrics.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks)**:

| Aspect | LOC | FP |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Definition (1 mark)** | Counts physical source code lines. | Counts user-visible logical functions. |
| **Language Dependency (1 mark)** | **Language-dependent** (same function in C vs Java differs). | **Language-independent**. |
| **Measured When (1 mark)** | Only after coding. | As early as design (from requirements). |

---

## 5.2 Part B: Full-Length 14-Mark Questions (Module Internal Choice)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Per KTU 2024 ESE pattern, each Part B question carries **14 marks** with internal choice. Each sub-part is typically **7 marks**.

---

### **Question A (14 Marks) — COCOMO + Function Point**

**[KTU University Exam - Dec 2022, Modified 2024 Scheme]**

**(a)** A software project is estimated at **8 KLOC** and is to be developed by a small in-house team in a familiar domain. Using the **Basic COCOMO** model, calculate:
- (i) The total effort in person-months. (3 marks)
- (ii) The development duration in months. (3 marks)
- (iii) The average team size required. (1 mark)

**(b)** Explain the **Intermediate COCOMO** model. How does it differ from the **Basic COCOMO** model? What is the role of the **Effort Adjustment Factor (EAF)**? (7 marks)

#### Model Solution

**Part (a) — Numerical**:
- **Mode identified**: Organic (small team, in-house, familiar).
- **Coefficients**: $a = 2.4$, $b = 1.05$, $c = 2.5$, $d = 0.38$.

**(i) Effort Calculation**:

$$
E = 2.4 \times (8)^{1.05}
$$

$$
(8)^{1.05} = 8 \times 8^{0.05} = 8 \times 1.096 \approx 8.77
$$

$$
E = 2.4 \times 8.77 = 21.05 \text{ person-months}
$$

**(ii) Duration Calculation**:

$$
D = 2.5 \times (21.05)^{0.38}
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
\ln(21.05) &\approx 3.047 \\
0.38 \times 3.047 &\approx 1.158 \\
e^{1.158} &\approx 3.184
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
D = 2.5 \times 3.184 = 7.96 \approx 8 \text{ months}
$$

**(iii) Team Size**:

$$
\text{Team Size} = \frac{E}{D} = \frac{21.05}{7.96} \approx 2.64 \approx 3 \text{ persons}
$$

**Part (b) — Theory**:

- **Basic COCOMO** uses only size (KLOC) to estimate effort and duration. It is used at the **proposal / feasibility stage** when cost driver information is unavailable.
- **Intermediate COCOMO** extends Basic by introducing **15 Cost Drivers** (Effort Multipliers, EM). It is applied at the **design stage** when subsystem-level information is available.
- **Effort Adjustment Factor (EAF)** = $\prod_{i=1}^{15} \text{EM}_i$, where each EM is rated on a 6-point scale (Very Low to Extra High) with values typically from 0.70 to 1.66.
- **Detailed COCOMO** further refines Intermediate by applying the formula at the **module level** with 17 cost drivers, producing the most accurate estimate.
- **Why Intermediate is more accurate**: Real-world projects are not uniform; cost drivers (e.g., analyst capability, database size, reliability requirements) can shift effort by ±50% or more. Ignoring them produces biased estimates.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) — Function Point + Estimation Techniques**

**[KTU University Exam - June 2023, Modified 2024 Scheme]**

**(a)** Define **Function Point Analysis**. Explain the procedure to compute the **adjusted Function Points** with the formula. (7 marks)

**(b)** Compare and contrast the following estimation techniques: **Expert Judgment**, **Analogy-based**, and **Decomposition (WBS-based)**. Which is most accurate and why? (7 marks)

#### Model Solution

**Part (a) — Theory + Formula**:

**Definition (2 marks)**: Function Point Analysis, developed by Allan Albrecht at IBM (1979), is a **language-independent** method of measuring software size from the **user's perspective**, based on the logical functions delivered to the user.

**Five Components (2 marks)**: External Inputs (EI), External Outputs (EO), External Inquiries (EQ), Internal Logical Files (ILF), External Interface Files (EIF). Each is classified as Simple / Average / Complex and weighted.

**Adjusted FP Formula (2 marks)**:

$$
FP = \text{UFP} \times \big(0.65 + 0.01 \times \sum_{i=1}^{14} F_i\big)
$$

Where UFP is the unadjusted function points (raw weighted count), and $F_i$ are the ratings of the 14 General System Characteristics on a 0-to-5 scale.

**Procedure (1 mark)**:
1. Count and classify the 5 components.
2. Compute UFP by multiplying counts by complexity weights and summing.
3. Rate the 14 GSCs and compute VAF.
4. Multiply UFP × VAF to get adjusted FP.

**Part (b) — Comparative Essay**:

| Aspect | Expert Judgment | Analogy-Based | Decomposition (WBS) |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Approach** | Top-down, opinion-based | Top-down, comparison-based | Bottom-up, structured |
| **Data Required** | Senior experts | Completed similar projects | Detailed requirements |
| **Cost** | Very low | Low | High |
| **Accuracy** | Lowest | Moderate | **Highest** |
| **Speed** | Fastest | Moderate | Slowest |
| **Bias** | High | Moderate | Low |
| **Best Use Phase** | Concept / Feasibility | Early design | Detailed design / construction |
| **Auditable** | No | Partially | **Yes (full traceability)** |

**Most Accurate (3 marks)**: **Decomposition (WBS-based)** is the most accurate because it breaks the project into small, well-understood components that can be estimated individually with high confidence, and the **aggregation** of these accurate estimates is more reliable than a single global guess. The WBS also provides a **traceable audit trail**, making it the preferred technique for high-stakes projects (e.g., defense, medical software). However, it is the most expensive and time-consuming.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout**
> - **Common mistake 1**: Confusing **PM (person-months)** with **calendar months**. Effort is *work* time; duration is *calendar* time. They are related by team size, not equal.
> - **Common mistake 2**: Using wrong mode coefficients (e.g., using Embedded's $a=3.6$ for an organic project). Always re-read the problem text for mode cues.
> - **Common mistake 3**: Forgetting to apply the **VAF** in FPA — students often just state UFP, losing 1–2 marks.
> - **Common mistake 4**: Writing $E = a \times KLOC^b$ without stating which mode. **Always declare the mode first** to earn the first mark.
> - **Common mistake 5**: Mixing up COCOMO and COCOMO II coefficients. COCOMO II uses $A = 2.94$ by default and the *exponent* $B$ is computed from 5 scale factors, not from a mode table.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Software Estimation**

- ☐ **Four parameters to estimate**: Size, Effort, Duration, Cost — always cite all four in definition questions.
- ☐ **Two size measures**: LOC (language-dependent) and FP (language-independent). FP is preferred for early estimates.
- ☐ **FP formula** = UFP × (0.65 + 0.01 × ΣFᵢ). VAF range: **0.65 to 1.35**.
- ☐ **Five FP components**: EI, EO, EQ, ILF, EIF. Each weighted by Simple/Avg/Complex.
- ☐ **14 GSC characteristics** in FPA — names not required, but the formula range (0.65–1.35) is mandatory.
- ☐ **COCOMO Basic**: $E = a \times (KLOC)^b$, $D = c \times (E)^d$. Three modes: **Organic (2.4, 1.05)**, **Semi-Detached (3.0, 1.12)**, **Embedded (3.6, 1.20)**.
- ☐ **COCOMO Intermediate**: adds **15 cost drivers**, multiplied as **EAF**.
- ☐ **COCOMO II**: $E = A \times \text{Size}^B \times \prod EM_i$, with $A = 2.94$ default, $B$ from 5 scale factors.
- ☐ **Use-Case Points** = (UUCW + UAW) × TCF × ECF, with 20–28 person-hours per UCP.
- ☐ **Estimation is iterative** — never claim an estimate is final; always mention refinement as the project progresses.
- ☐ **Estimation is not a guarantee** — always state that estimates are *probabilistic ranges*, not exact values.
- ☐ **Estimation techniques**: Expert Judgment (fast/biased), Analogy (practical/agile), Decomposition (accurate/slow), Empirical models (auditable/scalable).
- ☐ **Mention uncertainty** in every answer — KTU examiners reward the use of **range estimates** (optimistic, most likely, pessimistic) over single-point estimates.
- ☐ **For numerical questions**: always **state the mode**, **write the formula**, **substitute step-by-step**, and **box the final answer** with units.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Basis for Software Estimation
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEITT633 - SOFTWARE PROJECT MANAGEMENT
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Basis for Software Estimation

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Software Estimation** is the disciplined process of forecasting the essential project parameters — **size**, **effort**, **duration**, **cost**, and **resource requirement** — of a software system *before* the actual development work begins, using historical data, mathematical models, and expert judgment.

In KTU 2024 Scheme terminology (aligned with *Software Project Management* by Bob Hughes, Mike Cotterell, Rajib Mall), estimation forms the **foundational input** for every downstream project activity: scheduling, budgeting, staffing, risk planning, and contractual negotiation. Without a credible estimate, a software project cannot be approved, funded, or scheduled.

> [!NOTE]
> **Core Estimate Variables (the "BIG 5"):**
> - **Size** — How big is the system? (LOC, FP, UCP, COSMIC)
> - **Effort** — Person-months / person-hours of work
> - **Duration** — Calendar time to deliver
> - **Cost** — Monetary budget in ₹ or USD
> - **Resources** — Team size & skill mix

## 1.2 Intuitive Analogy — "The House-Building Parallel"

Imagine a family asks a contractor: *"How much will it cost to build our house?"* Before the contractor can quote a price, he must first estimate the **size** of the house (square feet, number of rooms). Only after sizing it can he estimate materials (bricks, cement), labor (masons, electricians), time (months), and money (₹).

Software estimation works **identically**:
1. First estimate the **size** of the software (LOC / Function Points).
2. Then convert size into **effort** (how many person-months).
3. Then derive **duration, cost, and team size** from effort.

> [!TIP]
> **Golden Rule of Estimation:** *You cannot estimate effort without first estimating size.* Size is the *basis*; effort, duration, and cost are *derivatives*.

## 1.3 Why Software Estimation Is the "Basis" for Project Management

The word **"Basis"** in this module is critical — it means the *root metric* on which the entire project plan rests. KTU examiners frequently test this hierarchy:

$$
\text{Size (LOC/FP)} \;\xrightarrow{\text{Productivity}}\; \text{Effort} \;\xrightarrow{\text{Division}}\; \text{Duration} \;\xrightarrow{\text{Rate}}\; \text{Cost}
$$

| KTU Terminology | Engineering Meaning |
|---|---|
| **Size** | Volume of the deliverable software |
| **Effort** | Human work consumed (person-months) |
| **Productivity** | Output per unit input (FP/person-month) |
| **Schedule** | Calendar time (months) |
| **Cost** | Effort × burdened labor rate |

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Four Pillars of Software Size Estimation

Software estimation rests on **four well-established sizing bases**, each with its own worldview:

### Pillar 1 — Lines of Code (LOC / KLOC)
- **Philosophy:** Size = the *physical text* of the program.
- **Counting rule:** Count only *delivered, executable source instructions* (excluding comments, blank lines, and reused code).
- **Unit:** **LOC** (Lines of Code) or **KLOC** (Thousands of Lines of Code).
- **Pros:** Simple, intuitive, language-neutral after conversion.
- **Cons:** Requires coding language choice *before* sizing — a chicken-and-egg problem; penalises terse languages (Java) vs verbose (COBOL).

> [!NOTE]
> **COCOMO's LOC Definition (Barry Boehm, 1981):**
> *"A line of code is a source statement that is delivered and contributes to the functionality of the program — declarations and compiler directives excluded."*

### Pillar 2 — Function Point Analysis (FPA) — Allan Albrecht, IBM 1979
- **Philosophy:** Size = *value delivered to the user*, not code volume.
- **Five function types counted:**
  1. **External Inputs (EI)** — data entering the system from outside.
  2. **External Outputs (EO)** — data leaving the system to the outside.
  3. **External Inquiries (EQ)** — online input that produces an immediate output.
  4. **External Interface Files (EIF)** — files referenced by *another* system.
  5. **Internal Logical Files (ILF)** — files maintained *within* the system.

### Pillar 3 — Use Case Points (UCP) — Gustav Karner, 1993
- **Philosophy:** Size = *complexity of use cases and actors*.
- Derives an intermediate measure (UUCP) and adjusts for technical & environmental factors.

### Pillar 4 — COSMIC Function Points — COSMIC Consortium, 1998
- **Philosophy:** Size = *number of data movements* (Entry, Exit, Read, Write) across functional processes.
- Suitable for **real-time, embedded, and object-oriented** systems where classic FP struggles.

## 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet (High-Yield)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Memorise every formula below — KTU 2024 Scheme questions on this topic are 80% formula-driven.

| # | Formula | Meaning of Symbols | Boundary / Range |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $FP = UFP \times VAF$ | Function Point = Unadjusted FP × Value Adjustment Factor | $VAF \in [0.65,\; 1.35]$ |
| 2 | $VAF = 0.65 + 0.01 \times TDI$ | Value Adjustment Factor | $TDI \in [0,\; 70]$ |
| 3 | $UFP = \sum_{i=1}^{5} (C_i \times W_i)$ | Sum of (Count × Weight) over 5 function types | — |
| 4 | $TDI = \sum_{j=1}^{14} DI_j$ | Total Degree of Influence (14 General System Characteristics) | Each $DI_j \in [0,\; 5]$ |
| 5 | $Effort = A \times (KLOC)^B \times EAF$ | COCOMO Basic — person-months | Organic: $A=2.4, B=1.05$ |
| 6 | $Duration = C \times (Effort)^D$ | COCOMO time equation (months) | Organic: $C=2.5, D=0.38$ |
| 7 | $Productivity = FP / Effort$ | FP per person-month | Industry avg: **6–10 FP/pm** |
| 8 | $Avg\,LOC\,per\,FP$ | Language productivity ratio | Java≈53, C≈128, COBOL≈107 |
| 9 | $UCP = UUCP \times TCF \times EF$ | Use Case Points | $TCF \in [0.6,\; 1.4]$ |
| 10 | $TCF = 0.6 + 0.01 \times \sum TCF_i$ | Technical Complexity Factor (13 factors) | Each $TCF_i \in [0,\; 5]$ |
| 11 | $EF = 1.4 + (-0.03) \times \sum EF_i$ | Environmental Factor (8 factors) | Each $EF_i \in [0,\; 5]$ |
| 12 | $UUCP = UAW + UUCW$ | Unadjusted Use Case Points = Actor Weight + Use Case Weight | — |
| 13 | $CFP = \sum (E + X + R + W)$ | COSMIC FP = sum of data movements | 4 movement types |
| 14 | $Effort = KLOC / Productivity$ | Direct effort derivation | — |

## 2.3 The 14 General System Characteristics (GSCs) for VAF

These are the **14 factors** whose influence degrees ($DI_j$, rated 0–5) are summed into $TDI$. KTU has asked specific GSC lists in previous papers:

1. Data communication
2. Distributed data processing
3. Performance objectives
4. Heavily used configuration
5. Transaction rate
6. Online data entry
7. End-user efficiency
8. Online update
9. Complex processing
10. Reusability
11. Installation ease
12. Operational ease
13. Multiple sites
14. Facilitate change

## 2.4 Weight Assignment Table (Classic FPA)

| Function Type | Low | Average | High |
|---|---|---|---|
| **EI** | 3 | 4 | 6 |
| **EO** | 4 | 5 | 7 |
| **EQ** | 3 | 4 | 6 |
| **ILF** | 7 | 10 | 15 |
| **EIF** | 5 | 7 | 10 |

> [!TIP]
> **Mnemonic to remember function types:** **"I²OQ"** → **I**nternal files, **I**nterface files, **O**utputs, **Q**ueries (then Inputs).

## 2.5 LOC vs FP — When to Use Which?

| Criterion | LOC | Function Points |
|---|---|---|
| **Available early?** | ❌ Needs coding complete | ✅ Available from requirements |
| **Language dependent?** | ✅ Yes | ❌ Largely language-independent |
| **User-comprehensible?** | ❌ | ✅ (measures user functions) |
| **Used in COCOMO?** | ✅ Native input | ✅ Converted via backfiring |

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3 — Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Exhaustive Derivation — Function Point Calculation

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Worked Example (KTU-style): *A payroll system has the following:*
> - *8 External Inputs (Average complexity)*
> - *5 External Outputs (Average)*
> - *3 External Inquiries (Low)*
> - *2 Internal Logical Files (Average)*
> - *1 External Interface File (Low)*
> - *Sum of influence degrees TDI = 42*

### Step 1 — Compute Unadjusted Function Points (UFP)

Apply weights from the standard FPA table:

$$
UFP_{EI} = 8 \times 4 = 32
$$

$$
UFP_{EO} = 5 \times 5 = 25
$$

$$
UFP_{EQ} = 3 \times 3 = 9
$$

$$
UFP_{ILF} = 2 \times 10 = 20
$$

$$
UFP_{EIF} = 1 \times 5 = 5
$$

Sum all sub-totals:

$$
\begin{aligned}
UFP &= UFP_{EI} + UFP_{EO} + UFP_{EQ} + UFP_{ILF} + UFP_{EIF} \\
    &= 32 + 25 + 9 + 20 + 5 \\
    &= 91 \text{ UFP}
\end{aligned}
$$

> **Interpretation:** The system delivers *91 unadjusted function points* of user-visible functionality *before* any complexity/quality adjustment.

### Step 2 — Compute Value Adjustment Factor (VAF)

Given $TDI = 42$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
VAF &= 0.65 + 0.01 \times TDI \\
    &= 0.65 + 0.01 \times 42 \\
    &= 0.65 + 0.42 \\
    &= 1.07
\end{aligned}
$$

> **Sanity check:** $TDI=0 \Rightarrow VAF=0.65$ (minimum); $TDI=70 \Rightarrow VAF=1.35$ (maximum). Our $1.07$ sits within the valid range. ✅

### Step 3 — Compute Adjusted Function Points (FP)

$$
\begin{aligned}
FP &= UFP \times VAF \\
   &= 91 \times 1.07 \\
   &= 97.37 \text{ FP}
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 4 — Convert FP to Effort (assuming productivity = 8 FP/person-month)

$$
\begin{aligned}
Effort &= \frac{FP}{Productivity} \\
        &= \frac{97.37}{8} \\
        &= 12.17 \text{ person-months}
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 5 — Convert Effort to Duration (COCOMO Organic mode: $C=2.5, D=0.38$)

$$
\begin{aligned}
Duration &= C \times (Effort)^D \\
          &= 2.5 \times (12.17)^{0.38}
\end{aligned}
$$

Compute $(12.17)^{0.38}$:

$$
(12.17)^{0.38} = e^{0.38 \times \ln(12.17)} = e^{0.38 \times 2.4988} = e^{0.9495} \approx 2.584
$$

$$
\therefore \; Duration \approx 2.5 \times 2.584 \approx 6.46 \text{ months}
$$

### Step 6 — Compute Average Team Size

$$
\begin{aligned}
Team\,Size &= \frac{Effort}{Duration} \\
           &= \frac{12.17}{6.46} \\
           &\approx 1.88 \;\Rightarrow\; 2 \text{ persons}
\end{aligned}
$$

---

## 3.2 Analytical Matrix — Conversion Between Bases

### LOC ↔ Function Point Conversion (Backfiring Table)

> Language-dependent average LOC delivered per Function Point (used to convert estimates either direction).

| Language | Avg LOC / FP | Productivity (FP/pm) |
|---|---|---|
| Assembly | 320 | 4 |
| C | 128 | 6 |
| COBOL | 107 | 5 |
| Pascal | 90 | 6 |
| **C++** | 64 | 6 |
| **Java** | 53 | 7 |
| **Python** | 46 | 8 |
| 4GL / SQL | 16 | 10 |
| Excel macros | 6 | 12 |

**Backfiring formula:**

$$
LOC = FP \times \text{Avg LOC per FP}
$$

**Example:** A Java system is estimated at $FP = 200$.

$$
LOC = 200 \times 53 = 10{,}600 \; LOC = 10.6 \; KLOC
$$

### COCOMO Effort Constants (Reference for estimation)

| Project Class | A | B | Description |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Organic** | 2.4 | 1.05 | Small team, familiar environment |
| **Semi-Detached** | 3.0 | 1.12 | Medium team, mixed experience |
| **Embedded** | 3.6 | 1.20 | Tight constraints, real-time |

## 3.3 Worked Derivation — Use Case Points (UCP)

> **Given:** 4 Simple Actors, 2 Average Actors, 1 Complex Actor. Use cases: 3 Simple, 5 Average, 2 Complex. $\sum TCF_i = 35$, $\sum EF_i = 18$.

### Step 1 — Compute Unadjusted Actor Weight (UAW)

Actor weights: Simple=1, Average=2, Complex=3.

$$
\begin{aligned}
UAW &= (4 \times 1) + (2 \times 2) + (1 \times 3) \\
    &= 4 + 4 + 3 = 11
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 2 — Compute Unadjusted Use Case Weight (UUCW)

Use case weights: Simple=5, Average=10, Complex=15.

$$
\begin{aligned}
UUCW &= (3 \times 5) + (5 \times 10) + (2 \times 15) \\
     &= 15 + 50 + 30 = 95
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 3 — UUCP

$$
UUCP = UAW + UUCW = 11 + 95 = 106
$$

### Step 4 — TCF

$$
TCF = 0.6 + 0.01 \times 35 = 0.6 + 0.35 = 0.95
$$

### Step 5 — EF

$$
EF = 1.4 + (-0.03) \times 18 = 1.4 - 0.54 = 0.86
$$

### Step 6 — UCP

$$
UCP = UUCP \times TCF \times EF = 106 \times 0.95 \times 0.86 \approx 86.6
$$

### Step 7 — Effort (typical 20 person-hours per UCP)

$$
Effort = 86.6 \times 20 = 1732 \text{ person-hours} \approx 9.62 \text{ person-months}
$$

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4 — Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Estimation Hierarchy (Block-Level Functional Architecture)

The diagram below maps the *basis-of-estimation* cascade — every block depends on the block immediately above it.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[User Requirements SRS Document] --> B{Choose Size Basis}
    B --> C1[Lines of Code LOC]
    B --> C2[Function Points FP]
    B --> C3[Use Case Points UCP]
    B --> C4[COSMIC Function Points]
    C1 --> D[Apply Productivity Model COCOMO]
    C2 --> D
    C3 --> D
    C4 --> D
    D --> E[Effort Person Months]
    E --> F1[Duration via Calendar Equation]
    E --> F2[Cost via Labor Rate]
    E --> F3[Team Size via Effort over Duration]
    F1 --> G[Project Plan Schedule Budget Risk]
    F2 --> G
    F3 --> G
```

## 4.2 Function Point Calculation Process Flow

This flowchart shows the *exact* computational pipeline used by IFPUG-certified Function Point analysts.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph Inputs
        EI[Count External Inputs]
        EO[Count External Outputs]
        EQ[Count External Inquiries]
        ILF[Count Internal Logical Files]
        EIF[Count External Interface Files]
    end
    subgraph Weights
        WEI[Weight Table Low Avg High]
    end
    Inputs --> M1[Multiply Counts by Weights]
    WEI --> M1
    M1 --> UFP[Unadjusted Function Points UFP]
    subgraph GSC
        G1[Rate 14 General System Characteristics 0 to 5]
    end
    G1 --> TDI[Sum TDI 0 to 70]
    TDI --> VAF[VAF equals 0.65 plus 0.01 times TDI]
    UFP --> FP[FP equals UFP times VAF]
    VAF --> FP
    FP --> EFF[Effort equals FP divided by Productivity]
```

## 4.3 Comparative Positioning of the Four Size Bases

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph CodeCentric[Code Centric Methods]
        L1[LOC KLOC]
    end
    subgraph UserCentric[User Function Centric]
        L2[Function Points FPA]
    end
    subgraph ActorCentric[Use Case Centric]
        L3[Use Case Points UCP]
    end
    subgraph MovementCentric[Data Movement Centric]
        L4[COSMIC Function Points]
    end
    L1 --> M1[Need code to size - late]
    L2 --> M2[Size from requirements - early]
    L3 --> M3[Size from use cases - early]
    L4 --> M4[Size from functional processes - very early]
    M1 --> N1[Best for maintenance estimation]
    M2 --> N2[Best for MIS and business apps]
    M3 --> N3[Best for OO and UML projects]
    M4 --> N4[Best for real time embedded systems]
```

## 4.4 VAF Sensitivity Boundary Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[TDI equals 0] --> B[VAF equals 0.65 minimum impact]
    C[TDI equals 35] --> D[VAF equals 1.00 neutral baseline]
    E[TDI equals 70] --> F[VAF equals 1.35 maximum impact]
    G[Best Case TDI 0] --> H[FP shrinks by 35 percent]
    I[Worst Case TDI 70] --> J[FP grows by 35 percent]
    D --> K[Ideal adjusted FP equals UFP]
```

> [!TIP]
> **Board-Exam Visual Hint:** When a KTU examiner asks *"Why is FP more reliable than LOC?"*, draw the hierarchy diagram (4.1) showing FP is available at the **requirements stage**, while LOC requires completed code — making FP the *earlier and more strategic* basis.

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]
**List and explain the five components used in Function Point Analysis (FPA).**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
The five function types counted in FPA are:
1. **External Inputs (EI):** Data entering the system from external sources that update internal files. *Example: a user-submitted registration form.*
2. **External Outputs (EO):** Reports or messages leaving the system. *Example: a generated pay-slip.*
3. **External Inquiries (EQ):** Online inputs that retrieve data without updating files. *Example: searching a customer record.*
4. **Internal Logical Files (ILF):** Logical data maintained within the application boundary. *Example: an in-house `Employee` table.*
5. **External Interface Files (EIF):** Files referenced but not maintained by the system. *Example: a third-party tax-rate API table.*

Each is rated as Low/Average/High and multiplied by a standard weight table to compute $UFP$.

*(1 mark for listing all 5; 1 mark for correct definitions; 1 mark for example OR weight mention.)*

---

### Q2. [KTU University Exam – June 2024]
**Why is Function Point Analysis preferred over LOC for early software estimation?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
1. **Availability at requirements stage:** FP can be computed from the SRS *before coding begins*, whereas LOC requires the source code to exist. *(1 mark)*
2. **Language independence:** FP measures user-visible functionality and is unaffected by the choice of programming language; LOC penalises terse languages. *(1 mark)*
3. **Better user understanding:** Customers comprehend "97 function points" more readily than "10,600 LOC". This improves contractual clarity and project tracking. *(1 mark)*

---

## Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Module Internal Choice)

---

### QUESTION A (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]`

> **A (a)** Define Function Point Analysis. Compute the Unadjusted Function Points (UFP) for the following system using the standard FPA weight table. *(7 marks)*

| Function Type | Count | Complexity |
|---|---|---|
| External Inputs | 10 | Average |
| External Outputs | 6 | Average |
| External Inquiries | 4 | Low |
| Internal Logical Files | 3 | High |
| External Interface Files | 2 | Average |

#### Model Solution (a):

**Definition (2 marks):** FPA, developed by Allan Albrecht at IBM in 1979, is a method to measure software size by quantifying the user-visible functionality delivered by the system. It is independent of programming language and is computed from the five function types: EI, EO, EQ, ILF, and EIF.

**Computation (5 marks):**

Apply the weight table:

- $EI$ Average $= 4$
- $EO$ Average $= 5$
- $EQ$ Low $= 3$
- $ILF$ High $= 15$
- $EIF$ Average $= 7$

$$
\begin{aligned}
UFP_{EI} &= 10 \times 4 = 40 \\
UFP_{EO} &= 6 \times 5 = 30 \\
UFP_{EQ} &= 4 \times 3 = 12 \\
UFP_{ILF} &= 3 \times 15 = 45 \\
UFP_{EIF} &= 2 \times 7 = 14
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
UFP &= 40 + 30 + 12 + 45 + 14 \\
    &= 141 \text{ Function Points}
\end{aligned}
$$

---

> **A (b)** If the sum of 14 General System Characteristics (TDI) is **45**, compute the adjusted FP and estimate the effort in person-months assuming a productivity of **9 FP/person-month**. Also, estimate the project duration using COCOMO Organic mode. *(7 marks)*

#### Model Solution (b):

**Step 1 — VAF (1 mark):**

$$
VAF = 0.65 + 0.01 \times 45 = 0.65 + 0.45 = 1.10
$$

**Step 2 — Adjusted FP (1 mark):**

$$
FP = UFP \times VAF = 141 \times 1.10 = 155.1 \; FP
$$

**Step 3 — Effort (2 marks):**

$$
Effort = \frac{FP}{Productivity} = \frac{155.1}{9} \approx 17.23 \text{ person-months}
$$

**Step 4 — Duration using COCOMO Organic ($C=2.5, D=0.38$) (3 marks):**

$$
Duration = 2.5 \times (17.23)^{0.38}
$$

Compute the exponent:

$$
(17.23)^{0.38} = e^{0.38 \times \ln(17.23)} = e^{0.38 \times 2.847} = e^{1.082} \approx 2.95
$$

$$
Duration \approx 2.5 \times 2.95 \approx 7.37 \text{ months}
$$

**Final Answer:** $FP = 155.1$, $Effort = 17.23$ person-months, $Duration \approx 7.37$ months.

---

### QUESTION B (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`

> **B (a)** With a neat diagram, explain the four bases of software size estimation. Compare LOC and Function Points on **at least four** parameters. *(7 marks)*

#### Model Solution (a):

**Diagram (2 marks):** *(Draw a block diagram showing the four pillars — LOC, FP, UCP, COSMIC — feeding into Effort, which then derives Duration, Cost, and Team Size. Reference Section 4.1.)*

**Explanation (3 marks):**
- **LOC (Lines of Code):** Physical source-line measure; used in COCOMO; language-dependent.
- **FPA (Function Points):** User-function measure based on EI, EO, EQ, ILF, EIF; IFPUG standard.
- **UCP (Use Case Points):** OO measure based on actor and use-case complexity.
- **COSMIC FP:** Data-movement measure (Entry, Exit, Read, Write) for real-time systems.

**Comparison Table (2 marks):**

| Parameter | LOC | Function Points |
|---|---|---|
| Stage available | After coding | At requirements stage |
| Language dependency | High | None |
| Customer comprehension | Low | High |
| Suitable for maintenance | Yes | Limited |
| Used by COCOMO | Yes | After backfiring conversion |

---

> **B (b)** A project is estimated at **250 Function Points**. The development language is **Java**. Convert this to KLOC and estimate effort using COCOMO basic model (Organic: $A=2.4, B=1.05$). Assume EAF = 1.0. *(7 marks)*

#### Model Solution (b):

**Step 1 — Convert FP to LOC (2 marks):**
Java average LOC per FP = **53** (from backfiring table).

$$
LOC = 250 \times 53 = 13{,}250 \; LOC = 13.25 \; KLOC
$$

**Step 2 — Apply COCOMO Basic formula (3 marks):**

$$
Effort = A \times (KLOC)^B \times EAF
$$

$$
Effort = 2.4 \times (13.25)^{1.05} \times 1.0
$$

Compute $(13.25)^{1.05}$:

$$
(13.25)^{1.05} = e^{1.05 \times \ln(13.25)} = e^{1.05 \times 2.584} = e^{2.713} \approx 15.06
$$

$$
Effort \approx 2.4 \times 15.06 \approx 36.14 \text{ person-months}
$$

**Step 3 — Duration (2 marks):**
Using $C=2.5, D=0.38$:

$$
Duration = 2.5 \times (36.14)^{0.38}
$$

$$
(36.14)^{0.38} = e^{0.38 \times 3.586} = e^{1.363} \approx 3.91
$$

$$
Duration \approx 2.5 \times 3.91 \approx 9.77 \text{ months}
$$

**Final Answer:** $KLOC = 13.25$, $Effort = 36.14$ person-months, $Duration \approx 9.77$ months.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls on This Topic:**
> 1. **Forgetting EAF** in COCOMO — full marks lost on Effort calculation. Always write the formula with $EAF$ explicitly, even if value is 1.0.
> 2. **Using wrong COCOMO mode constants** — Students mix Organic and Embedded constants. Memorise $A,B$ triplet (2.4/1.05, 3.0/1.12, 3.6/1.20).
> 3. **VAF range violation** — If your computed VAF < 0.65 or > 1.35, you have made an arithmetic error. Always sanity-check the bound.
> 4. **Backfiring direction confusion** — *FP → LOC uses multiplication; LOC → FP uses division by language ratio.*
> 5. **Counting comments / blank lines as LOC** — COCOMO explicitly excludes them. Examiners deduct 1 mark.
> 6. **UCP without TCF and EF** — Bare UUCP without adjustments scores zero on the "adjusted" UCP question.

---

## Topic Recap — Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Revision Checklist for KTU Board Exam:**

- [x] **Hierarchy:** Size → Effort → Duration → Cost → Team (never invert).
- [x] **Five FPA components:** EI, EO, EQ, ILF, EIF (mnemonic **I²OQ** + I).
- [x] **Weight table:** Low / Average / High → memorise EI=3/4/6, EO=4/5/7, EQ=3/4/6, ILF=7/10/15, EIF=5/7/10.
- [x] **VAF formula:** $0.65 + 0.01 \times TDI$, where $TDI = \sum_{j=1}^{14} DI_j$, $DI_j \in [0,5]$.
- [x] **VAF bounds:** $[0.65,\; 1.35]$ — never exceed.
- [x] **COCOMO Basic:** $Effort = A \times (KLOC)^B \times EAF$.
- [x] **Constants to memorise:** Organic (2.4, 1.05, 2.5, 0.38); Semi-Detached (3.0, 1.12, 2.5, 0.35); Embedded (3.6, 1.20, 2.5, 0.32).
- [x] **Backfiring table:** Java = 53, C = 128, Python = 46, COBOL = 107 LOC/FP.
- [x] **UCP formula:** $UCP = UUCP \times TCF \times EF$; $UUCP = UAW + UUCW$; $TCF \in [0.6, 1.4]$.
- [x] **EF formula:** $1.4 - 0.03 \times \sum EF_i$.
- [x] **COSMIC FP** counts only **4 data movement types**: Entry, Exit, Read, Write.
- [x] **Productivity** typical range: **6 – 10 FP / person-month**.
- [x] **LOC counting rule:** *Delivered, executable source instructions*; exclude comments, blanks, reused code.
- [x] **Effort unit:** Person-months (1 person-month = 152 person-hours in COCOMO).

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Software Effort Estimation Techniques
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEITT633 - SOFTWARE PROJECT MANAGEMENT
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Software Effort Estimation Techniques

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

# 📘 Software Effort Estimation Techniques — Module 2

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> **Software Effort Estimation** is the systematic process of predicting the **quantitative effort** (typically measured in **Person-Months (PM)** or **Person-Hours**) required to complete a software project, encompassing development, testing, deployment, and maintenance activities. As per the KTU 2024 Scheme (aligned with the SWEBOK Guide and IEEE Software Engineering Standards), effort estimation is one of the most critical activities in the **Software Project Planning** phase and forms the basis for schedule, cost, and resource planning.

Under the KTU syllabus framework, effort estimation is classified as a **planning-level decision-making activity** that operates under conditions of **uncertainty**, particularly during the early phases of the Software Development Life Cycle (SDLC) when the software architecture, detailed requirements, and final code structure are not yet fully defined.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!TIP]
> **Real-World Analogy — The Renovation Contractor**

Imagine you are renovating a 1,500 sq. ft. house. Before signing the contract, the contractor must estimate **how much time, money, and manpower** the job will require. He has three ways to arrive at this estimate:
>
> 1. **Experience-based (Expert Judgment):** "I have done 100 such renovations — this one should take about 4 months."
> 2. **Comparison-based (Analogous Estimation):** "I did a similar 1,500 sq. ft. house last year in 5 months; this one has a fancy kitchen, so maybe 6 months."
> 3. **Calculation-based (Parametric Models):** "For a 1,500 sq. ft. house with a modern kitchen, my formula says: Effort = Area × Complexity × Labor Rate = roughly 7 months."
>
> Software Effort Estimation works **exactly the same way**. The "house" is the **software project**, the "area" is the **software size** (Lines of Code, Function Points), and the "complexity" is determined by project characteristics like reliability, database size, and execution time constraints. The estimator (project manager) uses one or a combination of these techniques to forecast effort.

### 1.3 Importance in KTU 2024 Curriculum

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why this topic is a HIGH-YIELD KTU Module 2 Topic:**
> - Effort estimation is the **foundation of all project planning**.
> - Questions are regularly asked in both **University Examinations (ESE)** and **Continuous Internal Evaluations (CIE)**.
> - Marks distribution typically carries **7–14 mark analytical questions** involving COCOMO calculations.
> - Directly maps to **Course Outcome (CO2)** of most KTU SPM courses: *"Estimate the effort, cost, and schedule of a software project using standard models."*

### 1.4 Classification of Software Effort Estimation Techniques

Software effort estimation techniques are broadly classified into **5 major categories** by the KTU syllabus:

1. **Expert Judgment Based Techniques** (Delphi, Wideband Delphi)
2. **Analogy / Comparison-Based Techniques** (Analogous Estimation)
3. **Algorithmic / Parametric Techniques** (COCOMO, COCOMO II, Function Point Analysis)
4. **Machine Learning Based Techniques** (CBR, Regression Trees, ANN)
5. **Combination / Hybrid Techniques** (Expert Judgment + Algorithmic Fusion)

> [!NOTE]
> **Standard Metric Definitions used throughout this module:**
> - **Effort (E):** Person-Months (PM)
> - **Schedule (T):** Months
> - **Software Size:** KLOC (Thousand Lines of Code) or FP (Function Points)
> - **Productivity (P):** KLOC / PM or FP / PM
> - **Cost per Person-Month:** Currency (₹, $, €)

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Algorithmic Estimation Models — The COCOMO Suite

#### 2.1.1 Basic COCOMO Model (Boehm, 1981)

The **Constructive Cost Model (COCOMO)** proposed by **Barry W. Boehm** is the most widely tested and academically referenced estimation model in KTU examinations. The **Basic COCOMO** estimates effort as a function of **KLOC (Kilo Lines of Code)** raised to a power, scaled by a project-type multiplier.

#### 2.1.2 Intermediate COCOMO Model

The **Intermediate COCOMO** extends Basic COCOMO by introducing **15 cost drivers** (multipliers) that adjust the nominal effort based on product, hardware, personnel, and project attributes.

#### 2.1.3 Detailed COCOMO Model

The **Detailed COCOMO** further refines intermediate COCOMO by applying cost drivers at **3 different phases** of the project (product, design, implementation) rather than project-wide.

### 2.2 Operational Logic Step-by-Step (Basic COCOMO)

| Step | Operation | Description |
|:---:|:---|:---|
| 1 | **Determine Software Size** | Estimate KLOC from requirements (typically via Function Points → Backfiring). |
| 2 | **Classify Project Type** | Identify as **Organic**, **Semi-Detached**, or **Embedded**. |
| 3 | **Identify Coefficients** | Select `a` and `b` from the project type lookup table. |
| 4 | **Compute Nominal Effort** | Apply $E = a \cdot (\text{KLOC})^b$ to get nominal effort in **Person-Months**. |
| 5 | **Adjust with EAF (Intermediate)** | Multiply by **Effort Adjustment Factor** (product of 15 cost driver ratings). |
| 6 | **Compute Schedule** | Use $T = c \cdot E^d$ to derive development time. |
| 7 | **Compute Average Staffing** | Use $\text{Avg Staff} = E / T$. |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The 3 Project Modes in COCOMO:**
> - **Organic Mode:** Small teams, simple applications, in-house environment. Coefficients: $a = 2.4$, $b = 1.05$.
> - **Semi-Detached Mode:** Medium-size teams, mixed experience, medium complexity. Coefficients: $a = 3.0$, $b = 1.12$.
> - **Embedded Mode:** Tight constraints (real-time, safety-critical). Coefficients: $a = 3.6$, $b = 1.20$.

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> **All formulas below are required for solving KTU Module 2 numerical problems.**

| # | Model | Formula | Variables / Units |
|:---:|:---|:---|:---|
| 1 | **Basic COCOMO — Effort** | $E = a \cdot (\text{KLOC})^b$ | $E$ = Effort (PM), KLOC = Thousand Lines of Code, $a,b$ = mode coefficients |
| 2 | **Basic COCOMO — Schedule** | $T_{\text{dev}} = c \cdot (E)^d$ | $T_{\text{dev}}$ = Development Time (Months), $c,d$ = mode coefficients |
| 3 | **Average Staff Size** | $\text{Staff} = E / T_{\text{dev}}$ | Staff in persons |
| 4 | **Productivity** | $P = \text{KLOC} / E$ | KLOC / PM |
| 5 | **Intermediate COCOMO** | $E = a \cdot (\text{KLOC})^b \cdot \text{EAF}$ | EAF = Effort Adjustment Factor |
| 6 | **EAF** | $\text{EAF} = \prod_{i=1}^{15} \text{EM}_i$ | EM = Effort Multiplier for each cost driver |
| 7 | **Function Point to LOC** | $\text{LOC} = \text{FP} \times \text{LOC}_{\text{per\_FP}}$ | LOC per FP varies by language |
| 8 | **Function Point (FP)** | $\text{FP} = \text{UFP} \times \text{VAF}$ | UFP = Unadjusted FP, VAF = Value Adjustment Factor |
| 9 | **VAF** | $\text{VAF} = 0.65 + 0.01 \cdot \sum_{i=1}^{14} \text{FI}_i$ | FI = Environmental Complexity Factor (0–5) |
| 10 | **Use Case Points** | $\text{UCP} = (\text{UUCW} + \text{UAW}) \times \text{TCF} \times \text{ECF}$ | UUCW = Unadjusted Use Case Weight, UAW = Unadjusted Actor Weight |
| 11 | **Three-Point / PERT** | $E = \dfrac{O + 4M + P}{6}$ | O = Optimistic, M = Most Likely, P = Pessimistic |
| 12 | **Standard Deviation (PERT)** | $\sigma = \dfrac{P - O}{6}$ | Range uncertainty |
| 13 | **Wideband Delphi** | Consensus-based iterative estimation | Multiple rounds, convergence to consensus |

### 2.4 Function Point (FP) Analysis

**Function Point Analysis (FPA)** was introduced by **Allan Albrecht** at IBM (1979) to provide a **language-independent** measure of software size. FP analysis considers the **functionality delivered to the user**, independent of programming language.

The 5 functional components measured:

1. **External Inputs (EI)** — User inputs that update internal files.
2. **External Outputs (EO)** — Outputs that provide information to users (reports, screens).
3. **External Inquiries (EQ)** — Interactive inputs that retrieve data.
4. **External Interface Files (EIF)** — Files referenced by the system but maintained by other systems.
5. **Internal Logical Files (ILF)** — Files maintained within the system boundary.

### 2.5 COCOMO II (Brief Overview)

> [!NOTE]
> **COCOMO II** is the successor to COCOMO and is used for **modern, object-oriented, and spiral development**. It consists of **3 sub-models**:
> - **Application Composition Model:** Used during early prototyping.
> - **Early Design Model:** Used after requirements are defined, before architecture.
> - **Post-Architecture Model:** Used after architecture is established (most detailed).

The **Post-Architecture COCOMO II** formula is:

$$E = A \cdot (\text{Size})^B \cdot \prod_{i=1}^{n} \text{EM}_i$$

where **Size** is measured in **KSLOC** (or **Function Points**), **A** is a calibration constant (typically **2.94**), and **EM** are 17 cost drivers.

### 2.6 Use Case Points (UCP) — Modern OO Estimation

The **Use Case Point (UCP)** method, developed by **Gustafson Karner (2000)**, adapts FPA for object-oriented projects. The steps are:

1. **Classify Actors** as Simple, Average, or Complex (assign weights).
2. **Classify Use Cases** as Simple, Average, or Complex (assign weights).
3. **Compute UUCW + UAW** (Unadjusted Use Case Weight + Unadjusted Actor Weight).
4. **Compute TCF** (Technical Complexity Factor).
5. **Compute ECF** (Environmental Complexity Factor).
6. **Final formula:** $\text{UCP} = (\text{UUCW} + \text{UAW}) \times \text{TCF} \times \text{ECF}$
7. **Effort:** $E = \text{UCP} \times \text{PF}$ (Productivity Factor).

### 2.7 Real-World Engineering Utility

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Where these techniques are used in industry:**
> - **COCOMO:** Used by US DoD, NASA, defence software procurements.
> - **FP Analysis:** Used by Indian IT giants (TCS, Infosys, Wipro) for fixed-price contracts.
> - **UCP:** Used by Rational Rose / IBM Rational for OO projects.
> - **PERT Estimation:** Used in risk-driven scheduling and probabilistic contracts.
> - **Story Points:** Used in **Agile/Scrum** teams (relative estimation via Planning Poker).

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->

## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Problem 1 — Basic COCOMO Calculation

> [!NOTE]
> **Worked-Out Numerical Problem (KTU Board Pattern)**

**Question:** A software project is estimated to be **33.6 KLOC** in size. The project type is **Semi-Detached**. Calculate:
1. Nominal Effort (Person-Months)
2. Development Time (Months)
3. Average Staffing Required
4. Productivity (KLOC / PM)

**Given Data:**
- KLOC = **33.6**
- Project Mode = **Semi-Detached**
- For Semi-Detached: $a = 3.0$, $b = 1.12$, $c = 2.5$, $d = 0.35$

#### Step 1 — Nominal Effort Calculation

We begin by applying the Basic COCOMO effort equation:

$$E = a \cdot (\text{KLOC})^b$$

Substituting the values:

$$E = 3.0 \cdot (33.6)^{1.12}$$

We now compute the exponent term:

$$\begin{aligned}
\log_{10}(33.6) &= 1.5263 \\
1.12 \times 1.5263 &= 1.7095 \\
(33.6)^{1.12} &= 10^{1.7095} \\
&= 51.23
\end{aligned}$$

Therefore:

$$\begin{aligned}
E &= 3.0 \times 51.23 \\
E &= 153.69 \text{ Person-Months}
\end{aligned}$$

#### Step 2 — Development Time Calculation

$$T_{\text{dev}} = c \cdot (E)^d$$

Substituting:

$$T_{\text{dev}} = 2.5 \cdot (153.69)^{0.35}$$

Computing the power:

$$\begin{aligned}
\log_{10}(153.69) &= 2.1865 \\
0.35 \times 2.1865 &= 0.7653 \\
(153.69)^{0.35} &= 10^{0.7653} \\
&= 5.828
\end{aligned}$$

Therefore:

$$\begin{aligned}
T_{\text{dev}} &= 2.5 \times 5.828 \\
T_{\text{dev}} &= 14.57 \text{ Months}
\end{aligned}$$

#### Step 3 — Average Staffing

$$\begin{aligned}
\text{Staff} &= E / T_{\text{dev}} \\
&= 153.69 / 14.57 \\
&= 10.55 \approx 11 \text{ Persons}
\end{aligned}$$

#### Step 4 — Productivity

$$\begin{aligned}
P &= \text{KLOC} / E \\
&= 33.6 / 153.69 \\
&= 0.2186 \text{ KLOC / PM}
\end{aligned}$$

**Final Answer Box:**

| Parameter | Symbol | Value |
|:---|:---|:---|
| Nominal Effort | $E$ | **153.69 PM** |
| Development Time | $T_{\text{dev}}$ | **14.57 Months** |
| Average Staff | $\text{Staff}$ | **11 Persons** |
| Productivity | $P$ | **0.2186 KLOC/PM** |

### 3.2 Problem 2 — Intermediate COCOMO with EAF

**Question:** For the same project as above, the project manager has rated 4 cost drivers as follows:
- **RELY (Required Reliability):** High = **1.15**
- **DATA (Database Size):** High = **1.08**
- **TIME (Execution Time Constraint):** High = **1.11**
- **CPLX (Product Complexity):** Nominal = **1.00**
- All other 11 cost drivers are **Nominal = 1.00**.

Calculate the **adjusted effort**.

#### Step 1 — Compute EAF

The EAF is the product of all 15 Effort Multipliers (EM). Since 11 are nominal (1.00), they contribute nothing to the multiplication:

$$\begin{aligned}
\text{EAF} &= 1.15 \times 1.08 \times 1.11 \times 1.00 \times \underbrace{1 \times 1 \times \ldots \times 1}_{11 \text{ times}} \\
&= 1.15 \times 1.08 \times 1.11 \\
&= 1.3786
\end{aligned}$$

#### Step 2 — Adjusted Effort

$$E_{\text{adj}} = a \cdot (\text{KLOC})^b \cdot \text{EAF}$$

$$E_{\text{adj}} = 3.0 \times (33.6)^{1.12} \times 1.3786$$

$$E_{\text{adj}} = 153.69 \times 1.3786 = 211.89 \text{ PM}$$

> [!TIP]
> **Key Insight:** The 4 non-nominal cost drivers increased effort from **153.69 PM** to **211.89 PM** — an increase of **~37.9%**. This demonstrates the **enormous impact** of even a few non-nominal cost drivers.

### 3.3 Problem 3 — Three-Point / PERT Estimation

**Question:** A project has been estimated using three-point estimation:
- Optimistic (O) = **40 Person-Months**
- Most Likely (M) = **60 Person-Months**
- Pessimistic (P) = **120 Person-Months**

Calculate the expected effort and standard deviation.

#### Step 1 — Expected Effort

$$E_{\text{exp}} = \dfrac{O + 4M + P}{6}$$

$$E_{\text{exp}} = \dfrac{40 + 4(60) + 120}{6}$$

$$E_{\text{exp}} = \dfrac{40 + 240 + 120}{6} = \dfrac{400}{6} = 66.67 \text{ PM}$$

#### Step 2 — Standard Deviation

$$\sigma = \dfrac{P - O}{6} = \dfrac{120 - 40}{6} = \dfrac{80}{6} = 13.33 \text{ PM}$$

#### Step 3 — 95% Confidence Interval

The 95% confidence range is $E_{\text{exp}} \pm 2\sigma$:

$$\begin{aligned}
\text{Lower Bound} &= 66.67 - 2(13.33) = 40.01 \text{ PM} \\
\text{Upper Bound} &= 66.67 + 2(13.33) = 93.33 \text{ PM}
\end{aligned}$$

**Interpretation:** We are **95% confident** the project effort lies between **40 PM** and **93.33 PM**.

### 3.4 Problem 4 — Function Point to KLOC Conversion

**Question:** A software project has been estimated at **500 Function Points**. The estimated language is **C**, with an average of **128 LOC per FP**. Calculate the equivalent KLOC.

#### Step 1 — LOC Calculation

$$\text{LOC} = \text{FP} \times \text{LOC}_{\text{per\_FP}}$$

$$\text{LOC} = 500 \times 128 = 64{,}000 \text{ LOC}$$

#### Step 2 — Convert to KLOC

$$\text{KLOC} = 64{,}000 / 1000 = 64 \text{ KLOC}$$

This KLOC value can now be plugged directly into the COCOMO formula for effort estimation.

### 3.5 Analytical Matrix — Comparison of Techniques

| # | Technique | Basis | Best For | Accuracy | Difficulty |
|:---:|:---|:---|:---|:---|:---|
| 1 | **Expert Judgment** | Historical experience | Early-stage estimates | Low–Medium | Low |
| 2 | **Analogous Estimation** | Comparison to past projects | Similar projects | Medium | Low |
| 3 | **COCOMO (Basic)** | KLOC + project mode | When size is known | Medium | Medium |
| 4 | **COCOMO (Intermediate)** | KLOC + 15 cost drivers | Detailed planning | High | High |
| 5 | **COCOMO II** | Size + scale factors | OO / modern projects | High | High |
| 6 | **Function Point** | User functionality | Size measurement | Medium | Medium |
| 7 | **Use Case Points** | Actors + Use Cases | OO projects | Medium | Medium |
| 8 | **PERT / 3-Point** | O, M, P estimates | Risk-aware estimates | High (under uncertainty) | Low |
| 9 | **Wideband Delphi** | Iterative consensus | Removing bias | High | Medium |
| 10 | **Story Points (Agile)** | Relative sizing | Scrum / Agile | Medium | Low |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Mermaid Diagram — Estimation Workflow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Project Requirements] --> B[Determine Software Size]
    B --> B1[Lines of Code KLOC]
    B --> B2[Function Points FP]
    B --> B3[Use Case Points UCP]
    B1 --> C{Choose Estimation Model}
    B2 --> C
    B3 --> C
    C --> C1[Basic COCOMO]
    C --> C2[Intermediate COCOMO]
    C --> C3[COCOMO II]
    C --> C4[PERT / 3-Point]
    C --> C5[Expert Judgment]
    C1 --> D[Compute Nominal Effort]
    C2 --> D
    C3 --> D
    C4 --> D
    C5 --> D
    D --> E[Apply Cost Drivers / EAF]
    E --> F[Adjusted Effort in Person-Months]
    F --> G[Compute Schedule Tdev]
    G --> H[Compute Average Staffing]
    H --> I[Final Project Plan]
```

### 4.2 Mermaid Diagram — COCOMO Project Mode Classification

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Identify Project Type] --> B{Application Complexity?}
    B -->|Low - Inhouse| C[Organic Mode]
    B -->|Medium - Mixed| D[Semi-Detached Mode]
    B -->|High - Real-time Safety| E[Embedded Mode]
    C --> F[Coefficients a=2.4 b=1.05]
    D --> G[Coefficients a=3.0 b=1.12]
    E --> H[Coefficients a=3.6 b=1.20]
    F --> I[Apply Effort Equation]
    G --> I
    H --> I
    I --> J[Final Effort Estimate]
```

### 4.3 Mermaid Diagram — Function Point Analysis Block Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Determine 5 Function Components] --> A1[External Inputs EI]
    A --> A2[External Outputs EO]
    A --> A3[External Inquiries EQ]
    A --> A4[Internal Logical Files ILF]
    A --> A5[External Interface Files EIF]
    A1 --> B[Assign Complexity Weights]
    A2 --> B
    A3 --> B
    A4 --> B
    A5 --> B
    B --> C[Compute Unadjusted FP UFP]
    C --> D[Determine 14 General System Characteristics]
    D --> E[Compute VAF = 0.65 plus 0.01 times sum FI]
    E --> F[Final FP = UFP times VAF]
    F --> G[Convert to LOC using Language Tables]
    G --> H[Input into COCOMO for Effort]
```

### 4.4 Mermaid Diagram — PERT Estimation Distribution

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Optimistic O] --> C[PERT Weighted Average]
    B[Most Likely M] --> C
    D[Pessimistic P] --> C
    C --> E[Expected Effort E = O + 4M + P over 6]
    D --> F[Standard Deviation sigma]
    A --> F
    F --> G[95 percent CI: E plus minus 2 sigma]
    E --> G
    G --> H[Final Range Estimate]
```

### 4.5 Mermaid Diagram — Wideband Delphi Iteration

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Round 1 - Anonymous Independent Estimate] --> B[Coordinator Aggregates Estimates]
    B --> C[Compute Median and Spread]
    C --> D{Difference less than Threshold?}
    D -->|No| E[Provide Anonymous Feedback to Estimators]
    E --> A
    D -->|Yes| F[Consensus Reached]
    F --> G[Final Effort Estimate]
```

### 4.6 Block-Level Functional Architecture — Cost Drivers in Intermediate COCOMO

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Intermediate COCOMO - 15 Cost Drivers] --> B[Product Attributes]
    A --> C[Hardware Attributes]
    A --> D[Personnel Attributes]
    A --> E[Project Attributes]
    B --> B1[RELY Required Reliability]
    B --> B2[DATA Database Size]
    B --> B3[CPLX Product Complexity]
    C --> C1[TIME Execution Time]
    C --> C2[STOR Main Storage]
    C --> C3[VIRT Virtual Machine Volatility]
    C --> C4[TURN Computer Turnaround Time]
    D --> D1[ACAP Analyst Capability]
    D --> D2[AEXP Applications Experience]
    D --> D3[PCAP Programmer Capability]
    D --> D4[VEXP Virtual Machine Experience]
    D --> D5[LEXP Language Experience]
    E --> E1[MODP Modern Programming Practices]
    E --> E2[TOOL Software Tools Usage]
    E --> E3[SCED Schedule Constraint]
    B1 --> F[EAF = Product of all 15 EMi]
    B2 --> F
    B3 --> F
    C1 --> F
    C2 --> F
    C3 --> F
    C4 --> F
    D1 --> F
    D2 --> F
    D3 --> F
    D4 --> F
    D5 --> F
    E1 --> F
    E2 --> F
    E3 --> F
    F --> G[Adjusted Effort = Nominal Effort times EAF]
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->

## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

#### **Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2023]**

**What is meant by the term "Effort" in software project management? List any two software effort estimation techniques.**

**Model Answer:**

In software project management, **Effort** refers to the total amount of **human labor** required to complete a software project, typically measured in **Person-Months (PM)** or **Person-Hours (PH)**. Effort encapsulates all activities including requirements gathering, design, coding, testing, and deployment. It is a **planning-level estimate** made during the early phases of the SDLC and serves as the basis for cost and schedule computation.

Two commonly used software effort estimation techniques are:
1. **Expert Judgment Method** — Based on historical experience and expert opinion.
2. **COCOMO Model** — An algorithmic, parametric model proposed by Boehm using KLOC and project type.

#### **Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2022]**

**Differentiate between Basic COCOMO and Intermediate COCOMO models.**

**Model Answer:**

| Feature | Basic COCOMO | Intermediate COCOMO |
|:---|:---|:---|
| **Formula** | $E = a \cdot (\text{KLOC})^b$ | $E = a \cdot (\text{KLOC})^b \cdot \text{EAF}$ |
| **Inputs** | Size (KLOC), Project Mode | Size (KLOC), Mode, **15 Cost Drivers** |
| **Accuracy** | Lower | Higher |
| **Complexity** | Simple to apply | Requires detailed cost driver assessment |
| **Phases** | Project-wide estimate | Still project-wide but with adjusted multipliers |

### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning:**
> - Always **show the table of coefficients** before substituting in COCOMO — failing to identify the correct project mode costs 2–3 marks.
> - **Show every intermediate calculation step** (e.g., $\log_{10}$ conversions) — examiners allocate marks for **methodology**, not just final answer.
> - **Convert units carefully:** LOC vs KLOC vs MLOC is a frequent error source.
> - **For 14-mark questions, you must solve BOTH sub-parts (a) AND (b)**. Do not stop after (a).

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam – May 2024]**

**(a)** Explain the **Basic COCOMO model** in detail with the formulae for effort, development time, and average staffing. List the **three project modes** with their respective coefficients. **[7 Marks]**

**(b)** A software project is classified as **Organic** with an estimated size of **8 KLOC**. Compute the **nominal effort**, **development time**, and **average staffing** using Basic COCOMO. **[7 Marks]**

---

##### **Model Solution — Part (a)**

The Basic COCOMO (Constructive Cost Model) was proposed by **Barry W. Boehm** in 1981. It is the simplest form of COCOMO, providing a quick estimation of effort, schedule, and staffing using only the **estimated software size (in KLOC)** and the **project mode**.

The three fundamental equations of Basic COCOMO are:

$$E = a \cdot (\text{KLOC})^b \quad \text{(Effort in Person-Months)}$$

$$T_{\text{dev}} = c \cdot (E)^d \quad \text{(Development Time in Months)}$$

$$\text{Staff} = E / T_{\text{dev}} \quad \text{(Average Number of Personnel)}$$

The **three project modes** and their coefficients are:

| Mode | Description | $a$ | $b$ | $c$ | $d$ |
|:---|:---|:---:|:---:|:---:|:---:|
| **Organic** | Small teams, simple applications, in-house | **2.4** | **1.05** | **2.5** | **0.38** |
| **Semi-Detached** | Medium-size teams, mixed experience | **3.0** | **1.12** | **2.5** | **0.35** |
| **Embedded** | Tight constraints, real-time / safety-critical | **3.6** | **1.20** | **2.5** | **0.32** |

Basic COCOMO is **fast, easy, and requires minimal data**, but it does **not** account for differences in hardware, personnel capability, or product complexity — hence its accuracy is limited.

##### **Model Solution — Part (b)**

**Given:**
- Project Mode = **Organic**
- Size = **8 KLOC**
- From table: $a = 2.4$, $b = 1.05$, $c = 2.5$, $d = 0.38$

**Step 1 — Nominal Effort:**

$$E = a \cdot (\text{KLOC})^b = 2.4 \cdot (8)^{1.05}$$

Computing $(8)^{1.05}$:

$$\begin{aligned}
\log_{10}(8) &= 0.9031 \\
1.05 \times 0.9031 &= 0.9483 \\
(8)^{1.05} &= 10^{0.9483} = 8.875
\end{aligned}$$

Therefore:

$$E = 2.4 \times 8.875 = 21.30 \text{ Person-Months}$$

**Step 2 — Development Time:**

$$T_{\text{dev}} = c \cdot (E)^d = 2.5 \cdot (21.30)^{0.38}$$

Computing $(21.30)^{0.38}$:

$$\begin{aligned}
\log_{10}(21.30) &= 1.3284 \\
0.38 \times 1.3284 &= 0.5048 \\
(21.30)^{0.38} &= 10^{0.5048} = 3.198
\end{aligned}$$

Therefore:

$$T_{\text{dev}} = 2.5 \times 3.198 = 7.99 \approx 8.0 \text{ Months}$$

**Step 3 — Average Staffing:**

$$\text{Staff} = E / T_{\text{dev}} = 21.30 / 8.0 = 2.66 \approx 3 \text{ Persons}$$

**Final Answers:**
- Effort $E =$ **21.30 Person-Months**
- Development Time $T_{\text{dev}} =$ **8.0 Months**
- Average Staffing = **3 Persons**

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**

**(a)** Describe the **PERT (three-point) estimation technique** with formula. How does it differ from single-point estimation? **[7 Marks]**

**(b)** A project has the following three-point estimates:
- Optimistic Effort = **30 Person-Months**
- Most Likely Effort = **50 Person-Months**
- Pessimistic Effort = **100 Person-Months**

Calculate the **expected effort**, **standard deviation**, and the **95% confidence interval** for the project effort. **[7 Marks]**

---

##### **Model Solution — Part (a)**

The **PERT (Program Evaluation and Review Technique) Three-Point Estimation** is a probabilistic estimation method that improves accuracy by considering the **uncertainty** inherent in estimation. Unlike single-point estimation (which produces only one value), PERT uses **three estimates**:

1. **Optimistic (O):** Best-case scenario estimate.
2. **Most Likely (M):** Realistic, expected-case estimate.
3. **Pessimistic (P):** Worst-case scenario estimate.

The weighted average is computed using the **Beta Distribution** assumption:

$$E_{\text{exp}} = \dfrac{O + 4M + P}{6}$$

The standard deviation is calculated as:

$$\sigma = \dfrac{P - O}{6}$$

**Differences from Single-Point Estimation:**

| Feature | Single-Point | PERT (Three-Point) |
|:---|:---|:---|
| **Values Used** | One estimate | Three (O, M, P) |
| **Uncertainty** | Ignored | Explicitly modeled |
| **Output** | Single value | Expected value + confidence range |
| **Risk Awareness** | None | High (via $\sigma$ and CI) |
| **Use Case** | Routine tasks | Complex, novel projects |

##### **Model Solution — Part (b)**

**Given:** O = 30, M = 50, P = 100

**Step 1 — Expected Effort:**

$$E_{\text{exp}} = \dfrac{30 + 4(50) + 100}{6} = \dfrac{30 + 200 + 100}{6} = \dfrac{330}{6} = 55 \text{ PM}$$

**Step 2 — Standard Deviation:**

$$\sigma = \dfrac{P - O}{6} = \dfrac{100 - 30}{6} = \dfrac{70}{6} = 11.67 \text{ PM}$$

**Step 3 — 95% Confidence Interval:**

The 95% CI is calculated as $E_{\text{exp}} \pm 2\sigma$:

$$\begin{aligned}
\text{Lower Limit} &= 55 - 2(11.67) = 55 - 23.34 = 31.66 \text{ PM} \\
\text{Upper Limit} &= 55 + 2(11.67) = 55 + 23.34 = 78.34 \text{ PM}
\end{aligned}$$

**Final Answers:**
- Expected Effort = **55 Person-Months**
- Standard Deviation $\sigma =$ **11.67 PM**
- 95% Confidence Interval = **[31.66 PM, 78.34 PM]**

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Software Effort Estimation Techniques**

- ⭐ **Effort** is measured in **Person-Months (PM)** and forms the basis of all project planning.
- ⭐ **Basic COCOMO** uses only **KLOC + Project Mode**, giving **Nominal Effort**.
