- ⭐ **Intermediate COCOMO** adds **EAF** (15 cost drivers), giving **Adjusted Effort**.
- ⭐ **COCOMO Project Modes:** Organic ($a=2.4$, $b=1.05$) → Semi-Detached ($a=3.0$, $b=1.12$) → Embedded ($a=3.6$, $b=1.20$).
- ⭐ **Schedule Equation:** $T_{\text{dev}} = 2.5 \cdot E^d$ where $d$ decreases from 0.38 → 0.32 as complexity increases.
- ⭐ **Average Staffing:** $E / T_{\text{dev}}$ always gives non-integer values — round UP to nearest person.
- ⭐ **FP Analysis** is **language-independent**, while **KLOC is language-dependent**.
- ⭐ **VAF Formula:** $0.65 + 0.01 \times \sum (\text{GSC ratings})$ — note the **0.65** base value is critical.
- ⭐ **PERT Formula:** $E = (O + 4M + P)/6$ — the **4× weight on M** reflects its higher probability.
- ⭐ **Standard Deviation** in PERT: $\sigma = (P-O)/6$ — used to compute confidence intervals.
- ⭐ **95% Confidence Interval:** $E_{\text{exp}} \pm 2\sigma$ (covers 95% of outcomes under normal distribution).
- ⭐ **Use Case Points:** $(\text{UUCW} + \text{UAW}) \times \text{TCF} \times \text{ECF}$ — best for OO projects.
- ⭐ **Wideband Delphi** uses **iterative anonymous rounds** to converge toward consensus — eliminates group bias.
- ⭐ **COCOMO II** uses **17 cost drivers** and a calibration constant $A = 2.94$ for the post-architecture model.
- ⭐ **Always show tables of coefficients and intermediate $\log_{10}$ steps** in KTU numerical answers.

> [!TIP]
> **KTU Examination Tip:**
> - For 7-mark numerical questions, the **expected length is 1 full page** with all steps shown.
> - For 14-mark questions, expect **2 sub-parts each requiring 1 full page** of solution.
> - Practice **at least 5 numerical problems** on each formula before the exam.
> - Memorize the **3 project mode coefficient tables** and the **PERT formula** — these appear in **almost every ESE**.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Bottom-up and Top-down Estimation
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEITT633 - SOFTWARE PROJECT MANAGEMENT
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Bottom-up and Top-down Estimation

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Software Project Estimation** is the process of predicting the quantitative attributes of a software project — **effort, cost, duration, and resources** — required to complete it within defined scope and quality constraints.

Estimation approaches are broadly classified into two opposing strategic paradigms:

**1. Top-Down Estimation (Global-to-Detailed)**
A macroscopic estimation strategy that begins with an **overall project view** and decomposes it into high-level subsystems or phases. It relies heavily on **historical project data, expert judgment, and parametric models** to produce a global estimate without deep knowledge of internal components. (Example: COCOMO, Analogous Estimation, Delphi Method)

**2. Bottom-Up Estimation (Detailed-to-Global)**
A microscopic estimation strategy that begins with the **decomposition of the project into the smallest manageable Work Breakdown Structure (WBS) elements**, estimates each component individually, and aggregates them upward to derive the total project estimate. (Example: WBS-Based Estimation, Activity-Based Costing, Use-Case Point Method)

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Real-World Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Think of building a 3-BHK house in Kerala:**

| Approach | Analogy | What you actually do |
|----------|---------|----------------------|
| **Top-Down** | You walk around your neighbourhood, see similar houses and ask: *"How much did Mr. Pillai's 3-BHK cost in 2023?"* You extrapolate and arrive at a ballpark figure quickly. | Fast, cheap, but vague — ignores your specific plot, soil type, design. |
| **Bottom-Up** | You sit with the architect, count every brick, bag of cement, door, window, fan, light fitting, labour-hour for plumbing, electrical, tiling, painting — and sum every line item. | Slow, expensive, but highly accurate and reveals hidden costs. |

The exact same dichotomy exists in software projects — Top-Down answers *"How big is the iceberg?"* from a distance; Bottom-Up answers *"Exactly how many cubic metres of ice does each submerged piece contain?"*

### 1.3 Standard Metrics Used in Estimation

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following are the **standard quantitative metrics** used in KTU-aligned estimation theory:
> - **KLOC** = Thousand Lines of Code (size metric)
> - **FP** = Function Points (functionality metric)
> - **UCP** = Use-Case Points (object-oriented metric)
> - **E** = Effort (Person-Months or Person-Hours)
> - **D** = Development Time (Months)
> - **P** = Productivity (FP/PM or KLOC/PM)
> - **EAF** = Effort Adjustment Factor (product of 15 cost drivers in COCOMO)
> - **WBS** = Work Breakdown Structure
> - **CPI / SPI** = Cost / Schedule Performance Index (Earned Value Management)

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Top-Down Estimation — Operational Decomposition

The top-down approach follows a **funnel-shaped information flow** where global historical parameters constrain the estimate before any local analysis.

**Step 1 — Project Classification**
Classify the project type (Organic / Semi-detached / Embedded in COCOMO; or similar-size category).

**Step 2 — Parametric Calibration**
Select the historical baseline. For example, use the **analogue project's known effort, size, and EAF** as the reference.

**Step 3 — Scaling Adjustment**
Apply scaling logic:

$$E_{new} = E_{ref} \times \frac{Size_{new}}{Size_{ref}} \times \frac{EAF_{new}}{EAF_{ref}}$$

**Why?** Because the new project is *similar* but not *identical* — only the *delta* in size and the *delta* in environmental complexity should be honoured.

**Step 4 — Risk & Contingency Overlay**
Apply a **management contingency** (typically 10%–25%) for unknowns.

**Step 5 — Output Generation**
Single global value: effort, duration, cost.

**How it works in practice:** The Delphi technique iterates rounds of expert anonymous estimates until convergence (typically 3–4 rounds). COCOMO uses three static coefficients $a, b$ per project class.

### 2.2 Bottom-Up Estimation — Operational Decomposition

The bottom-up approach follows a **pyramid-shaped information flow** where detailed WBS leaves are estimated first and aggregated upward.

**Step 1 — Decomposition (WBS Creation)**
Break the project into **Work Packages (WPs)** — the lowest level at which work can be reliably estimated (typically 8–80 hours of effort).

**Step 2 — Per-Task Estimation**
For each WP, estimate three things: **effort (person-hours), duration (calendar days), resources (people/skills)**.

**Step 3 — Aggregation**
Sum across all WPs. Apply **integration overhead factor** (typically 5%–15%) for inter-module coordination.

**Step 4 — Risk Buffer**
Add contingency at task-level AND aggregate-level.

**Step 5 — Reconciliation**
Cross-check the bottom-up total against top-down sanity check — if variance > 30%, reconcile.

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> The following table consolidates **all KTU-exam-relevant formulas** for estimation.

| # | Formula Name | Equation | Units | When to Use |
|---|--------------|----------|-------|-------------|
| 1 | Basic COCOMO (Organic) | $E = 2.4 \times (KLOC)^{1.05}$ | Person-Months (PM) | Small teams, familiar environment |
| 2 | Basic COCOMO (Semi-detached) | $E = 3.0 \times (KLOC)^{1.12}$ | PM | Medium-size, mixed experience |
| 3 | Basic COCOMO (Embedded) | $E = 3.6 \times (KLOC)^{1.20}$ | PM | Tight constraints, real-time systems |
| 4 | Basic COCOMO Duration | $D = 2.5 \times E^{0.38}$ | Months | All project classes |
| 5 | Intermediate COCOMO | $E = a \times (KLOC)^b \times EAF$ | PM | When 15 cost drivers known |
| 6 | Analogous Estimation | $E_{new} = E_{old} \times (Size_{new}/Size_{old})$ | PM | Quick relative scaling |
| 7 | Delphi Convergence | $\mu = \frac{1}{n}\sum E_i$, $\sigma$ checked | PM | Expert-based refinement |
| 8 | Bottom-Up Aggregation | $E_{total} = \sum_{i=1}^{n} E_i \times (1 + I_f)$ | PM | Detailed WBS-based |
| 9 | Productivity | $P = Size / E$ | KLOC/PM or FP/PM | Benchmarking |
| 10 | Function Point | $FP = UFP \times VAF$ | dimensionless | Size independent of language |
| 11 | Cost per FP | $Cost = FP \times Rate$ | currency | Pricing SLAs |
| 12 | Estimate Confidence Interval | $E_{range} = \mu \pm z \cdot \sigma / \sqrt{n}$ | PM | Risk-aware quoting |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> In the table above, $EAF$ denotes the **product of all 15 cost driver multipliers**, $I_f$ denotes the **integration factor**, $UFP$ is the **Unadjusted Function Point count**, and $VAF$ is the **Value Adjustment Factor** ($0.65 + 0.01 \times \sum F_i$).

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Bidding & Tendering** — Top-down wins (firms must quote within hours).
- **Project Execution Planning** — Bottom-up wins (PM must staff, schedule, procure).
- **Portfolio Decisions** — Top-down (sanity-check 100 projects in a day).
- **Contract Negotiations** — Bottom-up (every milestone must be defensible).
- **Earned Value Tracking** — Bottom-up baseline enables meaningful CPI/SPI computation.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Detailed Derivation: Basic COCOMO (Top-Down Parametric)

The Basic COCOMO model was derived empirically by **Barry Boehm in 1981** by regressing effort against size across **63 NASA projects**. The general form is:

$$E = a \times (KLOC)^b$$

The constants $a$ and $b$ were obtained via **log-linear regression**:

$$\ln(E) = \ln(a) + b \cdot \ln(KLOC)$$

This is the equation of a straight line in log-log space, where:
- **Intercept** = $\ln(a)$ (productivity baseline)
- **Slope** = $b$ (economies/diseconomies of scale)

Solving for the three project classes yields the canonical triplets shown earlier.

**Worked Numerical Example (KTU-pattern)**

A semi-detached project is estimated at **15 KLOC**. Compute basic effort and duration.

$$\begin{aligned}
E &= a \times (KLOC)^b \\
&= 3.0 \times (15)^{1.12} \\
&= 3.0 \times e^{1.12 \cdot \ln(15)} \\
&= 3.0 \times e^{1.12 \times 2.7081} \\
&= 3.0 \times e^{3.0330} \\
&= 3.0 \times 20.76 \\
&\approx 62.28 \text{ PM}
\end{aligned}$$

$$\begin{aligned}
D &= 2.5 \times E^{0.38} \\
&= 2.5 \times (62.28)^{0.38} \\
&= 2.5 \times e^{0.38 \cdot \ln(62.28)} \\
&= 2.5 \times e^{0.38 \times 4.1311} \\
&= 2.5 \times e^{1.5698} \\
&= 2.5 \times 4.804 \\
&\approx 12.01 \text{ months}
\end{aligned}$$

**Result:** $E \approx 62.3$ Person-Months, $D \approx 12$ Months.

### 3.2 Detailed Derivation: Bottom-Up Aggregation with Integration Factor

Suppose a system has 5 modules with the following estimated efforts: **M1 = 8 PM, M2 = 12 PM, M3 = 6 PM, M4 = 10 PM, M5 = 9 PM**. The integration factor $I_f = 0.10$ (10%). Compute the total effort.

$$\begin{aligned}
E_{total} &= \left(\sum_{i=1}^{5} E_i\right) \times (1 + I_f) \\
&= (E_1 + E_2 + E_3 + E_4 + E_5) \times 1.10 \\
&= (8 + 12 + 6 + 10 + 9) \times 1.10 \\
&= 45 \times 1.10 \\
&= 49.5 \text{ PM}
\end{aligned}$$

**Interpretation:** The 4.5 PM of integration overhead covers inter-module interface testing, integration meetings, defect-fixing from mismatched contracts, and system-level verification — costs that cannot be attributed to any single module.

### 3.3 Detailed Derivation: Analogous Estimation (Top-Down Variant)

**Problem:** A completed project had **20 KLOC**, took **40 PM**, with EAF = 1.0. A new similar project is sized at **30 KLOC** with EAF = 1.15. Estimate the new project's effort using analogous (top-down) scaling.

$$\begin{aligned}
E_{new} &= E_{old} \times \frac{Size_{new}}{Size_{old}} \times \frac{EAF_{new}}{EAF_{old}} \\
&= 40 \times \frac{30}{20} \times \frac{1.15}{1.00} \\
&= 40 \times 1.5 \times 1.15 \\
&= 69.0 \text{ PM}
\end{aligned}$$

**Interpretation:** A 50% size increase (not 50% effort increase, due to sub-linear scaling) compounded with 15% environmental complexity produces 69 PM — a **72.5% effort increase**. This is the very characteristic non-linearity that makes top-down estimation useful but also risky if misunderstood.

### 3.4 Comparative Analytical Matrix (Top-Down vs Bottom-Up)

| Dimension | Top-Down | Bottom-Up |
|-----------|----------|-----------|
| **Information Flow** | Global $\rightarrow$ Local | Local $\rightarrow$ Global |
| **Data Required** | Historical aggregate data | Detailed task-level data |
| **Speed of Estimation** | Very fast (hours–days) | Slow (days–weeks) |
| **Accuracy** | Low–Moderate ($\pm$ 30–50%) | High ($\pm$ 5–15%) |
| **Cost of Estimation** | Cheap | Expensive (man-hours of analysts) |
| **Best Project Stage** | Feasibility, Bidding | Execution planning, Contracting |
| **Risk Visibility** | Aggregate risk only | Component-level risks |
| **Expertise Needed** | Senior experts, modelers | Component architects, leads |
| **Sensitivity to Change** | Robust to scope churn | Highly sensitive to WBS changes |
| **Tool Support** | COCOMO, SEER-SEM, Excel | MS Project, JIRA, Primavera |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Functional Architecture Flow — Top-Down Estimation Process

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    startA([Project Idea Initiated]) --> stepB[Collect Historical Data from Similar Projects]
    stepB --> stepC[Classify Project Type Organic Semi-Detached Embedded]
    stepC --> stepD[Select Parametric Model e.g. COCOMO]
    stepD --> stepE[Apply Scaling Equations E equals a times KLOC to power b]
    stepE --> stepF[Apply Adjustment Factor EAF]
    stepF --> stepG[Add Management Contingency 10 to 25 percent]
    stepG --> stepH[Validate with Expert Delphi Rounds]
    stepH --> stepI[Output Global Effort Cost Duration]
    stepI --> stepJ([Single Quoted Estimate Ready])
```

### 4.2 Functional Architecture Flow — Bottom-Up Estimation Process

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    startK([Project Charter Available]) --> stepL[Create Work Breakdown Structure WBS]
    stepL --> stepM[Decompose to Work Packages 8 to 80 hours each]
    stepM --> stepN[Estimate Per WP Effort Duration Resources]
    stepN --> stepO[Cross-Check Estimates with Task Leads]
    stepO --> stepP[Aggregate Component Efforts via Summation]
    stepP --> stepQ[Apply Integration Factor 5 to 15 percent]
    stepQ --> stepR[Add Risk Buffer per WP and Aggregate]
    stepR --> stepS[Reconcile with Top-Down Sanity Check]
    stepS --> stepT([Detailed Defensible Baseline Ready])
```

### 4.3 Combined Hybrid Decision Flow

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    startU([Estimation Trigger]) --> decisionV{Project Stage?}
    decisionV -- Feasibility or Bid --> pathW[Use Top-Down]
    decisionV -- Execution Planning --> pathX[Use Bottom-Up]
    decisionV -- Sanity Check Phase --> pathY[Use Both and Reconcile]
    pathW --> outZ([Final Estimate])
    pathX --> outZ
    pathY --> outZ
```

### 4.4 Hierarchical WBS Example Schematic (Bottom-Up View)

```mermaid
graph TD
    projA[Project Banking App] --> modB[Module 1 Authentication]
    projA --> modC[Module 2 Transactions]
    projA --> modD[Module 3 Reporting]
    projA --> modE[Module 4 Admin Panel]
    modB --> wpB1[WP1 Login UI]
    modB --> wpB2[WP2 OAuth Integration]
    modB --> wpB3[WP3 MFA Logic]
    modC --> wpC1[WP4 Transfer Engine]
    modC --> wpC2[WP5 Ledger Sync]
    modD --> wpD1[WP6 PDF Generator]
    modD --> wpD2[WP7 Dashboard]
    modE --> wpE1[WP8 User Mgmt]
    modE --> wpE2[WP9 Audit Logs]
```

### 4.5 Side-by-Side Comparison Block Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph topDownGroup[TOP DOWN APPROACH]
        tdA[Historical Data] --> tdB[Parametric Model] --> tdC[Global Estimate]
    end
    subgraph bottomUpGroup[BOTTOM UP APPROACH]
        buA[WBS Leaves] --> buB[Per Task Estimate] --> buC[Aggregation] --> buD[Detailed Estimate]
    end
    topDownGroup -.Reconciliation.-> bottomUpGroup
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **`[KTU University Exam – May 2024 Model Paper – Module 2]`**

**Q1. Differentiate between top-down and bottom-up estimation in software project management. List two advantages of each.**
*(3 marks — Blooms Level: Understand)*

**Model Answer:**
- **Top-down** starts from the global project view and decomposes into subsystems; uses historical data and parametric models (e.g., COCOMO). *Advantages:* fast, low-cost, requires less detail, suitable for early-stage bidding.
- **Bottom-up** decomposes the project into a WBS and estimates each Work Package, then aggregates upward. *Advantages:* highly accurate, exposes component-level risks, defensible baseline for tracking.

---

> **`[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Model Paper – Module 2]`**

**Q2. What is the Delphi technique? How does it support top-down estimation?**
*(3 marks — Blooms Level: Remember + Understand)*

**Model Answer:**
The Delphi technique is an **iterative, anonymous expert-judgment method** where multiple rounds of independent estimates are collected from experts, statistical feedback (mean, variance) is shared between rounds without revealing identities, and the process repeats until **convergence** (typically 3–4 rounds). It supports top-down estimation by providing a calibrated global estimate without requiring a detailed WBS, leveraging expert intuition in lieu of granular data, and reducing bias through anonymity.

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks, Internal Choice)

> **`[KTU University Exam – Model Paper aligned to 2024 Scheme – Module 2]`**

#### ⭐ Question A (14 Marks)

**Q A). (a)** Explain the **Top-Down estimation approach** in software project management in detail. Discuss the Basic COCOMO model with its three project classes, including the formulae for effort and duration. *(7 marks — Blooms Level: Understand + Apply)*

**(b)** An organisation completed a project of **25 KLOC** that required **80 Person-Months** of effort under an EAF of **1.0**. A new similar project is estimated at **40 KLOC** with an EAF of **1.25**. Using the **Analogous Estimation** method (Top-Down variant), calculate the estimated effort for the new project. Show all steps clearly. *(7 marks — Blooms Level: Apply + Analyse)*

##### Model Solution to Q A(a):

**Definition:** Top-down estimation is a macro-level forecasting approach that begins with the overall project scope and produces a single global estimate by leveraging historical project data, expert opinion, and parametric models. It does **not** require a detailed WBS.

**Steps Involved:**
1. Project classification (Organic / Semi-detached / Embedded).
2. Historical data collection from past similar projects.
3. Parametric model selection (COCOMO, Function Points, etc.).
4. Application of scaling equations.
5. Adjustment via EAF (in Intermediate COCOMO).
6. Contingency overlay (10%–25%).
7. Validation via Delphi or expert review.

**Basic COCOMO Model (Boehm, 1981):**

$$E = a \times (KLOC)^b, \quad D = 2.5 \times E^d$$

| Project Class | a | b | d | Description |
|---------------|---|---|---|-------------|
| Organic | 2.4 | 1.05 | 0.38 | Small teams, familiar environment |
| Semi-detached | 3.0 | 1.12 | 0.35 | Medium teams, mixed experience |
| Embedded | 3.6 | 1.20 | 0.32 | Tight real-time constraints |

**Why three classes?** Empirical regression on 63 NASA projects showed that project *context* — not just size — governs productivity. The exponent $b > 1$ implies diseconomies of scale (larger projects need disproportionately more effort).

##### Model Solution to Q A(b):

Given: $E_{old} = 80$ PM, $Size_{old} = 25$ KLOC, $EAF_{old} = 1.0$, $Size_{new} = 40$ KLOC, $EAF_{new} = 1.25$.

Formula:

$$E_{new} = E_{old} \times \frac{Size_{new}}{Size_{old}} \times \frac{EAF_{new}}{EAF_{old}}$$

Substituting:

$$\begin{aligned}
E_{new} &= 80 \times \frac{40}{25} \times \frac{1.25}{1.0} \\
&= 80 \times 1.6 \times 1.25 \\
&= 80 \times 2.0 \\
&= 160 \text{ PM}
\end{aligned}$$

**Answer: Estimated effort for the new project is 160 Person-Months.**

**Interpretation:** A 60% size increase and 25% complexity increase yielded a **100% effort doubling**, demonstrating the non-linear nature of software effort scaling.

---

#### ⭐ Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice

**Q B). (a)** Explain the **Bottom-Up estimation approach** with a neat diagram of a Work Breakdown Structure (WBS). Discuss its merits and demerits. *(7 marks — Blooms Level: Understand + Apply)*

**(b)** A software project is decomposed into **6 modules** with the following individual efforts: **12, 18, 8, 15, 10, and 7 Person-Months**. The **integration overhead factor** is **8%**. Calculate:
- (i) The sum of module efforts.
- (ii) The integration overhead in PM.
- (iii) The total estimated project effort.

Also recommend, with justification, whether a **Top-Down sanity check** should be performed. *(7 marks — Blooms Level: Apply + Analyse + Evaluate)*

##### Model Solution to Q B(a):

**Definition:** Bottom-up estimation decomposes the project into the smallest manageable Work Packages (WPs), estimates each one, and aggregates the results to derive the total project estimate. The WBS is the structural backbone.

**Steps:**
1. Decompose project into modules (1st level of WBS).
2. Decompose modules into Work Packages (WPs) — each 8–80 hours.
3. Estimate effort, duration, and resource needs per WP using expert judgment + historical data.
4. Aggregate WPs into modules; modules into project.
5. Apply integration overhead (interfaces, system testing).
6. Add risk buffers.
7. Reconcile with top-down estimate.

**WBS Diagram (already shown in Section 4.4 above — exam version should be hand-drawn or reproduced.)**

**Merits:**
- High accuracy ($\pm$ 5–15%).
- Component-level risk visibility.
- Defensible baseline for Earned Value tracking.
- Supports accurate scheduling.

**Demerits:**
- Time-consuming and expensive to produce.
- Requires detailed knowledge of internals — not feasible in early stages.
- Highly sensitive to WBS churn.
- May underestimate integration complexity if WBS is too clean.

##### Model Solution to Q B(b):

**Given:** Module efforts = {12, 18, 8, 15, 10, 7} PM. Integration factor $I_f = 0.08$.

**(i) Sum of module efforts:**

$$E_{sum} = 12 + 18 + 8 + 15 + 10 + 7 = 70 \text{ PM}$$

**(ii) Integration overhead:**

$$\begin{aligned}
E_{integration} &= E_{sum} \times I_f \\
&= 70 \times 0.08 \\
&= 5.6 \text{ PM}
\end{aligned}$$

**(iii) Total project effort:**

$$\begin{aligned}
E_{total} &= E_{sum} + E_{integration} \\
&= 70 + 5.6 \\
&= 75.6 \text{ PM}
\end{aligned}$$

**Recommendation:** **Yes**, a Top-Down sanity check **must** be performed. Justification:
1. Bottom-up assumes the WBS is exhaustive — but unknown unknowns always exist (e.g., regulatory compliance, post-deployment support).
2. A COCOMO or analogous estimate using historical data from similar projects should be computed.
3. If the Top-Down result differs from 75.6 PM by **more than 30%**, the WBS must be re-examined and reconciled — this protects the project from systematic under-estimation.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls**
> 1. **Unit Confusion (Loses 1–2 marks):** Many students write "KLOC" where they mean "Person-Months". Effort is in PM, size is in KLOC. Don't mix.
> 2. **Forgetting EAF:** In Intermediate COCOMO problems, students often compute $E = a(KLOC)^b$ and stop. EAF must be multiplied if given.
> 3. **Mixing up Duration Formula:** Duration $D$ uses constant 2.5, NOT the project-class constant $a$. Common mistake.
> 4. **WBS Level Errors:** A WBS with modules only is incomplete; examiners expect at least one more level (Work Packages).
> 5. **No Aggregation Explanation:** Just summing without explaining the integration overhead loses easy marks.
> 6. **Skipping Justification for Sanity Checks:** The "why" of reconciliation matters as much as the arithmetic.

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Rapid-Revision Checklist

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Must-Remember Points for KTU 2024 — Module 2 Examination**

- ☐ **Top-Down** = Global $\rightarrow$ Local; **Bottom-Up** = Local $\rightarrow$ Global.
- ☐ Top-Down uses **historical data + parametric models** (COCOMO, Delphi, Analogous).
- ☐ Bottom-Up uses **WBS decomposition + per-task estimation + aggregation**.
- ☐ **Basic COCOMO** triplets to memorise: Organic (2.4, 1.05), Semi-detached (3.0, 1.12), Embedded (3.6, 1.20).
- ☐ Duration formula is **always** $D = 2.5 \times E^{0.38}$ (Basic) — same constant for all classes.
- ☐ Intermediate COCOMO adds **EAF = product of 15 cost drivers**.
- ☐ Analogous formula: $E_{new} = E_{old} \times (Size_{new}/Size_{old}) \times (EAF_{new}/EAF_{old})$.
- ☐ Bottom-Up aggregation: $E_{total} = (\sum E_i) \times (1 + I_f)$.
- ☐ Top-Down is faster but less accurate; Bottom-Up is slower but more accurate.
- ☐ **Hybrid Approach:** Always cross-check Bottom-Up with Top-Down for sanity.
- ☐ WBS Work Packages should be **8–80 hours** of effort for reliable estimation.
- ☐ Integration overhead is typically **5%–15%** of summed module efforts.
- ☐ Delphi technique uses **3–4 anonymous rounds** for convergence.
- ☐ Productivity = Size / Effort; benchmark for Indian context: ~5–10 FP/PM.
- ☐ KTU typically asks 3-mark conceptual questions and 14-mark problem-solving with internal choice.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Function Point Analysis
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEITT633 - SOFTWARE PROJECT MANAGEMENT
> - **Module:** Module Module 2
> - **Topic:** Function Point Analysis

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Function Point Analysis (FPA)** is a standardized, empirical technique for the measurement of software size by quantifying the **functionality** that a software system delivers to its users, *independent of the programming language, technology, or development methodology* used to build it. It was introduced by **Allan J. Albrecht** of IBM in **1979** and later refined under the **International Function Point Users Group (IFPUG)** standard.

In the context of the **KTU 2024 Scheme**, Function Point Analysis is positioned as the most reliable **size-oriented** estimation technique, allowing project managers to:
- Estimate **effort, cost, and duration** before coding begins.
- Compare productivity across teams, languages, and platforms.
- Benchmark software products against industry standards (e.g., ISBSG repository).

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Analogy — The House Blueprint System**
> 
> Imagine you are an architect. Instead of counting the number of bricks (which depends on the type of brick, mortar, and worker), you count **functional units** of the house:
> - **Rooms (Internal Logical Files)** — what the house *stores/contains*.
> - **Doors (External Inquiries)** — how a user *asks for information*.
> - **Windows (External Outputs)** — what the house *shows the world*.
> - **Electrical Switches (External Inputs)** — what the user *controls*.
> - **Garage Connection to Main Gate (External Interface Files)** — how the house *shares data with the outside*.
> 
> The **Function Point (FP)** is the count of these functional units, weighted by how *complex* each unit is. Just like a 3-BHK plan is larger than a 1-BHK regardless of whether it is built in Kerala or Punjab, a system's FP count represents its **logical size**, independent of construction style.

## 1.3 Core Metrics Highlighted

> [!TIP]
> **Key KTU High-Yield Terms to Memorize:**
> - **UFP** — Unadjusted Function Points (raw count)
> - **CAF** — Complexity Adjustment Factor
> - **FP / AFP** — Adjusted (Final) Function Points
> - **EI, EO, EQ, ILF, EIF** — The 5 Function Types
> - **GSC** — General System Characteristics (14 of them)

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Five Function Types (KTU Frequently Asked)

Every piece of user-visible functionality in a software application belongs to **exactly one** of these five categories:

| # | Function Type | Symbol | Definition | User-Perspective Analogy |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **External Inputs** | **EI** | Data entering the system across the boundary from outside that maintains ILFs. | Filling a bank deposit form. |
| 2 | **External Outputs** | **EO** | Data leaving the system across the boundary that presents information to the user (reports, screens). | A printed monthly bank statement. |
| 3 | **External Inquiries** | **EQ** | An input-output pair that retrieves data without altering any ILF. | Balance enquiry at an ATM. |
| 4 | **Internal Logical Files** | **ILF** | A logically related group of user-recognizable data wholly maintained within the application boundary. | The master "Customer Account" table. |
| 5 | **External Interface Files** | **EIF** | A logically related group of user-recognizable data referenced by the application but maintained by *another* application. | Tax data shared by the Income Tax portal. |

## 2.2 The Three Complexity Levels

For each function type, complexity is graded as:

- **Low** — minimal logic, few fields
- **Average (Medium)** — moderate logic
- **High** — extensive logic, many fields, complex relationships

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (Cheat Sheet)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Memorize these three core equations. They appear in nearly every KTU university exam question on FPA.

$$
\boxed{UFP = \sum_{i=1}^{5} (\text{Count}_i \times \text{Weight}_i)}
$$

$$
\boxed{CAF = 0.65 + 0.01 \times \sum_{j=1}^{14} D_j}
$$

$$
\boxed{FP = UFP \times CAF}
$$

### 2.3.1 Standard Weighting Matrix (IFPUG — Memorize This!)

| Function Type | Low Weight | Average Weight | High Weight |
|---|---|---|---|
| **External Inputs (EI)** | **3** | **4** | **6** |
| **External Outputs (EO)** | **4** | **5** | **7** |
| **External Inquiries (EQ)** | **3** | **4** | **6** |
| **Internal Logical Files (ILF)** | **7** | **10** | **15** |
| **External Interface Files (EIF)** | **5** | **7** | **10** |

> [!NOTE]
> **Boundary Rule:** ILF weight $(max = 15)$ is always *higher* than EIF weight $(max = 10)$ because an ILF is **maintained** by the application, while an EIF is only **referenced**.

### 2.3.2 CAF Boundary Conditions

| Quantity | Minimum Value | Maximum Value | Real-World Meaning |
|---|---|---|---|
| Each Degree of Influence $D_j$ | $0$ | $5$ | $0$ = Not present, $5$ = Strong influence |
| Sum of all 14 GSCs $\Sigma D_j$ | $0$ | $70$ | — |
| **CAF** | **0.65** | **1.35** | Low-end = project is trivial; High-end = project is extremely complex |

## 2.4 The 14 General System Characteristics (GSCs)

> [!TIP]
> KTU often asks for a list of these 14 characteristics. Memorize in three mnemonic groups.

**Group A — Operational / Communication (1–5)**
1. Data communications
2. Distributed data processing
3. Performance criteria
4. Heavily used configuration
5. Transaction rate

**Group B — User / Update (6–8)**
6. Online data entry
7. End-user efficiency
8. Online update

**Group C — Internal / Reuse / Multi-site (9–14)**
9. Complex processing
10. Reusability
11. Installation ease
12. Operational ease
13. Multiple sites
14. Facilitate change

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

FPA is the **foundation of industry-standard parametric models** such as **COCOMO II** and **SLIM**. By converting FP into **Lines of Code (LOC)** using language-specific conversion tables, project managers obtain a single, technology-aware, effort-driving metric. Modern Agile shops use **Story Points → Function Points** mappings to maintain traceability from sprint backlogs to high-level effort estimates.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 The Complete FPA Procedure (Five Mandatory Steps)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The **IFPUG Counting Practices Manual** defines these five steps. Any deviation in order results in inaccurate counts and is a common KTU valuation pitfall.

**Step 1 — Determine the type of FP count:** Development, Enhancement, or Application (baseline) count.

**Step 2 — Identify the application boundary:** Decide what is *inside* (the application) versus *outside* (external systems or users).

**Step 3 — Count the unadjusted function points (UFP):** Tally each of the 5 function types, classify complexity, and apply weights.

**Step 4 — Determine the Complexity Adjustment Factor (CAF):** Score each of the 14 GSCs on a 0–5 scale; sum; plug into formula.

**Step 5 — Calculate Adjusted FP:** $\text{FP} = \text{UFP} \times \text{CAF}$.

---

## 3.2 Exhaustive Worked Example (KTU Exam-Style)

> A new **Library Management System** has the following user-visible functionality:
> - **12 External Inputs** (Low complexity)
> - **8 External Outputs** (Average complexity)
> - **5 External Inquiries** (Low complexity)
> - **4 Internal Logical Files** (Average complexity)
> - **2 External Interface Files** (High complexity)
> 
> The 14 GSCs received the following Degree of Influence scores:
> **3, 4, 2, 1, 5, 3, 4, 2, 4, 3, 1, 2, 3, 4**.
> 
> *Compute the Adjusted Function Point count.*

### 3.2.1 Step A — Calculate UFP

For each row, compute $\text{Count} \times \text{Weight}$ from the standard weighting matrix in Section 2.3.1.

$$
\text{UFP}_{EI} = 12 \times W_{EI,Low}
$$
$$
\text{UFP}_{EI} = 12 \times 3 = 36
$$

$$
\text{UFP}_{EO} = 8 \times W_{EO,Avg}
$$
$$
\text{UFP}_{EO} = 8 \times 5 = 40
$$

$$
\text{UFP}_{EQ} = 5 \times W_{EQ,Low}
$$
$$
\text{UFP}_{EQ} = 5 \times 3 = 15
$$

$$
\text{UFP}_{ILF} = 4 \times W_{ILF,Avg}
$$
$$
\text{UFP}_{ILF} = 4 \times 10 = 40
$$

$$
\text{UFP}_{EIF} = 2 \times W_{EIF,High}
$$
$$
\text{UFP}_{EIF} = 2 \times 10 = 20
$$

Now sum all five components:

$$
\text{UFP} = 36 + 40 + 15 + 40 + 20
$$

$$
\text{UFP} = 151
$$

### 3.2.2 Step B — Calculate $\Sigma D_j$ (Sum of GSC Degrees)

We use a left-to-right associative sum:

$$
\Sigma D_j = 3 + 4 + 2 + 1 + 5
$$

$$
\Sigma D_j = 15 \text{ (after first 5 terms)}
$$

$$
\Sigma D_j = 15 + 3 + 4 + 2 + 4
$$

$$
\Sigma D_j = 30 \text{ (after first 10 terms)}
$$

$$
\Sigma D_j = 30 + 3 + 1 + 2 + 3
$$

$$
\Sigma D_j = 39 \text{ (after first 14 terms — final)}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Verification (Upper Bound):** Max possible $\Sigma D_j = 14 \times 5 = 70$. Our value $39 \le 70$ ✓. Boundary rule respected.

### 3.2.3 Step C — Calculate CAF

$$
\text{CAF} = 0.65 + 0.01 \times \Sigma D_j
$$

$$
\text{CAF} = 0.65 + 0.01 \times 39
$$

$$
\text{CAF} = 0.65 + 0.39
$$

$$
\text{CAF} = 1.04
$$

> Verification: $0.65 \le 1.04 \le 1.35$ ✓

### 3.2.4 Step D — Calculate Final Adjusted Function Points

$$
\text{FP} = \text{UFP} \times \text{CAF}
$$

$$
\text{FP} = 151 \times 1.04
$$

$$
\text{FP} = 157.04
$$

**Final Result:** The Library Management System measures **≈ 157 Function Points**.

---

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Quick Conversion to Effort

After obtaining FP, KTU often asks you to convert to **Person-Months** using a productivity factor $P$ (typical industry value: **P ≈ 0.05 PM/FP** for COCOMO II early design):

$$
\text{Effort (PM)} = \text{FP} \times P
$$

$$
\text{Effort} = 157.04 \times 0.05 \approx 7.85 \text{ Person-Months}
$$

$$
\text{Schedule (Months)} = 2.5 \times (\text{Effort})^{0.38}
$$

$$
\text{Schedule} = 2.5 \times (7.85)^{0.38} \approx 5.83 \text{ Months}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Inverse Conversion (FP → LOC):** Use language-specific conversion tables. For example:
> - Java: ~53 LOC / FP
> - C: ~130 LOC / FP
> - Python: ~35 LOC / FP
> 
> Hence LOC = FP × Conversion Factor.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 End-to-End FPA Process Flowchart

> The following **block-level functional architecture flow** maps every step of FPA — from requirement gathering to final size estimation — without requiring physical drawing tools.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    stepA["START Project Initiation"] --> stepB["Step 1 Determine FP Count Type Development Enhancement Application"]
    stepB --> stepC["Step 2 Identify Application Boundary Internal vs External"]
    stepC --> stepD["Step 3a Identify User Functionalities"]
    stepD --> stepE["Step 3b Classify Into Five Types EI EO EQ ILF EIF"]
    stepE --> stepF["Step 3c Assign Complexity Low Average High"]
    stepF --> stepG["Step 3d Apply Standard Weights 3 to 15"]
    stepG --> stepH["Step 3e Compute UFP SUM Count x Weight"]
    stepH --> stepI["Step 4a Score Fourteen GSCs Each From Zero to Five"]
    stepI --> stepJ["Step 4b Compute SUM D sub j"]
    stepJ --> stepK["Step 4c Compute CAF 0.65 plus 0.01 times SUM"]
    stepK --> stepL["Step 5 Compute FP equals UFP times CAF"]
    stepL --> stepM{"FP Acceptable"}
    stepM -->|YES| stepN["Output Use For Effort Cost Schedule Estimation"]
    stepM -->|NO| stepO["Recheck Boundary Counts and GSC Scores"]
    stepO --> stepD
    stepN --> stepP["END Final FP Count Delivered"]
```

## 4.2 Function Point Type Hierarchy (Conceptual Map)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    rootA["FUNCTION POINTS TOTAL FP"] --> rootB["Unadjusted FP UFP"]
    rootA --> rootC["Adjusted FP via CAF"]
    rootB --> rootD["Internal Logical Files ILF"]
    rootB --> rootE["External Interface Files EIF"]
    rootB --> rootF["External Inputs EI"]
    rootB --> rootG["External Outputs EO"]
    rootB --> rootH["External Inquiries EQ"]
    rootD --> rootI["Weight 7 10 15"]
    rootE --> rootJ["Weight 5 7 10"]
    rootF --> rootK["Weight 3 4 6"]
    rootG --> rootL["Weight 4 5 7"]
    rootH --> rootM["Weight 3 4 6"]
```

## 4.3 The 14 GSC Input Layer

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    gscX["Fourteen General System Characteristics GSC Inputs"] --> gscY["Each Scored Zero to Five"]
    gscY --> gscZ["Sum D sub j Range 0 to 70"]
    gscZ --> gscW["CAF equals 0.65 plus 0.01 times Sum"]
    gscW --> gscV["Apply to UFP for Final FP"]
```

> [!TIP]
> **Diagram Interpretation Tip for KTU Exams:** Always label your diagram with the three equations from Section 2.3 beside the relevant block — this visually shows the marker that you understand *why* each step matters.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. Define Function Point Analysis. Mention any two advantages of FPA over LOC-based estimation.
**[KTU University Exam — June 2024 | Model Question Paper Set B]**

**Model Answer:**

> [!NOTE]
> **Definition:** Function Point Analysis (FPA) is a sizing technique that measures software size by quantifying the functionality delivered to the user based on five categories — EI, EO, EQ, ILF, EIF — independent of programming language.

**Two Advantages:**
1. **Language/Tool Independence:** FPA measures logical size, so the same FP count applies whether the system is coded in Java, Python, or COBOL — unlike LOC.
2. **Available Early in Lifecycle:** FPA can be counted as soon as requirements are stable (i.e., *before* coding), enabling early project estimates.

*(3 marks — 1 for definition + 1 each for two advantages)*

---

### Q2. Differentiate between Internal Logical File (ILF) and External Interface File (EIF).
**[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023 | Supplementary Exam]**

**Model Answer:**

| Aspect | **ILF** | **EIF** |
|---|---|---|
| **Maintained By** | The application *being counted* | Some *other* application |
| **Direction** | Internal ownership | External reference only |
| **Highest Weight** | 15 | 10 |
| **Example** | The application's own `STUDENT` table | A `GST_TAX` table maintained by the Tax portal |

*(3 marks — comparative differentiation with example)*

---

## 5.2 PART B — Full Descriptive Question (14 Marks, Module-Internal Choice)

### 🔹 QUESTION A (14 Marks) — **Function Point Calculation**

**[KTU University Exam — June 2024 | End Semester Evaluation]**

> The following data was collected during FPA of a **Hospital Management System**:
> - 18 External Inputs (Average complexity)
> - 6 External Outputs (High complexity)
> - 9 External Inquiries (Low complexity)
> - 5 Internal Logical Files (Low complexity)
> - 3 External Interface Files (Average complexity)
> 
> The 14 GSC influence scores obtained are:
> **2, 3, 1, 4, 2, 3, 1, 4, 2, 3, 1, 4, 2, 3**.
> 
> **(a)** Compute the Unadjusted Function Point count. *(7 marks)*
> **(b)** Compute the CAF and the final adjusted FP. *(7 marks)*

#### 📘 Model Solution — Part (a) — 7 Marks

Step 1 — Apply standard weights per function type:

$$
\text{EI: } 18 \times 4 = 72
$$

$$
\text{EO: } 6 \times 7 = 42
$$

$$
\text{EQ: } 9 \times 3 = 27
$$

$$
\text{ILF: } 5 \times 7 = 35
$$

$$
\text{EIF: } 3 \times 7 = 21
$$

Step 2 — Sum all components:

$$
\text{UFP} = 72 + 42 + 27 + 35 + 21
$$

$$
\text{UFP} = 197
$$

*(Part (a) awarded 7 marks: 1 mark per component × 5 + 2 marks for the summation and final answer.)*

---

#### 📘 Model Solution — Part (b) — 7 Marks

Step 1 — Compute $\Sigma D_j$:

$$
\Sigma D_j = (2+3+1+4+2) + (3+1+4+2+3) + (1+4+2+3)
$$

$$
\Sigma D_j = 12 + 13 + 10 = 35
$$

Step 2 — Compute CAF:

$$
\text{CAF} = 0.65 + 0.01 \times 35
$$

$$
\text{CAF} = 0.65 + 0.35
$$

$$
\text{CAF} = 1.00
$$

Step 3 — Compute Adjusted FP:

$$
\text{FP} = \text{UFP} \times \text{CAF} = 197 \times 1.00 = 197
$$

**Result:** The Hospital Management System = **197 Function Points**.

*(Part (b) awarded 7 marks: 2 for sum, 2 for CAF, 3 for final FP.)*

---

### 🔹 QUESTION B (14 Marks) — **Theoretical / Conceptual Depth**

**[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023 | End Semester Evaluation]**

> **(a)** List and explain the **five function types** used in Function Point Analysis. For each, give **one real-world example** from a banking domain. *(7 marks)*
> 
> **(b)** Explain the **14 General System Characteristics** used in computing the Complexity Adjustment Factor. Mention the range of CAF values and justify why CAF has both a lower bound (0.65) and an upper bound (1.35). *(7 marks)*

#### 📘 Model Solution — Part (a) — 7 Marks

| # | Type | Meaning | Banking Example |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **EI — External Input** | Data entering the application from outside, maintains ILFs. | A customer fills an online **loan application form**. |
| 2 | **EO — External Output** | Reports or screens sent out of the system. | The **monthly account statement** generated for the customer. |
| 3 | **EQ — External Inquiry** | Simple input–output without modifying any ILF. | Customer checks the **account balance** via mobile app. |
| 4 | **ILF — Internal Logical File** | Data maintained within the application's boundary. | The **Customer Master Table** managed by the bank's core system. |
| 5 | **EIF — External Interface File** | Data maintained by an external system but referenced. | **CIBIL credit history** file maintained by CIBIL bureau. |

*(1.5 marks for list + 0.7 × 5 = 3.5 marks for meaning and example. Total 7 marks, with 1 mark for clean tabular presentation.)*

---

#### 📘 Model Solution — Part (b) — 7 Marks

**The 14 GSCs (memorize in 3 groups as in Section 2.4):**

1. Data communications
2. Distributed data processing
3. Performance criteria
4. Heavily used configuration
5. Transaction rate
6. Online data entry
7. End-user efficiency
8. Online update
9. Complex processing
10. Reusability
11. Installation ease
12. Operational ease
13. Multiple sites
14. Facilitate change

Each GSC receives a Degree of Influence $D_j \in \{0, 1, 2, 3, 4, 5\}$ where:
- **0** — Not present or no influence
- **1** — Incidental influence
- **2** — Moderate influence
- **3** — Average influence
- **4** — Significant influence
- **5** — Strong influence throughout

**CAF Range Computation:**

- **Minimum**: All $D_j = 0 \Rightarrow \text{CAF} = 0.65 + 0.01 \times 0 = 0.65$
- **Maximum**: All $D_j = 5 \Rightarrow \text{CAF} = 0.65 + 0.01 \times 70 = 1.35$

**Justification:**
- The constant **0.65** is empirically derived (Albrecht 1983) and represents a *baseline* that no real system goes below — even trivial projects carry some complexity.
- The constant **0.01 × 70 = 0.70** is the maximum *uplift*, capping the multiplier at **1.35** to prevent unrealistic estimates for projects with massive operational complexity.

*(7 marks — 2 marks for the 14-list, 2 marks for scoring scale, 3 marks for CAF range + justification.)*

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Marks-Loss Pitfalls — Read Before Your Exam!**
> 
> 1. **Forgetting the 0.65 baseline.** Many students write $CAF = 0.01 \times \Sigma D_j$. This **silently inflates** estimates for simple projects. Always include $0.65$.
> 2. **Confusing ILF and EIF weight limits.** ILF goes up to **15**; EIF only up to **10**. The boundary rule is strict.
> 3. **Confusing EQ with EI.** EQ does **NOT** update any ILF. EI does. Mixing them is a recurring 3-mark loss in Part A.
> 4. **Forgetting the order of operations in 14-GSC summing.** Group them in batches of 5 + 5 + 4 to avoid arithmetic slippage. Examiners will not award marks if the **final FP** is wrong even if the formula is correct.
> 5. **Not converting FP → LOC** when COCOMO II is mentioned in the same question. KTU's combined-topic Part B questions expect both.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

- ✅ FPA was created by **Allan J. Albrecht (IBM, 1979)** and standardized by **IFPUG**.
- ✅ FPA measures **logical size**, not physical size — it is **language-independent**.
- ✅ **5 Function Types:** EI, EO, EQ, ILF, EIF.
- ✅ **3 Complexity Levels per type:** Low, Average, High.
- ✅ Memorize the weight matrix (especially ILF High = **15**, EIF High = **10**).
- ✅ Three master equations: $\text{UFP} = \sum \text{Count} \times \text{Weight}$, $\text{CAF} = 0.65 + 0.01 \times \Sigma D_j$, $\text{FP} = \text{UFP} \times \text{CAF}$.
- ✅ **14 GSCs** scored 0–5 each; max sum = **70**; CAF always lies in $[0.65,\, 1.35]$.
- ✅ Boundary rule: an application **maintains** an ILF; it only **references** an EIF.
- ✅ Application boundary must be drawn **before** counting — wrong boundary = wrong count.
- ✅ FP can be converted to **LOC** (language-specific) and used as input to **COCOMO II**.
- ✅ Effort (PM) = FP × Productivity (typical 0.05 PM/FP in COCOMO II early design).
- ✅ Always present calculations in **tabular form** in Part B — easier for the examiner to award step-marks.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: COCOMO II
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEITT633 - SOFTWARE PROJECT MANAGEMENT
> - **Topic:** COOMO II
> - **Module:** Module 2

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: CORE TECHNICAL DEFINITION & INTUITIVE OVERVIEW

## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

**COCOMO II (COnstructive COst MOdel II)** is an advanced, hierarchy-based software cost estimation model developed by **Barry W. Boehm** and his colleagues at the University of Southern California (USC) Center for Software Engineering in **1995** as a successor to the original COCOMO (1981). It is an *empirical*, *regression-based* estimation framework that predicts the **Person-Months (PM)**, **schedule (TDEV)**, and **cost** of a software project based on its **size**, **scale drivers**, and **effort multipliers**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Scheme Definition:** COCOMO II is a three-stage, hierarchical software effort estimation model that uses **Size (KSLOC or Function Points)**, **Scale Drivers (B exponent)**, and **Effort Multipliers (EMs)** to compute the Person-Months required to develop a software system, with increasing model fidelity as the project lifecycle progresses.

## 1.2 The Three Stages of COCOMO II

| Stage | Model Name | When Used | Primary Size Metric |
|-------|------------|-----------|---------------------|
| **Stage 1** | Application Composition Model | Early prototyping / requirements | **Object Points** |
| **Stage 2** | Early Design Model | Early design phase (concept-to-design) | **KSLOC or Unadjusted Function Points** |
| **Stage 3** | Post-Architecture Model | Detailed post-architecture development | **KSLOC** (most detailed, 17 EMs) |

> [!NOTE]
> For the KTU 2024 SPM examination, **Stage 3 (Post-Architecture Model)** carries the highest weightage in numerical problems and derivations.

## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — "The Construction Cost Estimator"

Think of COCOMO II as a **smart construction cost estimator** for building a house:
- **Size (KSLOC)** = the area of the house (square feet). Bigger house → more cost.
- **Scale Drivers** = the *inherent difficulty* of building the house (hilly terrain, strict regulations). They make small or large projects harder/easier in a non-linear way.
- **Effort Multipliers (EMs)** = the *site-specific conditions* (weather, labor skill, material quality). Each multiplies the cost by a known factor.
- **Phases** = the construction stages (foundation, walls, roof). Each phase has a different percentage of total effort.
- The estimator multiplies all these together to get a realistic **Person-Month** estimate, just like a contractor gives a quote for building a house.

> [!TIP]
> **Memory Trick:** **"S-S-E-E-P"** → **S**ize, **S**cale drivers, **E**ffort Multipliers, **E**xponent **B**, **P**hases — these are the 5 pillars of COCOMO II calculations.

## 1.4 Key Constants and Metrics

- **Calibration Constant (A) = 2.94** (for Stages 2 and 3)
- **A = 2.94** in Application Composition Model as well
- **Default Scale Factor Sum (B - 1.01) range:** 0.00 to ~0.26
- **Exponent B range:** 1.01 to ~1.26
- **Schedule equation exponent base:** 0.28
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: DEEP THEORETICAL ANALYSIS & KTU HIGH-YIELD FORMULA SHEET

## 2.1 Theoretical Foundation — Why COCOMO II?

The original COCOMO (1981) was designed for **traditional waterfall** development of mainframe systems. With the advent of **object-oriented programming**, **rapid application development (RAD)**, **COTS (Commercial Off-The-Shelf) reuse**, and **spiral/lifecycle process models**, the old model became inadequate. COCOMO II was redesigned to handle:
- Modern software development paradigms
- Reuse-driven development
- Early lifecycle estimation (when requirements are unclear)
- Incremental and evolutionary delivery

## 2.2 The Core Effort Equation

The **master equation** of COCOMO II Post-Architecture Model is:

$$
PM = A \times Size^B \times \prod_{i=1}^{17} EM_i
$$

Where:
- **PM** = Person-Months of effort
- **A** = Multiplicative calibration constant (= **2.94** for Post-Architecture)
- **Size** = Software size in **KSLOC** (thousands of source lines of code)
- **B** = Scale factor exponent = $1.01 + 0.01 \times \sum_{j=1}^{5} SF_j$
- **EM_i** = Effort Multiplier for cost driver *i* (17 in Post-Architecture)

## 2.3 The Five Scale Drivers (B Exponent)

| # | Scale Driver | Symbol | Captures | Rating Levels |
|---|--------------|--------|----------|---------------|
| 1 | **Precedentedness** | PREC | Familiarity with prior similar projects | 6 levels (Very Low → Extra High) |
| 2 | **Development Flexibility** | FLEX | Degree of freedom in meeting requirements | 6 levels |
| 3 | **Architecture / Risk Resolution** | RESL | Risk resolution & architectural design completeness | 6 levels |
| 4 | **Team Cohesion** | TEAM | Stakeholder collaboration & team consistency | 6 levels |
| 5 | **Process Maturity** | PMAT | CMM/CMMI level of the organization | 6 levels |

**Rating Values (SF_i):**

| Rating | Very Low | Low | Nominal | High | Very High | Extra High |
|--------|----------|-----|---------|------|-----------|------------|
| **SF_i value** | 5.07 | 3.72 | 2.68 | 1.24 | 0.66 | -0.24 |

## 2.4 The 17 Effort Multipliers (EMs) — Post-Architecture Model

The EMs are grouped into **four categories**:

| Category | EM Count | EMs |
|----------|----------|-----|
| **Product** | 4 | RELY, DATA, CPLX, RUSE |
| **Platform** | 3 | TIME, STOR, PVOL |
| **Personnel** | 5 | ACAP, PCAP, PCON, APEX, PLEX, LTEX (6 actually, but 5 grouped) |
| **Project** | 3 | TOOL, SITE, SCED |

> [!NOTE]
> The exact count in Post-Architecture is **17 EMs**. Personnel has **6**: ACAP, PCAP, PCON, APEX, PLEX, LTEX.

**Full 17 EMs (with meanings):**
1. **RELY** – Required Software Reliability
2. **DATA** – Database Size
3. **CPLX** – Product Complexity
4. **RUSE** – Required Reusability
5. **TIME** – Execution Time Constraint
6. **STOR** – Main Storage Constraint
7. **PVOL** – Platform Volatility
8. **ACAP** – Analyst Capability
9. **PCAP** – Programmer Capability
10. **PCON** – Personnel Continuity
11. **APEX** – Applications Experience
12. **PLEX** – Platform Experience
13. **LTEX** – Language and Toolset Experience
14. **TOOL** – Use of Software Tools
15. **SITE** – Multisite Development
16. **SCED** – Required Development Schedule
17. **DOC** – Documentation match to lifecycle needs *(alternative naming)*

> [!IMPORTANT]
> For KTU numericals, the **specific EM rating table** (with values for each of the 6 levels per EM) is generally given in the question. Memorize the **17 EM names** and their **4 categories**.

## 2.5 The Phase Sensitive Distribution

COCOMO II distributes effort across **5 development phases**:

| Phase | Symbol | % of Total Effort (Default) |
|-------|--------|------------------------------|
| Inception | INC | 6% |
| Elaboration | ELA | 17% |
| Construction | CON | 65% |
| Transition | TRN | 10% |
| Post-Transition | POST | 2% |

## 2.6 Schedule Equation (TDEV)

The development time in months is given by:

$$
TDEV = \left[ 3.67 \times PM^{(0.28 + 0.2 \times (B - 1.01))} \right] \times \frac{SCED\%}{100}
$$

Where **SCED%** is the rating value of the SCED effort multiplier expressed as a percentage.

## 2.7 Application Composition Model (Stage 1)

For prototyping projects, size is measured in **Object Points**, not KSLOC:

$$
NOP = (OP \times (100 - \text{reuse\%})/100) + \text{NewOP}
$$

$$
PM = \left(\frac{NOP}{PROD}\right) \times NOP
$$

Where **PROD** is the productivity rate depending on developer's experience and capability (CSDI: Composite Speed of DIsplay integration) and **SST** (Screen Simulation Tool) usage.

## 2.8 KTU High-Yield Formula Cheat Sheet

| # | Concept | Formula | Notes |
|---|---------|---------|-------|
| 1 | **Master Effort Equation** | $PM = A \times Size^B \times \prod EM_i$ | A = 2.94 |
| 2 | **Exponent B** | $B = 1.01 + 0.01 \times \sum_{j=1}^{5} SF_j$ | Sum of 5 scale drivers |
| 3 | **Schedule TDEV** | $TDEV = 3.67 \times PM^{(0.28 + 0.2(B - 1.01))} \times SCED\%/100$ | In months |
| 4 | **Object Points** | $NOP = (OP \times (100 - \%reuse)/100) + \text{NewOP}$ | Stage 1 only |
| 5 | **Phase Effort** | $PM_{phase} = PM \times (\% \text{ for that phase})$ | 5 phases |
| 6 | **Average Staffing** | $\text{Staff} = PM / TDEV$ | FTE count |
| 7 | **Productivity** | $Size / PM$ | KSLOC per Person-Month |

> [!WARNING]
> **Absolute Value Rule:** In tables above, $\sum$ and $\prod$ are used instead of vertical bars. Never write `|x|` inside KTU formula tables — use `\vert` or `\mid` to prevent table breakage.

## 2.9 Real-World Utility

COCOMO II is used in industry for:
- **Bid preparation** and **contract negotiations** for software projects
- **Project budgeting** and **resource planning**
- **Make-or-buy decisions** (build vs. COTS reuse)
- **Process improvement benchmarking** (USAF, DoD, NASA, and many Fortune 500 IT firms use COCOMO II variants)
- **Risk assessment** through scale factor sensitivity analysis
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: STEP-BY-STEP DERIVATIONS & ANALYTICAL MATRIX

## 3.1 Worked Numerical Problem 1 — Post-Architecture Effort Calculation

> **Problem Statement (KTU Model):**
> A software project is estimated to have a size of **8 KSLOC**. The five scale drivers are rated as follows: PREC = Low, FLEX = Nominal, RESL = High, TEAM = Nominal, PMAT = High. The product of the 17 Effort Multipliers is **EM = 1.45**. Compute the **Person-Months (PM)** and the **development time (TDEV in months)**. Assume SCED is rated as Nominal (100%).

### Step-by-Step Solution:

**Step 1: Identify the calibration constant and size**

The Post-Architecture model is used (most detailed), so:
- A = 2.94
- Size = 8 KSLOC

**Step 2: Look up the scale factor (SF) values for each rating**

From the KTU 2024 standard COCOMO II table:

$$
\begin{aligned}
SF_{PREC} &= 3.72 \quad (\text{Low}) \\
SF_{FLEX} &= 2.68 \quad (\text{Nominal}) \\
SF_{RESL} &= 1.24 \quad (\text{High}) \\
SF_{TEAM} &= 2.68 \quad (\text{Nominal}) \\
SF_{PMAT} &= 1.24 \quad (\text{High})
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3: Compute the sum of scale factors**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\sum SF &= 3.72 + 2.68 + 1.24 + 2.68 + 1.24 \\
&= 6.40 + 2.48 + 2.68 \\
&= 11.56
\end{aligned}
$$

> Note: The arithmetic is verified: 3.72 + 2.68 = 6.40; 1.24 + 2.68 = 3.92; 6.40 + 3.92 = 10.32; 10.32 + 1.24 = 11.56. ✓

**Step 4: Calculate the exponent B**

$$
B = 1.01 + 0.01 \times \sum SF
$$

$$
B = 1.01 + 0.01 \times 11.56
$$

$$
B = 1.01 + 0.1156
$$

$$
B = 1.1256
$$

**Step 5: Compute Size^B**

$$
Size^B = 8^{1.1256}
$$

Using logarithms:
$$
\begin{aligned}
\log(8^{1.1256}) &= 1.1256 \times \log(8) \\
&= 1.1256 \times 0.90309 \\
&= 1.01675
\end{aligned}
$$

Therefore:
$$
8^{1.1256} = 10^{1.01675} \approx 10.40
$$

**Step 6: Apply the master effort equation**

$$
PM = A \times Size^B \times \prod EM_i
$$

$$
PM = 2.94 \times 10.40 \times 1.45
$$

$$
PM = 30.576 \times 1.45
$$

$$
PM \approx 44.34 \text{ Person-Months}
$$

**Step 7: Calculate TDEV (assuming SCED = 100%)**

$$
TDEV = 3.67 \times PM^{(0.28 + 0.2 \times (B - 1.01))}
$$

Compute the schedule exponent:
$$
\begin{aligned}
B - 1.01 &= 1.1256 - 1.01 = 0.1156 \\
0.28 + 0.2 \times 0.1156 &= 0.28 + 0.02312 \\
&= 0.30312
\end{aligned}
$$

Therefore:
$$
TDEV = 3.67 \times (44.34)^{0.30312}
$$

Compute the power term:
$$
\begin{aligned}
\log(44.34^{0.30312}) &= 0.30312 \times \log(44.34) \\
&= 0.30312 \times 1.6468 \\
&= 0.4992
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
44.34^{0.30312} = 10^{0.4992} \approx 3.157
$$

Therefore:
$$
TDEV = 3.67 \times 3.157 \approx 11.59 \text{ months}
$$

**Step 8: Compute the average staffing**

$$
\text{Staff} = \frac{PM}{TDEV} = \frac{44.34}{11.59} \approx 3.83 \approx 4 \text{ FTE}
$$

### Final Result Table:

| Quantity | Value |
|----------|-------|
| **Person-Months (PM)** | **≈ 44.34 PM** |
| **Development Time (TDEV)** | **≈ 11.59 months** |
| **Average Staffing** | **≈ 4 FTE** |
| **Productivity** | **8 / 44.34 ≈ 0.18 KSLOC/PM** |

---

## 3.2 Worked Numerical Problem 2 — Application Composition Model

> **Problem Statement (KTU Model):**
> A prototyping project has **40 screens** with an average of **3 views per screen**, **2 data tables** reused, and **12 server data tables** (new). The developer is rated "**High**" capability with full CSDI/SST tool usage. Compute the Person-Months for the prototype.

### Step-by-Step Solution:

**Step 1: Identify Object Point (OP) weights**

For a simple screen (3 views average), the **complexity weight = 2** (average).

> Note: In a standard 3-view screen, the weight is "Average" = 2 per screen.

**Step 2: Compute Object Points for screens**

$$
OP_{screens} = 40 \text{ screens} \times 2 = 80 \text{ OP}
$$

**Step 3: Compute Object Points for reports (none in this problem)** → 0 OP.

**Step 4: Compute Object Points for reused/new data tables**

For reused tables, weight is **Low = 3**; for new tables, weight is **Average = 4**.

$$
OP_{data} = (2 \text{ reused} \times 3) + (12 \text{ new} \times 4)
$$

$$
OP_{data} = 6 + 48 = 54 \text{ OP}
$$

**Step 5: Total Object Points**

$$
OP_{total} = 80 + 0 + 54 = 134 \text{ OP}
$$

**Step 6: Apply Reuse Percentage**

Assume 30% reuse adjustment:
$$
NOP = 134 \times (1 - 0.30) = 93.8 \approx 94 \text{ NOP}
$$

**Step 7: Look up PROD (productivity)**

For "High" capability developer with full CSDI/SST tools:
$$
PROD = 13 \text{ NOP/Person-Month}
$$

**Step 8: Compute Person-Months**

$$
PM = \frac{NOP}{PROD} = \frac{94}{13} \approx 7.23 \text{ Person-Months}
$$

### Final Result:
- **Person-Months ≈ 7.23 PM**
- A small, fast prototype as expected for Stage 1.

---

## 3.3 Derivation of the B Exponent

> **Why B = 1.01 + 0.01 × ΣSF?**

This was empirically derived by **Barry Boehm et al. (1995, 1997, 2000)** from a regression analysis of 161 software projects. The base value **1.01** represents the theoretical lower bound (when scale drivers are Extra High, so ΣSF → 0, and the project has near-linear economy/diseconomy of scale). The factor **0.01** is the empirically determined weight for each scale driver's incremental contribution to project complexity.

**Derivation flow:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{When all SF are at "Extra High" (each } SF = -0.24\text{):} \\
\sum SF &= 5 \times (-0.24) = -1.20 \\
B &= 1.01 + 0.01 \times (-1.20) = 1.01 - 0.012 = 0.998
\end{aligned}
$$

This gives economy of scale (B < 1). Conversely:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{When all SF are at "Very Low" (each } SF = 5.07\text{):} \\
\sum SF &= 5 \times 5.07 = 25.35 \\
B &= 1.01 + 0.01 \times 25.35 = 1.2635
\end{aligned}
$$

This gives significant diseconomy of scale (B > 1).
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: STRUCTURAL DIAGRAMS & SCHEMATICS

## 4.1 COCOMO II Hierarchical Stage Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Start([Software Project Initiated]) --> Q1{Stage of Lifecycle?}
    Q1 -->|Requirements/Prototyping| S1[Stage 1: Application Composition Model]
    Q1 -->|Early Design| S2[Stage 2: Early Design Model]
    Q1 -->|Post-Architecture Dev| S3[Stage 3: Post-Architecture Model]
    S1 --> S1A[Size: Object Points NOP]
    S1A --> S1B[Productivity PROD NOP per PM]
    S1B --> S1C[PM equals NOP divided by PROD]
    S2 --> S2A[Size: KSLOC or UFP]
    S2A --> S2B[5 Scale Drivers SF]
    S2B --> S2C[7 Effort Multipliers EMs]
    S2C --> S2D[PM equals 2.94 times Size to power B times product of EMs]
    S3 --> S3A[Size: KSLOC]
    S3A --> S3B[5 Scale Drivers SF]
    S3B --> S3C[17 Effort Multipliers EMs]
    S3C --> S3D[Phase Distribution: INC ELA CON TRN POST]
    S3D --> S3E[PM equals 2.94 times Size to power B times product of EMs]
    S3E --> S3F[TDEV: Schedule Calculation]
    S2D --> S3F
    S3F --> Final([Output: PM, TDEV, Staff, Cost])
```

## 4.2 COCOMO II Input-Process-Output Functional Block Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph INPUTS[INPUT PARAMETERS]
        I1[Software Size KSLOC]
        I2[5 Scale Drivers]
        I3[17 Effort Multipliers]
        I4[SCED Rating for Schedule]
    end
    subgraph PROCESS[COOMO II COMPUTATION ENGINE]
        P1[Sum Scale Factors: Sum of SF]
        P2[Compute Exponent B: 1.01 plus 0.01 times Sum]
        P3[Compute Effort: 2.94 times Size to power B times product of EMs]
        P4[Compute Schedule: 3.67 times PM to power schedule exponent]
        P5[Compute Phase Distribution: PM times phase percentage]
    end
    subgraph OUTPUTS[ESTIMATION OUTPUTS]
        O1[Person Months PM]
        O2[Development Time TDEV]
        O3[Average Staffing]
        O4[Cost equals PM times Salary Rate]
        O5[Phase-wise Effort and Schedule]
    end
    I1 --> P3
    I2 --> P1 --> P2 --> P3
    I3 --> P3
    I4 --> P4
    P3 --> O1 --> P4
    P3 --> O5
    P4 --> O2 --> O3
    O1 --> O4
```

## 4.3 The 17 Effort Multipliers — Category Map

```mermaid
graph TD
    EM[17 Effort Multipliers] --> CAT1[Product Category]
    EM --> CAT2[Platform Category]
    EM --> CAT3[Personnel Category]
    EM --> CAT4[Project Category]
    CAT1 --> RELY[RELY Required Reliability]
    CAT1 --> DATA[DATA Database Size]
    CAT1 --> CPLX[CPLX Product Complexity]
    CAT1 --> RUSE[RUSE Required Reusability]
    CAT2 --> TIME[TIME Execution Time]
    CAT2 --> STOR[STOR Main Storage]
    CAT2 --> PVOL[PVOL Platform Volatility]
    CAT3 --> ACAP[ACAP Analyst Capability]
    CAT3 --> PCAP[PCAP Programmer Capability]
    CAT3 --> PCON[PCON Personnel Continuity]
    CAT3 --> APEX[APEX Application Experience]
    CAT3 --> PLEX[PLEX Platform Experience]
    CAT3 --> LTEX[LTEX Language and Tool Experience]
    CAT4 --> TOOL[TOOL Use of Software Tools]
    CAT4 --> SITE[SITE Multisite Development]
    CAT4 --> SCED[SCED Required Development Schedule]
```

## 4.4 COCOMO II Phase Distribution (5-Phase Lifecycle)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    P1[Inception: 6 percent PM] --> P2[Elaboration: 17 percent PM]
    P2 --> P3[Construction: 65 percent PM]
    P3 --> P4[Transition: 10 percent PM]
    P4 --> P5[Post-Transition: 2 percent PM]
    P5 --> End([Project Complete])
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 SCHEME EXAMINATION QUESTION BANK & TOPIC RECAP

## 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1: [KTU University Exam – May 2024 Model]
**List and briefly explain the three stages of COCOMO II with their appropriate size metrics.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**
COCOMO II consists of **three hierarchical stages** based on project lifecycle progression:

1. **Application Composition Model (Stage 1):** Used in early prototyping and requirements phase. Size is measured in **Object Points (NOP)**, calculated from screens, reports, and data tables.

2. **Early Design Model (Stage 2):** Used during early system and software design. Size is measured in **KSLOC** or **Unadjusted Function Points (UFP)**. Uses 5 scale drivers and 7 effort multipliers.

3. **Post-Architecture Model (Stage 3):** Used during detailed construction and integration. Size is measured in **KSLOC**. Uses 5 scale drivers, **17 effort multipliers**, and phase distribution — this is the most detailed and most accurate stage. **(3 marks)**

---

### Question 2: [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Model]
**Explain the concept of Scale Drivers in COCOMO II. Name all five.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**
**Scale Drivers** in COCOMO II capture the **inherent project complexity** that causes **economy or diseconomy of scale**. They determine the exponent **B** in the master equation. The five scale drivers are:

1. **PREC** – Precedentedness (familiarity with prior similar projects)
2. **FLEX** – Development Flexibility (degree of constraint in meeting requirements)
3. **RESL** – Architecture and Risk Resolution
4. **TEAM** – Team Cohesion
5. **PMAT** – Process Maturity (CMM/CMMI level)

Each is rated on a **6-point scale** (Very Low to Extra High) with SF values from **-0.24 to 5.07**. The exponent is calculated as $B = 1.01 + 0.01 \times \sum SF_i$. **(3 marks)**

---

## 5.2 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks with Internal Choice)

> **KTU Internal Choice Rule:** Students answer **either** Question A **or** Question B.

---

### Question A (14 Marks) — [KTU ESE Model 2024]

**A software project is estimated at 10 KSLOC. Compute the Person-Months, Development Time, and Average Staffing using the COCOMO II Post-Architecture Model.**

Given:
- Scale Driver Ratings: PREC = Nominal, FLEX = High, RESL = Nominal, TEAM = Low, PMAT = High
- Product of 17 Effort Multipliers = **1.32**
- SCED = Nominal (100%)

**(a) Compute the Person-Months (PM).  (7 Marks)**
**(b) Compute the Development Time (TDEV in months) and Average Staffing.  (7 Marks)**

#### Model Solution (a):

**Step 1: Identify the model parameters**
- A = 2.94
- Size = 10 KSLOC

**Step 2: Look up scale factor values from KTU table**

$$
\begin{aligned}
SF_{PREC} &= 2.68 \quad (\text{Nominal}) \\
SF_{FLEX} &= 1.24 \quad (\text{High}) \\
SF_{RESL} &= 2.68 \quad (\text{Nominal}) \\
SF_{TEAM} &= 3.72 \quad (\text{Low}) \\
SF_{PMAT} &= 1.24 \quad (\text{High})
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3: Sum of scale factors**

$$
\sum SF = 2.68 + 1.24 + 2.68 + 3.72 + 1.24 = 11.56
$$

**Step 4: Compute exponent B**

$$
B = 1.01 + 0.01 \times 11.56 = 1.1256
$$

**Step 5: Compute Size^B**

$$
10^{1.1256} = 10^{1.0} \times 10^{0.1256} = 10 \times 1.336 \approx 13.36
$$

**Step 6: Compute PM**

$$
PM = 2.94 \times 13.36 \times 1.32
$$

$$
PM = 39.28 \times 1.32 \approx 51.85 \text{ Person-Months}
$$

**∴ Person-Months ≈ 51.85 PM** **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution (b):

**Step 1: Compute the schedule exponent**

$$
\begin{aligned}
B - 1.01 &= 0.1256 \\
0.28 + 0.2 \times 0.1256 &= 0.30512
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2: Compute PM to the power of the schedule exponent**

$$
\begin{aligned}
51.85^{0.30512} &= 10^{0.30512 \times \log_{10}(51.85)} \\
&= 10^{0.30512 \times 1.7148} \\
&= 10^{0.5232} \\
&\approx 3.336
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3: Apply TDEV formula (SCED = 100%)**

$$
TDEV = 3.67 \times 3.336 \approx 12.24 \text{ months}
$$

**Step 4: Compute Average Staffing**

$$
\text{Staff} = \frac{51.85}{12.24} \approx 4.24 \approx 5 \text{ FTE}
$$

**∴ Development Time ≈ 12.24 months, Average Staffing ≈ 5 FTE** **(7 marks)**

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — [KTU ESE Model 2024 — Alternative Choice]

**Explain the COCOMO II Post-Architecture Model in detail. List the 5 Scale Drivers and the 17 Effort Multipliers with their categories. Discuss how the exponent B is calculated and its effect on project effort estimation.**

**(a) Discuss the architecture of the COCOMO II Post-Architecture Model, the 5 Scale Drivers, and the exponent B calculation.  (7 Marks)**
**(b) List the 17 Effort Multipliers grouped into categories, explain the Phase Sensitive Distribution, and write the Schedule equation.  (7 Marks)**

#### Model Solution (a):

The **COCOMO II Post-Architecture Model** is the **third and most detailed stage** of COCOMO II, applied during the **construction and post-construction phases** of a software project when the software architecture is well understood and detailed design is in progress.

**Architecture of the Model:**
The Post-Architecture Model uses the **master equation**:

$$
PM = A \times Size^B \times \prod_{i=1}^{17} EM_i
$$

Where:
- **A = 2.94** (calibration constant)
- **Size** = software size in KSLOC
- **B** = scale exponent based on 5 scale drivers
- **EM_i** = 17 effort multipliers

**The 5 Scale Drivers:**

| # | Symbol | Driver | What it captures |
|---|--------|--------|------------------|
| 1 | PREC | Precedentedness | Familiarity with similar prior projects |
| 2 | FLEX | Development Flexibility | Strictness of conformance to requirements |
| 3 | RESL | Architecture / Risk Resolution | Risk analysis and architectural design completeness |
| 4 | TEAM | Team Cohesion | Synchronization and collaboration among stakeholders |
| 5 | PMAT | Process Maturity | CMM/CMMI level of the organization |

Each driver is rated on a **6-point scale** (Very Low, Low, Nominal, High, Very High, Extra High) with corresponding SF values: **5.07, 3.72, 2.68, 1.24, 0.66, -0.24**.

**Exponent B Calculation:**

$$
B = 1.01 + 0.01 \times \sum_{j=1}^{5} SF_j
$$

**Effect of B on Effort:**
- If all scale drivers are at "Extra High", B → 0.998 (**economy of scale**, cost grows sub-linearly with size).
- If all scale drivers are at "Very Low", B → 1.263 (**diseconomy of scale**, cost grows super-linearly with size).
- Larger B means small increases in size cause **disproportionately large effort** increases. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution (b):

**The 17 Effort Multipliers (grouped by category):**

| Category | Count | Effort Multipliers (Names) |
|----------|-------|------------------------------|
| **Product** | 4 | RELY (Required Reliability), DATA (Database Size), CPLX (Product Complexity), RUSE (Required Reusability) |
| **Platform** | 3 | TIME (Execution Time Constraint), STOR (Main Storage Constraint), PVOL (Platform Volatility) |
| **Personnel** | 6 | ACAP (Analyst Capability), PCAP (Programmer Capability), PCON (Personnel Continuity), APEX (Applications Experience), PLEX (Platform Experience), LTEX (Language & Toolset Experience) |
| **Project** | 3 | TOOL (Use of Software Tools), SITE (Multisite Development), SCED (Required Development Schedule) |

> Note: An additional 17th EM may be **DOC** (Documentation) in some COCOMO II variants. Total = 4+3+6+3 = 16; with DOC = 17.

**Phase Sensitive Distribution:**
COCOMO II distributes effort across **5 development phases** instead of the traditional 4-phase waterfall. The default phase percentages are:

| Phase | Symbol | % of Total PM |
|-------|--------|---------------|
| Inception | INC | 6% |
| Elaboration | ELA | 17% |
| Construction | CON | 65% |
| Transition | TRN | 10% |
| Post-Transition | POST | 2% |

For a project with 51.85 PM, Construction phase alone consumes ≈ 33.7 PM.

**Schedule Equation:**

$$
TDEV = \left[ 3.67 \times PM^{(0.28 + 0.2 \times (B - 1.01))} \right] \times \frac{SCED\%}{100}
$$

Where TDEV is the total development time in months and SCED% is the percentage rating of the SCED effort multiplier. **(7 marks)**

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Loss Pitfalls in COCOMO II Problems:**
>
> 1. **Wrong SF values:** Students often confuse "Nominal = 2.68" with "High = 1.24". Memorize the table: 5.07 / 3.72 / 2.68 / 1.24 / 0.66 / **-0.24**. ⛔ *Loss: 1–2 marks per question.*
>
> 2. **Forgetting the "0.01" coefficient in B:** Writing B = 1.01 + SF_sum (without the 0.01 multiplier) is the **most common error**. This gives a wildly incorrect B, leading to wrong PM. ⛔ *Loss: 2–3 marks.*
>
> 3. **Misapplying A:** Using A = 1.0 (from original COCOMO) instead of A = 2.94 (COCOMO II). ⛔ *Loss: 1 mark.*
>
> 4. **Confusing Stage 1 (Object Points) with Stage 3 (KSLOC):** Object Points apply only to the Application Composition Model. KSLOC applies to Stages 2 and 3. ⛔ *Loss: 2 marks.*
>
> 5. **SCED in TDEV:** Forgetting to divide by 100 when SCED% is given as a percentage. ⛔ *Loss: 1 mark.*
>
> 6. **Phase percentages must sum to 100%:** 6 + 17 + 65 + 10 + 2 = 100. ⛔ *Loss: 1 mark if mis-stated.*
>
> 7. **Unit confusion:** PM is in **Person-Months**, TDEV is in **Months**, Staff = PM/TDEV gives **FTE (Full-Time Equivalents)**. ⛔ *Loss: 1 mark.*

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

- ✅ **COCOMO II** was developed by **Barry Boehm et al. at USC (1995–2000)** as the successor to COCOMO 81.
- ✅ It has **3 hierarchical stages** with increasing detail: **Application Composition → Early Design → Post-Architecture**.
- ✅ **Master equation:** $PM = 2.94 \times Size^B \times \prod EM_i$ for Stage 2 & 3.
- ✅ **Exponent B:** $B = 1.01 + 0.01 \times \sum SF_j$ where SF comes from **5 scale drivers**.
- ✅ **5 Scale Drivers (memorize):** **PREC, FLEX, RESL, TEAM, PMAT**.
- ✅ **SF rating values (6 levels):** **5.07, 3.72, 2.68, 1.24, 0.66, -0.24** (Very Low → Extra High).
- ✅ **17 Effort Multipliers** are grouped into **Product (4), Platform (3), Personnel (6), Project (3)** [or **4+3+5+3+1(DOC)=17** depending on variant].
- ✅ **5 Development Phases:** **INC (6%), ELA (17%), CON (65%), TRN (10%), POST (2%)** — sum = 100%.
- ✅ **Schedule equation:** $TDEV = 3.67 \times PM^{(0.28 + 0.2(B-1.01))} \times SCED\%/100$.
- ✅ **Stage 1 uses Object Points (NOP)**, not KSLOC. NOP depends on screens, reports, and data tables.
- ✅ **Staffing = PM / TDEV** (gives average FTE).
- ✅ **Cost = PM × average monthly salary** (not part of COCOMO directly, but an industry extension).
- ✅ B > 1 → **diseconomy of scale**; B < 1 → **economy of scale**.
- ✅ **Productivity = Size / PM** (KSLOC per Person-Month).
- ✅ COCOMO II supports **reuse, COTS, modern OO paradigms** — unlike COCOMO 81.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Cost Estimation
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEITT633 - SOFTWARE PROJECT MANAGEMENT
> - **Module:** Module Module 2
> - **Topic:** Cost Estimation

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

# 1. Cost Estimation — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

**Cost Estimation** in software engineering is the *quantitative, predictive process of approximating the monetary, effort, and time resources* required to complete a software project, before the actual development begins. According to the **IEEE Standard 1058.1** and KTU 2024 SPM syllabus, cost estimation is the cornerstone of the **Project Management Plan (PMP)** because it directly drives scheduling, staffing, budgeting, and risk management.

In formal terms:

$$
\text{Cost} = f(\text{Project Size}, \text{Product Complexity}, \text{Developer Capability}, \text{Environmental Factors})
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** Cost estimation is *not* just predicting money — it is the prediction of **effort** (Person-Months), **duration** (calendar months), and **cost** (currency units), derived from measurable product/project attributes.

## 1.2 The Three Pillars of Cost Estimation

| Pillar | Symbol | Unit | Purpose |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Effort** | $E$ | Person-Months (PM) | Total human labor required |
| **Duration** | $D$ | Calendar Months (T) | Project timeline |
| **Cost** | $C$ | ₹ / $ / € | Actual monetary expenditure |

> $C = E \times \text{Labour Rate per Person-Month}$

## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — "The House Construction Analogy"

Imagine you are asked to build a house. Before laying the first brick, you must answer:

1. *How many bricks will I need?* → This is **Size Estimation** (LOC / Function Points)
2. *How many workers and how many days?* → This is **Effort & Duration Estimation**
3. *How much money for cement, steel, labour?* → This is **Cost Estimation**

Now imagine you misjudge the number of bricks by 50%. The result is either a half-built house (under-estimation) or a wasteful pile of materials (over-estimation). **Software cost estimation is exactly this — but instead of bricks, we count lines of code (LOC) or functional requirements.**

> [!NOTE]
> **Why is estimation hard?** Unlike civil engineering, software is *invisible, intellectual, and highly variable*. Requirements change, productivity differs across teams, and complexity is hard to measure. Hence, multiple estimation techniques exist, each attacking the problem from a different angle.

## 1.4 Where Cost Estimation Fits in the SPM Lifecycle

The estimation activity is performed **early in the planning phase** (feasibility study / detailed planning) and is **iteratively refined** as the project progresses. KTU 2024 follows a *progressive elaboration* model:

$$
\text{Rough Estimate} \rightarrow \text{Budgetary Estimate} \rightarrow \text{Definitive Estimate} \rightarrow \text{Control Estimate}
$$

The accuracy improves as more information becomes available.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Classification of Cost Estimation Techniques

KTU 2024 categorizes software cost estimation techniques into **five families**:

### A) Expert Judgment / Analogous Methods
- Relies on the intuition and past experience of senior professionals.
- **Analogy-based:** Compare current project with a *similar completed project*.
- Quick but highly subjective.

### B) Decomposition (WBS-Based)
- Break project into **Work Breakdown Structure (WBS)** components.
- Estimate each small piece, then aggregate.
- More accurate but time-consuming.

### C) Empirical / Parametric Models
- Mathematical equations derived from historical project data.
- Examples: **COCOMO, COCOMO II, Putnam, SLIM.**

### D) Function Point Analysis (FPA)
- Estimates from *user-visible functionality*, not LOC.
- Language-independent.

### E) Machine Learning / AI-Based Models (Modern Add-on)
- Regression, neural networks, Bayesian models trained on past project data.

## 2.2 Function Point Analysis (FPA) — Detailed
