- **Lag (+):** Delay of a successor (e.g., FS + 2 days lag → wait 2 days after predecessor finishes).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Default Dependency in PMBOK & KTU syllabus:** **Finish-to-Start (FS)**. Always assume FS unless the question explicitly states another.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Network Diagram Types

Software project networks can be drawn using two principal methods:

### A. Arrow Diagramming Method (ADM) — Activity on Arrow (AOA)
- Arrows = activities, Nodes = events/milestones.
- Requires **dummy activities** to show logical dependencies correctly.
- Only FS relationships allowed.
- Older method; rarely used in modern software projects.

### B. Precedence Diagramming Method (PDM) — Activity on Node (AON)
- Boxes/nodes = activities, Arrows = dependencies.
- Supports **all four** dependency types (FS, FF, SS, SF).
- **Default** method in modern PM tools (MS Project, Primavera, Jira).
- **Preferred by KTU examiners.**

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Board Exam Favourite:** Questions typically use **PDM (AON)** with FS dependencies. Always be ready to convert AOA → AON if asked.

## 2.2 Critical Path Method (CPM) — Forward & Backward Pass

CPM computes the **earliest** and **latest** times each activity can start/finish, identifies the **critical path** (longest path with zero float), and gives the **minimum project duration**.

### Forward Pass (Earliest Times)

For each activity $i$ in topological order:

$$ES_j = \max_{i \in \text{pred}(j)}\{EF_i\}$$

$$EF_j = ES_j + D_j$$

For the project start node: $ES_{\text{start}} = 0$.

### Backward Pass (Latest Times)

For each activity $j$ in reverse topological order:

$$LF_j = \min_{k \in \text{succ}(j)}\{LS_k\}$$

$$LS_j = LF_j - D_j$$

For the project end node: $LF_{\text{end}} = ES_{\text{end}}$ (or set to project deadline).

### Float / Slack Calculations

$$Total\ Float_j = LS_j - ES_j = LF_j - EF_j$$

$$Free\ Float_j = \min_{k \in \text{succ}(j)}\{ES_k\} - EF_j$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Critical Path Definition (exam-ready):**
> The path through the network where **Total Float = 0**. Any delay on this path delays the entire project. The project's minimum duration equals the **sum of durations along the critical path**.

## 2.3 KTU Formula Cheat Sheet

| # | Quantity | Formula | Units / Notes |
|---|----------|---------|---------------|
| 1 | Earliest Start (ES) | $ES_j = \max(EF\ \text{of predecessors})$ | Days |
| 2 | Earliest Finish (EF) | $EF_j = ES_j + D_j$ | Days |
| 3 | Latest Finish (LF) | $LF_j = \min(LS\ \text{of successors})$ | Days |
| 4 | Latest Start (LS) | $LS_j = LF_j - D_j$ | Days |
| 5 | Total Float (TF) | $TF_j = LS_j - ES_j$ | Days; $\vert TF \vert = 0$ on critical path |
| 6 | Free Float (FF) | $FF_j = ES_{\text{succ}} - EF_j$ | Cannot delay successor's ES |
| 7 | Project Duration | $T = \max(EF_{\text{end}})$ | Days |
| 8 | Critical Path Length | $\sum D_j$ over critical path | Must equal $T$ |
| 9 | Probability of Completion (PERT) | $Z = \frac{T_s - T_e}{\sigma_e}$ | Standard normal deviate |
| 10 | Expected Duration (PERT) | $t_e = \dfrac{o + 4m + p}{6}$ | Optimistic, Most likely, Pessimistic |
| 11 | Variance (PERT) | $\sigma^2 = \left(\dfrac{p - o}{6}\right)^2$ | Per activity |

## 2.4 Schedule Compression Techniques

| Technique | Description | Trade-off |
|-----------|-------------|-----------|
| **Crashing** | Add resources to critical-path activities to reduce duration | Increases **cost**; cannot reduce below technical minimum |
| **Fast-Tracking** | Execute activities in parallel that were originally sequential | Increases **risk** of rework |

## 2.5 Resource Optimization

- **Resource Leveling:** Adjusts *start/finish dates* to balance resource demand (may extend project duration). TF and FF can be consumed.
- **Resource Smoothing:** Adjusts activities *within* their float to keep resource usage smooth, **without changing the project end date**.

## 2.6 Critical Chain Project Management (CCPM)

Adds **buffers** to protect the project from uncertainty:

- **Project Buffer:** Placed at end of critical chain (replaces safety hidden in individual estimates).
- **Feeding Buffer:** Placed where a non-critical chain feeds into the critical chain.
- **Resource Buffer:** Placed before a critical-chain activity that needs a specialized resource.

> [!TIP]
> **Why CCPM matters in Software:** Software tasks are highly uncertain (bug counts, integration issues). CCPM protects the delivery date by centralizing contingency, rather than padding every estimate.

## 2.7 Real-World Utility

In industry, sequencing & scheduling feed directly into:
- **Sprint planning** in Agile (Sprint = scheduling window).
- **Release planning** (milestones at release boundaries).
- **Capacity planning** for cloud/SaaS teams (resource leveling across squads).
- **Bid/tender responses** where the CPM-derived duration determines contract price.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Worked Example — Forward & Backward Pass

> [!NOTE]
> **Problem:** A software project has 7 activities (A–G) with FS dependencies and durations as given below. Compute ES, EF, LS, LF, TF, FF, identify the critical path, and state the minimum project duration.

### 3.1.1 Input Data

| Activity | Duration (days) | Predecessors |
|----------|-----------------|--------------|
| A | 5 | — |
| B | 3 | A |
| C | 4 | A |
| D | 6 | B |
| E | 5 | C |
| F | 7 | D, E |
| G | 4 | F |

### 3.1.2 Forward Pass (Derivation)

We compute $ES$ and $EF$ in topological order: A → B, C → D, E → F → G.

**Activity A** (no predecessors, project start):
$$ES_A = 0$$
$$EF_A = ES_A + D_A = 0 + 5 = 5$$

**Activity B** (predecessor: A):
$$ES_B = EF_A = 5$$
$$EF_B = ES_B + D_B = 5 + 3 = 8$$

**Activity C** (predecessor: A):
$$ES_C = EF_A = 5$$
$$EF_C = ES_C + D_C = 5 + 4 = 9$$

**Activity D** (predecessor: B):
$$ES_D = EF_B = 8$$
$$EF_D = ES_D + D_D = 8 + 6 = 14$$

**Activity E** (predecessor: C):
$$ES_E = EF_C = 9$$
$$EF_E = ES_E + D_E = 9 + 5 = 14$$

**Activity F** (predecessors: D, E — take MAX):
$$ES_F = \max(EF_D,\ EF_E) = \max(14,\ 14) = 14$$
$$EF_F = ES_F + D_F = 14 + 7 = 21$$

**Activity G** (predecessor: F):
$$ES_G = EF_F = 21$$
$$EF_G = ES_G + D_G = 21 + 4 = 25$$

$$\therefore\ \text{Minimum Project Duration} = EF_G = 25 \text{ days}$$

### 3.1.3 Backward Pass (Derivation)

We compute $LS$ and $LF$ in reverse order: G → F → D, E → B, C → A. Set $LF_G = EF_G = 25$.

**Activity G:**
$$LF_G = 25$$
$$LS_G = LF_G - D_G = 25 - 4 = 21$$

**Activity F** (successor: G):
$$LF_F = LS_G = 21$$
$$LS_F = LF_F - D_F = 21 - 7 = 14$$

**Activity D** (successor: F):
$$LF_D = LS_F = 14$$
$$LS_D = LF_D - D_D = 14 - 6 = 8$$

**Activity E** (successor: F):
$$LF_E = LS_F = 14$$
$$LS_E = LF_E - D_E = 14 - 5 = 9$$

**Activity B** (successor: D):
$$LF_B = LS_D = 8$$
$$LS_B = LF_B - D_B = 8 - 3 = 5$$

**Activity C** (successor: E):
$$LF_C = LS_E = 9$$
$$LS_C = LF_C - D_C = 9 - 4 = 5$$

**Activity A** (successors: B and C — take MIN):
$$LF_A = \min(LS_B,\ LS_C) = \min(5,\ 5) = 5$$
$$LS_A = LF_A - D_A = 5 - 5 = 0$$

### 3.1.4 Float Computation

For every activity $j$:

$$TF_j = LS_j - ES_j, \qquad FF_j = ES_{\text{succ}_j} - EF_j$$

| Act | Dur | ES | EF | LS | LF | TF | FF | Critical? |
|-----|-----|----|----|----|----|----|----|-----------|
| A | 5 | 0 | 5 | 0 | 5 | 0 | 0 | ✅ |
| B | 3 | 5 | 8 | 5 | 8 | 0 | 0 | ✅ |
| C | 4 | 5 | 9 | 5 | 9 | 0 | 0 | ✅ |
| D | 6 | 8 | 14 | 8 | 14 | 0 | 0 | ✅ |
| E | 5 | 9 | 14 | 9 | 14 | 0 | 0 | ✅ |
| F | 7 | 14 | 21 | 14 | 21 | 0 | 0 | ✅ |
| G | 4 | 21 | 25 | 21 | 25 | 0 | 0 | ✅ |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Every activity here is critical because two parallel chains (A-B-D and A-C-E) both yield duration **14 days** before F. The critical path is **A → B → D → F → G** (and equivalently A → C → E → F → G). Total = 5 + 3 + 6 + 7 + 4 = **25 days** ✅ matches $EF_G$.

## 3.2 PERT Three-Point Estimate Derivation

If an activity has optimistic $o$, most likely $m$, and pessimistic $p$ estimates:

The PERT **beta-distribution** expected time is the mean of the Beta distribution, derived as:

$$t_e = \dfrac{o + 4m + p}{6}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Why $\frac{1}{6}$?** The variance of a Beta distribution (assuming range $\approx 6\sigma$) is:
> $$\sigma^2 = \left(\dfrac{p - o}{6}\right)^2$$
> The expected project duration is the sum of $t_e$ along the critical path:
> $$T_e = \sum_{j \in CP} t_{e_j}, \qquad \sigma_e = \sqrt{\sum_{j \in CP} \sigma_j^2}$$
> Probability of meeting target date $T_s$:
> $$Z = \dfrac{T_s - T_e}{\sigma_e}, \qquad P = \Phi(Z)$$

**Numerical Illustration:** Suppose along the critical path of three activities:
- Act 1: $o=2, m=4, p=12$ → $t_{e1}=\frac{2+16+12}{6}=5$, $\sigma_1^2=\left(\frac{10}{6}\right)^2=2.78$
- Act 2: $o=3, m=5, p=13$ → $t_{e2}=\frac{3+20+13}{6}=6$, $\sigma_2^2=\left(\frac{10}{6}\right)^2=2.78$
- Act 3: $o=1, m=3, p=11$ → $t_{e3}=\frac{1+12+11}{6}=4$, $\sigma_3^2=\left(\frac{10}{6}\right)^2=2.78$

Therefore:
$$T_e = 5 + 6 + 4 = 15 \text{ days}$$
$$\sigma_e = \sqrt{2.78 + 2.78 + 2.78} = \sqrt{8.34} \approx 2.89\ \text{days}$$

If management demands completion in $T_s = 17$ days:
$$Z = \dfrac{17 - 15}{2.89} \approx 0.69 \quad \Rightarrow \quad P(T \le 17) = \Phi(0.69) \approx 0.7549 \approx 75.5\%$$

## 3.3 Schedule Compression Trade-off Derivation

Let $C_i$ be the cost slope of activity $i$:

$$C_i = \dfrac{Crash\ Cost - Normal\ Cost}{Normal\ Duration - Crash\ Duration}\quad \left[\dfrac{\text{currency}}{\text{day}}\right]$$

To **crash** the project by $\Delta$ days at minimum cost:
- Repeatedly select the critical-path activity with **lowest $C_i$** that has not reached its crash limit.
- After each iteration, **recompute the critical path** (a new path may become critical).
- Stop when target duration is reached or no further compression is possible.

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 AON (Precedence) Network for the Worked Example

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    startNode([Start]) --> actA["A : 5d"]
    actA --> actB["B : 3d"]
    actA --> actC["C : 4d"]
    actB --> actD["D : 6d"]
    actC --> actE["E : 5d"]
    actD --> actF["F : 7d"]
    actE --> actF
    actF --> actG["G : 4d"]
    actG --> endNode([End])
```

> **Reading the diagram:** Two parallel branches (B–D and C–E) converge at F. Both have length 14, so F is reached at day 14 regardless of branch. The critical path runs A → (B–D) → F → G or A → (C–E) → F → G.

## 4.2 High-Level Scheduling Process Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    p1["WBS and Activity List"] --> p2["Sequence Activities: identify dependencies"]
    p2 --> p3["Build Network Diagram AON or AOA"]
    p3 --> p4["Estimate Activity Durations"]
    p4 --> p5["Forward Pass: compute ES EF"]
    p5 --> p6["Backward Pass: compute LS LF"]
    p6 --> p7["Compute Float and Identify Critical Path"]
    p7 --> p8{"Within Target Duration?"}
    p8 -- No --> p9["Apply Crashing or Fast Tracking"]
    p9 --> p3
    p8 -- Yes --> p10["Apply Resource Leveling and Smoothing"]
    p10 --> p11["Add Buffers if CCPM adopted"]
    p11 --> p12["Publish Schedule Baseline and Gantt Chart"]
```

## 4.3 Gantt Chart Schematic (Block-Level)

```mermaid
gantt
    title Software Project Gantt Chart (Days 1 to 25)
    dateFormat  D
    axisFormat  %d
    section Planning
    A Requirements Analysis        :a1, 0, 5d
    section Design
    B System Design                :a2, after a1, 3d
    C Database Design              :a3, after a1, 4d
    section Build
    D Module Coding Frontend       :a4, after a2, 6d
    E Module Coding Backend        :a5, after a3, 5d
    section Integration
    F Integration and Testing      :a6, after a4 a5, 7d
    section Release
    G UAT and Deployment           :a7, after a6, 4d
    section Milestone
    M1 Sign Off                    :milestone, 0, 0d
    M2 Code Complete               :milestone, after a4 a5, 0d
    M3 Go Live                     :milestone, after a7, 0d
```

## 4.4 Crashing Decision Block Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    q1["Identify all critical paths"] --> q2["List cost slope C_i of each activity"]
    q2 --> q3["Pick the activity with minimum C_i"]
    q3 --> q4{"Is crash limit reached?"}
    q4 -- No --> q5["Crash by 1 day, add cost"]
    q5 --> q6["Recompute critical paths"]
    q6 --> q7{"Target duration met?"}
    q7 -- No --> q2
    q7 -- Yes --> q8["Stop: optimal crash plan"]
    q4 -- Yes --> q9["Pick next lowest C_i and retry"]
    q9 --> q4
```

> [!TIP]
> **KTU Visual Tip:** Even if the question does not ask for a diagram, drawing a clean AON network + a float table earns **easy 2–3 marks** in Part B answers.

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`
**Differentiate between Activity Sequencing and Schedule Development in software project management.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Aspect | Activity Sequencing | Schedule Development |
|--------|--------------------|-----------------------|
| **Goal** | Identify the *logical order* of activities | Convert the *logical order* into a *time-phased calendar plan* |
| **Output** | Project network diagram (AON/AOA) | Schedule baseline + Gantt chart |
| **Inputs** | WBS, activity list, dependencies | Network + duration estimates + resources + calendars |
| **Focus** | Logic | Timing and resource feasibility |

(1 mark each for clear distinction + 1 mark for output identification.)

---

### Q2. `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`
**List and briefly explain the four types of dependencies in PDM.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
1. **Finish-to-Start (FS):** Successor can start only after predecessor finishes. *(1 mark)*
2. **Start-to-Start (SS):** Successor can start only after predecessor starts. *(0.5 mark)*
3. **Finish-to-Finish (FF):** Successor can finish only after predecessor finishes. *(0.5 mark)*
4. **Start-to-Finish (SF):** Successor can finish only after predecessor starts. *(0.5 mark)*
5. Mention that FS is the most common and is the **default** in PMBOK. *(0.5 mark)*

---

## 5.2 Part B — Module Internal Choice (14 Marks)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:**
> 1. **Do not confuse Total Float with Free Float.** Free float is bounded by the *earliest* start of the immediate successor, while total float uses the *latest* start. Marks deducted: up to 2.
> 2. In the **backward pass**, when an activity has *multiple successors*, students often forget to take the **MIN** of successor LS values — leading to a wrong critical path. Always show the MIN explicitly.
> 3. **PERT vs CPM:** If the question mentions uncertainty/optimistic/pessimistic, it is **PERT**; if it gives a single deterministic duration, it is **CPM**. Mixing the two forfeits marks.
> 4. **Fast-tracking vs Crashing:** Fast-tracking changes the *network* (parallelism), crashing changes the *duration* (resources). Examiners check this distinction carefully.
> 5. **Milestones have zero duration.** Do not allocate days to them in the schedule.

---

### Question A (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2022]`

**(a)** Construct the precedence network (AON) for a software project with the following activities, and perform a forward and backward pass to identify the **critical path** and project duration. *(7 marks)*

| Activity | Duration (days) | Predecessors |
|----------|-----------------|--------------|
| A | 6 | — |
| B | 4 | A |
| C | 3 | A |
| D | 5 | B |
| E | 7 | C |
| F | 4 | D, E |
| G | 2 | F |

**Model Solution (7 marks):**

**Step 1 — Draw the AON diagram.** *(1 mark)*

Nodes: A → B, C → D, E → F → G, with B → D, C → E, D & E → F, F → G.

**Step 2 — Forward Pass.** *(2 marks)*

| Activity | Duration | ES | EF |
|----------|----------|----|----|
| A | 6 | 0 | 6 |
| B | 4 | 6 | 10 |
| C | 3 | 6 | 9 |
| D | 5 | 10 | 15 |
| E | 7 | 9 | 16 |
| F | 4 | max(15,16) = 16 | 20 |
| G | 2 | 20 | 22 |

**Step 3 — Backward Pass.** *(2 marks)*

| Activity | Duration | LS | LF |
|----------|----------|----|----|
| G | 2 | 20 | 22 |
| F | 4 | 16 | 20 |
| E | 7 | 9 | 16 |
| D | 5 | 11 | 16 |
| C | 3 | 6 | 9 |
| B | 4 | 7 | 11 |
| A | 6 | 0 | 6 |

**Step 4 — Critical Path.** *(1 mark)*

Critical Path: **A → C → E → F → G** (since these activities have TF = 0; B–D has TF = 1 day).

**Project Duration = 6 + 3 + 7 + 4 + 2 = 22 days.** *(1 mark)*

---

**(b)** Explain the difference between **Crashing** and **Fast-Tracking** as schedule compression techniques. When would a project manager prefer one over the other? *(7 marks)*

**Model Solution (7 marks):**

1. **Crashing (3 marks):**
   - Definition: Adding *resources* (e.g., extra developers, overtime) to critical-path activities to reduce their duration. *(1)*
   - Cost implication: Increases project cost; cannot reduce below the **crash duration** (technical minimum). *(1)*
   - Risk implication: Low risk — scope and quality preserved. *(1)*

2. **Fast-Tracking (3 marks):**
   - Definition: Performing activities in *parallel* that were originally planned sequentially, by changing dependencies. *(1)*
   - Cost implication: Cost-neutral or cost-saving (less calendar time = less overhead). *(1)*
   - Risk implication: High risk — rework, integration defects, dependency conflicts. *(1)*

3. **When to prefer:** *(1 mark)*
   - Choose **Crashing** when scope is fixed, budget permits, and quality cannot be compromised (e.g., compliance-driven projects).
   - Choose **Fast-Tracking** when time-to-market is critical and the team can absorb integration risk (e.g., MVP product launches).

---

### Question B (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - May 2023]`

**(a)** Consider a software project with the following PERT estimates. Compute the **expected duration, variance, and the probability of completing the project in 30 days.** *(7 marks)*

| Activity | Optimistic (o) | Most likely (m) | Pessimistic (p) | Predecessors |
|----------|----------------|------------------|------------------|--------------|
| A | 2 | 4 | 12 | — |
| B | 3 | 5 | 13 | A |
| C | 1 | 3 | 11 | A |
| D | 4 | 6 | 14 | B |
| E | 2 | 4 | 12 | C |
| F | 3 | 5 | 13 | D, E |

**Model Solution (7 marks):**

**Step 1 — Compute $t_e$ and $\sigma^2$ for each activity.** *(3 marks)*

Using $t_e = \frac{o + 4m + p}{6}$ and $\sigma^2 = \left(\frac{p - o}{6}\right)^2$:

| Activity | $t_e$ | $\sigma^2$ |
|----------|-------|------------|
| A | $\frac{2+16+12}{6}=5$ | $\left(\frac{10}{6}\right)^2=2.78$ |
| B | $\frac{3+20+13}{6}=6$ | $2.78$ |
| C | $\frac{1+12+11}{6}=4$ | $2.78$ |
| D | $\frac{4+24+14}{6}=7$ | $\left(\frac{10}{6}\right)^2=2.78$ |
| E | $\frac{2+16+12}{6}=5$ | $2.78$ |
| F | $\frac{3+20+13}{6}=6$ | $2.78$ |

**Step 2 — Identify Critical Path.** *(2 marks)*

Two parallel branches:
- Path 1: A → B → D → F = 5 + 6 + 7 + 6 = **24 days**
- Path 2: A → C → E → F = 5 + 4 + 5 + 6 = **20 days**

Critical path: **A → B → D → F** (longer).

**Step 3 — Expected project duration and variance.** *(1 mark)*

$$T_e = 24 \text{ days}, \qquad \sigma_e^2 = 2.78 + 2.78 + 2.78 + 2.78 = 11.12$$
$$\sigma_e = \sqrt{11.12} \approx 3.33 \text{ days}$$

**Step 4 — Probability of completion in $T_s = 30$ days.** *(1 mark)*

$$Z = \frac{30 - 24}{3.33} \approx 1.80$$
$$P(T \le 30) = \Phi(1.80) \approx 0.9641 = 96.41\%$$

---

**(b)** Explain **Resource Leveling** and **Resource Smoothing** with suitable examples. State the conditions under which each is used. *(7 marks)*

**Model Solution (7 marks):**

1. **Resource Leveling (3 marks):**
   - Definition: A resource optimization technique in which the *start and finish dates* of activities are adjusted (using float) so that resource demand does not exceed predefined limits. *(1)*
   - **May extend the project duration** beyond the critical-path finish. *(1)*
   - **Example:** Two activities (Coding-A and Coding-B) both need 5 developers simultaneously, but only 7 are available. Coding-B is delayed (within its float) so both never overlap fully. *(1)*

2. **Resource Smoothing (3 marks):**
   - Definition: Adjusting activities *within* their float to keep resource usage smooth, **without changing the project end date**. *(1)*
   - Project completion date is **not extended**. *(1)*
   - **Example:** A testing activity with 5 days of free float is shifted earlier to avoid a weekend when the QA team is unavailable. *(1)*

3. **When to use (1 mark):**
   - **Leveling:** When resources are *scarce* (more demand than supply) — projects must be extended.
   - **Smoothing:** When resources are *adequate in total* but unevenly distributed across time — finish date is sacrosanct.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist — Sequencing & Scheduling Activities**

- **Sequencing ≠ Scheduling.** Sequencing = logic; Scheduling = timing + resources.
- **PDM (AON)** is the default method in modern software PM. Supports FS, SS, FF, SF. **Default dependency = FS.**
- **ADM (AOA)** uses arrows for activities and needs **dummy activities** to show correct logic.
- **Forward pass** uses **MAX** of predecessor EFs; **Backward pass** uses **MIN** of successor LSs.
- **Critical Path** = longest path with **Total Float = 0**. Project duration = sum of durations on this path.
- **Total Float** = $LS - ES$; **Free Float** = $ES_{\text{succ}} - EF$. Float is a *resource* the PM can spend.
- **PERT formula** $t_e = \frac{o + 4m + p}{6}$; **variance** $\sigma^2 = \left(\frac{p - o}{6}\right)^2$.
- **Probability of meeting schedule** uses $Z = \frac{T_s - T_e}{\sigma_e}$ and standard normal table $\Phi(Z)$.
- **Crashing** = add resources, increase **cost**, preserve scope/quality. **Fast-tracking** = parallelise, reduce **time**, increase **risk**.
- **Resource Leveling** may extend the project; **Resource Smoothing** does not.
- **CCPM** uses **Project Buffer, Feeding Buffer, Resource Buffer** to protect the delivery date.
- **Milestones** are zero-duration events, not activities. Never allocate days to them.
- **Lead** = acceleration of successor; **Lag** = deliberate delay of successor. Both modify dependency logic.
- In a KTU 14-mark answer: always draw the **AON diagram (2 marks) + float table (3 marks) + critical path (2 marks)** before any text — these are easy wins.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Network Planning Models
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PEITT633 - SOFTWARE PROJECT MANAGEMENT > - **Module:** Module 3 > - **Topic:** Network Planning Models ## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition > [!IMPORTANT] > **Network Planning Models** are quantitative, mathematical-graph-based management tools used to plan, schedule, monitor, and control the sequence of activities in a software project. They represent project activities and their interdependencies as a logical **Directed Acyclic Graph (DAG)**, enabling managers to compute earliest/latest start and finish times, identify the **Critical Path**, and analyze schedule risk. The two principal network planning techniques used in Software Project Management (per KTU 2024 syllabus) are: | Acronym | Full Form | Core Assumption | |---------|-----------|-----------------| | **CPM** | Critical Path Method | Activity durations are **deterministic** (single, known estimate) | | **PERT** | Program Evaluation and Review Technique | Activity durations are **probabilistic** (optimistic, most likely, pessimistic) | ### 1.2 Key Terminology (KTU Glossary) - **Activity**: A discrete unit of work that consumes time and resources. - **Event (Node/Milestone)**: A point in time marking the start or completion of one or more activities. It consumes **zero time and zero resources**. - **Predecessor**: An activity that must be completed before another can start. - **Successor**: An activity that cannot begin until its predecessor is completed. - **Dummy Activity**: A fictitious zero-duration activity used solely to maintain logical correctness in **AOA (Activity-on-Arrow)** networks. - **Critical Path**: The **longest-duration path** through the network that determines the minimum project duration. Any delay on this path delays the entire project. - **Float / Slack**: The amount of time an activity can be delayed without delaying the project completion (Total Float) or its successors (Free Float). ### 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — "The Train Journey" > [!NOTE] > **Imagine a train journey from Kochi to Delhi.** The train must stop at multiple stations (Events/Nodes). The time taken between two stations is the **Activity**. Some stations (routes) are mandatory and fast (non-critical), while some routes are slow and unavoidable (Critical Path). If a slow route gets delayed, the entire train reaches Delhi late. Network Planning Models help the station master identify *which* route is the slowest *before* the journey starts, so extra resources (more engines, parallel tracks) can be deployed on that route. ### 1.4 AOA vs AON — Two Ways to Draw the Same Project > [!IMPORTANT] > - **AOA (Activity on Arrow / Arrow Diagram Method)**: Arrows = Activities, Nodes = Events. Requires **dummy activities**. > - **AON (Activity on Node / Precedence Diagram Method)**: Boxes/Nodes = Activities, Arrows = Logical dependencies. **No dummy activities needed.** KTU 2024 syllabus prefers AON for software projects. ### 1.5 Standard Metrics (KTU Notation Convention) - **EST** — Earliest Start Time - **EFT** — Earliest Finish Time - **LST** — Latest Start Time - **LFT** — Latest Finish Time - **TF** — Total Float - **FF** — Free Float - **IF** — Independent Float - **μ (te)** — Expected activity duration - **σ²** — Variance of activity duration --- ## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ### 2.1 Why Network Planning? The Underlying Logic 1. **Visualizing Dependencies**: Software projects contain hundreds of tasks (coding, testing, deployment). A network converts this chaos into a logical chain of dependencies. 2. **Time Estimation**: Using mathematical formulas, the project manager computes the **Earliest** and **Latest** time each activity can occur. 3. **Critical Path Identification**: The path where every activity has **zero float** is the critical path — it dictates the project deadline. 4. **Risk Analysis (PERT)**: Probabilistic models allow managers to quote *"There is a 90% probability the project finishes by date X"*. 5. **Resource Levelling & Crashing**: Knowing the critical path helps decide where to add resources (crashing) or smooth them (levelling). ### 2.2 The Two Methodologies in Depth #### A. Critical Path Method (CPM) - **Assumption**: Each activity has a **single, fixed duration**. - **Best suited for**: Repetitive software projects where historical data is available (e.g., maintenance releases). - **Output**: Exact project duration, Critical Path, and Float for every activity. #### B. Program Evaluation and Review Technique (PERT) - **Assumption**: Each activity duration is a **random variable following a Beta distribution**. - **Three time estimates required**: - **o** = Optimistic time (best-case scenario, no problems) - **m** = Most likely time (normal conditions) - **p** = Pessimistic time (worst-case scenario) - **Best suited for**: Novel R&D software projects with high uncertainty (e.g., AI/ML development). ### 2.3 The Forward Pass (Computing EST & EFT) > [!NOTE] > The **Forward Pass** starts from the project start node and moves *towards* the end, computing the earliest time each event/activity can occur. **Logic Steps:** 1. Set the **EST of the first activity = 0** (project kick-off). 2. For every subsequent activity: $$\text{EST} = \max(\text{EFT of all immediate predecessors})$$ 3. Compute **EFT**: $$\text{EFT} = \text{EST} + \text{Duration (or Expected Time } t_e \text{)}$$ 4. The **maximum EFT at the final node = Expected Project Duration**. ### 2.4 The Backward Pass (Computing LST & LFT) > [!NOTE] > The **Backward Pass** starts from the project end node and moves *backwards* to the start, computing the latest time an activity can occur without delaying the project. **Logic Steps:** 1. Set the **LFT of the last activity = Expected Project Duration**. 2. For every preceding activity: $$\text{LFT} = \min(\text{LST of all immediate successors})$$ 3. Compute **LST**: $$\text{LST} = \text{LFT} - \text{Duration}$$ ### 2.5 Float Calculations (The Heart of Network Analysis) | Float Type | Formula | Meaning | |------------|---------|---------| | **Total Float (TF)** | $\text{TF} = \text{LST} - \text{EST}$ | Delay an activity can tolerate *without delaying the project* | | **Free Float (FF)** | $\text{FF} = \text{EST of successor} - \text{EFT of current activity}$ | Delay an activity can tolerate *without delaying any successor* | | **Independent Float (IF)** | $\text{IF} = \text{FF} - \text{Slack of predecessor}$ | Delay an activity can tolerate *assuming predecessor is at LST* | | **Critical Activity** | $\text{TF} = 0$ | Any delay delays the project | ### 2.6 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet > [!IMPORTANT] > The following table summarizes **all high-yield formulas** for KTU Module 3 problems. Master these — they appear in nearly every Part B question. | # | Formula | Description | Used In | |---|---------|-------------|---------| | 1 | $t_e = \dfrac{o + 4m + p}{6}$ | Expected Time (PERT, Beta distribution mean) | PERT | | 2 | $\sigma^2 = \left(\dfrac{p - o}{6}\right)^2$ | Variance of activity duration | PERT Risk | | 3 | $\sigma = \dfrac{p - o}{6}$ | Standard Deviation | PERT Risk | | 4 | $T_E = \sum t_e$ along path | Expected Project Duration | CPM/PERT | | 5 | $\sigma^2_{\text{project}} = \sum \sigma^2_{\text{critical path}}$ | Project Variance (sum of variances on CP) | PERT Probability | | 6 | $Z = \dfrac{T_s - T_E}{\sigma_{\text{project}}}$ | Z-score for probability calculation | PERT Probability | | 7 | $\text{EST} = \max(\text{EFT of predecessors})$ | Earliest Start | Forward Pass | | 8 | $\text{EFT} = \text{EST} + t_e$ | Earliest Finish | Forward Pass | | 9 | $\text{LFT} = \min(\text{LST of successors})$ | Latest Finish | Backward Pass | | 10 | $\text{LST} = \text{LFT} - t_e$ | Latest Start | Backward Pass | | 11 | $\text{TF} = \text{LST} - \text{EST}$ | Total Float | Slack Analysis | | 12 | $\text{FF} = \text{EST}_{j} - \text{EFT}_{i}$ | Free Float (j = successor of i) | Slack Analysis | ### 2.7 Real-World Engineering Utility - **Software**: Used by Microsoft, TCS, and Infosys in agile-hybrid models to plan sprint dependencies and release dates. - **Construction**: Skyscraper construction uses CPM to pour concrete and erect steel in the right order. - **Aerospace**: NASA used PERT for the Apollo program — a benchmark for probabilistic project planning. - **Healthcare**: Hospital surgery scheduling uses network models to sequence pre-op, surgery, and recovery. --- ## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ### 3.1 Derivating the PERT Expected Time Formula (Beta Distribution Mean) **Theoretical Basis**: PERT assumes the activity duration $X$ follows a **Beta distribution** bounded between optimistic time $o$ and pessimistic time $p$, with mode $m$. The Beta probability density function (general form) is: $$ f(x) = \frac{1}{B(\alpha, \beta)} (x - o)^{\alpha - 1} (p - x)^{\beta - 1} $$ For PERT's standard form, it is assumed that the Beta distribution parameters are $\alpha + \beta = 6$, and the mode is at the most-likely estimate $m$. **Step 1**: The mode of the Beta distribution is given by: $$ \text{Mode} = o + \frac{\alpha - 1}{\alpha + \beta - 2} (p - o) $$ **Step 2**: Since mode $= m$ and $\alpha + \beta = 6$ (the PERT assumption): $$ m = o + \frac{\alpha - 1}{4} (p - o) $$ **Step 3**: Solving for $\alpha$: $$ \frac{\alpha - 1}{4} (p - o) = m - o $$ $$ \alpha - 1 = \frac{4(m - o)}{p - o} $$ $$ \alpha = 1 + \frac{4(m - o)}{p - o} $$ **Step 4**: Since $\alpha + \beta = 6$, we have $\beta = 6 - \alpha$: $$ \beta = 5 - \frac{4(m - o)}{p - o} $$ **Step 5**: The **mean** of the Beta distribution is: $$ \mu = \frac{\alpha}{\alpha + \beta} \cdot (p - o) + o $$ **Step 6**: Substituting $\alpha + \beta = 6$ and the value of $\alpha$: $$ \mu = \frac{\alpha}{6} (p - o) + o $$ **Step 7**: Plugging $\alpha$ from Step 3: $$ \mu = \frac{1}{6} \left[ 1 + \frac{4(m - o)}{p - o} \right] (p - o) + o $$ $$ \mu = \frac{1}{6} \left[ (p - o) + 4(m - o) \right] + o $$ $$ \mu = \frac{p - o + 4m - 4o + 6o}{6} $$ $$ \mu = \frac{o + 4m + p}{6} $$ **Final Derived Result**: $$ \boxed{\, t_e = \frac{o + 4m + p}{6} \,} $$ > [!NOTE] > This is the **mean (expected value)** of the Beta distribution under PERT's standard assumptions. KTU expects students to *quote* this formula but *understand* its derivation for 14-mark questions. ### 3.2 Deriving Project Variance for PERT **Step 1**: The variance of a Beta distribution is: $$ \sigma^2 = \frac{\alpha \beta}{(\alpha + \beta)^2 (\alpha + \beta + 1)} (p - o)^2 $$ **Step 2**: With $\alpha + \beta = 6$, PERT assumes $\alpha \approx \beta \approx 1$ asymptotically, leading to the approximation: $$ \sigma^2 \approx \frac{(p - o)^2}{36} $$ **Step 3**: Because activity durations on a path are **independent random variables**, the variance of the sum equals the sum of variances: $$ \sigma^2_{\text{project}} = \sum_{i \in \text{CP}} \sigma^2_i = \sum_{i \in \text{CP}} \left( \frac{p_i - o_i}{6} \right)^2 $$ ### 3.3 The Probability Calculation Derivation **Step 1**: The **Central Limit Theorem** allows us to approximate the project duration distribution as **Normal** with: - Mean $= T_E$ (expected project duration) - Standard deviation $= \sigma_{\text{project}}$ **Step 2**: The probability that the project finishes by a scheduled date $T_s$ is: $$ P(T \le T_s) = P\left(Z \le \frac{T_s - T_E}{\sigma_{\text{project}}}\right) $$ **Step 3**: The Z-score is computed and looked up in the **standard normal table**: $$ \boxed{\, Z = \frac{T_s - T_E}{\sigma_{\text{project}}} \,} $$ ### 3.4 Worked Example — Forward & Backward Pass (CPM) > [!NOTE] > **Project**: 7 activities with the following data. Compute EST, EFT, LST, LFT, TF, and identify the Critical Path. | Activity | Predecessor | Duration | |----------|-------------|----------| | A | — | 4 | | B | — | 6 | | C | A | 5 | | D | A | 7 | | E | B, C | 4 | | F | D | 3 | | G | E, F | 5 | **Step 1 — Forward Pass:** - **EST(A)** = 0, **EFT(A)** = 0 + 4 = **4** - **EST(B)** = 0, **EFT(B)** = 0 + 6 = **6** - **EST(C)** = EFT(A) = 4, **EFT(C)** = 4 + 5 = **9** - **EST(D)** = EFT(A) = 4, **EFT(D)** = 4 + 7 = **11** - **EST(E)** = max(EFT(B), EFT(C)) = max(6, 9) = **9**, EFT(E) = 9 + 4 = **13** - **EST(F)** = EFT(D) = 11, **EFT(F)** = 11 + 3 = **14** - **EST(G)** = max(EFT(E), EFT(F)) = max(13, 14) = **14**, EFT(G) = 14 + 5 = **19** **Project Duration = 19 days** **Step 2 — Backward Pass:** - **LFT(G)** = 19, **LST(G)** = 19 - 5 = **14** - **LFT(F)** = LST(G) = 14, **LST(F)** = 14 - 3 = **11** - **LFT(E)** = LST(G) = 14, **LST(E)** = 14 - 4 = **10** - **LFT(D)** = LST(F) = 11, **LST(D)** = 11 - 7 = **4** - **LFT(C)** = LST(E) = 10, **LST(C)** = 10 - 5 = **5** - **LFT(B)** = LST(E) = 10, **LST(B)** = 10 - 6 = **4** - **LFT(A)** = min(LST(C), LST(D)) = min(5, 4) = **4**, **LST(A)** = 4 - 4 = **0** **Step 3 — Total Float Calculation:** | Activity | EST | EFT | LST | LFT | TF | Critical? | |----------|-----|-----|-----|-----|----|-----------| | A | 0 | 4 | 0 | 4 | **0** | ✓ | | B | 0 | 6 | 4 | 10 | 4 | ✗ | | C | 4 | 9 | 5 | 10 | 1 | ✗ | | D | 4 | 11 | 4 | 11 | **0** | ✓ | | E | 9 | 13 | 10 | 14 | 1 | ✗ | | F | 11 | 14 | 11 | 14 | **0** | ✓ | | G | 14 | 19 | 14 | 19 | **0** | ✓ | **Critical Path: A → D → F → G (Duration = 19 days)** ✅ --- ## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ### 4.1 AON Network Diagram for the Worked Example ```mermaid graph TD startNode((Start)) --> actA[Activity A
Duration 4] startNode --> actB[Activity B
Duration 6] actA --> actC[Activity C
Duration 5] actA --> actD[Activity D
Duration 7] actB --> actE[Activity E
Duration 4] actC --> actE actD --> actF[Activity F
Duration 3] actE --> actG[Activity G
Duration 5] actF --> actG actG --> endNode((End)) style actA fill:#ff6b6b,stroke:#333,stroke-width:3px,color:#fff style actD fill:#ff6b6b,stroke:#333,stroke-width:3px,color:#fff style actF fill:#ff6b6b,stroke:#333,stroke-width:3px,color:#fff style actG fill:#ff6b6b,stroke:#333,stroke-width:3px,color:#fff style startNode fill:#90EE90,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px style endNode fill:#FFB6C1,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px ``` > [!IMPORTANT] > **Red Nodes** = Critical Path activities (A → D → F → G). **Green Node** = Start. **Pink Node** = End. Critical path activities form an unbroken chain from start to end with **zero total float**. ### 4.2 PERT/CPM Process Flow — Block-Level Functional Architecture ```mermaid graph TD input1[Project Scope
and WBS] --> step1 input2[Activity List
with Dependencies] --> step1 input3[Time Estimates
CPM single or PERT 3 values] --> step1 step1[Step 1 Draw Network
AON or AOA] --> step2 step2[Step 2 Forward Pass
Compute EST and EFT] --> step3 step3[Step 3 Backward Pass
Compute LST and LFT] --> step4 step4[Step 4 Compute Floats
TF FF and IF] --> step5 step5{Step 5 TF equals 0?} step5 -- Yes --> step6[Add to Critical Path] step5 -- No --> step7[Mark as Non Critical] step6 --> step8[Step 6 Identify
Critical Path] step7 --> step8 step8 --> step9{Is it PERT?} step9 -- Yes --> step10[Compute te sigma and Variance] step9 -- No --> step11[Output CPM Schedule] step10 --> step12[Compute Z score and
Probability of Completion] step12 --> step11 step11[Final Output
Project Schedule and Risk Report] style step1 fill:#4ECDC4,color:#fff style step2 fill:#4ECDC4,color:#fff style step3 fill:#4ECDC4,color:#fff style step4 fill:#4ECDC4,color:#fff style step6 fill:#FF6B6B,color:#fff style step8 fill:#FF6B6B,color:#fff style step11 fill:#95E1D3,color:#000 ``` ### 4.3 Standard Normal Distribution Curve (for PERT Probability) > [!NOTE] > This is a **conceptual ASCII representation** of how Z-score maps to probability. In PERT, the project duration follows a Normal distribution centered at $T_E$ with spread $\sigma_{\text{project}}$. ``` Probability Density ▲ │ ▓▓▓▓ │ ▓▓▓▓ ▓▓▓▓▓▓▓ │ ▓▓▓▓▓▓▓ ▓▓▓▓▓▓▓▓▓▓▓▓▓▓▓▓▓▓▓ ▓▓▓▓▓▓▓▓▓▓▓▓▓▓▓▓▓▓▓▓▓ ▓▓▓▓▓▓▓▓▓▓▓▓▓▓▓▓▓▓▓▓▓▓▓ ──┼──────┼──────┼──────────▶ Time -3σ T_E T_E+3σ │ (Z = 0 at T_E) P(T ≤ T_s) = Area under curve from -∞ to T_s ``` --- ## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each) --- **Q1. [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023] Define Critical Path. Why is it important in software project scheduling?** **Model Answer (3 marks):** > [!NOTE] > The **Critical Path** is the **longest-duration path** through a project network that determines the **minimum time required to complete the project**. It consists of activities with **zero total float (TF = 0)**, meaning any delay in any critical activity directly delays the entire project completion date. > > **Importance in Software Project Scheduling**: > 1. **Schedule Baseline**: It defines the project's earliest possible completion date. > 2. **Resource Focus**: Project managers concentrate additional resources (developers, testers) on critical activities to compress the schedule. > 3. **Risk Identification**: Critical activities demand closer monitoring as they are bottlenecks. > 4. **Stakeholder Communication**: Provides a realistic, data-driven deadline to clients. > > *(Award 1 mark each for definition, identification criterion (TF=0), and any 2 importance points.)* --- **Q2. [KTU University Exam - May 2024] Differentiate between CPM and PERT. (Any 3 points)** **Model Answer (3 marks):** | Parameter | CPM | PERT | |-----------|-----|------| | **Time Estimate** | Single deterministic value | Three estimates: $o$, $m$, $p$ | | **Focus** | Time-Cost trade-off | Time-Probability trade-off | | **Best For** | Repetitive, well-known projects | R&D, novel, high-uncertainty projects | | **Duration Model** | Fixed | Probabilistic (Beta distribution) | | **Output** | Critical path & float | Critical path, variance, completion probability | *(Award 1 mark for each valid contrasting point. Table format not mandatory.)* --- ### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Choose ONE) --- #### **Question A (14 Marks) — PERT-Based Numerical Problem** **[KTU University Exam - Dec 2022 / Model Paper 2024]** A software project consists of **8 activities (A–H)**. The three time estimates (in weeks) and dependencies are given below: | Activity | Predecessors | Optimistic (o) | Most Likely (m) | Pessimistic (p) | |----------|--------------|----------------|------------------|------------------| | A | — | 2 | 3 | 10 | | B | — | 3 | 5 | 7 | | C | A | 1 | 2 | 3 | | D | A | 4 | 5 | 12 | | E | B, C | 3 | 6 | 9 | | F | D | 2 | 4 | 6 | | G | E | 1 | 2 | 3 | | H | F, G | 2 | 4 | 6 | ### Part (a) — 7 Marks **Compute the expected time ($t_e$) and variance ($\sigma^2$) for each activity. Find the expected project duration and the critical path.** **Model Solution:** **Step 1: Apply $t_e = (o + 4m + p)/6$ and $\sigma^2 = ((p - o)/6)^2$** | Activity | $o$ | $m$ | $p$ | $t_e = (o+4m+p)/6$ | $\sigma^2 = ((p-o)/6)^2$ | |----------|----|----|----|--------------------|--------------------------| | A | 2 | 3 | 10 | $(2+12+10)/6 = 24/6 = \mathbf{4.00}$ | $((10-2)/6)^2 = (8/6)^2 = \mathbf{1.778}$ | | B | 3 | 5 | 7 | $(3+20+7)/6 = 30/6 = \mathbf{5.00}$ | $((7-3)/6)^2 = (4/6)^2 = \mathbf{0.444}$ | | C | 1 | 2 | 3 | $(1+8+3)/6 = 12/6 = \mathbf{2.00}$ | $((3-1)/6)^2 = (2/6)^2 = \mathbf{0.111}$ | | D | 4 | 5 | 12 | $(4+20+12)/6 = 36/6 = \mathbf{6.00}$ | $((12-4)/6)^2 = (8/6)^2 = \mathbf{1.778}$ | | E | 3 | 6 | 9 | $(3+24+9)/6 = 36/6 = \mathbf{6.00}$ | $((9-3)/6)^2 = (6/6)^2 = \mathbf{1.000}$ | | F | 2 | 4 | 6 | $(2+16+6)/6 = 24/6 = \mathbf{4.00}$ | $((6-2)/6)^2 = (4/6)^2 = \mathbf{0.444}$ | | G | 1 | 2 | 3 | $(1+8+3)/6 = 12/6 = \mathbf{2.00}$ | $((3-1)/6)^2 = (2/6)^2 = \mathbf{0.111}$ | | H | 2 | 4 | 6 | $(2+16+6)/6 = 24/6 = \mathbf{4.00}$ | $((6-2)/6)^2 = (4/6)^2 = \mathbf{0.444}$ | **Step 2: Forward Pass (Find EST, EFT)** | Activity | EST | Duration ($t_e$) | EFT | |----------|-----|------------------|-----| | A | 0 | 4 | 4 | | B | 0 | 5 | 5 | | C | EFT(A) = 4 | 2 | 6 | | D | EFT(A) = 4 | 6 | 10 | | E | max(EFT(B), EFT(C)) = max(5, 6) = 6 | 6 | 12 | | F | EFT(D) = 10 | 4 | 14 | | G | EFT(E) = 12 | 2 | 14 | | H | max(EFT(F), EFT(G)) = max(14, 14) = 14 | 4 | **18** | **Expected Project Duration $T_E$ = 18 weeks** ✅ **Step 3: Backward Pass (Find LST, LFT)** | Activity | LFT | Duration | LST | |----------|-----|----------|-----| | H | 18 | 4 | 14 | | G | LST(H) = 14 | 2 | 12 | | F | LST(H) = 14 | 4 | 10 | | E | LST(G) = 12 | 6 | 6 | | D | LST(F) = 10 | 6 | 4 | | C | LST(E) = 6 | 2 | 4 | | B | LST(E) = 6 | 5 | 1 | | A | min(LST(C), LST(D)) = min(4, 4) = 4 | 4 | 0 | **Step 4: Total Float (TF = LST - EST) and Critical Path** | Activity | EST | EFT | LST | LFT | TF | Critical? | |----------|-----|-----|-----|-----|----|-----------| | A | 0 | 4 | 0 | 4 | **0** | ✓ | | B | 0 | 5 | 1 | 6 | 1 | ✗ | | C | 4 | 6 | 4 | 6 | **0** | ✓ | | D | 4 | 10 | 4 | 10 | **0** | ✓ | | E | 6 | 12 | 6 | 12 | **0** | ✓ | | F | 10 | 14 | 10 | 14 | **0** | ✓ | | G | 12 | 14 | 12 | 14 | **0** | ✓ | | H | 14 | 18 | 14 | 18 | **0** | ✓ | **Critical Path: A → C → E → G → H** (Duration = 4+2+6+2+4 = **18 weeks**) ✅ ### Part (b) — 7 Marks **Compute the project variance and standard deviation. What is the probability of completing the project in 20 weeks?** **Model Solution:** **Step 1: Project Variance** Only critical path variances are summed: $$ \sigma^2_{\text{project}} = \sigma^2_A + \sigma^2_C + \sigma^2_E + \sigma^2_G + \sigma^2_H $$ $$ \sigma^2_{\text{project}} = 1.778 + 0.111 + 1.000 + 0.111 + 0.444 = \mathbf{3.444 \text{ weeks}^2} $$ **Step 2: Project Standard Deviation** $$ \sigma_{\text{project}} = \sqrt{3.444} = \mathbf{1.856 \text{ weeks}} $$ **Step 3: Z-Score for $T_s = 20$ weeks** $$ Z = \frac{T_s - T_E}{\sigma_{\text{project}}} = \frac{20 - 18}{1.856} = \frac{2}{1.856} = \mathbf{1.078} $$ **Step 4: Probability from Standard Normal Table** From Z-tables, $P(Z \le 1.08) \approx 0.8599$ (approximately 0.86). > [!IMPORTANT] > **Conclusion**: There is approximately an **85.99% probability** (≈ 86%) that the project will be completed within 20 weeks. ✅ --- #### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative CPM-Based Problem** **[KTU University Exam - May 2023]** A software development project has the following activities. Construct the network, identify the critical path, and compute all floats. | Activity | Predecessors | Duration (days) | |----------|--------------|-----------------| | A | — | 6 | | B | — | 8 | | C | A | 4 | | D | A | 5 | | E | B | 7 | | F | C | 3 | | G | D, E | 6 | | H | F, G | 4 | ### Part (a) — 7 Marks **Draw the AON network and compute the critical path with project duration.** **Model Solution:** **Step 1: Forward Pass** | Activity | EST | Duration | EFT | |----------|-----|----------|-----| | A | 0 | 6 | 6 | | B | 0 | 8 | 8 | | C | EFT(A) = 6 | 4 | 10 | | D | EFT(A) = 6 | 5 | 11 | | E | EFT(B) = 8 | 7 | 15 | | F | EFT(C) = 10 | 3 | 13 | | G | max(EFT(D), EFT(E)) = max(11, 15) = 15 | 6 | 21 | | H | max(EFT(F), EFT(G)) = max(13, 21) = 21 | 4 | **25** | **Project Duration = 25 days** ✅ **Step 2: Backward Pass** | Activity | LFT | Duration | LST | |----------|-----|----------|-----| | H | 25 | 4 | 21 | | G | LST(H) = 21 | 6 | 15 | | F | LST(H) = 21 | 3 | 18 | | E | LST(G) = 15 | 7 | 8 | | D | LST(G) = 15 | 5 | 10 | | C | LST(F) = 18 | 4 | 14 | | B | LST(E) = 8 | 8 | 0 | | A | min(LST(C), LST(D)) = min(14, 10) = 10 | 6 | 4 | **Step 3: Total Float Table** | Activity | EST | EFT | LST | LFT | TF | Critical? | |----------|-----|-----|-----|-----|----|-----------| | A | 0 | 6 | 4 | 10 | 4 | ✗ | | B | 0 | 8 | 0 | 8 | **0** | ✓ | | C | 6 | 10 | 14 | 18 | 8 | ✗ | | D | 6 | 11 | 10 | 15 | 4 | ✗ | | E | 8 | 15 | 8 | 15 | **0** | ✓ | | F | 10 | 13 | 18 | 21 | 8 | ✗ | | G | 15 | 21 | 15 | 21 | **0** | ✓ | | H | 21 | 25 | 21 | 25 | **0** | ✓ | **Critical Path: B → E → G → H (Duration = 8+7+6+4 = 25 days)** ✅ ### Part (b) — 7 Marks **Compute Free Float and Independent Float for all non-critical activities. If activity G is delayed by 3 days, what is the impact on the project?** **Model Solution:** **Step 1: Free Float (FF = EST of successor - EFT of current)** | Activity | EFT | EST of Successor | FF = EST(succ) - EFT(curr) | |----------|-----|------------------|----------------------------| | A | 6 | min(6,6) = 6 (for C and D) | 6 - 6 = **0** | | C | 10 | 10 (for F) | 10 - 10 = **0** | | D | 11 | 15 (for G) | 15 - 11 = **4** | | F | 13 | 21 (for H) | 21 - 13 = **8** | > [!NOTE] > Free float for A is 0 because both C and D start exactly when A finishes. For multi-successor cases, the **minimum EST** of successors is used. **Step 2: Independent Float (IF = FF - TF of predecessor)** | Activity | FF | TF of Predecessor | IF = FF - TF(predecessor) | |----------|----|--------------------|---------------------------| | C | 0 | TF(A) = 4 | 0 - 4 = **-4** (capped at 0) | | D | 4 | TF(A) = 4 | 4 - 4 = **0** | | F | 8 | TF(C) = 8 | 8 - 8 = **0** | > [!IMPORTANT] > **Independent Float cannot be negative**. When IF < 0, it is reported as 0. Negative IF means the activity would need its predecessor to also be delayed. **Step 3: Impact of 3-day delay in Activity G** - Activity G has **TF = 0** (it is on the critical path). - TF = 0 means **any delay directly delays the project**. - A 3-day delay in G → Project completion shifts from 25 days to **28 days**. > [!WARNING] > **Conclusion**: A 3-day delay in G delays the entire project by 3 days, since G is a critical activity with zero float. ✅ --- ### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout > [!WARNING] > **Common Mark-Loss Pitfalls in Network Planning Problems:** > 1. **Forgetting to compute $t_e$ first**: In PERT problems, students often apply CPM logic on raw $o, m, p$ values. *Always compute expected times first using the PERT formula.* > 2. **Variance only on Critical Path**: Project variance is the **sum of variances of critical path activities ONLY**, not all activities. This is the most common error. > 3. **Z-Score Sign**: Students confuse $(T_s - T_E)$ with $(T_E - T_s)$. If $T_s < T_E$, the Z-score is **negative**, and probability will be less than 0.5. > 4. **Multiple Successor Logic**: In forward pass, EST = **max** of predecessor EFTs. In backward pass, LFT = **min** of successor LSTs. Reversing max/min loses full marks. > 5. **Independent Float Negativity**: Reporting negative IF without correcting to 0 is marked wrong. IF = max(0, FF - TF_predecessor). > 6. **Units in Variance**: Variance units are always **squared** (e.g., weeks²), while standard deviation has the same unit as duration. --- ### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!IMPORTANT] > **Rapid Revision Checklist for Network Planning Models:** - ☐ **CPM** = deterministic durations; **PERT** = probabilistic (Beta distribution) durations. - ☐ PERT Expected Time: $t_e = (o + 4m + p) / 6$ — derived from Beta mean under $\alpha + \beta = 6$ assumption. - ☐ PERT Variance: $\sigma^2 = ((p - o)/6)^2$ — also called the $(p - o)/6$ rule. - ☐ **Forward Pass**: EST = max(predecessor EFTs); EFT = EST + duration. - ☐ **Backward Pass**: LFT = min(successor LSTs); LST = LFT - duration. - ☐ **Total Float** = LST - EST = LFT - EFT. Critical activities have TF = 0. - ☐ **Free Float** = EST(successor) - EFT(current); never negative if activity is non-critical. - ☐ **Independent Float** = FF - TF(predecessor); clamped at 0 if negative. - ☐ **Critical Path** = the unique (or one of few) longest paths from start to end. - ☐ **Project Variance** = sum of variances **only along critical path** (CLT assumption). - ☐ **Probability of Completion** = use Normal distribution with Z = $(T_s - T_E) / \sigma_{\text{project}}$. - ☐ **AOA** uses dummy activities; **AON** does not. KTU prefers AON. - ☐ In PERT, due to Central Limit Theorem, project duration approximates Normal for large projects. - ☐ Remember the **3 standard deviations rule**: 68.27% within $\pm 1\sigma$, 95.45% within $\pm 2\sigma$, 99.73% within $\pm 3\sigma$. - ☐ Always state units in the final answer (days, weeks, months). - ☐ Critical path is **not necessarily unique** — multiple paths can have the same maximum duration. - ☐ Network must be **acyclic** (no loops). If a cycle appears, the dependency table has an error. ---

topics.title: Forward Pass, Backward Pass, Identifying Critical Path
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEITT633 - SOFTWARE PROJECT MANAGEMENT
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Forward Pass, Backward Pass, Identifying Critical Path

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

In the **Critical Path Method (CPM)**, the network of a software project is analyzed using two systematic passes — the **Forward Pass** and the **Backward Pass** — to compute the time-window for every activity, and to identify the **Critical Path** (the longest chain of dependent activities that determines the minimum project duration).

| Term | Symbol | Formal Definition |
|---|---|---|
| Early Start | **ES** | The earliest time an activity can begin, given that all its predecessor activities are complete. |
| Early Finish | **EF** | The earliest time an activity can finish, calculated as $ES + Duration$. |
| Late Start | **LS** | The latest time an activity can begin **without delaying** the project finish date. |
| Late Finish | **LF** | The latest time an activity can finish **without delaying** the project finish date, calculated as $LS + Duration$. |
| Total Float | **TF** | The amount of time an activity can be delayed **without delaying** the overall project completion. |
| Free Float | **FF** | The amount of time an activity can be delayed **without delaying** any **immediate successor**. |
| Critical Path | **CP** | The sequence of activities with **zero total float**; the longest path through the network. |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** For the course *PEITT633 – Software Project Management*, the Critical Path Method is a **high-weightage concept** in Module 3. Board questions frequently ask students to (a) perform the Forward Pass, (b) perform the Backward Pass, and (c) identify the Critical Path from a given Activity-on-Node (AON) network.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Wedding Planning" Intuition

Imagine you are planning a **wedding ceremony** with several tasks: booking the venue, sending invitations, arranging catering, hiring the photographer, and conducting the ceremony. Some tasks can happen in parallel (e.g., booking venue and sending invitations), but others must wait (e.g., catering cannot start until the venue is booked, and the ceremony cannot start until catering and photographer are both ready).

- The **Forward Pass** is like asking: *"If we start today, what is the EARLIEST date each task can finish?"*
- The **Backward Pass** is like asking: *"Working BACKWARDS from the wedding day, what is the LATEST date each task can start without making us late?"*
- The **Critical Path** is the chain of tasks where there is **zero wiggle room** — delay any one of them, and the entire wedding is delayed. These are the "make-or-break" tasks.

This is exactly how project managers schedule software releases, construction projects, and research timelines.

> [!NOTE]
> **Mnemonic Tip:** *Forward = "How soon?"* | *Backward = "How late?"* | *Float = "How much slack do we have?"* | *Critical Path = "Where we have NO slack."*

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Operational Logic of the Forward Pass

The Forward Pass is executed **left-to-right** through the network. The procedure is:

1. **Initialize** the Early Start (ES) of the **first activity** (or activities with no predecessors) to **0**.
2. **Compute Early Finish:** For every activity $i$,
$$EF_i = ES_i + D_i$$
   where $D_i$ is the duration of activity $i$.
3. **Propagate to successors:** The ES of a successor activity equals the **maximum** of the EFs of **all** its immediate predecessors. If activity $j$ has predecessors $p_1, p_2, \dots, p_k$, then:
$$ES_j = \max_{i \in \text{pred}(j)} EF_i$$
4. **Project Duration:** The final project completion time $T$ is the maximum EF among all terminal (end) activities:
$$T = \max_{i \in \text{terminal}} EF_i$$

## 2.2 Operational Logic of the Backward Pass

The Backward Pass is executed **right-to-left** through the network. The procedure is:

1. **Initialize** the Late Finish (LF) of all **terminal activities** to the project duration $T$.
2. **Compute Late Start:** For every activity $i$,
$$LS_i = LF_i - D_i$$
3. **Propagate to predecessors:** The LF of an activity equals the **minimum** of the LSs of **all** its immediate successors. If activity $i$ has successors $s_1, s_2, \dots, s_m$, then:
$$LF_i = \min_{j \in \text{succ}(i)} LS_j$$
4. **Start Node Check:** The LF of the very first activity should equal the project duration $T$ — this serves as a self-verification step.

## 2.3 Float Computations

There are two types of float that students must distinguish:

- **Total Float (TF):** How much the activity can slip without delaying the project.
$$TF_i = LS_i - ES_i = LF_i - EF_i$$

- **Free Float (FF):** How much the activity can slip without delaying any immediate successor.
$$FF_i = \min_{j \in \text{succ}(i)} ES_j - EF_i$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Critical Rule:** An activity is on the **Critical Path** if and only if $TF_i = 0$. The Critical Path is the unique (or sometimes multiple) longest path with zero slack, and its length equals the project duration $T$.

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Concept | Formula | Boundary / Verification Condition |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Early Finish | $EF_i = ES_i + D_i$ | Always $\geq ES_i$ |
| 2 | Early Start of successor $j$ | $ES_j = \max\limits_{i \in \text{pred}(j)} EF_i$ | $ES_{\text{start}} = 0$ |
| 3 | Late Start | $LS_i = LF_i - D_i$ | Always $\leq LF_i$ |
| 4 | Late Finish of activity $i$ | $LF_i = \min\limits_{j \in \text{succ}(i)} LS_j$ | $LF_{\text{end}} = T$ |
| 5 | Total Float | $TF_i = LS_i - ES_i$ | $TF_i \geq 0$ for valid network |
| 6 | Free Float | $FF_i = \min\limits_{j \in \text{succ}(i)} ES_j - EF_i$ | $FF_i \leq TF_i$ |
| 7 | Critical Activity Test | $TF_i = 0$ | All such activities form the CP |
| 8 | Project Duration | $T = \max\limits_{i \in \text{terminal}} EF_i$ | Equal to sum of durations on CP |
| 9 | Self-Check (Start Node) | $LF_{\text{start}} = T$ and $LS_{\text{start}} = 0$ | If violated, network is erroneous |
| 10 | Independent Float | $IF_i = \max(0, \min ES_j - \max LF_p - D_i)$ | Used in free-float based scheduling |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Intuition:** Total Float represents the *project-level* slack (delay shifts the project). Free Float represents the *successor-level* slack (delay does not affect the next activity but may affect the project).

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

In **software project management**, identifying the Critical Path directly informs:
- **Risk Management:** Focus mitigation efforts on critical activities, since their delay propagates to the project end.
- **Resource Leveling:** Non-critical activities can be resource-shared or even outsourced without harming deadlines.
- **Crashing Decisions:** When compressing schedules (e.g., under a "go-live" date pressure), the project manager must invest in shortening *critical* activities — accelerating a non-critical activity yields zero benefit.
- **Agile Translation:** In modern DevOps pipelines, the critical path corresponds to the **longest sequential chain of CI/CD stages, code reviews, and integration tests** that determines release cadence.

---

# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Comprehensive Worked Example — AON Network

> [!NOTE]
> **Problem Statement:** Consider a software development project with **8 activities (A through H)** and the following dependencies and durations:

| Activity | Duration (days) | Predecessors |
|---|---|---|
| A | 5 | — |
| B | 3 | A |
| C | 7 | A, B |
| D | 4 | A |
| E | 6 | C, D |
| F | 2 | D |
| G | 8 | E, F |
| H | 5 | G, C |

**Tasks:** (1) Perform the Forward Pass to find ES and EF for all activities. (2) Perform the Backward Pass to find LS and LF. (3) Compute Total Float and Free Float. (4) Identify the Critical Path and Project Duration.

---

### 3.2 Step 1 — Forward Pass (Left-to-Right)

#### Activity A (no predecessor):

$$ES_A = 0$$

$$EF_A = ES_A + D_A = 0 + 5 = 5$$

#### Activity B (predecessor: A):

$$ES_B = EF_A = 5$$

$$EF_B = ES_B + D_B = 5 + 3 = 8$$

#### Activity C (predecessors: A, B):

$$ES_C = \max(EF_A, EF_B) = \max(5, 8) = 8$$

$$EF_C = ES_C + D_C = 8 + 7 = 15$$

#### Activity D (predecessor: A):

$$ES_D = EF_A = 5$$

$$EF_D = ES_D + D_D = 5 + 4 = 9$$

#### Activity E (predecessors: C, D):

$$ES_E = \max(EF_C, EF_D) = \max(15, 9) = 15$$

$$EF_E = ES_E + D_E = 15 + 6 = 21$$

#### Activity F (predecessor: D):

$$ES_F = EF_D = 9$$

$$EF_F = ES_F + D_F = 9 + 2 = 11$$

#### Activity G (predecessors: E, F):

$$ES_G = \max(EF_E, EF_F) = \max(21, 11) = 21$$

$$EF_G = ES_G + D_G = 21 + 8 = 29$$

#### Activity H (predecessors: G, C):

$$ES_H = \max(EF_G, EF_C) = \max(29, 15) = 29$$

$$EF_H = ES_H + D_H = 29 + 5 = 34$$

#### Project Duration:

$$T = \max(\text{all terminal EFs}) = EF_H = 34 \text{ days}$$

---

### 3.3 Step 2 — Backward Pass (Right-to-Left)

We initialize $LF$ of all terminal activities to $T = 34$. Here, **H is the only terminal activity** that ends the project.

#### Activity H:

$$LF_H = T = 34$$

$$LS_H = LF_H - D_H = 34 - 5 = 29$$

#### Activity G (successors: H):

$$LF_G = LS_H = 29$$

$$LS_G = LF_G - D_G = 29 - 8 = 21$$

#### Activity F (successors: G):

$$LF_F = LS_G = 21$$

$$LS_F = LF_F - D_F = 21 - 2 = 19$$

#### Activity E (successors: G):

$$LF_E = LS_G = 21$$

$$LS_E = LF_E - D_E = 21 - 6 = 15$$

#### Activity C (successors: E, H):

$$LF_C = \min(LS_E, LS_H) = \min(15, 29) = 15$$

$$LS_C = LF_C - D_C = 15 - 7 = 8$$

#### Activity D (successors: E, F):

$$LF_D = \min(LS_E, LS_F) = \min(15, 19) = 15$$

$$LS_D = LF_D - D_D = 15 - 4 = 11$$

#### Activity B (successors: C):

$$LF_B = LS_C = 8$$

$$LS_B = LF_B - D_B = 8 - 3 = 5$$

#### Activity A (successors: B, C, D):

$$LF_A = \min(LS_B, LS_C, LS_D) = \min(5, 8, 11) = 5$$

$$LS_A = LF_A - D_A = 5 - 5 = 0$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Self-Verification:** $LS_A = 0$ and $LF_A = 5$, but wait — we need $LF_A = T = 34$? Actually **no** — the verification is that the LF of the *last* activity equals $T$ (satisfied: $LF_H = 34$). The start activity $LS_A = 0$ and its computed $LF_A = 5$ is just a coincidence based on this network's structure. The correct verification is $LS_{\text{start}} = 0$, which holds.

---

### 3.4 Step 3 — Float Calculations

#### Total Float (TF):

$$TF_i = LS_i - ES_i$$

| Activity | LS | ES | TF = LS − ES |
|---|---|---|---|
| A | 0 | 0 | **0** |
| B | 5 | 5 | **0** |
| C | 8 | 8 | **0** |
| D | 11 | 5 | **6** |
| E | 15 | 15 | **0** |
| F | 19 | 9 | **10** |
| G | 21 | 21 | **0** |
| H | 29 | 29 | **0** |

#### Free Float (FF):

$$FF_i = \min_{j \in \text{succ}(i)} ES_j - EF_i$$

| Activity | Successors' min ES | EF | FF = min(ES) − EF |
|---|---|---|---|
| A | min(5, 8, 5) = 5 | 5 | **0** |
| B | 8 | 8 | **0** |
| C | min(15, 29) = 15 | 15 | **0** |
| D | min(15, 9) = 9 | 9 | **0** |
| E | 21 | 21 | **0** |
| F | 21 | 11 | **10** |
| G | 29 | 29 | **0** |
| H | 34 (project end) | 34 | **0** |

---

### 3.5 Step 4 — Consolidated Schedule Table & Critical Path

| Activity | Duration | ES | EF | LS | LF | TF | FF | Critical? |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| A | 5 | 0 | 5 | 0 | 5 | 0 | 0 | ✔ |
| B | 3 | 5 | 8 | 5 | 8 | 0 | 0 | ✔ |
| C | 7 | 8 | 15 | 8 | 15 | 0 | 0 | ✔ |
| D | 4 | 5 | 9 | 11 | 15 | 6 | 0 | ✘ |
| E | 6 | 15 | 21 | 15 | 21 | 0 | 0 | ✔ |
| F | 2 | 9 | 11 | 19 | 21 | 10 | 10 | ✘ |
| G | 8 | 21 | 29 | 21 | 29 | 0 | 0 | ✔ |
| H | 5 | 29 | 34 | 29 | 34 | 0 | 0 | ✔ |

**Critical Path Identified:** Activities with $TF = 0$ in topological order:

$$\boxed{\textbf{A} \rightarrow \textbf{B} \rightarrow \textbf{C} \rightarrow \textbf{E} \rightarrow \textbf{G} \rightarrow \textbf{H}}$$

**Verification by sum of critical durations:**

$$5 + 3 + 7 + 6 + 8 + 5 = 34 \text{ days} = T \;\;\checkmark$$

---

### 3.6 Analytical Insights Derived from the Worked Example

- **D has Total Float = 6** and **Free Float = 0.** This means D can be delayed up to 6 days **without affecting the project**, but **any delay beyond 6** will push the project completion past 34 days.
- **F has both Total Float = 10 and Free Float = 10.** This means F can slip by 10 days without affecting E, G, H, or the project. F is the most "relaxed" activity.
- The **Critical Path** forms a single continuous chain from start to end with no detours — this is a desirable property for management, as it indicates a clearly identifiable bottleneck.

```mermaid
    classDef crit fill:#FFD700,stroke:#B8860B,stroke-width:3px,color:#000

```

## 4.2 Conceptual Block Diagram — Forward & Backward Pass Flow

The following block diagram shows the **algorithmic procedure** for the two passes. Notice how the Forward Pass feeds the project duration $T$ into the Backward Pass as a starting boundary condition.

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph FP["FORWARD PASS  Left to Right"]
        FP1["Step 1: Set ES of start activity = 0"] --> FP2["Step 2: For each activity i, compute EF_i = ES_i + D_i"]
        FP2 --> FP3["Step 3: For each successor j, set ES_j = max of predecessors EF"]
        FP3 --> FP4["Step 4: Project Duration T = max of terminal EFs"]
    end

    subgraph BP["BACKWARD PASS  Right to Left"]
        BP1["Step 1: Initialize LF of all terminal activities = T"] --> BP2["Step 2: For each activity i, compute LS_i = LF_i - D_i"]
        BP2 --> BP3["Step 3: For each predecessor i, set LF_i = min of successors LS"]
        BP3 --> BP4["Step 4: Verify LS of start activity = 0"]
    end

    FP4 -- "T = Project Duration" --> BP1

    BP4 --> FL["FLOAT COMPUTATION"]
    FP4 --> FL
    FL["TF = LS - ES \n FF = min ES of successors - EF"] --> CP["CRITICAL PATH = Activities with TF = 0"]
    CP --> OUT["CP Length = T"]
```

## 4.3 Gantt-Style Timeline of the Critical Path

The following Mermaid Gantt chart visualizes **only the critical path** along the project timeline, demonstrating that the critical chain occupies the full project duration without gaps.

```mermaid
gantt

    dateFormat  D

    C (7d)         :a3, after a2, 7d
    E (6d)         :a4, after a3, 6d
    G (8d)         :a5, after a4, 8d
    H (5d)         :a6, after a5, 5d
    section Non-Critical (slack shown)
    D (4d + 6 slack) :crit, 5, 4d
    F (2d + 10 slack) :crit, 9, 2d
```
