# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Paper Set B]
**Differentiate between Total Float and Free Float in project network analysis. Why is the Critical Path always the path with zero total float?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Aspect | Total Float (TF) | Free Float (FF) |
|---|---|---|
| Definition | Time an activity can be delayed without delaying the **entire project**. | Time an activity can be delayed without delaying any **immediate successor**. |
| Formula | $TF_i = LS_i - ES_i$ | $FF_i = \min ES_{j} - EF_i$ |
| Scope | Project-level slack | Successor-level slack |
| Relation | $FF_i \leq TF_i$ always | $FF_i$ can equal $TF_i$ (if no successor depends on its ES) |

The **Critical Path** is the longest path through the network and has $TF = 0$ because every activity on this path is the *bottleneck* — any delay immediately propagates to the project end, leaving no project-level slack. (1 mark for the critical-path reasoning)

---

### Q2. [KTU University Exam – June 2024, Expected Pattern]
**What is the significance of the Forward Pass in project scheduling? State the two fundamental formulas used.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

The **Forward Pass** computes the *earliest* time each activity can start and finish, given the network dependencies. It determines the **minimum project duration** $T$ and identifies the earliest-start schedule for all activities. (1 mark for definition + significance)

The two fundamental formulas are:

$$EF_i = ES_i + D_i \quad \text{(Early Finish = Early Start + Duration)}$$

$$ES_j = \max_{i \in \text{pred}(j)} EF_i \quad \text{(Early Start of successor = max EF of predecessors)}$$

(1 mark each for the two formulas)

---

## 5.2 Part B Question — Module 3 Internal Choice (14 Marks)

### Question A — 14 Marks **[KTU University Exam – May 2024 Pattern]**

A software project consists of the following activities. Draw the AON network and answer the following:

| Activity | A | B | C | D | E | F | G | H | I |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| Duration (weeks) | 4 | 6 | 2 | 5 | 3 | 7 | 4 | 6 | 5 |
| Predecessors | — | A | A | B | B | C, D | E | F, G | H |

**Part (a) [7 marks]:** Perform the **Forward Pass** and determine the **Project Duration**.

**Part (b) [7 marks]:** Perform the **Backward Pass**, compute the **Total Float** for each activity, and identify the **Critical Path**.

---

#### Model Solution — Question A

**Step 1: Forward Pass**

| Activity | Predecessors | ES = max(EF of preds) | Duration D | EF = ES + D |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| A | — | 0 | 4 | 4 |
| B | A | 4 | 6 | 10 |
| C | A | 4 | 2 | 6 |
| D | B | 10 | 5 | 15 |
| E | B | 10 | 3 | 13 |
| F | C, D | max(6, 15) = 15 | 7 | 22 |
| G | E | 13 | 4 | 17 |
| H | F, G | max(22, 17) = 22 | 6 | 28 |
| I | H | 28 | 5 | 33 |

**Project Duration $T = 33$ weeks.** (7 marks for forward pass including proper ES/EF computation and $T$)

---

**Step 2: Backward Pass**

Initialize $LF_I = 33$ (terminal activity).

| Activity | Successors | LF = min(LS of succs) | Duration D | LS = LF − D |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| I | — | 33 | 5 | 28 |
| H | I | 28 | 6 | 22 |
| G | H | 22 | 4 | 18 |
| F | H | 22 | 7 | 15 |
| E | G | 18 | 3 | 15 |
| D | F | 15 | 5 | 10 |
| C | F | 15 | 2 | 13 |
| B | D, E | min(10, 15) = 10 | 6 | 4 |
| A | B, C | min(4, 13) = 4 | 4 | 0 |

**Verification:** $LS_A = 0$ ✓

---

**Step 3: Total Float & Critical Path**

| Activity | ES | EF | LS | LF | TF = LS − ES | Critical? |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| A | 0 | 4 | 0 | 4 | **0** | ✔ |
| B | 4 | 10 | 4 | 10 | **0** | ✔ |
| C | 4 | 6 | 13 | 15 | 9 | ✘ |
| D | 10 | 15 | 10 | 15 | **0** | ✔ |
| E | 10 | 13 | 15 | 18 | 5 | ✘ |
| F | 15 | 22 | 15 | 22 | **0** | ✔ |
| G | 13 | 17 | 18 | 22 | 5 | ✘ |
| H | 22 | 28 | 22 | 28 | **0** | ✔ |
| I | 28 | 33 | 28 | 33 | **0** | ✔ |

**Critical Path:**

$$\boxed{\textbf{A} \rightarrow \textbf{B} \rightarrow \textbf{D} \rightarrow \textbf{F} \rightarrow \textbf{H} \rightarrow \textbf{I}}$$

**Verification of CP length:** $4 + 6 + 5 + 7 + 6 + 5 = 33 = T$ ✓ (7 marks for backward pass, floats, CP identification and verification)

---

### Question B — 14 Marks **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Pattern — Alternative Choice]**

A construction project has the activities and durations given below:

| Activity | 1-2 | 1-3 | 2-3 | 2-4 | 3-4 | 3-5 | 4-5 | 4-6 | 5-6 |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| Duration (days) | 6 | 5 | 2 | 7 | 3 | 8 | 4 | 5 | 6 |

**Part (a) [7 marks]:** Construct the AOA (Activity-on-Arrow) network, perform the **Forward Pass**, and determine the **earliest expected time (TE)** for each event and the project completion time.

**Part (b) [7 marks]:** Perform the **Backward Pass**, calculate the **Total Float** for each activity, and identify the **Critical Path**.

---

#### Model Solution — Question B

**Step 1: AOA Network Construction**

The network has 6 nodes (events) and 9 activities. Each activity is represented as an arrow connecting two nodes.

| Activity (i-j) | Duration (days) | Predecessor Events |
|---|---|---|
| 1-2 | 6 | — |
| 1-3 | 5 | — |
| 2-3 | 2 | 1-2 |
| 2-4 | 7 | 1-2 |
| 3-4 | 3 | 1-3, 2-3 |
| 3-5 | 8 | 1-3, 2-3 |
| 4-5 | 4 | 2-4, 3-4 |
| 4-6 | 5 | 2-4, 3-4 |
| 5-6 | 6 | 3-5, 4-5 |

---

**Step 2: Forward Pass — Earliest Event Times ($TE_j$)**

$$TE_1 = 0 \quad \text{(start event)}$$

$$TE_2 = TE_1 + D_{1-2} = 0 + 6 = 6$$

$$TE_3 = \max(TE_1 + D_{1-3}, TE_2 + D_{2-3}) = \max(0+5, 6+2) = \max(5, 8) = 8$$

$$TE_4 = \max(TE_2 + D_{2-4}, TE_3 + D_{3-4}) = \max(6+7, 8+3) = \max(13, 11) = 13$$

$$TE_5 = \max(TE_3 + D_{3-5}, TE_4 + D_{4-5}) = \max(8+8, 13+4) = \max(16, 17) = 17$$

$$TE_6 = \max(TE_4 + D_{4-6}, TE_5 + D_{5-6}) = \max(13+5, 17+6) = \max(18, 23) = 23$$

**Project Completion Time $T = TE_6 = 23$ days.** (7 marks)

---

**Step 3: Backward Pass — Latest Event Times ($TL_i$)**

$$TL_6 = TE_6 = 23 \quad \text{(end event)}$$

$$TL_5 = TL_6 - D_{5-6} = 23 - 6 = 17$$

$$TL_4 = \min(TL_6 - D_{4-6}, TL_5 - D_{4-5}) = \min(23-5, 17-4) = \min(18, 13) = 13$$

$$TL_3 = \min(TL_5 - D_{3-5}, TL_4 - D_{3-4}) = \min(17-8, 13-3) = \min(9, 10) = 9$$

$$TL_2 = \min(TL_4 - D_{2-4}, TL_3 - D_{2-3}) = \min(13-7, 9-2) = \min(6, 7) = 6$$

$$TL_1 = \min(TL_3 - D_{1-3}, TL_2 - D_{1-2}) = \min(9-5, 6-6) = \min(4, 0) = 0$$

**Verification:** $TL_1 = 0 = TE_1$ ✓ (Network is consistent)

---

**Step 4: Total Float for each Activity**

$$TF_{i-j} = TL_j - TE_i - D_{i-j}$$

| Activity (i-j) | $TE_i$ | $D_{i-j}$ | $TL_j$ | $TF = TL_j - TE_i - D_{i-j}$ | Critical? |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1-2 | 0 | 6 | 6 | 0 | ✔ |
| 1-3 | 0 | 5 | 9 | 4 | ✘ |
| 2-3 | 6 | 2 | 9 | 1 | ✘ |
| 2-4 | 6 | 7 | 13 | 0 | ✔ |
| 3-4 | 8 | 3 | 13 | 2 | ✘ |
| 3-5 | 8 | 8 | 17 | 1 | ✘ |
| 4-5 | 13 | 4 | 17 | 0 | ✔ |
| 4-6 | 13 | 5 | 23 | 5 | ✘ |
| 5-6 | 17 | 6 | 23 | 0 | ✔ |

**Critical Path:** Activities with $TF = 0$ in topological sequence:

$$\boxed{1 \rightarrow 2 \rightarrow 4 \rightarrow 5 \rightarrow 6 \quad \text{i.e., activities (1-2) → (2-4) → (4-5) → (5-6)}}$$

**Verification of CP length:** $6 + 7 + 4 + 6 = 23 = T$ ✓ (7 marks)

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Sign error in Backward Pass:** Students often write $LS = LF + D$ instead of $LS = LF - D$. This is the **#1 mark-loss reason** in CPM problems. Remember: $LS$ is *earlier* than $LF$ by the duration.
> 2. **Confusion between Float types:** Marks are deducted when students write $FF$ as the project-level slack. Always clarify: **Total Float = project-level, Free Float = successor-level**.
> 3. **Multiple-successor bug:** For activity with multiple successors, $LF_i = \min(LS_{\text{all succs}})$, NOT the sum or max. For activity with multiple predecessors, $ES_j = \max(EF_{\text{all preds}})$.
> 4. **Missing verification step:** The KTU examiner **rewards** students who show the critical-path sum equals the project duration $T$ — failing to do this costs 1–2 marks.
> 5. **AON vs AOA mix-up:** Read the question carefully. AON (Activity-on-Node) places activities in boxes; AOA (Activity-on-Arrow) places them on arrows with nodes being events.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist for Forward Pass, Backward Pass & Critical Path**

- **Forward Pass (Left → Right):** Computes *earliest* times. Use $EF_i = ES_i + D_i$ and $ES_j = \max(EF \text{ of predecessors})$.
- **Backward Pass (Right → Left):** Computes *latest* times. Use $LS_i = LF_i - D_i$ and $LF_i = \min(LS \text{ of successors})$.
- **Project Duration $T$:** Equals the maximum EF (Forward Pass) of all terminal activities and equals the length of the Critical Path.
- **Total Float $TF = LS - ES$:** Slack at the project level. **Zero $TF$ ⇒ Critical Activity.**
- **Free Float $FF = \min(ES_{\text{succ}}) - EF$:** Slack at the successor level. Always $\leq TF$.
- **Critical Path:** Sequence of zero-float activities from start to end. Its total duration **must** equal $T$ — this is the verification rule.
- **Network Verification:** $LS_{\text{start}} = 0$ and $LF_{\text{end}} = T$. If violated, the network has a logic error.
- **Multiple Critical Paths:** Possible when two or more chains both have $TF = 0$ and equal lengths. Report **all** such paths.
- **AON vs AOA:** AON is the modern, simpler convention; AOA is classical and requires dummy activities for parallel dependencies.
- **Float is always ≥ 0:** A negative float indicates the network is infeasible given the required completion date — escalate to project sponsor.
- **Managerial Insight:** Crashing, fast-tracking, and resource-leveling decisions **must** be made on the Critical Path — accelerating non-critical activities yields zero project benefit.
- **KTU Exam Tip:** Always tabulate the final schedule (ES, EF, LS, LF, TF, FF) — a well-organized table typically fetches the full 14 marks without dispute.
- **Mnemonic:** *"Forward finds how-soon; Backward finds how-late; Float finds how-much-slack; Critical finds no-slack."*

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Activity Float
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEITT633 - SOFTWARE PROJECT MANAGEMENT
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Activity Float

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

In **Software Project Management (SPM)**, an **Activity Float** (also called *slack*) is a scheduling metric that quantifies the amount of time an activity can be delayed beyond its earliest start without causing a delay to the **project completion date** or to the **start of any successor activity**.

Mathematically, an Activity Float represents the *degree of scheduling flexibility* permitted on a specific task within a project network diagram (typically an Activity-on-Arrow – AOA – or Activity-on-Node – AON – model). It is computed after completing both the **Forward Pass** (to determine Early Start – ES, and Early Finish – EF) and the **Backward Pass** (to determine Late Start – LS, and Late Finish – LF) over the project network.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Scheme Definition (Verbatim Style):**
> *"Activity Float is the amount of time a scheduled activity in a project network can be delayed without delaying the project completion date (Total Float), or without delaying the early start of any successor activity (Free Float)."*

The **Project Management Body of Knowledge (PMBOK)** also recognises Activity Float as a critical **Critical Path Method (CPM)** output that supports schedule compression, resource levelling, and risk-aware decision making.

## 1.2 The Four Canonical Categories of Activity Float

KTU examiners typically test **four** distinct types of float:

| Float Type | Core Question Answered | Exam Importance |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Total Float (TF)** | How much can this activity slip without delaying the project finish? | ★★★★★ (Most asked) |
| **Free Float (FF)** | How much can this activity slip without delaying any immediate successor? | ★★★★★ |
| **Independent Float (IF)** | How much can this activity slip assuming predecessors were LATE? | ★★★ |
| **Interfering Float (IntF)** | How much does this activity "eat into" the project slack? | ★★ |

## 1.3 Real-World Analogy — The "Train Platform" Model

Imagine you are at a **railway junction** and three trains must reach the platform:

* **Train A** is express and must arrive exactly at 8:00 AM.
* **Train B** has a 15-minute scheduled halt, but the platform is empty for 30 minutes.
* **Train C** is delayed by 10 minutes on the track.

The *Total Float* of Train B is the maximum delay it can absorb (e.g., 15 minutes) before Train A (the project deadline) is impacted. The *Free Float* is the maximum delay before Train C (a successor event) is impacted. The *Interfering Float* is the part of the float that *interferes* with the project's master clock.

> [!NOTE]
> **Intuition Hook:** If a task lies on the **Critical Path**, it has **Zero Float** — any delay *will* delay the project. If a task is non-critical, it has *positive float* — it has built-in "schedule cushion" that can absorb delays or be used for resource smoothing.

## 1.4 Why Activity Float Matters in Software Engineering

In a software project, floats are vital for:

1. **Schedule Resilience** – Buffering against late bug-fixes, code reviews, or testing overruns.
2. **Resource Levelling** – Freeing developers from critical-path tasks so they can assist on bottlenecks.
3. **Critical Path Identification** – Activities with **TF = 0** form the critical path; these *cannot* be delayed.
4. **Earned Value Management (EVM)** – Float consumption is a leading indicator of schedule risk.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Conceptual Foundation: The Time Window

For every activity *i*, the network gives us a **Time Window** of execution:

$$
\text{Window}_i = [\text{Early Start},\ \text{Late Finish}] = [ES_i,\ LF_i]
$$

The **duration** $t_i$ must fit *inside* this window. Therefore:

$$
t_i \le (LF_i - ES_i)
$$

The **slack** is the *spare capacity* of the window after accommodating the activity duration.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> A larger float ⇒ more schedule flexibility ⇒ less project risk. A zero float ⇒ **critical activity**.

## 2.2 Step-by-Step Logic of Each Float Type

### A. Total Float (TF)
* **Definition:** Maximum time an activity can be delayed without extending the project's completion date.
* **Logic:**
  1. Compute the latest allowable finish for the activity: $LF_i$.
  2. Compare it to the earliest possible finish: $EF_i$.
  3. Difference = $TF_i$.
* **Use Case:** Project manager uses TF to identify non-critical activities and to reallocate developer time.

### B. Free Float (FF)
* **Definition:** Maximum time an activity can be delayed without delaying the **early start of any successor**.
* **Logic:**
  1. Identify the **earliest** start of all immediate successors.
  2. Compute the difference between that earliest successor start and the activity's early finish.
  3. If multiple successors, use the **minimum** successor ES.
* **Use Case:** Useful for **crashing** the schedule — if a successor has its own slack, this activity's FF is consumed but the project is unaffected.

### C. Independent Float (IF)
* **Definition:** The amount of free time an activity has **assuming all its predecessors finished as late as possible** and **all its successors start as early as possible**.
* **Logic:**
  1. Take the minimum ES of all successors.
  2. Subtract the LF of the current activity and the activity's duration.
  3. If negative, IF = 0.
* **Use Case:** Measures the activity's *true* autonomy from neighbouring tasks.

### D. Interfering Float (IntF)
* **Definition:** The portion of Total Float that, if consumed, *will* delay the project.
* **Logic:** $IntF_i = TF_i - FF_i$.

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **MASTER FORMULA TABLE — Memorise this for KTU 2024 Exams**

| Float Type | Symbol | Formula | Verbal Form | Units |
| :--- | :---: | :--- | :--- | :---: |
| **Total Float** | $TF_i$ | $TF_i = LS_i - ES_i$ | Late Start − Early Start | Days |
| **Total Float (Alt)** | $TF_i$ | $TF_i = LF_i - EF_i$ | Late Finish − Early Finish | Days |
| **Free Float** | $FF_i$ | $FF_i = \min_{j \in S(i)} ES_j - EF_i$ | Min ES of successors − EF | Days |
| **Free Float (End Activity)** | $FF_{end}$ | $FF_{end} = T_p - EF_{end}$ | Project duration − EF | Days |
| **Independent Float** | $IF_i$ | $IF_i = \min_{j \in S(i)} ES_j - LF_i - t_i$ | Min ES(succ) − LF − duration | Days |
| **Independent Float (Safe)** | $IF_i$ | $IF_i = \max(0,\ \min ES_j - LF_i - t_i)$ | Same as above, floored at 0 | Days |
| **Interfering Float** | $IntF_i$ | $IntF_i = TF_i - FF_i$ | Total Float − Free Float | Days |

Where:
* $S(i)$ = the set of **immediate successor activities** of activity $i$.
* $T_p$ = the **project completion time** (length of the critical path).
* $t_i$ = the **duration** of activity $i$.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Pitfall #1:** A common student error is computing $FF$ using the **late start** of the successor. The correct value is the **early start** of the successor. Always re-read the definition carefully in the exam.

## 2.4 Engineering Utility of Activity Float

In a real software project, Activity Float is the *lingua franca* of schedule risk:

* **Agile Scrum analogy:** Float is the "spare capacity" between a Sprint commitment and the actual capability of the team. The Sprint Goal has a *critical path* of must-do stories (zero float) and a *backlog* of stretch goals (positive float).
* **DevOps Pipeline analogy:** A build job with zero float is on the deployment critical path; any failure blocks the release. A documentation build has free float — it can fail without halting production deployment.
* **Critical Chain Project Management (CCPM):** Buffers (Project Buffer, Feeding Buffer) are explicitly derived from the float of feeding chains.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Reference Network for Worked Example

Consider the following **Activity-on-Node (AON)** network for a small software build:

| Activity | Description | Duration (days) | Predecessor(s) |
| :---: | :--- | :---: | :---: |
| A | Requirements Gathering | 3 | — |
| B | System Design | 4 | A |
| C | Database Design | 2 | A |
| D | Frontend Coding | 5 | B |
| E | Backend Coding | 3 | C |
| F | Integration & Testing | 2 | D, E |

> [!NOTE]
> **Network Logic:** A → B → D → F, and A → C → E → F (parallel branches merge at F).

## 3.2 Forward Pass Calculation (Determines ES and EF)

The Forward Pass traverses the network from start to end, computing **Early Start (ES)** and **Early Finish (EF)**.

$$
ES_i = \max_{k \in P(i)} EF_k \quad ; \quad EF_i = ES_i + t_i
$$

For the start activity, $ES_A = 0$.

### Step-by-Step Forward Pass

$$
\begin{aligned}
ES_A &= 0 \\
EF_A &= ES_A + t_A = 0 + 3 = 3 \\[4pt]
ES_B &= EF_A = 3 \\
EF_B &= ES_B + t_B = 3 + 4 = 7 \\[4pt]
ES_C &= EF_A = 3 \\
EF_C &= ES_C + t_C = 3 + 2 = 5 \\[4pt]
ES_D &= EF_B = 7 \\
EF_D &= ES_D + t_D = 7 + 5 = 12 \\[4pt]
ES_E &= EF_C = 5 \\
EF_E &= ES_E + t_E = 5 + 3 = 8 \\[4pt]
ES_F &= \max(EF_D,\ EF_E) = \max(12,\ 8) = 12 \\
EF_F &= ES_F + t_F = 12 + 2 = 14
\end{aligned}
$$

**Project Duration $T_p = EF_F = 14$ days.**

## 3.3 Backward Pass Calculation (Determines LS and LF)

The Backward Pass traverses the network from end to start, computing **Late Start (LS)** and **Late Finish (LF)**.

$$
LF_i = \min_{j \in S(i)} LS_j \quad ; \quad LS_i = LF_i - t_i
$$

For the end activity, $LF_F = T_p = 14$.

### Step-by-Step Backward Pass

$$
\begin{aligned}
LF_F &= 14 \\
LS_F &= LF_F - t_F = 14 - 2 = 12 \\[4pt]
LF_E &= LS_F = 12 \\
LS_E &= LF_E - t_E = 12 - 3 = 9 \\[4pt]
LF_D &= LS_F = 12 \\
LS_D &= LF_D - t_D = 12 - 5 = 7 \\[4pt]
LF_C &= LS_E = 9 \\
LS_C &= LF_C - t_C = 9 - 2 = 7 \\[4pt]
LF_B &= LS_D = 7 \\
LS_B &= LF_B - t_B = 7 - 4 = 3 \\[4pt]
LF_A &= \min(LS_B,\ LS_C) = \min(3,\ 7) = 3 \\
LS_A &= LF_A - t_A = 3 - 3 = 0
\end{aligned}
$$

## 3.4 Total Float, Free Float, and Independent Float — Exhaustive Calculation

### Total Float (TF) for each activity:

$$
\begin{aligned}
TF_A &= LS_A - ES_A = 0 - 0 = 0 \\
TF_B &= LS_B - ES_B = 3 - 3 = 0 \\
TF_C &= LS_C - ES_C = 7 - 3 = 4 \\
TF_D &= LS_D - ES_D = 7 - 7 = 0 \\
TF_E &= LS_E - ES_E = 9 - 5 = 4 \\
TF_F &= LS_F - ES_F = 12 - 12 = 0
\end{aligned}
$$

### Free Float (FF) for each activity:

$$
\begin{aligned}
FF_A &= \min(ES_B,\ ES_C) - EF_A = \min(3, 3) - 3 = 0 \\
FF_B &= ES_D - EF_B = 7 - 7 = 0 \\
FF_C &= ES_E - EF_C = 5 - 5 = 0 \\
FF_D &= ES_F - EF_D = 12 - 12 = 0 \\
FF_E &= ES_F - EF_E = 12 - 8 = 4 \\
FF_F &= T_p - EF_F = 14 - 14 = 0
\end{aligned}
$$

### Independent Float (IF) for each activity:

$$
\begin{aligned}
IF_A &= \max(0,\ \min(ES_B, ES_C) - LF_A - t_A) = \max(0,\ 3 - 3 - 3) = 0 \\
IF_B &= \max(0,\ ES_D - LF_B - t_B) = \max(0,\ 7 - 7 - 4) = 0 \\
IF_C &= \max(0,\ ES_E - LF_C - t_C) = \max(0,\ 5 - 9 - 2) = 0 \\
IF_D &= \max(0,\ ES_F - LF_D - t_D) = \max(0,\ 12 - 12 - 5) = 0 \\
IF_E &= \max(0,\ ES_F - LF_E - t_E) = \max(0,\ 12 - 12 - 3) = 0 \\
IF_F &= 0 \quad \text{(terminal activity)}
\end{aligned}
$$

### Interfering Float (IntF):

$$
\begin{aligned}
IntF_A &= TF_A - FF_A = 0 - 0 = 0 \\
IntF_B &= TF_B - FF_B = 0 - 0 = 0 \\
IntF_C &= TF_C - FF_C = 4 - 0 = 4 \\
IntF_D &= TF_D - FF_D = 0 - 0 = 0 \\
IntF_E &= TF_E - FF_E = 4 - 4 = 0 \\
IntF_F &= TF_F - FF_F = 0 - 0 = 0
\end{aligned}
$$

## 3.5 Final Float Summary Table

| Activity | Duration | ES | EF | LS | LF | **TF** | **FF** | **IF** | **IntF** | Critical? |
| :---: | :---: | :---: | :---: | :---: | :---: | :---: | :---: | :---: | :---: | :---: |
| A | 3 | 0 | 3 | 0 | 3 | **0** | **0** | **0** | **0** | ✔ YES |
| B | 4 | 3 | 7 | 3 | 7 | **0** | **0** | **0** | **0** | ✔ YES |
| C | 2 | 3 | 5 | 7 | 9 | **4** | **0** | **0** | **4** | ✗ NO |
| D | 5 | 7 | 12 | 7 | 12 | **0** | **0** | **0** | **0** | ✔ YES |
| E | 3 | 5 | 8 | 9 | 12 | **4** | **4** | **0** | **0** | ✗ NO |
| F | 2 | 12 | 14 | 12 | 14 | **0** | **0** | **0** | **0** | ✔ YES |

**Critical Path:** A → B → D → F (Total Project Duration = 14 days)

> [!NOTE]
> **Observation (KTU Exam Gold):** Activity C has *Interfering Float* of 4 days, meaning any delay on C beyond 4 days *will* extend the project. Activity E has *Free Float* of 4 days, meaning it can slip by 4 days without affecting the successor F, but slipping beyond that *will* delay the project.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Project Network Diagram (Mermaid Flowchart)

The following Mermaid flowchart renders the **Activity-on-Node (AON) network** of the worked example, with float values annotated on the edges.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    startNode(["START"]) --> actA["A: Requirements (3d)"]
    actA --> actB["B: System Design (4d)"]
    actA --> actC["C: DB Design (2d)"]
    actB --> actD["D: Frontend (5d)"]
    actC --> actE["E: Backend (3d)"]
    actD --> actF["F: Integration (2d)"]
    actE --> actF
    actF --> endNode(["END - 14d"])

    actC -. "TF=4 / FF=0 / IntF=4" .-> actE
    actE -. "TF=4 / FF=4" .-> actF
```

## 4.2 Float-Type Relationship Block Diagram

The following architecture diagram illustrates how the **four float types** relate to each other and to the **Time Window** of an activity.

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    win["Activity Time Window: ES to LF"]
    dur["Activity Duration t_i"]
    win --> analysis

    subgraph analysis ["FLOAT ANALYSIS LAYER"]
        tfBlock["Total Float TF = LS - ES"]
        ffBlock["Free Float FF = min ES_succ - EF"]
        ifBlock["Independent Float IF = min ES_succ - LF - t"]
        intfBlock["Interfering Float IntF = TF - FF"]
    end

    tfBlock --> crit{"TF = 0 ?"}
    crit -- YES --> critPath["CRITICAL PATH - No Slack"]
    crit -- NO --> nonCrit["NON-CRITICAL - Has Slack"]

    tfBlock --> intfBlock
    ffBlock --> intfBlock
    ifBlock --> note["Worst-case slack"]
```

## 4.3 Time-Window Visualisation (Block Schematic)

```mermaid
gantt
    title Activity Time Window with Float Visualisation
    dateFormat  X
    axisFormat  %d

    section Critical Path
    A_RQS      :a1, 0, 3d
    B_DESIGN   :a2, 3, 4d
    D_FE_CODE  :a3, 7, 5d
    F_INTEG    :a4, 12, 2d

    section Parallel Non-Critical
    C_DB_DES   :crit, 3, 2d
    E_BE_CODE  :active, 5, 3d
    E_SLACK    :crit, 8, 4d
```

> [!NOTE]
> **How to read the Gantt chart:**
> * Solid bars represent the *earliest* execution window.
> * The dotted section after E (Backend Coding) represents its **Free Float** of 4 days — time available before the successor F must begin.
> * Critical Path activities (A, B, D, F) have **no slack** and are stacked with zero gap.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`
**Define Activity Float. Differentiate between Total Float and Free Float with suitable examples.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**Activity Float** is the amount of time a project activity can be delayed beyond its earliest start or finish without delaying the project completion date or the start of any successor activity.

**Total Float (TF):** It is the maximum time an activity can be delayed *without extending the overall project duration*. It is computed as $TF = LS - ES$ or $TF = LF - EF$. *Example:* If a documentation task has $LS = 8$ and $ES = 5$, then $TF = 3$ days, meaning the documentation can be delayed by up to 3 days without delaying the project.

**Free Float (FF):** It is the maximum time an activity can be delayed *without delaying the early start of any immediate successor activity*. It is computed as $FF = \min(ES_{\text{successor}}) - EF$. *Example:* If a UI mockup task finishes at Day 6 and the coding task can start at Day 8, then $FF = 8 - 6 = 2$ days, meaning the mockup can slip by 2 days without pushing the coding start.

**Key Difference:** Total Float considers the *entire project deadline*; Free Float considers *only the next immediate successor*. Therefore, $FF \le TF$ for any activity.

---

### Q2. `[KTU University Exam - June 2024]`
**What is the significance of 'Zero Float' on a project? How is it used to identify the critical path?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**Significance of Zero Float:**
An activity with **Total Float = 0** is called a **Critical Activity**. Such an activity:
1. Has **no scheduling flexibility** — any delay will directly delay the project.
2. Cannot be deferred, re-sequenced, or split without affecting the project completion date.
3. Must be **closely monitored** as it represents a schedule risk hotspot.
4. Often consumes disproportionate management attention and resources.

**Identification of Critical Path:**
The **Critical Path** is the **longest path** through the project network and consists of *all* activities with $TF = 0$. To find the critical path:
1. Perform the Forward Pass to get ES and EF for every activity.
2. Perform the Backward Pass to get LS and LF.
3. Compute $TF = LS - ES$ for each activity.
4. The activities for which $TF = 0$ form the critical path; tracing their predecessors/ successors reveals the critical sequence from project start to project finish.

*Example:* In the worked example, activities A, B, D, and F all have $TF = 0$, so the critical path is **A → B → D → F** with a duration of **14 days**.

---

## PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Module Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**(a)** Define Total Float, Free Float, Independent Float, and Interfering Float. For each, give the formula and a one-line meaning. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** For the project network given below, compute the **Early Start (ES)**, **Early Finish (EF)**, **Late Start (LS)**, **Late Finish (LF)**, **Total Float (TF)**, and **Free Float (FF)** for every activity. Identify the **critical path** and the **project duration**. **(7 marks)**

| Activity | A | B | C | D | E | F | G |
| :---: | :---: | :---: | :---: | :---: | :---: | :---: | :---: |
| Duration | 4 | 6 | 3 | 5 | 4 | 7 | 2 |
| Predecessor | — | A | A | B | C | D, E | F |

---

#### Model Solution

**(a) Definitions and Formulas (7 marks)**

$$
\begin{aligned}
&\textbf{1. Total Float (TF):} && TF = LS - ES = LF - EF \\
&\text{Means: Max delay without extending project completion.} \\[4pt]
&\textbf{2. Free Float (FF):} && FF = \min_{j \in S(i)} ES_j - EF_i \\
&\text{Means: Max delay without delaying early start of any successor.} \\[4pt]
&\textbf{3. Independent Float (IF):} && IF = \max(0,\ \min_{j \in S(i)} ES_j - LF_i - t_i) \\
&\text{Means: Slack assuming worst-case predecessor/successor alignment.} \\[4pt]
&\textbf{4. Interfering Float (IntF):} && IntF = TF - FF \\
&\text{Means: Part of float that, if consumed, will delay the project.}
\end{aligned}
$$

**(b) Network Computation (7 marks)**

**Forward Pass:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
ES_A &= 0, & EF_A &= 0 + 4 = 4 \\
ES_B &= EF_A = 4, & EF_B &= 4 + 6 = 10 \\
ES_C &= EF_A = 4, & EF_C &= 4 + 3 = 7 \\
ES_D &= EF_B = 10, & EF_D &= 10 + 5 = 15 \\
ES_E &= EF_C = 7, & EF_E &= 7 + 4 = 11 \\
ES_F &= \max(EF_D, EF_E) = \max(15, 11) = 15, & EF_F &= 15 + 7 = 22 \\
ES_G &= EF_F = 22, & EF_G &= 22 + 2 = 24
\end{aligned}
$$

**Project Duration $T_p = 24$ days.**

**Backward Pass:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
LF_G &= 24, & LS_G &= 24 - 2 = 22 \\
LF_F &= LS_G = 22, & LS_F &= 22 - 7 = 15 \\
LF_E &= LS_F = 15, & LS_E &= 15 - 4 = 11 \\
LF_D &= LS_F = 15, & LS_D &= 15 - 5 = 10 \\
LF_C &= LS_E = 11, & LS_C &= 11 - 3 = 8 \\
LF_B &= LS_D = 10, & LS_B &= 10 - 6 = 4 \\
LF_A &= \min(LS_B, LS_C) = \min(4, 8) = 4, & LS_A &= 4 - 4 = 0
\end{aligned}
$$

**Float Table:**

| Activity | Duration | ES | EF | LS | LF | **TF** | **FF** | Critical? |
| :---: | :---: | :---: | :---: | :---: | :---: | :---: | :---: | :---: |
| A | 4 | 0 | 4 | 0 | 4 | **0** | **0** | ✔ |
| B | 6 | 4 | 10 | 4 | 10 | **0** | **0** | ✔ |
| C | 3 | 4 | 7 | 8 | 11 | **4** | **0** | ✗ |
| D | 5 | 10 | 15 | 10 | 15 | **0** | **0** | ✔ |
| E | 4 | 7 | 11 | 11 | 15 | **4** | **4** | ✗ |
| F | 7 | 15 | 22 | 15 | 22 | **0** | **0** | ✔ |
| G | 2 | 22 | 24 | 22 | 24 | **0** | **0** | ✔ |

**Critical Path:** A → B → D → F → G  
**Project Duration:** 24 days

---

### Question B (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - June 2024]`

**(a)** Explain the concepts of **Total Float**, **Free Float**, **Independent Float**, and **Interfering Float** in network scheduling. Discuss their inter-relationship with a clear mathematical proof that $IF \le FF \le TF$ for any activity. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Given the following project network, draw the network diagram, perform forward and backward pass, and tabulate ES, EF, LS, LF, TF, and FF. Also identify the critical path. **(7 marks)**

| Activity | 1-2 | 1-3 | 2-4 | 3-4 | 3-5 | 4-5 | 4-6 | 5-6 |
| :---: | :---: | :---: | :---: | :---: | :---: | :---: | :---: | :---: |
| Duration | 5 | 8 | 6 | 4 | 3 | 2 | 7 | 5 |

---

#### Model Solution

**(a) Float Concepts and Proof of $IF \le FF \le TF$ (7 marks)**

**Conceptual Definitions:**

* **Total Float (TF):** $TF_i = LS_i - ES_i$ — the *gross* flexibility available on activity *i* relative to the project deadline.
* **Free Float (FF):** $FF_i = \min_{j \in S(i)} ES_j - EF_i$ — the *net* flexibility that does not impact successor *i*'s early start.
* **Independent Float (IF):** $IF_i = \max(0,\ \min_{j \in S(i)} ES_j - LF_i - t_i)$ — slack under the *worst-case* scheduling assumption.
* **Interfering Float (IntF):** $IntF_i = TF_i - FF_i$ — the part of total float that *will* eventually interfere with the project finish.

**Mathematical Proof that $IF \le FF \le TF$:**

*Step 1 — Prove $FF \le TF$:*  
By definition, $FF_i = \min ES_j - EF_i$. Since $EF_i = ES_i + t_i$, we have $FF_i = \min ES_j - ES_i - t_i$.  
Also, $TF_i = LS_i - ES_i = LF_i - EF_i$. Since $LS_i \le \min LS_j$ and $LS_j \le LF_j$ but $EF_j$ depends on $ES_j$, we conclude $FF_i \le \min ES_j - ES_i - t_i \le LS_i - ES_i = TF_i$.

*Step 2 — Prove $IF \le FF$:*  
By definition, $IF_i = \max(0, \min ES_j - LF_i - t_i)$.  
For the same activity *i*, $LF_i \ge EF_i$, so $\min ES_j - LF_i - t_i \le \min ES_j - EF_i - t_i + (EF_i - LF_i) \le \min ES_j - EF_i = FF_i$.  
Therefore, $IF_i \le FF_i$.

*Step 3 — Conclusion:*  
Combining Steps 1 and 2: $\quad IF \le FF \le TF \quad \blacksquare$

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Insight:** The chain $IF \le FF \le TF$ tells the project manager that *Independent Float is the most conservative* measure (a stricter view of slack), while *Total Float is the most optimistic* (gives the most scheduling room).

**(b) Network Analysis (7 marks)**

**Network Diagram (AOA):**

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    n1(("1")) -- "1-2: 5" --> n2(("2"))
    n1 -- "1-3: 8" --> n3(("3"))
    n2 -- "2-4: 6" --> n4(("4"))
    n3 -- "3-4: 4" --> n4
    n3 -- "3-5: 3" --> n5(("5"))
    n4 -- "4-5: 2" --> n5
    n4 -- "4-6: 7" --> n6(("6"))
    n5 -- "5-6: 5" --> n6
```

**Forward Pass (Node Times):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
E_1 &= 0 \\
E_2 &= E_1 + 5 = 5 \\
E_3 &= E_1 + 8 = 8 \\
E_4 &= \max(E_2 + 6,\ E_3 + 4) = \max(11, 12) = 12 \\
E_5 &= \max(E_3 + 3,\ E_4 + 2) = \max(11, 14) = 14 \\
E_6 &= \max(E_4 + 7,\ E_5 + 5) = \max(19, 19) = 19
\end{aligned}
$$

**Project Duration = 19 days.**

**Backward Pass (Node Times):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
L_6 &= 19 \\
L_5 &= L_6 - 5 = 14 \\
L_4 &= \min(L_6 - 7,\ L_5 - 2) = \min(12, 12) = 12 \\
L_3 &= \min(L_4 - 4,\ L_5 - 3) = \min(8, 11) = 8 \\
L_2 &= L_4 - 6 = 6 \\
L_1 &= \min(L_2 - 5,\ L_3 - 8) = \min(1, 0) = 0
\end{aligned}
$$

**Activity Float Table:**

| Activity | Duration | ES | EF | LS | LF | **TF** | **FF** | Critical? |
| :---: | :---: | :---: | :---: | :---: | :---: | :---: | :---: | :---: |
| 1-2 | 5 | 0 | 5 | 1 | 6 | **1** | 0 (min 5−5) | ✗ |
| 1-3 | 8 | 0 | 8 | 0 | 8 | **0** | 0 | ✔ |
| 2-4 | 6 | 5 | 11 | 6 | 12 | **1** | 1 (12−11) | ✗ |
| 3-4 | 4 | 8 | 12 | 8 | 12 | **0** | 0 | ✔ |
| 3-5 | 3 | 8 | 11 | 11 | 14 | **3** | 3 (14−11) | ✗ |
| 4-5 | 2 | 12 | 14 | 12 | 14 | **0** | 0 | ✔ |
| 4-6 | 7 | 12 | 19 | 12 | 19 | **0** | 0 | ✔ |
| 5-6 | 5 | 14 | 19 | 14 | 19 | **0** | 0 | ✔ |

**Critical Path:** 1 → 3 → 4 → 6 (length 19) **AND** 1 → 3 → 4 → 5 → 6 (length 19)  
*(Multiple critical paths exist — both have zero float.)*

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout — Where Students Lose Marks**
> 
> 1. **Forgetting to take MIN of successor ES in FF calculation.** When an activity has multiple successors, always use the *minimum* of their ES values.
> 2. **Mixing up LS and ES in TF computation.** Total Float is $LS - ES$ or equivalently $LF - EF$. Using the wrong pair (e.g., $LS - EF$) is a common mistake.
> 3. **Ignoring dummy activities in AOA networks.** Forgetting to add +0 duration for dummies during forward/backward pass leads to wrong float values.
> 4. **Not verifying the result.** A correct forward pass should yield project duration ≥ every individual $EF$, and the sum of TF values along a path should not exceed $T_p$. Always sanity-check.
> 5. **Failing to identify *all* critical paths.** In networks with parallel paths of equal length, *multiple* critical paths can exist. The student must list every path with $TF = 0$.

---

## Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Revision Checklist — Activity Float**
> 
> * ☐ **Activity Float** = amount of time a task can slip without harming the project or its successors.
> * ☐ **Total Float (TF):** $TF = LS - ES = LF - EF$. *Project-level* slack.
> * ☐ **Free Float (FF):** $FF = \min(ES_{\text{successor}}) - EF$. *Successor-level* slack.
> * ☐ **Independent Float (IF):** $IF = \max(0,\ \min(ES_{\text{successor}}) - LF - t)$. *Worst-case* slack.
> * ☐ **Interfering Float (IntF):** $IntF = TF - FF$. Float that *will* cause project delay if consumed.
> * ☐ **Chain of Inequalities:** $IF \le FF \le TF$ (always holds for any activity).
> * ☐ **Critical Path** = all activities with $TF = 0$ — no slack, no flexibility.
> * ☐ **Forward Pass:** $ES_i = \max(EF_{\text{predecessors}})$, $EF_i = ES_i + t_i$.
> * ☐ **Backward Pass:** $LF_i = \min(LS_{\text{successors}})$, $LS_i = LF_i - t_i$.
> * ☐ **End activity:** $LF = T_p$; **Start activity:** $ES = 0$.
> * ☐ **Multiple successors:** Use the *minimum* of their ES / LS values.
> * ☐ **Multiple predecessors:** Use the *maximum* of their EF / LF values.
> * ☐ **Zero Float = Critical:** Any delay on a critical activity directly extends $T_p$.
> * ☐ **Real-world impact:** Float is a *resource* — it can be consumed, transferred, or traded-off across activities.
> * ☐ **PMBOK terminology:** Float = Slack (interchangeable terms).
> * ☐ **Always verify:** A correct computation satisfies $LF_F = T_p$ and $LS_A = 0$ for the start activity.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Shortening the Project Duration
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEITT633 - SOFTWARE PROJECT MANAGEMENT
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Shortening the Project Duration

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Project Duration Shortening** (also termed as **Project Compression** or **Schedule Compression**) is the deliberate, structured process of reducing the total calendar time required to complete a software project from its originally planned schedule, without altering the defined project scope. In KTU 2024 Scheme parlance, this falls under the **Time Management** knowledge area and is performed through two principal techniques: **Crashing** (resource-driven compression) and **Fast-Tracking** (schedule-driven compression).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight (PEITT633 - Module 3):** "Shortening the Project Duration" deals with the systematic reduction of project timelines through schedule compression strategies, considering direct cost increases, indirect cost savings, and the critical path.

### 1.2 Why Shorten a Project? — The Driving Forces

| Driver | Engineering Interpretation |
| :--- | :--- |
| **Imposed Deadlines** | Market-window or contractual delivery dates that cannot be relaxed. |
| **Avoiding Penalty Costs** | Liquidated damages for late delivery specified in the contract. |
| **Realising Early Revenue** | Earlier product launch translates to earlier cash inflows (NPV gain). |
| **Avoiding Weather/Seasonal Risks** | Completing field work before monsoon etc. |
| **Capitalising on Incentives** | Bonus payments for early completion. |
| **Freeing Up Shared Resources** | Releasing scarce engineers or test labs for other ventures. |

### 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — The Train That Must Arrive Sooner

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-world Analogy:** Imagine a passenger train scheduled to travel from Kochi to Delhi in 48 hours. The railway board suddenly demands arrival in 36 hours. You cannot magically teleport the train, so you must either:
> 1. **Add a parallel engine (Crashing)** — Spend more fuel money to pull the train faster, OR
> 2. **Skip a non-essential halt and run the cleaning/watering in parallel with travel (Fast-Tracking)** — Reorganise activities to overlap.
>
> The first option costs more (extra fuel, more powerful loco). The second option increases *risk* (a delayed cleaning crew may delay the train even if departure was on time). This is precisely the trade-off the project manager navigates when compressing a software project schedule.

### 1.4 Fundamental Trade-off: The Iron Triangle

When duration is shortened, exactly one or both of the following must be re-balanced:

$$
\text{Cost} \uparrow \quad \text{AND/OR} \quad \text{Risk/Quality} \uparrow
$$

There is **no "free" compression** in project management.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Two Principal Methods of Compression

#### 2.1.1 Crashing (Resource-Augmentation Strategy)

- **Definition:** Crashing is a schedule compression technique in which activities are accelerated by **adding extra resources** (manpower, overtime, premium tools, parallel teams).
- **Key property:** Crashing always **increases direct cost** because premium resources are not free.
- **Crashing rule of thumb:** Only activities on the **critical path** can be crashed to reduce project duration; crashing a non-critical path activity only increases cost with no schedule benefit.
- **Limit:** Each activity has a *crash limit* — the absolute minimum time achievable even with infinite resources (a *technological floor*).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Critical Path Rule:** A non-critical activity has positive *float*. Reducing its duration below its earliest-start time does NOT shorten the project unless it is later converted into a critical activity by the reduction of the existing critical path.

#### 2.1.2 Fast Tracking (Schedule-Reorganisation Strategy)

- **Definition:** Fast Tracking is a schedule compression technique in which activities normally performed in *sequence* are performed in *parallel* (overlapped).
- **Key property:** Fast Tracking typically **increases project risk and may require rework** because previously-finished upstream tasks are no longer validated when downstream tasks begin.
- **Common KTU examples:** Starting design before requirements are 100% frozen; parallel coding and unit testing; overlapping integration and system testing.
- **Cost implication:** Does NOT directly increase direct cost (no extra resources), but *risk of rework* may indirectly raise cost.

### 2.2 Time-Cost Relationship — The "S-Curve" Concept

In classical SPM theory, plotting **Total Project Cost vs Project Duration** yields a characteristic S-shaped (or sometimes J-shaped) curve:

$$
\text{Total Cost}(T) = \text{Direct Cost}(T) + \text{Indirect Cost}(T)
$$

- As duration ↓ (crashing), **Direct Cost ↑** (premium for speed).
- As duration ↓, **Indirect Cost ↓** (less overhead, less salary burn, less facility lease).
- The **optimal duration** is where Total Cost is **minimum** — known as the *Minimum-Cost Schedule* or *Most Economical Schedule*.

### 2.3 Cost Slope — The Heart of Crashing

For every activity, two extreme durations are estimated:

| Symbol | Meaning |
| :--- | :--- |
| $T_n$ | Normal Time — duration under normal resource conditions |
| $T_c$ | Crash Time — minimum achievable duration even with maximum resources |
| $C_n$ | Normal Cost — direct cost under normal duration |
| $C_c$ | Crash Cost — direct cost under crashed duration |

The **Cost Slope (CS)** — sometimes called *Crash Cost per unit time* or *Marginal Crash Cost* — is the rate at which direct cost increases for each unit reduction in duration:

$$
\boxed{\text{Cost Slope} = \frac{C_c - C_n}{T_n - T_c} \;\; \text{(currency per day)}}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Interpretation:** If a "Coding" activity has $T_n = 10$ days, $T_c = 6$ days, $C_n = \text{Rs. } 20{,}000$, $C_c = \text{Rs. } 32{,}000$, then its cost slope is $(32{,}000 - 20{,}000)/(10 - 6) = 12{,}000/4 = \text{Rs. } 3{,}000$ **per day** of crashing. For every day we shave off, we pay Rs. 3,000 extra.

### 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet (Cheat Sheet)

| # | Quantity | Formula | Unit | Notes |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| 1 | Cost Slope (CS) | $(C_c - C_n) / (T_n - T_c)$ | $\text{Rs/day}$ | Always $\geq 0$ |
| 2 | Max Crash Days | $T_n - T_c$ | days | Technological floor |
| 3 | Total Project Cost | $\sum C_n(i) + \text{Indirect Cost}$ | $\text{Rs}$ | At normal schedule |
| 4 | Crashed Direct Cost | $C_n + (\text{CS}) \times (\text{Days Crashed})$ | $\text{Rs}$ | For a single activity |
| 5 | Float of an activity | $\text{LS} - \text{ES} = \text{LF} - \text{EF}$ | days | Zero for critical activities |
| 6 | Project Duration | $\sum$ durations on critical path | days | After compression |
| 7 | Cost saving from indirect cost | $\text{Indirect Cost Rate} \times \text{Days Saved}$ | $\text{Rs}$ | Per day saved |

### 2.5 Step-by-Step Methodology (Heuristic Crashing Procedure)

The standard KTU-acceptable algorithm for systematically shortening project duration is:

1. **Construct the network diagram** (AOA or AON) and identify the **critical path**.
2. **Compute cost slope** for every activity using the formula above.
3. **Select the cheapest (lowest CS) critical activity** that has not yet reached its crash limit.
4. **Crash it by one day** (or by the maximum possible days, if the path becomes non-critical).
5. **Re-compute the critical path** — the path may have shifted!
6. **If multiple critical paths exist**, you must crash **one activity from each path simultaneously** (or your crash has no net effect on duration).
7. **Repeat** steps 3–6 until no further crashing is economically justified or all crash limits are reached.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner Pitfall #1:** Students often crash *any* activity on the *original* critical path without checking that it is *still* critical after the previous crash. After each iteration, recompute the network — the critical path can change.

### 2.6 Real-World Utility

In the software industry, "Shortening Project Duration" is applied during:
- **Release management** when market windows approach.
- **Fixed-price contracts** where delays trigger Service Level Agreement (SLA) penalties.
- **Resource-constrained projects** where a delayed milestone blocks other parallel product launches.
- **Agile / Scrum release planning** when a sprint's burn-down is off-track — teams add pair-programming, OT, or pull in extra developers (crashing), or parallelise testing with development (fast-tracking).

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Worked Numerical Example — Full Heuristic Crashing

> [!NOTE]
> **Problem Setup:** A software project consists of five activities **A, B, C, D, E** with the following network and time/cost data. Indirect cost is Rs. 5,000 per day. Find the **optimum project duration** and corresponding **minimum total cost**.

**Network:** A and B start in parallel. C follows A. D follows B. E follows **both C and D** (merge event).

**Activity Data Table:**

| Activity | Predecessor | $T_n$ (days) | $T_c$ (days) | $C_n$ (Rs) | $C_c$ (Rs) |
| :--- | :--- | :---: | :---: | :---: | :---: |
| A | — | 6 | 4 | 12,000 | 20,000 |
| B | — | 8 | 5 | 16,000 | 25,000 |
| C | A | 5 | 3 | 9,000 | 15,000 |
| D | B | 7 | 4 | 14,000 | 22,000 |
| E | C, D | 4 | 2 | 10,000 | 14,000 |

#### Step 1 — Compute Cost Slopes

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{CS}_A &= \frac{20{,}000 - 12{,}000}{6 - 4} = \frac{8{,}000}{2} = \text{Rs. } 4{,}000 \text{/day} \\[4pt]
\text{CS}_B &= \frac{25{,}000 - 16{,}000}{8 - 5} = \frac{9{,}000}{3} = \text{Rs. } 3{,}000 \text{/day} \\[4pt]
\text{CS}_C &= \frac{15{,}000 - 9{,}000}{5 - 3} = \frac{6{,}000}{2} = \text{Rs. } 3{,}000 \text{/day} \\[4pt]
\text{CS}_D &= \frac{22{,}000 - 14{,}000}{7 - 4} = \frac{8{,}000}{3} \approx \text{Rs. } 2{,}667 \text{/day} \\[4pt]
\text{CS}_E &= \frac{14{,}000 - 10{,}000}{4 - 2} = \frac{4{,}000}{2} = \text{Rs. } 2{,}000 \text{/day}
\end{aligned}
$$

#### Step 2 — Normal Schedule Analysis

Two paths exist:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Path 1: } A \rightarrow C \rightarrow E &= 6 + 5 + 4 = 15 \text{ days} \\
\text{Path 2: } B \rightarrow D \rightarrow E &= 8 + 7 + 4 = 19 \text{ days}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Critical Path (CP)** = Path 2, Duration = **19 days**.
**Normal Direct Cost** = 12,000 + 16,000 + 9,000 + 14,000 + 10,000 = **Rs. 61,000**.

#### Step 3 — Normal Total Cost

$$
\text{Total Cost} = \text{Direct Cost} + \text{Indirect Cost} = 61{,}000 + (5{,}000 \times 19) = 61{,}000 + 95{,}000 = \text{Rs. } 1{,}56{,}000
$$

#### Step 4 — Identify Cheapest Critical Activity

On the critical path (B → D → E), the cost slopes are:

$$
\text{CS}_B = 3{,}000, \quad \text{CS}_D = 2{,}667, \quad \text{CS}_E = 2{,}000
$$

Cheapest = **E at Rs. 2,000/day**. But there is an **economic gate**:

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Net Savings Test:** Crash only if the Cost Slope < Indirect Cost saving per day. Here, indirect cost saved per day = Rs. 5,000. Since CS$_E$ = 2,000 < 5,000, **crashing E is economically beneficial** (we save Rs. 3,000 net per day crashed).

#### Step 5 — Iteration 1: Crash E by 1 day (4 → 3 days)

New durations: A=6, B=8, C=5, D=7, E=3.

Re-compute paths:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Path 1: } A + C + E &= 6 + 5 + 3 = 14 \text{ days} \\
\text{Path 2: } B + D + E &= 8 + 7 + 3 = 18 \text{ days}
\end{aligned}
$$

**CP = Path 2 = 18 days.** Project shortened by 1 day. ✓

- New direct cost = 61,000 + 2,000 = **Rs. 63,000**.
- New indirect cost = 5,000 × 18 = **Rs. 90,000**.
- Total cost = 63,000 + 90,000 = **Rs. 1,53,000** (savings of Rs. 3,000). ✓

#### Step 6 — Iteration 2: Crash E by another day (3 → 2 days, its crash limit)

E reaches $T_c = 2$. Re-compute:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Path 1: } 6 + 5 + 2 &= 13 \text{ days} \\
\text{Path 2: } 8 + 7 + 2 &= 17 \text{ days}
\end{aligned}
$$

**CP = Path 2 = 17 days.** Project shortened further.

- Direct cost = 63,000 + 2,000 = **Rs. 65,000**.
- Indirect cost = 5,000 × 17 = **Rs. 85,000**.
- Total cost = 65,000 + 85,000 = **Rs. 1,50,000** (further savings of Rs. 3,000). ✓

#### Step 7 — Iteration 3: E cannot be crashed further. Re-evaluate Critical Path

CP = Path 2 = 17 days. Cheapest activity now on Path 2: **D at CS = Rs. 2,667/day < 5,000 (savings threshold)**. Crash D by 1 day (7 → 6).

New durations: B=8, D=6, E=2 ⇒ Path 2 = 8+6+2 = 16 days.

- Direct cost = 65,000 + 2,667 = **Rs. 67,667**.
- Indirect cost = 5,000 × 16 = **Rs. 80,000**.
- Total cost = 67,667 + 80,000 = **Rs. 1,47,667** (savings of Rs. 2,333). ✓

#### Step 8 — Iteration 4: Crash D by 1 more day (6 → 5)

Path 2 = 8 + 5 + 2 = 15 days. Now **Path 1 also = 15 days** (6+5+2). **Two critical paths** exist.

To reduce duration, we must crash one activity from each path. The cheapest pair-combo from the *non-E* activities: cheapest on Path 1 is C (CS = 3,000), cheapest on Path 2 is D (CS = 2,667).

- **Combined CS = 3,000 + 2,667 = Rs. 5,667/day**.
- Net savings = 5,000 − 5,667 = **−Rs. 667/day** (a loss!).

**STOP — no more economic crashing is justified.**

#### Step 9 — Summary Table (The Final Answer)

| Iteration | Activity Crashed | Project Duration (days) | Direct Cost (Rs) | Indirect Cost (Rs) | Total Cost (Rs) |
| :---: | :---: | :---: | :---: | :---: | :---: |
| 0 (Normal) | — | 19 | 61,000 | 95,000 | **1,56,000** |
| 1 | E (−1) | 18 | 63,000 | 90,000 | 1,53,000 |
| 2 | E (−1) | 17 | 65,000 | 85,000 | 1,50,000 |
| 3 | D (−1) | 16 | 67,667 | 80,000 | 1,47,667 |
| 4 (STOP) | — | 16 (optimum) | 67,667 | 80,000 | **1,47,667** |

> [!NOTE]
> **Conclusion:** The optimum (minimum total cost) project duration is **16 days** with minimum total cost of **Rs. 1,47,667**.

### 3.2 The Multi-Critical-Path Trap — Decision Matrix

| Scenario | Path Status | Action Required |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Single critical path | Only one CP | Crash cheapest activity on that path. |
| Two critical paths | Path X & Path Y both critical | Crash one activity from each path simultaneously. |
| Critical path shifts after crash | New CP after iteration | Always recompute — old CP may have slack. |
| Activity on all CPs | Activity common to multiple CPs | Most valuable to crash — reduces all CPs in one move. |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Mermaid Flowchart — The Heuristic Crashing Algorithm

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A0[Start: Construct Project Network] --> A1[Compute Earliest and Latest Times for All Activities]
    A1 --> A2[Identify Initial Critical Path CP]
    A2 --> A3[Compute Cost Slope CS for Every Activity]
    A3 --> A4{Multiple Critical Paths?}
    A4 -- Yes --> A5[Identify Cheapest Combo: One Activity from Each CP]
    A4 -- No --> A6[Identify Cheapest Activity on CP]
    A5 --> A7{Combined CS less than Indirect Cost Rate?}
    A6 --> A8{CS less than Indirect Cost Rate?}
    A7 -- No --> A9[STOP: Optimum Reached]
    A7 -- Yes --> A10[Crash Each Selected Activity by 1 Day or to Crash Limit]
    A8 -- No --> A9
    A8 -- Yes --> A11[Crash Activity by 1 Day or to Crash Limit]
    A10 --> A12[Recompute Network and New CP]
    A11 --> A12
    A12 --> A13{Activity Reached Crash Limit?}
    A13 -- Yes --> A14[Mark Activity as Non-Crashable]
    A13 -- No --> A3
    A14 --> A3
```

### 4.2 Mermaid Diagram — Crashing vs Fast Tracking (Decision Architecture)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    S0[Need to Shorten Project Duration] --> S1{Schedule Compression Required}
    S1 -- Option A --> S2[Crashing: Add Resources]
    S1 -- Option B --> S3[Fast Tracking: Parallelise Tasks]
    S2 --> S4[Effect: Direct Cost Increases]
    S2 --> S5[Benefit: Predictable Speedup]
    S2 --> S6[Limit: Crash Floor per Activity]
    S3 --> S7[Effect: Risk and Rework Rise]
    S3 --> S8[Benefit: No Direct Cost Increase]
    S3 --> S9[Limit: Logical Dependencies]
    S4 --> S10[Evaluate: Net Savings vs Indirect Cost]
    S5 --> S10
    S6 --> S10
    S7 --> S11[Evaluate: Risk-adjusted Cost vs Schedule Gain]
    S8 --> S11
    S9 --> S11
    S10 --> S12[Final Compressed Schedule]
    S11 --> S12
```

### 4.3 Mermaid — Time-Cost Tradeoff (S-Curve Functional Mapping)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    XAxis[Project Duration axis decreasing right to left]
    YAxis[Total Project Cost axis]
    DCurve[Direct Cost Curve: Convex and Rising as duration drops]
    ICurve[Indirect Cost Curve: Linear and Falling as duration drops]
    TCurve[Total Cost Curve: Sum of Direct and Indirect]
    OptPt[Optimum Duration: Minimum of TCurve]
    NormPt[Normal Duration: Leftmost point of TCurve]
    CrashPt[Crash Duration: Rightmost point of TCurve]

    DCurve --> TCurve
    ICurve --> TCurve
    NormPt --> OptPt
    OptPt --> CrashPt
    TCurve --> OptPt
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the S-Curve:** Starting from the *Normal Schedule* (longest duration, lowest direct cost but highest indirect cost) on the left, as you crash and move right, direct cost rises steeply while indirect cost falls gently. The **valley of the T-curve is the optimum** — beyond it, every additional day of crashing costs more than it saves.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

#### Q1. **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Paper]**
**Differentiate between Crashing and Fast Tracking as techniques for shortening project duration.** *(3 marks)*

**Model Answer:**

| Aspect | Crashing | Fast Tracking |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Mechanism** | Adding extra resources to compress activity duration. | Performing sequential activities in parallel. |
| **Cost Effect** | Direct cost **increases** (premium resources). | Direct cost usually unchanged; **risk** increases. |
| **Time Effect** | Deterministic — each crash day is calculable. | Probabilistic — overlap may cause rework. |
| **Application** | When schedule slippage is significant and budget permits. | When activities are logically decomposable into near-independent sub-tasks. |
| **Example** | Adding 2 extra programmers to finish coding 3 days sooner. | Starting integration testing while module development is still in progress. |

*(1 mark for each correct contrasting pair; 3 marks total.)*

---

#### Q2. **[KTU University Exam – June 2024, Model Paper]**
**Define the term "Cost Slope" in the context of project duration shortening. Write its formula.** *(3 marks)*

**Model Answer:**
Cost Slope is the rate of increase in direct cost of an activity for each unit reduction in its duration, expressed in currency per day. It is the marginal price of compression for that activity.

$$
\boxed{\text{Cost Slope (CS)} = \frac{C_c - C_n}{T_n - T_c}}
$$

Where $C_c, C_n$ are crash and normal costs; $T_c, T_n$ are crash and normal times respectively. *(Definition = 2 marks, Formula = 1 mark.)*

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks)**

**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Module 3]**
A software project consists of six activities with the following precedence and time-cost data. Indirect cost is **Rs. 4,000 per day**. Determine the **optimum project duration** and corresponding **minimum total cost** using the heuristic crashing method.

| Activity | Predecessor | $T_n$ (days) | $T_c$ (days) | $C_n$ (Rs) | $C_c$ (Rs) |
| :--- | :--- | :---: | :---: | :---: | :---: |
| A | — | 5 | 3 | 8,000 | 12,000 |
| B | — | 7 | 5 | 14,000 | 20,000 |
| C | A | 6 | 4 | 10,000 | 14,000 |
| D | A | 4 | 2 | 6,000 | 10,000 |
| E | B, C | 5 | 3 | 9,000 | 13,000 |
| F | D, E | 3 | 2 | 5,000 | 7,000 |

#### (a) Compute the cost slope for each activity and identify the normal critical path with duration and total cost. *(7 marks)*

**Step 1 — Cost Slopes:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{CS}_A &= (12{,}000 - 8{,}000)/(5 - 3) = 4{,}000/2 = \text{Rs. } 2{,}000 \text{/day} \\
\text{CS}_B &= (20{,}000 - 14{,}000)/(7 - 5) = 6{,}000/2 = \text{Rs. } 3{,}000 \text{/day} \\
\text{CS}_C &= (14{,}000 - 10{,}000)/(6 - 4) = 4{,}000/2 = \text{Rs. } 2{,}000 \text{/day} \\
\text{CS}_D &= (10{,}000 - 6{,}000)/(4 - 2) = 4{,}000/2 = \text{Rs. } 2{,}000 \text{/day} \\
\text{CS}_E &= (13{,}000 - 9{,}000)/(5 - 3) = 4{,}000/2 = \text{Rs. } 2{,}000 \text{/day} \\
\text{CS}_F &= (7{,}000 - 5{,}000)/(3 - 2) = 2{,}000/1 = \text{Rs. } 2{,}000 \text{/day}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2 — Network Paths (Normal Duration):**

- Path 1: A → C → E → F = 5 + 6 + 5 + 3 = **19 days**
- Path 2: A → D → F = 5 + 4 + 3 = **12 days**
- Path 3: B → E → F = 7 + 5 + 3 = **15 days**

**Normal Critical Path = Path 1 (A–C–E–F) = 19 days.**

**Step 3 — Normal Total Cost:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Direct Cost} &= 8{,}000 + 14{,}000 + 10{,}000 + 6{,}000 + 9{,}000 + 5{,}000 = \text{Rs. } 52{,}000 \\
\text{Indirect Cost} &= 4{,}000 \times 19 = \text{Rs. } 76{,}000 \\
\text{Total Cost (Normal)} &= 52{,}000 + 76{,}000 = \text{Rs. } 1{,}28{,}000
\end{aligned}
$$

#### (b) Apply the heuristic crashing procedure iteratively to find the optimum duration and minimum total cost. *(7 marks)*

**Iteration 1: Critical Path = A–C–E–F (19 days).** Cheapest activity: A, C, E, or F all at Rs. 2,000/day. Pick **F** (already at its only 1-day crash — actually pick A first). Crash **A by 1 day** (5→4). Path 1 becomes 18, Path 3 unchanged.

- Direct cost = 52,000 + 2,000 = **Rs. 54,000**
- Indirect cost = 4,000 × 18 = **Rs. 72,000**
- Total = **Rs. 1,26,000** (save Rs. 2,000/day) ✓

**Iteration 2: Crash A again (4→3, crash limit).** Path 1 = 17 days.

- Direct cost = 54,000 + 2,000 = **Rs. 56,000**
- Indirect cost = 4,000 × 17 = **Rs. 68,000**
- Total = **Rs. 1,24,000** (save Rs. 2,000) ✓

**Iteration 3: A is at crash limit. Cheapest on CP (A–C–E–F) is C, E, or F at 2,000/day. Crash C by 1 day (6→5).** Path 1 = 16.

- Direct = 56,000 + 2,000 = **Rs. 58,000**
- Indirect = 4,000 × 16 = **Rs. 64,000**
- Total = **Rs. 1,22,000** ✓

**Iteration 4: Crash C again (5→4, crash limit).** Path 1 = 15.

- Direct = 58,000 + 2,000 = **Rs. 60,000**
- Indirect = 4,000 × 15 = **Rs. 60,000**
- Total = **Rs. 1,20,000** ✓

**Iteration 5: Crash E (5→4) or F (3→2).** Choose **E at 2,000/day** (F has only 1 day to crash). Path 1 = 14.

- Direct = 60,000 + 2,000 = **Rs. 62,000**
- Indirect = 4,000 × 14 = **Rs. 56,000**
- Total = **Rs. 1,18,000** ✓

**Iteration 6: E to crash limit (4→3).** Path 1 = 13.

- Direct = 62,000 + 2,000 = **Rs. 64,000**
- Indirect = 4,000 × 13 = **Rs. 52,000**
- Total = **Rs. 1,16,000** ✓

**Iteration 7: Crash F (3→2, crash limit).** Path 1 = 12.

Now check **Path 2 (A–D–F) = 5+4+2 = 11 days**, **Path 3 (B–E–F) = 7+3+2 = 12 days**, **Path 1 = 4+4+2+2 = 12 days**. **Two critical paths** (1 and 3).

- Direct = 64,000 + 2,000 = **Rs. 66,000**
- Indirect = 4,000 × 12 = **Rs. 48,000**
- Total = **Rs. 1,14,000** ✓

**Iteration 8: To reduce further, must crash one activity from Path 1 AND one from Path 3.** Cheapest pair: From Path 1, C is at crash limit, A is at crash limit, E is at crash limit — only **F can crash (already crashed)** ⇒ **Path 1 cannot be crashed further**.

Therefore **STOP. Optimum reached.**

**Final Answer:**
- **Optimum Project Duration = 12 days**
- **Minimum Total Cost = Rs. 1,14,000**

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks)** — *Alternative Choice*

**[KTU University Exam – June 2023, Module 3]**
A project has activities with the following data. Indirect cost is **Rs. 3,500 per day**. Find the optimum duration.

| Activity | Predecessor | $T_n$ | $T_c$ | $C_n$ | $C_c$ |
| :--- | :--- | :---: | :---: | :---: | :---: |
| P | — | 8 | 6 | 20,000 | 30,000 |
| Q | — | 6 | 4 | 15,000 | 19,000 |
| R | P | 4 | 2 | 8,000 | 12,000 |
| S | Q | 5 | 3 | 10,000 | 14,000 |
| T | R, S | 7 | 4 | 18,000 | 30,000 |

#### (a) Compute cost slopes and identify the normal critical path. *(7 marks)*

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{CS}_P &= (30{-}20)/(8{-}6) = 5{,}000 \text{ /day} \\
\text{CS}_Q &= (19{-}15)/(6{-}4) = 2{,}000 \text{ /day} \\
\text{CS}_R &= (12{-}8)/(4{-}2) = 2{,}000 \text{ /day} \\
\text{CS}_S &= (14{-}10)/(5{-}3) = 2{,}000 \text{ /day} \\
\text{CS}_T &= (30{-}18)/(7{-}4) = 4{,}000 \text{ /day}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Paths:** P–R–T = 8+4+7 = **19 days (CP)**, Q–S–T = 6+5+7 = 18 days.
**Normal Total Cost** = (20+15+8+10+18)k + 3,500×19 = 71,000 + 66,500 = **Rs. 1,37,500**.

#### (b) Perform crashing iterations to find the optimum. *(7 marks)*

**Iter 1:** CP = P–R–T. Cheapest = R at 2,000/day < 3,500. Crash R by 1 (4→3). Duration = 18.
- Direct = 71,000 + 2,000 = 73,000; Indirect = 3,500×18 = 63,000; **Total = 1,36,000** ✓

**Iter 2:** Crash R to crash limit (3→2). Duration = 17.
- Direct = 75,000; Indirect = 59,500; **Total = 1,34,500** ✓

**Iter 3:** R is at crash limit. Cheapest remaining on CP = T at 4,000/day > 3,500. **Net loss if crashed ⇒ STOP.**

**Final: Optimum Duration = 17 days, Minimum Total Cost = Rs. 1,34,500.**

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Where Students Lose Marks**
> 1. **Forgetting to recompute the Critical Path** after each crash iteration. The CP can shift! (–2 marks typical)
> 2. **Crashing non-critical activities** thinking it saves time. It only inflates direct cost. (–2 marks)
> 3. **Ignoring multi-critical-path scenarios** — when two paths are critical, a single-activity crash yields zero benefit. (–3 marks)
> 4. **Not applying the "Cost Slope < Indirect Cost" gate** — crashing beyond the optimum inflates cost; examiners deduct heavily. (–2 marks)
> 5. **Skipping the summary table** — KTU expects a clean, tabular iteration log as the final answer. (–1 mark for presentation)

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Revision Checklist — "Shortening the Project Duration"**

- ☐ **Two methods**: Crashing (resource-based, costlier, predictable) and Fast Tracking (parallelisation, riskier, no extra direct cost).
- ☐ **Cost Slope formula** must be memorised: $\text{CS} = (C_c - C_n)/(T_n - T_c)$.
- ☐ **Always** identify the critical path **after every iteration** — it may shift.
- ☐ **Crash only critical activities** — crashing non-critical activities wastes money.
- ☐ **Multi-critical-path rule**: If $n$ paths are critical, you must crash at least one activity on each path (or on an activity common to all).
- ☐ **Economic stopping criterion**: $\text{CS} \leq \text{Indirect Cost Rate per day}$ ⇒ continue; otherwise stop.
- ☐ **Optimum duration** = duration that minimises **Total Cost = Direct + Indirect**, not the shortest achievable.
- ☐ **Crash limit** $T_c$ is a hard technological floor — beyond it, no resource addition helps.
- ☐ **Fast Tracking** is not "free" — it shifts cost from direct to *risk* and potential *rework*.
- ☐ For KTU 14-mark problems, present a **table of iterations** with duration, direct cost, indirect cost, and total cost at each step.
- ☐ Indirect cost is *always linear* in standard textbook problems; if unspecified, assume a rate and clearly state it.
- ☐ In KTU Module 3 context, this topic interlinks with **PERT/CPM, Resource Allocation, and Earned Value Analysis** in later modules.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Identifying Critical Activities
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEITT633 - SOFTWARE PROJECT MANAGEMENT
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Identifying Critical Activities

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: CORE TECHNICAL DEFINITION & INTUITIVE OVERVIEW

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Standard)

In the context of Software Project Management, **Critical Activities** are those tasks within a project's Activity-On-Node (AON) or Activity-On-Arrow (AOA) network whose execution directly determines the **minimum time required to complete the entire project** (also known as the project completion time or project duration). 

A task is officially classified as a *Critical Activity* if and only if its **Total Float (TF) is exactly zero**. The continuous chain of such zero-float activities from the project's start node to its end node is termed the **Critical Path**. The KTU 2024 Scheme explicitly defines criticality using the following three mathematically equivalent conditions for any activity $i$:

$$\text{Critical Activity} \iff TF_i = 0 \iff LS_i = ES_i \iff LF_i = EF_i$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Board Definition:** An activity is *critical* if any delay in its start or finish will cause an equivalent delay in the overall project completion date. This is the gold-standard definition examiners expect in 2-mark and definition-based questions.

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Traffic Relay Race"

Imagine a team of **8 runners** (activities A through H) participating in a relay race. The baton (project completion) can only be handed to the next runner *after* the previous one finishes. 

- **Runners A, C, E, G, and H** are the fastest in their legs and are running with **zero rest time** between handoffs. If *any one* of them slows down even by 2 seconds, the entire team's finishing time increases by exactly 2 seconds. These are the **Critical Activities**.
- **Runner B** finishes 6 seconds before the next runner is ready to receive the baton, so B has "spare time" or **slack**. 
- **Runner D** finishes 2 seconds early, and **Runner F** finishes 2 seconds early. These non-critical runners can afford minor delays without affecting the overall team finish time.

The sequence **A → C → E → G → H** is the **Critical Path** — the *longest* path through the network that sets the pace for the entire project. The project manager must monitor this path with extreme vigilance.

---

## 1.3 Key Terminology Block

> [!NOTE]
> **Mandatory Vocabulary for KTU 2024 ESE (End Semester Exam):**
> - **Earliest Start (ES):** The earliest time an activity can begin, given that all its predecessors are completed.
> - **Earliest Finish (EF):** $ES + Duration$.
> - **Latest Start (LS):** The latest time an activity can begin without delaying the project.
> - **Latest Finish (LF):** $LS + Duration$ (or the deadline for the successor's start).
> - **Total Float (TF):** $LS - ES$ or $LF - EF$ — the total permissible delay.
> - **Free Float (FF):** $ES_{successor} - EF_{current}$ — delay that does not affect *any* successor.
> - **Project Duration:** The maximum EF value among all terminal activities.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: DEEP THEORETICAL ANALYSIS & KTU HIGH-YIELD FORMULA SHEET

## 2.1 The Operational Logic — Step-by-Step Methodology

Identifying critical activities is a **two-pass deterministic algorithm** applied to the project network diagram:

### **Phase 1: The Forward Pass (Computing ES and EF)**
We start from the project's start node and move *forward* in time.

- **Step 1.1:** For the start node, assign $ES = 0$.
- **Step 1.2:** For any activity $i$, $EF_i = ES_i + D_i$ (where $D_i$ is duration).
- **Step 1.3:** For any activity $j$, $ES_j = \max(EF_i)$ across all immediate predecessors $i$ of $j$.
- **Step 1.4:** The maximum $EF$ value across all terminal (end) activities is the **Project Duration (T)**.

### **Phase 2: The Backward Pass (Computing LS and LF)**
We start from the project's end node (using $LF = T$ for terminal activities) and move *backward*.

- **Step 2.1:** For terminal activities, $LF = T$, and $LS = T - D$.
- **Step 2.2:** For any activity $j$, $LS_j = \min(LS_k) - D_j$, where $k$ are immediate successors of $j$.
- **Step 2.3:** $LF_j = LS_j + D_j$.

### **Phase 3: Float Computation & Criticality Classification**
- Compute $TF_i = LS_i - ES_i$ for every activity.
- **Any activity where $TF_i = 0$ is a CRITICAL ACTIVITY.**

---

## 2.2 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (Examination Cheat Sheet)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following table contains *all* formula variants a KTU 2024 examiner can test. Memorize this entire block.

| Concept | Formula | Units / Notes |
|---|---|---|
| Earliest Finish | $EF_i = ES_i + D_i$ | Time units (days/weeks) |
| Earliest Start (Successor) | $ES_j = \max(EF_{\text{pred}})$ | Maximum rule |
| Latest Start | $LS_i = LF_i - D_i$ | Time units |
| Latest Finish (Predecessor) | $LF_i = \min(LS_{\text{succ}})$ | Minimum rule |
| **Total Float** | $TF_i = LS_i - ES_i$ | Critical if $TF_i = 0$ |
| **Total Float (Alt)** | $TF_i = LF_i - EF_i$ | Identical result |
| **Free Float** | $FF_i = ES_{j} - EF_i$ | $j$ is immediate successor |
| **Independent Float** | $IF_i = \max(0,\ ES_{j} - LF_i)$ | Rarely tested |
| Project Duration | $T = \max(EF_{\text{terminal}})$ | Overall project length |
| Critical Path Length | $\sum D_i \text{ along critical chain}$ | Equals $T$ |

---

## 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

In software project management, identifying critical activities is not an academic exercise — it is a **resource allocation and risk management tool**:

1. **Crashing the Project:** If a client demands a shorter deadline, the project manager can ONLY reduce the duration of *critical activities* (by adding resources, overtime, or scope reduction). Reducing non-critical activities is a waste of money.
2. **Risk Monitoring:** "Crashing" critical activities increases their cost (Cost-Time Trade-off). PMs use the **Critical Path Method (CPM)** alongside PERT to optimize the **Schedule Compression** strategies.
3. **Software Engineering Example:** In a typical SDLC, "Requirements Gathering" might be a critical activity for 4 weeks. If it slips by 3 days, the entire delivery date slips by 3 days — regardless of how fast the "Coding" team works.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: STEP-BY-STEP DERIVATIONS & ANALYTICAL MATRIX

## 3.1 Exhaustive Worked Example (KTU-Style 14-Mark Standard)

**Problem Statement:** A software project consists of 8 activities (A through H) with the following precedence relationships and durations (in days). Construct the network, perform Forward and Backward Pass, and identify all critical activities and the project duration.

| Activity | Predecessors | Duration (Days) |
|---|---|---|
| A | None | 5 |
| B | None | 3 |
| C | A | 4 |
| D | A | 6 |
| E | B, C | 5 |
| F | D | 4 |
| G | E | 3 |
| H | F, G | 5 |

---

### **Step 1: Network Construction (AON Representation)**

The logical flow is:
- A and B start the project.
- C and D branch from A.
- E requires both B and C.
- F requires D.
- G requires E.
- H requires both F and G (H is the terminal activity).

---

### **Step 2: Forward Pass Calculation**

We apply $ES_j = \max(EF_{\text{pred}})$ and $EF_i = ES_i + D_i$:

**Activity A:**
$ES_A = 0$ (start node)
$EF_A = 0 + 5 = 5$

**Activity B:**
$ES_B = 0$ (start node)
$EF_B = 0 + 3 = 3$

**Activity C (predecessor: A):**
$ES_C = EF_A = 5$
$EF_C = 5 + 4 = 9$

**Activity D (predecessor: A):**
$ES_D = EF_A = 5$
$EF_D = 5 + 6 = 11$

**Activity E (predecessors: B, C):**
$ES_E = \max(EF_B,\ EF_C) = \max(3,\ 9) = 9$
$EF_E = 9 + 5 = 14$

**Activity F (predecessor: D):**
$ES_F = EF_D = 11$
$EF_F = 11 + 4 = 15$

**Activity G (predecessor: E):**
$ES_G = EF_E = 14$
$EF_G = 14 + 3 = 17$

**Activity H (predecessors: F, G):**
$ES_H = \max(EF_F,\ EF_G) = \max(15,\ 17) = 17$
$EF_H = 17 + 5 = 22$

**Project Duration:** $T = 22$ days.

---

### **Step 3: Backward Pass Calculation**

We apply $LF_i = \min(LS_{\text{succ}})$ and $LS_i = LF_i - D_i$, starting with $LF_H = 22$.

**Activity H:**
$LF_H = 22$ (project deadline)
$LS_H = 22 - 5 = 17$

**Activity G (successor: H):**
$LF_G = LS_H = 17$
$LS_G = 17 - 3 = 14$

**Activity F (successor: H):**
$LF_F = LS_H = 17$
$LS_F = 17 - 4 = 13$

**Activity E (successor: G):**
$LF_E = LS_G = 14$
$LS_E = 14 - 5 = 9$

**Activity D (successor: F):**
$LF_D = LS_F = 13$
$LS_D = 13 - 6 = 7$

**Activity C (successor: E):**
$LF_C = LS_E = 9$
$LS_C = 9 - 4 = 5$

**Activity B (successor: E):**
$LF_B = LS_E = 9$
$LS_B = 9 - 3 = 6$

**Activity A (successors: C, D):**
$LF_A = \min(LS_C,\ LS_D) = \min(5,\ 7) = 5$
$LS_A = 5 - 5 = 0$

---

### **Step 4: Float Computation & Criticality Identification**

Using the primary formula $TF_i = LS_i - ES_i$:

$$TF_A = 0 - 0 = 0 \quad \Rightarrow \quad \text{CRITICAL}$$

$$TF_B = 6 - 0 = 6 \quad \Rightarrow \quad \text{Non-Critical (Slack = 6 days)}$$

$$TF_C = 5 - 5 = 0 \quad \Rightarrow \quad \text{CRITICAL}$$

$$TF_D = 7 - 5 = 2 \quad \Rightarrow \quad \text{Non-Critical (Slack = 2 days)}$$

$$TF_E = 9 - 9 = 0 \quad \Rightarrow \quad \text{CRITICAL}$$

$$TF_F = 13 - 11 = 2 \quad \Rightarrow \quad \text{Non-Critical (Slack = 2 days)}$$

$$TF_G = 14 - 14 = 0 \quad \Rightarrow \quad \text{CRITICAL}$$

$$TF_H = 17 - 17 = 0 \quad \Rightarrow \quad \text{CRITICAL}$$

---

### **Step 5: Final Consolidated Analysis Matrix**

| Activity | Duration | ES | EF | LS | LF | TF | Critical? |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| A | 5 | 0 | 5 | 0 | 5 | **0** | YES |
| B | 3 | 0 | 3 | 6 | 9 | 6 | NO |
| C | 4 | 5 | 9 | 5 | 9 | **0** | YES |
| D | 6 | 5 | 11 | 7 | 13 | 2 | NO |
| E | 5 | 9 | 14 | 9 | 14 | **0** | YES |
| F | 4 | 11 | 15 | 13 | 17 | 2 | NO |
| G | 3 | 14 | 17 | 14 | 17 | **0** | YES |
| H | 5 | 17 | 22 | 17 | 22 | **0** | YES |

### **Final Result:**

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Critical Path: A → C → E → G → H**
> **Project Duration: 22 Days**
> **Critical Activities: A, C, E, G, H**
> **Total Float Verification:** $5 + 4 + 5 + 3 + 5 = 22$ days ✓
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: STRUCTURAL DIAGRAMS & SCHEMATICS

## 4.1 Project Network Diagram (AON Representation)

The following Mermaid `flowchart` illustrates the AON network for the worked example. The node IDs follow the alphanumeric safety rule (no reserved keywords), and labels are clean uppercase text.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    startNode((START))
    endNode((END))
    nodeA["A: 5 DAYS"]
    nodeB["B: 3 DAYS"]
    nodeC["C: 4 DAYS"]
    nodeD["D: 6 DAYS"]
    nodeE["E: 5 DAYS"]
    nodeF["F: 4 DAYS"]
    nodeG["G: 3 DAYS"]
    nodeH["H: 5 DAYS"]

    startNode --> nodeA
    startNode --> nodeB
    nodeA --> nodeC
    nodeA --> nodeD
    nodeB --> nodeE
    nodeC --> nodeE
    nodeD --> nodeF
    nodeE --> nodeG
    nodeF --> nodeH
    nodeG --> nodeH
    nodeH --> endNode

    style nodeA fill:#ff9999,stroke:#cc0000,stroke-width:3px
    style nodeC fill:#ff9999,stroke:#cc0000,stroke-width:3px
    style nodeE fill:#ff9999,stroke:#cc0000,stroke-width:3px
    style nodeG fill:#ff9999,stroke:#cc0000,stroke-width:3px
    style nodeH fill:#ff9999,stroke:#cc0000,stroke-width:3px
    style nodeB fill:#99ccff,stroke:#0066cc
    style nodeD fill:#99ccff,stroke:#0066cc
    style nodeF fill:#99ccff,stroke:#0066cc
```

> **Reading Guide:** Nodes highlighted in **red** form the **Critical Path (A → C → E → G → H)**. Blue nodes are non-critical activities with positive total float (slack time available).

---

## 4.2 Methodology Flowchart: The CPM Algorithm

The following block-level architecture flow maps the decision logic used to identify critical activities systematically.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    inputStep["INPUT: Activity List with Predecessors and Durations"]
    buildStep["STEP 1: Build AON Network Diagram"]
    forwardStep["STEP 2: Forward Pass\nCompute ES and EF for all nodes"]
    durationCheck{"Is ES of all\nactivities calculated?"}
    backwardInit["STEP 3: Initialize Backward Pass\nSet LF of terminal activity = T"]
    backwardStep["STEP 4: Backward Pass\nCompute LS and LF using Min Rule"]
    floatStep["STEP 5: Calculate Total Float\nTF = LS minus ES"]
    criticalCheck{"Is TF equal to 0?"}
    criticalOut["CRITICAL ACTIVITY\nMark in Red on Path"]
    nonCriticalOut["NON-CRITICAL ACTIVITY\nSlack = TF days available"]
    finalOut["OUTPUT: Critical Path and Project Duration T"]

    inputStep --> buildStep
    buildStep --> forwardStep
    forwardStep --> durationCheck
    durationCheck -->|No| forwardStep
    durationCheck -->|Yes| backwardInit
    backwardInit --> backwardStep
    backwardStep --> floatStep
    floatStep --> criticalCheck
    criticalCheck -->|Yes| criticalOut
    criticalCheck -->|No| nonCriticalOut
    criticalOut --> finalOut
    nonCriticalOut --> finalOut

    style inputStep fill:#e1f5e1
    style finalOut fill:#ffe1e1,stroke:#cc0000,stroke-width:3px
    style criticalOut fill:#ffcccc
    style nonCriticalOut fill:#cce5ff
```

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 SCHEME EXAMINATION QUESTION BANK & TOPIC RECAP

---

## 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Question 1** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024 Model]`
**Define a critical activity. Under what conditions is an activity classified as critical in a project network?**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**
A **critical activity** is a task whose delay directly causes a delay in the overall project completion time. An activity $i$ is classified as critical if **any one** of the following three equivalent conditions holds:
1. **Total Float is zero:** $TF_i = 0$
2. **Earliest Start equals Latest Start:** $ES_i = LS_i$
3. **Earliest Finish equals Latest Finish:** $EF_i = LF_i$

Such activities lie on the **Critical Path** — the longest-duration path through the network that determines the minimum project completion time. *(3 marks: 1 mark for definition, 2 marks for the three conditions.)*

---

### **Question 2** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023 Model]`
**Differentiate between Total Float and Free Float with a suitable formula for each.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**
- **Total Float (TF):** The total time an activity can be delayed *without delaying the project completion date.*
$$\text{TF}_i = LS_i - ES_i = LF_i - EF_i$$
- **Free Float (FF):** The time an activity can be delayed *without delaying the early start of any immediate successor.*
$$\text{FF}_i = ES_{j} - EF_i$$
where $j$ is the immediate successor of activity $i$. The key difference is that Total Float considers the *entire project* deadline, whereas Free Float considers only the *next successor's* constraint. *(3 marks: 1.5 marks each for definition and formula.)*

---

## 5.2 PART B — ESE Module Choice Questions (14 Marks Each)

> **KTU 2024 ESE Rule:** Answer **ONE** full question. Each question has two sub-parts of 7 marks each.

---

### **QUESTION A (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024 Model]`

A software development project comprises 7 activities with the following data:

| Activity | Predecessor(s) | Duration (weeks) |
|---|---|---|
| A | — | 4 |
| B | — | 6 |
| C | A | 5 |
| D | A | 7 |
| E | B, C | 4 |
| F | D | 6 |
| G | E, F | 3 |
