**Step 1 — Recall the formula.**
$$EMV = P \times I$$

**Step 2 — Compute $EMV_X$.**

$$EMV_X = (0.80 \times +25,00,000) + (0.20 \times -8,00,000)$$

$$EMV_X = 20,00,000 + (-1,60,000)$$

$$EMV_X = +18,40,000$$

**Step 3 — Compute $EMV_Y$.**

$$EMV_Y = (0.60 \times +35,00,000) + (0.40 \times -5,00,000)$$

$$EMV_Y = 21,00,000 + (-2,00,000)$$

$$EMV_Y = +19,00,000$$

**Step 4 — Risk Exposure calculation.**

$$EL_X = (0.80 \times 25,00,000) - EMV_X = 20,00,000 - 18,40,000 = 1,60,000$$
*(Equivalent formula $EL = P \times \vert Loss \vert$ when applied to downside.)*

$$EL_Y = (0.40 \times 5,00,000) = 2,00,000$$

**Step 5 — Decision Table.**

| Approach | $EMV$ (₹) | Risk Exposure (₹) | Verdict |
|---|---|---|---|
| X (Native) | 18,40,000 | 1,60,000 | Lower risk, lower reward |
| Y (Flutter) | 19,00,000 | 2,00,000 | Higher reward, higher risk |

**Step 6 — Recommendation (2 marks).**
- If the firm is **risk-averse**, choose **Approach X** (lower exposure).
- If the firm is **risk-seeking** and targets **higher profit**, choose **Approach Y** because $EMV_Y > EMV_X$ by ₹60,000.
- Since KTU rubric rewards EMV-based choice, recommend **Approach Y** as the EMV-maximizing alternative while flagging its higher exposure for monitoring.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam – June 2024, Modified] **ALTERNATIVE CHOICE**

**Q.B (a)** What is a **Risk Register**? Explain its structure with a sample template suitable for a B.Tech major project. **(7 marks)**

**Model Answer:**

**Definition (1 mark):**
A **Risk Register** (or Risk Log) is a project document used to record all identified risks, their analysis, planned responses, owners, and current status. It is a living document maintained throughout the project.

**Structure / Columns (5 marks):**

| Column | Description |
|---|---|
| **Risk ID** | Unique identifier (e.g., R-001) |
| **Risk Description** | Cause + Event + Effect statement |
| **Category** | Technical / External / Organizational / PM |
| **Probability (P)** | Numerical (0–1) or qualitative (L/M/H) |
| **Impact (I)** | Severity rating (1–5) or monetary value |
| **Risk Score** | $P \times I$ |
| **Risk Owner** | Single accountable person |
| **Response Strategy** | Avoid/Transfer/Mitigate/Accept/Exploit/Share/Enhance |
| **Trigger / Warning Sign** | Symptom that risk is materializing |
| **Contingency Plan** | Action if risk occurs |
| **Residual Risk** | Remaining risk after response |
| **Status** | Active / Occurred / Closed |

**Sample Row for B.Tech Project (1 mark):**

| Field | Value |
|---|---|
| Risk ID | R-001 |
| Description | Team member drops the project mid-semester |
| Category | Organizational |
| Probability | 0.40 |
| Impact | ₹ High (project delay) |
| Score | 0.40 × High = High |
| Owner | Project Guide |
| Strategy | **Mitigate** — assign pair-programming buddies |
| Trigger | Member absent > 3 consecutive meetings |
| Contingency | Reassign modules, seek replacement |

---

**Q.B (b)** Explain the **four response strategies for threats** and **four response strategies for opportunities** with software project examples. Construct a Probability-Impact matrix and label the zones. **(7 marks)**

**Model Answer:**

**Threat Response Strategies (3.5 marks):**

1. **Avoid** — Eliminate the threat by changing the plan.
   - *Example:* Replace an untested third-party API with a proven in-house library to avoid integration risk.

2. **Transfer** — Shift consequence to a third party.
   - *Example:* Take an insurance policy or sign a fixed-price contract with the vendor so cost overruns are borne by them.

3. **Mitigate** — Reduce probability and/or impact.
   - *Example:* Conduct prototype testing in Sprint 1 to mitigate technical risk of unknown framework.

4. **Accept** — Acknowledge and prepare a contingency plan.
   - *Example:* Accept that minor UI bugs may remain; budget a 2-week polish sprint.

**Opportunity Response Strategies (3.5 marks):**

1. **Exploit** — Ensure the opportunity occurs.
   - *Example:* Assign the senior architect to a high-visibility AI feature to guarantee first-mover advantage.

2. **Share** — Allocate ownership to a third party best able to capture it.
   - *Example:* Joint venture with a marketing firm for app launch.

3. **Enhance** — Increase probability and/or impact.
   - *Example:* Add an analytics module to amplify monetisation potential.

4. **Accept** — Willing to benefit if it occurs.
   - *Example:* Reinvest any scope-reduction savings into R&D.

**Probability-Impact Matrix (Already drawn in Section 3.4):** Red Zone (≥3.0), Yellow Zone (1.5–2.9), Green Zone (<1.5).

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Do not confuse** "Avoid" (eliminate the cause) with "Mitigate" (reduce P or I). Many students write them as synonyms — instant loss of 1–2 marks.
> 2. **EMV must include sign convention** — positive for gain, negative for loss. Writing all values as positive loses 1 mark.
> 3. **Risk Register** must have **all 12 columns**, not just 4–5. Examiners specifically check for "Risk Owner," "Trigger," and "Residual Risk."
> 4. **Probability-Impact matrix** must show *both axes* labeled and *zones* (Red/Yellow/Green) marked. A plain 5×5 grid without zones scores only 4/7.
> 5. **Threats vs Opportunities** — many students answer only the four threat strategies and skip opportunities. Always mention both.
> 6. **RPN formula** $S \times O \times D$ must be written exactly; partial marks for forgetting Detection ($D$).

---

## 📌 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Rapid Revision Checklist — Risk Planning**

- ✅ **Risk** = Uncertainty × Consequence (positive or negative).
- ✅ **Six Phases** = Plan → Identify → Qualitative → Quantitative → Respond → Monitor.
- ✅ **EMV formula:** $EMV = P \times I$ — use **signed impacts**.
- ✅ **Risk Exposure:** $EL = P \times L$ — drives contingency reserve.
- ✅ **RPN (FMEA):** $RPN = S \times O \times D$ — ranks technical failure modes.
- ✅ **Threat strategies** = Avoid, Transfer, Mitigate, Accept.
- ✅ **Opportunity strategies** = Exploit, Share, Enhance, Accept.
- ✅ **Risk Register** has **12 columns**; the **Risk Owner** column is mandatory.
- ✅ **Triggers** are the early-warning signs that a risk is materializing — must be defined.
- ✅ **Residual Risk** = risk remaining after the response — must always be documented.
- ✅ **Probability-Impact Matrix** zones: **Red ≥ 3.0**, **Yellow 1.5–2.9**, **Green < 1.5**.
- ✅ **SWOT** is for identification; **Fishbone** is for root-cause analysis.
- ✅ **Delphi Technique** = anonymous iterative expert consensus (no peer pressure bias).
- ✅ **Monte Carlo Simulation** runs the project schedule/cost through thousands of iterations to find probability distribution of outcomes.
- ✅ **RBS (Risk Breakdown Structure)** hierarchically categorizes risks by source (Technical, External, Organizational, PM).
- ✅ **Risk Burndown** charts show cumulative exposure decreasing over time — proof of effective risk planning.
- ✅ **CMMI Level 3+** requires formal Risk Management; this is frequently cited in KTU answers for bonus marks.

> [!TIP]
> **Last-Minute Mnemonic — "SAFE for Threats, EASE for Opportunities":**
> - **S**trategies for **T**hreats: **A**void, **T**ransfer, **M**itigate, **A**ccept (but reversed letters spell SAFE → use as you like).
> - Better: **"ATMA"** for threats (Avoid-Transfer-Mitigate-Accept) and **"ESHA"** for opportunities (Exploit-Share-Enhance-Accept).
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Risk Evaluation
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEITT633 - SOFTWARE PROJECT MANAGEMENT
> - **Module:** Module Module 3
> - **Topic:** Risk Evaluation

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Risk Evaluation**, in the context of the KTU 2024 Scheme Software Project Management framework, is the systematic process of **prioritizing identified risks** by comparing their estimated probability of occurrence against their assessed impact on project objectives (cost, time, quality, and scope). It forms the third stage of the broader **Risk Management Process**, which is structured as:

1. Risk Identification → 2. Risk Analysis → 3. **Risk Evaluation** → 4. Risk Treatment/Response → 5. Risk Monitoring.

According to the **PMBOK (Project Management Body of Knowledge)** terminology adopted across KTU curriculum, risk evaluation synthesizes the outputs of qualitative and quantitative risk analysis to determine which risks require immediate response, which can be accepted, and which warrant further analysis. The result is a **rank-ordered list of risks** (often called a *Risk Register* or *Risk Log*).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight (Module 3):** Students must clearly distinguish between *Risk Analysis* (computing probability and consequence values) and *Risk Evaluation* (deciding the significance/rank of the risk against pre-defined threshold criteria). Examiners frequently test this distinction as a 3-mark question.

## 1.2 Intuitive Analogy — The "Driving in Kerala Monsoon" Model

Imagine you are planning a long road trip from **Thiruvananthapuram to Kasaragod** during the **Southwest Monsoon**. Before starting, you list potential hazards:

- **Hazard A:** Heavy rain reduces visibility (Probability: High, Impact: Medium)
- **Hazard B:** Landslide at a ghat section (Probability: Low, Impact: Very High)
- **Hazard C:** Tyre puncture (Probability: Medium, Impact: Low)

**Risk Evaluation** is the act of sitting down *before* the trip and asking: *"Given my limited budget for precautions, which hazard deserves my immediate attention, which one I just live with, and which one needs a backup plan?"*

You would:
- Buy premium wipers and high-quality headlights (Response to A).
- Avoid the ghat section or travel with a backup vehicle (Response to B).
- Carry a puncture kit and ignore it otherwise (Response to C).

This triage — the **prioritization based on Probability × Impact** — is exactly what Risk Evaluation does for a software project.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Insight:** Risk Evaluation is fundamentally a **decision-making** activity, not a calculation activity. The numbers inform the decision, but the decision itself is a managerial judgment.

## 1.3 Standard KTU Terminology

| Term | Definition |
|---|---|
| **Risk** | An uncertain event or condition that, if it occurs, has a positive or negative effect on project objectives. |
| **Probability (P)** | The likelihood (0 to 1, or 0% to 100%) that the risk event will occur. |
| **Impact (I)** | The consequence or severity (often rated Low/Medium/High) on cost, schedule, or quality. |
| **Risk Score (R)** | A numerical value computed to rank risks: $R = P \times I$ (qualitative) or $R = EMV$ (quantitative). |
| **Risk Threshold** | A pre-defined cut-off value of Risk Score above which the risk is treated as significant. |
| **Risk Appetite** | The degree of uncertainty an organization is willing to accept in pursuit of its objectives. |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Three Pillars of Risk Evaluation

### Pillar 1: Qualitative Risk Evaluation
- Uses **subjective scales** (e.g., 1 = Very Low, 5 = Very High) for both probability and impact.
- Output: A **Risk Matrix / Probability-Impact (P-I) Grid**.
- Fast, low-cost, suitable for most KTU-style case studies and exam problems.

### Pillar 2: Quantitative Risk Evaluation
- Uses **numerical models** such as Expected Monetary Value (EMV), Decision Tree Analysis, Monte Carlo Simulation, and Sensitivity Analysis (Tornado Diagrams).
- Output: A numerical ranking often in monetary units (₹).
- Used for high-value, mission-critical software projects.

### Pillar 3: Risk Categorization (for Strategic Evaluation)
- Risks are grouped by **origin** (Technical, External, Organizational, Project Management) or by **affected objective** (Cost, Schedule, Quality, Scope).
- This allows targeted response planning (e.g., all "External" risks may be handled by a single contractual clause).

## 2.2 The Probability-Impact (P-I) Risk Matrix

The **Risk Matrix** is the single most important diagram in Risk Evaluation for KTU. It is a 2D grid where:
- **X-axis** = Impact (Consequence severity)
- **Y-axis** = Probability (Likelihood)
- **Cell Value** = Risk Score or Risk Rating (Low / Medium / High / Critical)

A typical KTU-style 5×5 matrix (Probability: 1–5, Impact: 1–5):

| Probability \ Impact | Negligible (1) | Minor (2) | Moderate (3) | Major (4) | Catastrophic (5) |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Almost Certain (5)** | 5 – M | 10 – M | 15 – H | 20 – H | 25 – VH |
| **Likely (4)** | 4 – L | 8 – M | 12 – H | 16 – H | 20 – VH |
| **Possible (3)** | 3 – L | 6 – M | 9 – M | 12 – H | 15 – H |
| **Unlikely (2)** | 2 – L | 4 – L | 6 – M | 8 – M | 10 – M |
| **Rare (1)** | 1 – L | 2 – L | 3 – L | 4 – L | 5 – M |

> [!NOTE]
> **Cell value interpretation:**
> - **L (Low, score 1–4):** Accept / Monitor. Do not invest heavily.
> - **M (Medium, score 5–10):** Mitigation recommended.
> - **H (High, score 11–16):** Active response required (Avoid / Transfer / Mitigate).
> - **VH (Very High, score 17–25):** Escalate to senior management; consider project termination or major replan.

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Memorize the following formulas — they appear in 3-mark and 14-mark KTU questions with very high frequency.**

| # | Formula / Concept | Expression | When to Use |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Risk Score (Qualitative)** | $R = P \times I$ | Risk Matrix / P-I Grid problems |
| 2 | **Expected Monetary Value** | $EMV = P \times C$ | Decision Tree, single-event monetary analysis |
| 3 | **EMV (Multi-branch)** | $EMV = \sum_{i=1}^{n} P_i \times C_i$ | Decision Trees with multiple outcomes |
| 4 | **Risk Exposure / Impact Value** | $RE = P \times L$ | Where $L$ = Loss (monetary consequence) |
| 5 | **Contingency Reserve** | $CR = \sum EMV_{threats} - \sum EMV_{opportunities}$ | Budgeting for risk response |
| 6 | **Standard Deviation of Cost** | $\sigma = \sqrt{\sum P_i (C_i - \bar{C})^2}$ | Quantitative schedule/cost risk |
| 7 | **Expected Cost (Mean)** | $\bar{C} = \sum P_i C_i$ | Monte Carlo / Decision Tree analysis |
| 8 | **Relative Risk Ranking** | $Rank_i = \dfrac{R_i}{\sum R_j} \times 100\%$ | Prioritization in percentage form |
| 9 | **Risk Reduction Leverage** | $RRL = \dfrac{(R_{\text{before}} - R_{\text{after}})}{Cost\_of\_Response}$ | Cost-effectiveness of mitigation |
| 10 | **Risk Appetite Threshold** | $R_{threshold} = R_{score} \ge k$ | Filter for "significant" risks |

> **Boundary Conditions & Important Notes:**
> - $P \in [0, 1]$ (or 0% to 100%). Never negative.
> - $I$ is project-context specific; always cite the scale used (1–5 or 1–10).
> - **Opportunities** (positive risks) have *negative* cost or *positive* EMV.
> - For a **Decision Tree**, the optimal decision is the branch with the **highest EMV** (for opportunities) or **lowest expected cost** (for threats).

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

Risk Evaluation is not just an academic exercise. In the **Indian IT industry** (e.g., TCS, Infosys, Wipro project bids for government projects such as **Kerala State IT Mission** or **Smart City initiatives**), the **Bid Risk Evaluation Report** is a mandatory deliverable. Project managers use Risk Evaluation to:
- Set the **contingency budget** (often 8–15% of total project cost).
- Decide whether to **bid or no-bid** on a tender.
- Justify **insurance premiums** and contract clauses.
- Determine **escalation paths** to senior management.

In safety-critical domains (aerospace, medical software), risk evaluation feeds directly into **FMEA (Failure Mode and Effects Analysis)** and **RPN (Risk Priority Number)** calculations.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Worked Numerical Examples

## 3.1 Derivation: Expected Monetary Value (EMV) from First Principles

**EMV** is derived from the fundamental concept of *mathematical expectation* in probability theory. For a discrete random variable $X$ representing the monetary outcome of a risk event, the expected value is:

$$
E[X] = \sum_{i=1}^{n} P(X = x_i) \cdot x_i
$$

**Step-by-step reasoning:**

**Step 1:** Identify all possible mutually exclusive outcomes of the risk event.
Let the outcomes be $x_1, x_2, \ldots, x_n$.

**Step 2:** Identify the probability of each outcome occurring.
Let $P(x_i)$ be the probability that outcome $x_i$ materializes. By the axioms of probability:

$$
\sum_{i=1}^{n} P(x_i) = 1, \quad P(x_i) \ge 0 \; \forall i
$$

**Step 3:** Define the consequence (cost or benefit) of each outcome.
Let $C_i$ be the monetary consequence (negative for threats, positive for opportunities) of outcome $i$.

**Step 4:** Apply the definition of mathematical expectation.

$$
EMV = \sum_{i=1}^{n} P(x_i) \cdot C_i
$$

**Step 5:** Interpret the result.
- If $EMV > 0$ → Net opportunity; consider exploiting.
- If $EMV < 0$ → Net threat; consider mitigation/avoidance.
- If $EMV = 0$ → Risk-neutral outcome.

For the **simple two-outcome case** (risk either occurs or it doesn't):

$$
EMV = P \cdot C_{occur} + (1 - P) \cdot C_{not\_occur}
$$

Since $C_{not\_occur} = 0$ (no impact if it doesn't happen), this reduces to:

$$
EMV = P \cdot C
$$

This is the formula KTU examiners expect in 3-mark direct questions.

## 3.2 Worked Example 1: KTU-Style Risk Score Calculation (14-mark pattern)

> **Problem:** A software project has identified the following four risks. The project manager uses a 1–5 scale for both Probability and Impact. Compute the Risk Score ($R = P \times I$) and rank the risks in descending order of severity. Identify the risks that must be escalated.

| Risk ID | Description | Probability (P) | Impact (I) |
|---|---|---|---|
| R1 | Key developer resigns mid-sprint | 4 | 5 |
| R2 | Third-party API has downtime | 2 | 3 |
| R3 | Customer changes requirements late | 5 | 4 |
| R4 | Server hardware fails | 1 | 4 |

**Step-by-step Solution:**

**For R1:** Key developer resigns mid-sprint.

$$
R_1 = P_1 \times I_1 = 4 \times 5 = 20
$$

**For R2:** Third-party API has downtime.

$$
R_2 = P_2 \times I_2 = 2 \times 3 = 6
$$

**For R3:** Customer changes requirements late.

$$
R_3 = P_3 \times I_3 = 5 \times 4 = 20
$$

**For R4:** Server hardware fails.

$$
R_4 = P_4 \times I_4 = 1 \times 4 = 4
$$

**Final Ranked Table:**

| Rank | Risk ID | Description | Risk Score (R) | Category |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 (tie) | R1 | Key developer resigns | 20 – VH | Organizational |
| 1 (tie) | R3 | Customer changes requirements | 20 – VH | External |
| 3 | R2 | API downtime | 6 – M | Technical |
| 4 | R4 | Server hardware fails | 4 – L | Technical |

**Escalation Decision:** R1 and R3 must be **escalated to senior management** since both have a Risk Score of 20 (Very High). The response strategy for R1 is *knowledge transfer and cross-training* (Mitigation). The response for R3 is *formal change control process* (Avoidance).

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistake:** Many KTU students compute R1 and R3 as 9 each by *adding* instead of multiplying. The KTU Valuation Key mandates **multiplication**, not addition.

## 3.3 Worked Example 2: Decision Tree with EMV (7-mark sub-part pattern)

> **Problem:** A Kerala-based startup must choose between two software architecture options for an e-commerce platform:
> - **Option A (Monolith):** Low upfront cost (₹10 lakh). If market demand is high, profit is ₹50 lakh. If low, loss is ₹10 lakh.
> - **Option B (Microservices):** High upfront cost (₹20 lakh). If market demand is high, profit is ₹80 lakh. If low, loss is ₹5 lakh.
>
> Probability of high demand: $P(H) = 0.6$. Probability of low demand: $P(L) = 0.4$.
> Determine the optimal decision using EMV.

**Step-by-step Solution:**

**Step 1:** Compute the net profit (or loss) for each branch.

For **Option A:**
- High demand: Net = $+50 - 10 = +40$ lakh.
- Low demand: Net = $-10 - 10 = -20$ lakh.

For **Option B:**
- High demand: Net = $+80 - 20 = +60$ lakh.
- Low demand: Net = $-5 - 20 = -25$ lakh.

**Step 2:** Compute the EMV for each option.

$$
EMV_A = (P_H \times C_{A,H}) + (P_L \times C_{A,L})
$$

$$
EMV_A = (0.6 \times 40) + (0.4 \times (-20))
$$

$$
EMV_A = 24 + (-8) = 16 \; \text{lakh}
$$

$$
EMV_B = (P_H \times C_{B,H}) + (P_L \times C_{B,L})
$$

$$
EMV_B = (0.6 \times 60) + (0.4 \times (-25))
$$

$$
EMV_B = 36 + (-10) = 26 \; \text{lakh}
$$

**Step 3:** Choose the option with the maximum EMV.

Since $EMV_B = 26 > EMV_A = 16$, **Option B (Microservices)** is the optimal decision.

**Step 4:** Compute the Expected Opportunity Loss (EOL) — Optional for full marks.

$$
EOL_A = 26 - 16 = 10 \; \text{lakh}
$$

$$
EOL_B = 0 \; \text{lakh}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Why is Option B still chosen despite higher initial cost?** Because the *expected* gain from a high-demand scenario (weighted by its 0.6 probability) outweighs the larger potential loss in the low-demand scenario. This is the essence of **quantitative risk evaluation**.

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix: Risk Response Strategies Mapping

| Risk Score Range | Risk Level | Recommended Response | Example |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 – 4 | Low | **Accept** | "Cosmetic UI bug appears once a month" |
| 5 – 10 | Medium | **Mitigate / Monitor** | "Slight delay in vendor delivery" |
| 11 – 16 | High | **Avoid / Transfer / Mitigate actively** | "Single point of failure in auth server" |
| 17 – 25 | Very High | **Escalate / Avoid / Project Replan** | "Primary client threatens to cancel contract" |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The four canonical **Risk Response Strategies** (tested in KTU 14-mark answers) are:
> 1. **Avoid** — Eliminate the threat by changing the plan.
> 2. **Mitigate** — Reduce probability or impact.
> 3. **Transfer** — Shift to a third party (insurance, outsourcing).
> 4. **Accept** — Acknowledge and create a contingency plan.
>
> For **opportunities** (positive risks), the strategies are: **Exploit, Enhance, Share, Accept**.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 The Risk Management Lifecycle (Mermaid Flow)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Project Initiation] --> B[Risk Identification]
    B --> C[Risk Analysis Qualitative]
    C --> D[Risk Evaluation Ranking and Prioritization]
    D --> E{Threshold Exceeded?}
    E -- Yes --> F[Risk Response Planning]
    E -- No --> G[Accept and Monitor]
    F --> H[Implement Response Strategy]
    H --> I[Risk Monitoring and Review]
    I --> J{Risk Materialized?}
    J -- Yes --> K[Execute Contingency Plan]
    J -- No --> L[Continue Monitoring]
    K --> I
    L --> I
    I --> M[Lessons Learned for Next Project]
    G --> I
```

## 4.2 Probability-Impact Risk Matrix (Mermaid Block-Level Architecture)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph Probability_Axis[Probability Axis - Vertical]
        P5[Almost Certain 5]
        P4[Likely 4]
        P3[Possible 3]
        P2[Unlikely 2]
        P1[Rare 1]
    end
    subgraph Impact_Axis[Impact Axis - Horizontal]
        I1[Negligible 1]
        I2[Minor 2]
        I3[Moderate 3]
        I4[Major 4]
        I5[Catastrophic 5]
    end
    subgraph Risk_Zones[Risk Zones]
        Z1[Low Zone 1 to 4 - Accept]
        Z2[Medium Zone 5 to 10 - Mitigate]
        Z3[High Zone 11 to 16 - Active Response]
        Z4[Critical Zone 17 to 25 - Escalate]
    end
    P5 --> Z4
    P4 --> Z3
    P3 --> Z2
    P2 --> Z2
    P1 --> Z1
    I5 --> Z4
    I4 --> Z3
    I3 --> Z2
    I2 --> Z1
    I1 --> Z1
```

## 4.3 Decision Tree for EMV Analysis (Mermaid Schematic)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Start[Decision Node - Choose Architecture] --> A[Option A - Monolith]
    Start --> B[Option B - Microservices]
    A --> A1[High Demand 0.6]
    A --> A2[Low Demand 0.4]
    B --> B1[High Demand 0.6]
    B --> B2[Low Demand 0.4]
    A1 --> R1[Outcome Net 40 Lakh]
    A2 --> R2[Outcome Net minus 20 Lakh]
    B1 --> R3[Outcome Net 60 Lakh]
    B2 --> R4[Outcome Net minus 25 Lakh]
    R1 --> E1[EMV A equals 16 Lakh]
    R2 --> E1
    R3 --> E2[EMV B equals 26 Lakh]
    R4 --> E2
    E1 --> Final[Choose Max EMV]
    E2 --> Final
    Final --> Choice[Selected Option B - Microservices]
```

## 4.4 Risk Register Block Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph RiskRegister[Risk Register Components]
        R1[Risk ID]
        R2[Description]
        R3[Category]
        R4[Probability]
        R5[Impact]
        R6[Risk Score]
        R7[Rank]
        R8[Response Strategy]
        R9[Owner]
        R10[Status]
    end
    R1 --> R2 --> R3 --> R4 --> R5 --> R6 --> R7 --> R8 --> R9 --> R10
    R6 --> Filter{Threshold Check}
    Filter -- Pass --> Action[Active Treatment]
    Filter -- Fail --> Watch[Watchlist]
```
