## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: Resource Histogram and Peak Demand

A **Resource Histogram** is a bar chart plotting *resource units required* (Y-axis) against *time periods* (X-axis). The Peak Demand is the maximum height of the histogram. The KTU 2024 syllabus requires students to derive peak demand from a project schedule.

**Given Data (Standard KTU-style problem):**

A 5-activity software project with the following schedule (time in weeks):

| Activity | Duration (weeks) | Developers Required |
|----------|------------------|---------------------|
| A — Requirements Gathering | 2 | 3 |
| B — System Design | 3 | 4 |
| C — Coding | 4 | 5 |
| D — Testing | 2 | 2 |
| E — Deployment | 1 | 3 |

Precedence: A → B → C → D → E (sequential, finish-to-start).

**Step 1 — Determine the activity time windows.**

Because the activities are purely sequential (each starts only when the previous finishes), the start week of activity *i* is the sum of durations of all preceding activities.

$$
\begin{aligned}
ES_A &= 0, \quad EF_A = 0 + 2 = 2 \\
ES_B &= EF_A = 2, \quad EF_B = 2 + 3 = 5 \\
ES_C &= EF_B = 5, \quad EF_C = 5 + 4 = 9 \\
ES_D &= EF_C = 9, \quad EF_D = 9 + 2 = 11 \\
ES_E &= EF_D = 11, \quad EF_E = 11 + 1 = 12
\end{aligned}
$$

> **Logic:** $ES$ = Early Start (when an activity can begin); $EF$ = Early Finish. With Finish-to-Start precedence and no lag, $ES_{i+1} = EF_i$.

**Step 2 — Build the Resource Histogram table.**

For each week $t = 0, 1, 2, \ldots, 12$, sum the developer requirement of every *active* activity.

$$
\begin{aligned}
R(t) &= \sum_{i \in Active(t)} U_i
\end{aligned}
$$

| Week $t$ | Active Activities | $R(t)$ Developers |
|----------|-------------------|-------------------|
| 0 | A | 3 |
| 1 | A | 3 |
| 2 | B | 4 |
| 3 | B | 4 |
| 4 | B | 4 |
| 5 | C | 5 |
| 6 | C | 5 |
| 7 | C | 5 |
| 8 | C | 5 |
| 9 | D | 2 |
| 10 | D | 2 |
| 11 | E | 3 |

**Step 3 — Identify Peak Demand.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
PD = \max_{t} R(t) &= \max\{3, 3, 4, 4, 4, 5, 5, 5, 5, 2, 2, 3\} \\
PD &= 5 \text{ developers}
\end{aligned}
$$

> **Interpretation:** During weeks 5–8, the project demands a peak of 5 developers simultaneously. If only 4 developers are available, **resource over-allocation** occurs and **resource levelling** is mandatory.

**Step 4 — Compute Total Resource Cost (TRC).**

Assume the developer hourly rate is $R = ₹500/\text{hour}$ and a standard work week is 40 hours.

$$
\begin{aligned}
TRC &= R \times \sum_{t=0}^{11} R(t) \times 40 \\
&= 500 \times (3+3+4+4+4+5+5+5+5+2+2+3) \times 40 \\
&= 500 \times 45 \times 40 \\
&= ₹9,00,000
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 5 — Compute Average Utilization (if 5 developers are hired permanently for 12 weeks).**

Available developer-weeks = $5 \times 12 = 60$.
Required developer-weeks = $45$.

$$
\begin{aligned}
RU &= \left( \frac{45}{60} \right) \times 100\% = 75\%
\end{aligned}
$$

This is a healthy utilization — the team is productive but not over-allocated.

### 3.2 Analytical Decision Matrix — Resource Levelling vs. Resource Smoothing

The KTU 2024 examiner expects students to *justify* which technique to apply given the project scenario.

| Decision Criterion | Resource Levelling | Resource Smoothing |
|--------------------|--------------------|---------------------|
| **Critical Path Affected?** | Yes (path may change) | No (critical path preserved) |
| **Project Duration Changes?** | Yes (extended) | No (kept within free float) |
| **When to Use?** | Resource is *over-allocated* beyond capacity | Resource is fluctuating but within capacity |
| **Mathematical Operation** | Delay activities until resource free | Shift activities *within* their float window |
| **Float Used** | Total Float (may consume all) | Free Float only |
| **Typical Tool** | MS Project "Level Resource" with auto-delay | Manual re-sequencing within slack |
| **Cost Implication** | Higher (extended duration = more overhead) | Lower (only re-sequencing cost) |

> [!NOTE]
> **Decision Rule (Examiner-Ready Statement):**
> If the resource demand curve exceeds the resource availability line **at any point**, you **must** use *Resource Levelling*. If the demand curve fits under the availability line but you want to *flatten* peaks and *fill* valleys for smoother workflow, use *Resource Smoothing*.

### 3.3 Worked Analytical Example — Identifying the Critical Resource

**Problem:** A project has 3 software modules. Module X needs 1 senior architect, Module Y needs 1 senior architect, Module Z needs 1 junior developer. Only **one senior architect** is available; three junior developers are available.

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Resource Demand Index (RDI)} &= \frac{\text{Demand}}{\text{Supply}} \times 100\% \\
RDI_{architect} &= \frac{2}{1} \times 100\% = 200\% \quad \text{(CRITICAL — over-allocated)} \\
RDI_{junior} &= \frac{1}{3} \times 100\% = 33.3\% \quad \text{(under-utilized)}
\end{aligned}
$$

> **Conclusion:** The senior architect is the **Critical Resource** (bottleneck). The Theory of Constraints (Goldratt) dictates that the entire project throughput is limited by this resource. Mitigation options: (a) hire a contractor, (b) train a junior developer, (c) redesign modules X and Y so they can be parallelized without architect overlap.

## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Resource Lifecycle Flow (Mermaid Block Diagram)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Project Charter Initiated] --> B[Resource Planning]
    B --> C[Resource Identification - 4M Taxonomy]
    C --> D[Resource Estimation - hours, units, cost]
    D --> E[Resource Acquisition / Recruitment]
    E --> F[Resource Schedule Development]
    F --> G[Resource Allocation to Activities]
    G --> H{Resource Constraint Hit?}
    H -- Yes --> I[Resource Levelling or Smoothing]
    I --> F
    H -- No --> J[Activity Execution with Allocated Resources]
    J --> K[Resource Utilization Tracking]
    K --> L[Performance Reporting - RU, RE, BR]
    L --> M{Project End?}
    M -- No --> G
    M -- Yes --> N[Resource Release and Lessons Learned]
```

> **Reading the Diagram:** The loop `F → H → I → F` is the **Resource Control Loop** — whenever the schedule violates a resource constraint, levelling triggers a re-schedule, which is then re-checked. This is a classic *Plan-Do-Check-Act* (PDCA) cycle applied to resources.

### 4.2 Resource Breakdown Structure (RBS) — Hierarchical View

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    R0[PROJECT RESOURCES]
    R0 --> R1[Human Resources]
    R0 --> R2[Equipment Resources]
    R0 --> R3[Material Resources]
    R0 --> R4[Financial Resources]

    R1 --> R1A[Project Manager]
    R1 --> R1B[Architect]
    R1 --> R1C[Developers]
    R1 --> R1D[Testers]
    R1 --> R1E[DevOps Engineer]

    R2 --> R2A[Servers]
    R2 --> R2B[IDE Licenses]
    R2 --> R2C[Test Devices]

    R3 --> R3A[Cloud Compute Hours]
    R3 --> R3B[API Quotas]
    R3 --> R3C[Office Supplies]

    R4 --> R4A[Salary Budget]
    R4 --> R4B[Tool Subscriptions]
    R4 --> R4C[Contingency Reserve]
```

### 4.3 Over-Allocation Detection Schematic

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph Resource_Demand
    D1[Week 1: 3 devs needed]
    D2[Week 2: 5 devs needed]
    D3[Week 3: 4 devs needed]
    D4[Week 4: 6 devs needed]
    end

    subgraph Resource_Supply
    S1[Capacity: 4 devs only]
    end

    D1 --> Comp1{Within capacity?}
    Comp1 -- Yes --> OK1[OK]
    D2 --> Comp2{Within capacity?}
    Comp2 -- No --> Lev[Apply Resource Levelling]
    D3 --> Comp3{Within capacity?}
    Comp3 -- Yes --> OK2[OK]
    D4 --> Comp4{Within capacity?}
    Comp4 -- No --> Lev

    S1 -.feeds.-> Comp1
    S1 -.feeds.-> Comp2
    S1 -.feeds.-> Comp3
    S1 -.feeds.-> Comp4

    Lev --> Reschedule[Delay non-critical activities]
    Reschedule --> NewCheck[Re-evaluate peak demand]
```

> **Block-Level Functional Architecture Mapping:**
> This diagram is a *functional architecture flow* (rather than a physical drawing) showing how the **Resource Supply Line** acts as a horizontal ceiling, and any **Resource Demand Bar** that pierces this ceiling triggers the **Levelling Subsystem**, which then re-enters the scheduling loop.

### 4.4 Comparison Schematic: Levelling vs. Smoothing on a Timeline

```mermaid
gantt
    title Original vs. Levelled vs. Smoothed Schedule
    dateFormat  X
    axisFormat  %s

    section Original Plan
    Activity A (3 devs) :a1, 0, 2
    Activity B (5 devs) :a2, 2, 5
    Activity C (4 devs) :a3, 5, 9

    section Smoothed Plan
    Activity A (3 devs) :s1, 0, 2
    Activity B (4 devs - shifted) :s2, 2, 5
    Activity C (4 devs) :s3, 5, 9

    section Levelled Plan
    Activity A (3 devs) :l1, 0, 2
    Activity B (4 devs) :l2, 2, 6
    Activity C (4 devs) :l3, 6, 10
```

> **Interpretation:** In the *Smoothed* plan, the project ends at week 9 (same as original). In the *Levelled* plan, the project extends to week 10 because Activity B is delayed to fit capacity.

## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024 Model Paper]**
*Define the "Nature of Resources" in a software project and list any four inherent properties of resources.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
The Nature of Resources refers to the inherent characteristics and constraints of the assets (human, equipment, material, financial) used in a software project. The four key properties are:
1. **Scarcity** — Resources are finite and cannot be assumed infinite.
2. **Limited Duration of Availability** — Resources are available only in defined time windows.
3. **Heterogeneous Productivity** — Two identical resource units (e.g., two developers) may yield different output rates.
4. **Interdependence / Multi-Use** — Resources may be shared across activities or projects and must be synchronized.

*Mark distribution: 1 mark for definition + 2 marks for properties (0.5 each).*

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Retest Pattern]**
*Differentiate between Resource Levelling and Resource Smoothing. When would you prefer one over the other?*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
| Aspect | Resource Levelling | Resource Smoothing |
|--------|--------------------|--------------------|
| Schedule impact | Extends project duration | Keeps duration unchanged |
| Float used | Total float | Free float only |
| Critical path | May change | Preserved |
| **Used when** | Resource is *over-allocated* beyond capacity | Resource fluctuates but stays within capacity |

Levelling is preferred when peak demand exceeds capacity; smoothing is preferred for optimizing workflow stability within capacity constraints.

*Mark distribution: 2 marks for differentiation + 1 mark for application condition.*

---

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

---

#### Question A (14 Marks) — *[KTU University Exam – July 2024 Model Paper]*

**(a)** Explain in detail the **4-M classification of resources** in a software project. Discuss how each type of resource behaves with respect to scarcity, consumability, and substitutability. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A software project has the following schedule (Finish-to-Start precedence, no lag):

| Activity | Duration (weeks) | Predecessors | Developers Required |
|----------|------------------|--------------|---------------------|
| A | 3 | — | 4 |
| B | 4 | A | 6 |
| C | 2 | A | 3 |
| D | 5 | B, C | 5 |
| E | 3 | D | 4 |

Construct the **Resource Histogram**, compute the **Peak Demand**, and determine the **Total Resource Cost** if the developer rate is ₹600/hour and a week contains 40 working hours. **(7 marks)**

---

**Model Solution for Q.A(a) — 7 Marks:**

The **4-M Taxonomy** of software project resources is a structured classification that helps the project manager plan, track, and control resources uniformly.

1. **Manpower (Human Resources):** Includes project manager, business analyst, architect, developers, testers, DevOps engineers, UI/UX designers.
   *Scarcity:* High in specialized roles (e.g., security experts).
   *Consumability:* Low (reusable; subject to fatigue — 8 hrs/day limit).
   *Substitutability:* Moderate (junior + time can substitute senior).

2. **Machine (Equipment Resources):** Includes servers, IDE licenses, cloud VMs, test devices, build agents.
   *Scarcity:* Moderate; managed via procurement or cloud provisioning.
   *Consumability:* Time-bound (a rented cluster is consumed hour by hour).
   *Substitutability:* High (one cloud provider can replace another).

3. **Material (Consumable Resources):** Includes cloud CPU-hours, API call quotas, third-party paid libraries, stationery.
   *Scarcity:* Procurement-based; replenishable on order.
   *Consumability:* 100 % — once used, gone.
   *Substitutability:* Variable (an open-source library can replace a paid one).

4. **Money (Financial Resources):** The monetary budget allocated to the project.
   *Scarcity:* Defined by the approved budget; controlled by EVM metrics.
   *Consumability:* Linear over time (the *burn rate*).
   *Substitutability:* Universal — money can buy any other resource.

> This taxonomy enables the project manager to apply **different control strategies**: people need motivation and training; machines need maintenance; materials need inventory control; money needs cost tracking via EVM.

*Mark split: 1 mark per M-type (4 marks) + 1.5 marks for cross-cutting properties (scarcity/consumability/substitutability) + 1.5 marks for synthesis insight.*

---

**Model Solution for Q.A(b) — 7 Marks:**

**Step 1 — Compute Early Start (ES) and Early Finish (EF) for each activity.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
ES_A &= 0, &\quad EF_A &= 0 + 3 &= 3 \\
ES_B &= EF_A = 3, &\quad EF_B &= 3 + 4 &= 7 \\
ES_C &= EF_A = 3, &\quad EF_C &= 3 + 2 &= 5 \\
ES_D &= \max(EF_B, EF_C) = \max(7,5) = 7, &\quad EF_D &= 7 + 5 &= 12 \\
ES_E &= EF_D = 12, &\quad EF_E &= 12 + 3 &= 15
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2 — Tabulate activity time windows.**

| Activity | Start week | End week | Developers |
|----------|------------|----------|------------|
| A | 0 | 3 | 4 |
| B | 3 | 7 | 6 |
| C | 3 | 5 | 3 |
| D | 7 | 12 | 5 |
| E | 12 | 15 | 4 |

**Step 3 — Build the weekly Resource Histogram.**

| Week | Active Activities | Total Developers |
|------|-------------------|------------------|
| 0 | A | 4 |
| 1 | A | 4 |
| 2 | A | 4 |
| 3 | B, C | 6 + 3 = **9** |
| 4 | B, C | 6 + 3 = **9** |
| 5 | B | 6 |
| 6 | B | 6 |
| 7 | D | 5 |
| 8 | D | 5 |
| 9 | D | 5 |
| 10 | D | 5 |
| 11 | D | 5 |
| 12 | E | 4 |
| 13 | E | 4 |
| 14 | E | 4 |

**Step 4 — Identify Peak Demand.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
PD &= \max_{t}\{4,4,4,9,9,6,6,5,5,5,5,5,4,4,4\} \\
PD &= 9 \text{ developers (during weeks 3 and 4)}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 5 — Compute Total Resource Cost.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
TRC &= \sum_{t} R(t) \times 40 \text{ hours} \times ₹600/\text{hr} \\
&= (4+4+4+9+9+6+6+5+5+5+5+5+4+4+4) \times 40 \times 600 \\
&= 79 \times 40 \times 600 \\
&= ₹18,96,000
\end{aligned}
$$

> **Conclusion:** The project needs a peak of 9 developers simultaneously. If only 6 developers are available, the project manager must apply Resource Levelling to delay activity D (or split activity B's workstream) until sufficient resources are free.

*Mark split: 2 marks ES/EF calc + 2 marks histogram + 1 mark PD + 2 marks TRC formula & arithmetic.*

---

#### Question B (14 Marks) — *[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Model Paper]*

**(a)** Discuss the **Resource Breakdown Structure (RBS)** with a suitable diagram. How does it differ from the Work Breakdown Structure (WBS)? **(7 marks)**

**(b)** For a project, only **5 senior architects** are available for 10 weeks, but the schedule demands 8, 7, 6, 8, 5, 4, 7, 9, 6, 5 architects in successive weeks.
   (i) Draw the Resource Histogram and identify the **weeks of over-allocation**.
   (ii) Calculate the **Resource Levelling Delay** if the project originally ended at week 10 and the levelled schedule ends at week 13.
   (iii) Compute the **average Resource Utilization** assuming all 5 architects were retained for 13 weeks at ₹800/hour, 40 hours/week. **(7 marks)**

---

**Model Solution for Q.B(a) — 7 Marks:**

The **Resource Breakdown Structure (RBS)** is a hierarchical decomposition of all resources (human, equipment, material, financial) required by the project. It mirrors the WBS in structure but organizes resources rather than deliverables.

**RBS Hierarchy (for a software project):**
- **Level 0:** Project Resources
  - **Level 1:** Human Resources
    - Level 2: Project Manager, Architects, Developers, Testers, DevOps
  - **Level 1:** Equipment Resources
    - Level 2: Servers, IDE Licenses, Test Devices
  - **Level 1:** Material Resources
    - Level 2: Cloud Hours, API Quotas
  - **Level 1:** Financial Resources
    - Level 2: Salary, Tools, Contingency

| Aspect | WBS | RBS |
|--------|-----|-----|
| Focus | Deliverables (what is produced) | Resources (what is consumed) |
| Question Answered | "What work is to be done?" | "Who/What will do the work?" |
| Output | Work Packages | Resource Categories & Quantities |
| Drives | Activity Definition, Schedule | Resource Planning, Cost Aggregation |
| Combined Use | WBS + RBS → Responsibility Assignment Matrix (RAM/RACI) |

> The RBS enables the project manager to aggregate costs by *resource category*, identify skill gaps, and produce accurate resource histograms.

*Mark split: 3 marks RBS explanation + diagram; 2 marks WBS vs RBS comparison; 2 marks integration insight.*

---

**Model Solution for Q.B(b) — 7 Marks:**

**(i) Resource Histogram & Over-Allocation Detection:**

| Week | Demand | Supply | Over-Allocation? |
|------|--------|--------|------------------|
| 1 | 8 | 5 | **Yes (+3)** |
| 2 | 7 | 5 | **Yes (+2)** |
| 3 | 6 | 5 | **Yes (+1)** |
| 4 | 8 | 5 | **Yes (+3)** |
| 5 | 5 | 5 | No |
| 6 | 4 | 5 | No |
| 7 | 7 | 5 | **Yes (+2)** |
| 8 | 9 | 5 | **Yes (+4)** |
| 9 | 6 | 5 | **Yes (+1)** |
| 10 | 5 | 5 | No |

> **Over-allocated weeks: 1, 2, 3, 4, 7, 8, 9 (7 weeks of over-allocation).**

**(ii) Resource Levelling Delay:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
RLD &= T_{levelled} - T_{original} \\
&= 13 - 10 = 3 \text{ weeks}
\end{aligned}
$$

**(iii) Average Resource Utilization for 5 architects retained for 13 weeks:**

Total available architect-hours = $5 \times 13 \times 40 = 2600$ hours.
Total demanded architect-hours = $(8+7+6+8+5+4+7+9+6+5) \times 40 = 65 \times 40 = 2600$ hours.

$$
\begin{aligned}
RU &= \left( \frac{2600}{2600} \right) \times 100\% = 100\%
\end{aligned}
$$

Total Cost = $5 \times 13 \times 40 \times 800 = ₹20,80,000$.

> **Interpretation:** Despite the original over-allocation, levelling smooths the demand into a 13-week schedule that *exactly* matches the available architect capacity. **100 % utilization**, however, is a *burnout risk* — the manager should ideally plan for 80 % utilization to leave a 20 % buffer for unforeseen work.

*Mark split: 2 marks histogram, 1 mark over-allocation identification, 1 mark RLD, 2 marks RU calculation, 1 mark interpretation.*

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:**
> 1. **Confusing Levelling with Smoothing** — A *very common* mistake is to use these terms interchangeably. The examiner *specifically* tests whether you know that **levelling extends duration** while **smoothing preserves it**. Losing this distinction can cost 2–3 marks.
> 2. **Forgetting to convert weeks to hours** — When computing TRC, students often multiply *developer-weeks* by the hourly rate directly. **Always convert: developer-weeks × 40 hours/week × hourly rate.**
> 3. **Ignoring the "Nature" in the answer** — When asked to discuss the "Nature of Resources," many students list resource types but *omit the inherent properties* (scarcity, heterogeneity, interdependence). The word "Nature" is the key — **properties, not just types, are mandatory**.
> 4. **Resource Utilization > 100 % is a red flag** — In calculations, if your answer exceeds 100 %, it means over-allocation, *not* a "highly efficient project." Always comment on this.
> 5. **Peak Demand must be explicitly stated** — Do not just draw the histogram; **write the numerical PD value with the time period** (e.g., "PD = 9 developers during weeks 3–4").

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Rapid Revision Checklist — "Nature of Resources"**

- ✅ **Definition:** Resources are constrained assets; their *nature* is defined by scarcity, limited availability, heterogeneous productivity, interdependence, and substitutability.
- ✅ **4-M Classification:** Manpower, Machine, Material, Money — know an example of each in a software project.
- ✅ **Three Constraint Types:** Time-limited, Capacity-limited, Skill-limited. Skill-limited is the most dangerous (true bottleneck).
- ✅ **Key Metrics:**
   * **RU** = (Allocated / Available) × 100 %
   * **RE** = (Productive / Allocated) × 100 %
   * **TRC** = Σ (Rate × Hours)
   * **PD** = max over time of demand
   * **BR** = Cost / Time
- ✅ **Resource Histogram** plots demand vs. time; the highest bar is the Peak Demand.
- ✅ **Resource Levelling** changes the schedule (extends duration) to remove over-allocation.
- ✅ **Resource Smoothing** uses only free float to flatten demand without changing the critical path.
- ✅ **RBS** = Resource Breakdown Structure; complements WBS; together they feed the **RACI Matrix**.
- ✅ **Critical Resource** identified by **RDI > 100 %** — a *Theory of Constraints* bottleneck.
- ✅ **Healthy RU** range: **75 %–85 %** (100 % = burnout risk; < 50 % = under-utilization).
- ✅ **Decision Rule:** Demand > Supply ⇒ **Levelling**; Demand ≤ Supply but uneven ⇒ **Smoothing**.
- ✅ **Exam Tip:** Always (a) compute ES/EF first, (b) build the histogram, (c) state PD with the time window, (d) convert units carefully for TRC.
- ✅ **PMBOK 7th Edition Alignment:** Resources fall under the **Resource Management** performance domain; planning, estimating, acquiring, developing, managing, and controlling are the six sub-processes.
- ✅ **Critical Chain Project Management (CCPM)** introduced by Goldratt explicitly addresses the *nature* of resources by inserting **resource buffers** and **feeding buffers** alongside the critical path.
- ✅ **Numericals Always Include:** The hourly rate conversion (weeks × 40 hrs), the percentage calculation, and a concluding managerial interpretation sentence.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Identifying and Scheduling Resources
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEITT633 - SOFTWARE PROJECT MANAGEMENT
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Identifying and Scheduling Resources

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Identifying and Scheduling Resources — Formal Definition

In the context of the **KTU 2024 Scheme (PEITT633 — Software Project Management)**, the topic **"Identifying and Scheduling Resources"** is defined as the systematic process of determining the *people, hardware, software, financial capital, and time* required to execute each Work Breakdown Structure (WBS) activity, and subsequently arranging these resources along a calibrated project timeline to ensure optimized utilization, on-time delivery, and cost adherence.

The process is governed by the **PMBOK (Project Management Body of Knowledge)** framework and constitutes a critical activity within the **Project Schedule Management** and **Project Resource Management** knowledge areas.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition (Verbatim Intent):**
> *Identifying resources* refers to cataloguing human, hardware, software, and financial resources needed per WBS task. *Scheduling resources* refers to assigning these identified resources to specific tasks across a calendar timeline using techniques like **Gantt Charts, Critical Path Method (CPM), and Program Evaluation Review Technique (PERT)**.

### Core Components at a Glance

| Component | Definition | Example |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Human Resources** | Skilled personnel assigned to project tasks | Developers, Testers, Architects |
| **Hardware Resources** | Physical computing infrastructure | Servers, Workstations, Network Devices |
| **Software Resources** | Licenses, IDEs, frameworks, tools | MS Project, JIRA, Git, Docker |
| **Financial Resources** | Capital budget allocated for tasks | Salary, Infrastructure Cost, Licensing |
| **Time Resources** | Calendar duration allocated per task | Person-hours, Person-days |

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The Building Construction Metaphor

> [!NOTE]
> **Intuitive Analogy:** Think of *Identifying Resources* as the moment an architect (Project Manager) drafts the **Bill of Materials (BoM)** for a skyscraper — listing the steel, cement, cranes, masons, and electricians. *Scheduling Resources* is then creating the **Gantt chart on the architect's wall**, deciding *when* the crane comes on Day 3, when the masons arrive on Day 7, and when the electricians are needed on Day 40.

Without proper identification, the architect cannot procure materials. Without proper scheduling, materials arrive at the wrong time — causing either **idle workers (wasted cost)** or **resource conflicts (bottlenecks)**.

In a **software project**, this is identical: a developer (resource) cannot start coding the "Login Module" until the database schema is designed and approved. A tester cannot begin test case execution until the build is deployed to the QA environment.

## 1.3 The Resource Management Workflow

The end-to-end process flows through **four sequential phases**:

1. **Resource Identification** — Cataloguing *what* is needed.
2. **Resource Estimation** — Quantifying *how much* is needed (effort/duration).
3. **Resource Scheduling** — Assigning *when* and *who*.
4. **Resource Leveling & Smoothening** — Resolving *conflicts and over-allocations*.

> [!TIP]
> **KTU Exam Pearl:** Examiners frequently ask students to differentiate between **Resource Leveling** and **Resource Smoothening**. Always remember: *Leveling changes the **critical path** (can extend project end-date), whereas Smoothening **does not** change the critical path (only reshuffles float).*

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Theoretical Foundation of Resource Scheduling

Resource Scheduling is rooted in **Operations Research (OR)** and **Network Analysis**. The discipline emerged during the **1950s** when the U.S. Navy developed **PERT** for the Polaris Missile project, and **DuPont** developed **CPM** for plant maintenance projects. Both techniques were later merged for software project planning.

### 2.1.1 Key Terminology (Board-Exam Essentials)

- **Activity (Task):** A discrete unit of work consuming time and resources.
- **Event (Milestone):** A point in time marking the start or completion of an activity.
- **Predecessor:** An activity that must be completed before another begins.
- **Successor:** An activity that begins after a given activity ends.
- **Critical Path:** The longest path through the network; determines minimum project duration.
- **Float (Slack):** The amount of time an activity can be delayed without delaying the project.
- **Earliest Start (ES):** The earliest time an activity can begin.
- **Earliest Finish (EF):** ES + Duration.
- **Latest Start (LS):** The latest time an activity can begin without delaying the project.
- **Latest Finish (LF):** LS + Duration.

## 2.2 The CPM Algorithm — Forward & Backward Pass

### 2.2.1 Forward Pass (Calculating ES and EF)

The Forward Pass begins at the project start node (typically ES = 0 for the first activity) and progresses sequentially to the end of the network.

$$\begin{aligned}
ES_j &= \max(EF_i) \quad \text{for all predecessors } i \text{ of } j \\
EF_j &= ES_j + D_j
\end{aligned}$$

Where:
- $ES_j$ = Earliest Start of activity $j$
- $EF_i$ = Earliest Finish of predecessor $i$
- $D_j$ = Duration of activity $j$

### 2.2.2 Backward Pass (Calculating LS and LF)

The Backward Pass begins at the project end (LF = project deadline for the last activity) and progresses in reverse.

$$\begin{aligned}
LF_i &= \min(LS_j) \quad \text{for all successors } j \text{ of } i \\
LS_i &= LF_i - D_i
\end{aligned}$$

### 2.2.3 Float Calculation

$$\begin{aligned}
TF_j &= LS_j - ES_j = LF_j - EF_j \\
FF_j &= \min(ES_{\text{successors}}) - EF_j
\end{aligned}$$

Where **TF** = Total Float and **FF** = Free Float.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Critical Path Identification Rule:** Any activity with **TF = 0** lies on the Critical Path. The sum of durations along the critical path equals the **minimum project duration**.

## 2.3 PERT — Three-Point Estimation

PERT is used when activity durations are uncertain. It uses three estimates:

- **Optimistic Time ($t_o$):** Best-case scenario duration.
- **Most Likely Time ($t_m$):** Realistic, expected duration under normal conditions.
- **Pessimistic Time ($t_p$):** Worst-case scenario duration.

### 2.3.1 Expected Time ($t_e$)

$$\begin{aligned}
t_e &= \frac{t_o + 4t_m + t_p}{6}
\end{aligned}$$

### 2.3.2 Variance ($\sigma^2$)

$$\begin{aligned}
\sigma^2 &= \left( \frac{t_p - t_o}{6} \right)^2
\end{aligned}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Utility:** PERT is widely used in R&D, defense projects, and novel software development where activity duration cannot be estimated with certainty. CPM is preferred for construction and well-understood repetitive projects.

## 2.4 Resource Leveling vs. Resource Smoothening

| Parameter | Resource Leveling | Resource Smoothening |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Definition** | Adjusts activity start/finish dates to balance resource demand | Adjusts activities within their float to level resource usage |
| **Critical Path** | **Can change** the critical path | **Does not change** the critical path |
| **Project End Date** | **Can extend** the project deadline | **Cannot extend** the project deadline |
| **Used When** | Resources are limited (constrained) | Project deadline is fixed |
| **Float** | Activities may lose float | Only activities with float are reshuffled |

## 2.5 KTU Formula Sheet (Cheat Sheet)

> [!TIP]
> **Save this table — it covers 90% of KTU numerical questions on this topic.**

| Formula Name | Equation | Variables / Units |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Earliest Finish | $EF = ES + D$ | $D$ = Duration (days) |
| Latest Start | $LS = LF - D$ | $LF$ = Latest Finish (days) |
| Total Float | $TF = LS - ES$ | Output in days |
| Free Float | $FF = \min(ES_{\text{succ}}) - EF$ | Output in days |
| PERT Expected Time | $t_e = \dfrac{t_o + 4t_m + t_p}{6}$ | Output in days |
| PERT Variance | $\sigma^2 = \left(\dfrac{t_p - t_o}{6}\right)^2$ | Output in $\text{days}^2$ |
| PERT Std. Deviation | $\sigma = \dfrac{t_p - t_o}{6}$ | Output in days |
| Project Std. Deviation (Critical Path) | $\sigma_{\text{proj}} = \sqrt{\sum \sigma_i^2}$ | Sum along critical path |
| Project Probability (Z-score) | $Z = \dfrac{T_s - T_e}{\sigma_{\text{proj}}}$ | $T_s$ = Scheduled, $T_e$ = Expected |
| Cost Slope (Crashing) | $\text{Slope} = \dfrac{C_c - C_n}{T_n - T_c}$ | Cost per day reduction |

> **Real-World Engineering Utility:** The CPM/PERT framework is foundational in project management software such as **Microsoft Project, Primavera P6, Smartsheet, and OpenProject**. Every modern software organization uses these techniques, often automated behind AI-based scheduling engines, to manage software development lifecycles (SDLC).
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Worked-Out Example — CPM Network Analysis

> [!NOTE]
> **Problem Statement:** A software development project has the following activities. Construct the network, identify the critical path, and compute ES, EF, LS, LF, and TF for each activity.

| Activity | Duration (Days) | Predecessor |
| :--- | :---: | :--- |
| A | 6 | — |
| B | 9 | — |
| C | 4 | A |
| D | 7 | A |
| E | 5 | B, C |
| F | 8 | D |
| G | 6 | E, F |

### 3.1.1 Step 1 — Draw the Network (Logical Sequence)

```
Start → A → C → E → G → End
       ↓   ↓   ↑
       D → F → ┘
Start → B → E
```

### 3.1.2 Step 2 — Forward Pass (Calculate ES and EF)

**Activity A:** No predecessor.
$$\begin{aligned}
ES_A &= 0 \\
EF_A &= ES_A + D_A = 0 + 6 = 6
\end{aligned}$$

**Activity B:** No predecessor.
$$\begin{aligned}
ES_B &= 0 \\
EF_B &= 0 + 9 = 9
\end{aligned}$$

**Activity C:** Predecessor = A.
$$\begin{aligned}
ES_C &= EF_A = 6 \\
EF_C &= 6 + 4 = 10
\end{aligned}$$

**Activity D:** Predecessor = A.
$$\begin{aligned}
ES_D &= EF_A = 6 \\
EF_D &= 6 + 7 = 13
\end{aligned}$$

**Activity E:** Predecessors = B and C.
$$\begin{aligned}
ES_E &= \max(EF_B, EF_C) = \max(9, 10) = 10 \\
EF_E &= 10 + 5 = 15
\end{aligned}$$

**Activity F:** Predecessor = D.
$$\begin{aligned}
ES_F &= EF_D = 13 \\
EF_F &= 13 + 8 = 21
\end{aligned}$$

**Activity G:** Predecessors = E and F.
$$\begin{aligned}
ES_G &= \max(EF_E, EF_F) = \max(15, 21) = 21 \\
EF_G &= 21 + 6 = 27
\end{aligned}$$

### 3.1.3 Step 3 — Backward Pass (Calculate LF and LS)

Working backward from $LF_G = 27$.

**Activity G:**
$$\begin{aligned}
LF_G &= 27 \\
LS_G &= 27 - 6 = 21
\end{aligned}$$

**Activity F:** Successor = G.
$$\begin{aligned}
LF_F &= LS_G = 21 \\
LS_F &= 21 - 8 = 13
\end{aligned}$$

**Activity E:** Successor = G.
$$\begin{aligned}
LF_E &= LS_G = 21 \\
LS_E &= 21 - 5 = 16
\end{aligned}$$

**Activity D:** Successor = F.
$$\begin{aligned}
LF_D &= LS_F = 13 \\
LS_D &= 13 - 7 = 6
\end{aligned}$$

**Activity C:** Successor = E.
$$\begin{aligned}
LF_C &= LS_E = 16 \\
LS_C &= 16 - 4 = 12
\end{aligned}$$

**Activity B:** Successor = E.
$$\begin{aligned}
LF_B &= LS_E = 16 \\
LS_B &= 16 - 9 = 7
\end{aligned}$$

**Activity A:** Successors = C and D.
$$\begin{aligned}
LF_A &= \min(LS_C, LS_D) = \min(12, 6) = 6 \\
LS_A &= 6 - 6 = 0
\end{aligned}$$

### 3.1.4 Step 4 — Compute Total Float

$$\begin{aligned}
TF_A &= LS_A - ES_A = 0 - 0 = 0 \\
TF_B &= 7 - 0 = 7 \\
TF_C &= 12 - 6 = 6 \\
TF_D &= 6 - 6 = 0 \\
TF_E &= 16 - 10 = 6 \\
TF_F &= 13 - 13 = 0 \\
TF_G &= 21 - 21 = 0
\end{aligned}$$

### 3.1.5 Step 5 — Identify the Critical Path

**Critical Path:** A → D → F → G

**Project Duration:** $6 + 7 + 8 + 6 = 27$ days

### 3.1.6 Consolidated Result Table

| Activity | Duration | ES | EF | LS | LF | TF | Critical? |
| :--- | :---: | :---: | :---: | :---: | :---: | :---: | :--- |
| A | 6 | 0 | 6 | 0 | 6 | 0 | ✅ Yes |
| B | 9 | 0 | 9 | 7 | 16 | 7 | No |
| C | 4 | 6 | 10 | 12 | 16 | 6 | No |
| D | 7 | 6 | 13 | 6 | 13 | 0 | ✅ Yes |
| E | 5 | 10 | 15 | 16 | 21 | 6 | No |
| F | 8 | 13 | 21 | 13 | 21 | 0 | ✅ Yes |
| G | 6 | 21 | 27 | 21 | 27 | 0 | ✅ Yes |

## 3.2 Worked-Out Example — PERT Three-Point Estimation

> [!NOTE]
> **Problem:** For a software module, the optimistic time is 8 days, most likely time is 14 days, and pessimistic time is 26 days. Compute the expected time, variance, and standard deviation.

### Step 1 — Compute Expected Time

$$\begin{aligned}
t_e &= \frac{t_o + 4t_m + t_p}{6} \\
&= \frac{8 + 4(14) + 26}{6} \\
&= \frac{8 + 56 + 26}{6} \\
&= \frac{90}{6} = 15 \text{ days}
\end{aligned}$$

### Step 2 — Compute Variance

$$\begin{aligned}
\sigma^2 &= \left(\frac{t_p - t_o}{6}\right)^2 \\
&= \left(\frac{26 - 8}{6}\right)^2 \\
&= \left(\frac{18}{6}\right)^2 = (3)^2 = 9 \text{ days}^2
\end{aligned}$$

### Step 3 — Compute Standard Deviation

$$\begin{aligned}
\sigma &= \sqrt{\sigma^2} = \sqrt{9} = 3 \text{ days}
\end{aligned}$$

## 3.3 Worked-Out Example — PERT Probability Analysis

> [!NOTE]
> **Problem:** A software project has a critical path expected duration of 40 weeks with $\sigma_{\text{proj}} = 2.5$ weeks. The client has scheduled completion in 45 weeks. What is the probability of meeting the deadline?

### Step 1 — Compute Z-score

$$\begin{aligned}
Z &= \frac{T_s - T_e}{\sigma_{\text{proj}}} \\
&= \frac{45 - 40}{2.5} \\
&= \frac{5}{2.5} = 2.0
\end{aligned}$$

### Step 2 — Read Probability from Standard Normal Table

For $Z = 2.0$, the probability of completing within 45 weeks is approximately **97.72%**.

**Interpretation:** There is a **97.72% probability** of meeting the scheduled deadline of 45 weeks.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Resource Management Process Flow (Mermaid Block Diagram)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    startNode([Project Initiation]) --> idNode[Resource Identification]
    idNode --> estNode[Resource Estimation - Effort, Cost, Duration]
    estNode --> schNode[Resource Scheduling - CPM/PERT/Gantt]
    schNode --> allocNode[Resource Allocation - Assign to Tasks]
    allocNode --> conflictNode{Resource Conflict?}
    conflictNode -- Yes --> levelNode[Resource Leveling]
    levelNode --> smoothNode[Resource Smoothening]
    smoothNode --> finalNode[Finalized Schedule]
    conflictNode -- No --> finalNode
    finalNode --> monitorNode[Monitoring and Control]
    monitorNode --> endNode([Project Closure])

    style startNode fill:#4A90E2,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px,color:#fff
    style idNode fill:#F5A623,stroke:#333,stroke-width:1px,color:#fff
    style estNode fill:#F5A623,stroke:#333,stroke-width:1px,color:#fff
    style schNode fill:#F5A623,stroke:#333,stroke-width:1px,color:#fff
    style allocNode fill:#F5A623,stroke:#333,stroke-width:1px,color:#fff
    style conflictNode fill:#9013FE,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px,color:#fff
    style levelNode fill:#7ED321,stroke:#333,stroke-width:1px,color:#fff
    style smoothNode fill:#7ED321,stroke:#333,stroke-width:1px,color:#fff
    style finalNode fill:#50E3C2,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px,color:#fff
    style monitorNode fill:#D0021B,stroke:#333,stroke-width:1px,color:#fff
    style endNode fill:#4A90E2,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px,color:#fff
```

## 4.2 CPM Network Diagram (Worked Example Mapped)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    nodeStart([Start]) --> nodeA[A: 6 days]
    nodeStart --> nodeB[B: 9 days]
    nodeA --> nodeC[C: 4 days]
    nodeA --> nodeD[D: 7 days]
    nodeB --> nodeE[E: 5 days]
    nodeC --> nodeE
    nodeE --> nodeG[G: 6 days]
    nodeD --> nodeF[F: 8 days]
    nodeF --> nodeG
    nodeG --> nodeEnd([End])

    style nodeStart fill:#50E3C2,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    style nodeA fill:#F5A623,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px,color:#fff
    style nodeB fill:#F8E71C,stroke:#333,stroke-width:1px,color:#000
    style nodeC fill:#F8E71C,stroke:#333,stroke-width:1px,color:#000
    style nodeD fill:#F5A623,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px,color:#fff
    style nodeE fill:#F8E71C,stroke:#333,stroke-width:1px,color:#000
    style nodeF fill:#F5A623,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px,color:#fff
    style nodeG fill:#F5A623,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px,color:#fff
    style nodeEnd fill:#D0021B,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px,color:#fff
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Diagram Legend:** Orange (amber) nodes represent activities on the **Critical Path** (A → D → F → G). Yellow nodes represent non-critical activities with available float.

## 4.3 Resource Allocation Histogram (Conceptual View)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    titleNode[Resource Allocation Visualization] --> histNode[Developers Required per Week]
    histNode --> wk1[Week 1: 4 Devs]
    histNode --> wk2[Week 2: 6 Devs]
    histNode --> wk3[Week 3: 8 Devs - PEAK DEMAND]
    histNode --> wk4[Week 4: 5 Devs]
    histNode --> wk5[Week 5: 3 Devs]
    histNode --> wk6[Week 6: 7 Devs]
    wk1 --> balanceNode{Resource Leveling Needed?}
    wk3 --> balanceNode
    wk6 --> balanceNode
    balanceNode -- Yes --> solveNode[Apply Leveling - Shift Non-Critical Tasks]

    style titleNode fill:#4A90E2,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px,color:#fff
    style histNode fill:#F5A623,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px,color:#fff
    style wk3 fill:#D0021B,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px,color:#fff
    style balanceNode fill:#9013FE,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px,color:#fff
    style solveNode fill:#7ED321,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px,color:#fff
```

## 4.4 PERT Beta Distribution Curve (Conceptual)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    leftNode[Optimistic to - 3 sigma] --> midLeft[Optimistic to]
    midLeft --> peakNode[Peak: Most Likely Time]
    peakNode --> midRight[Pessimistic to]
    midRight --> rightNode[Pessimistic to + 3 sigma]
    peakNode --> meanNode[Mean: Expected Time]

    style leftNode fill:#4A90E2,stroke:#333,stroke-width:1px,color:#fff
    style midLeft fill:#7ED321,stroke:#333,stroke-width:1px,color:#fff
    style peakNode fill:#F5A623,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px,color:#fff
    style midRight fill:#F5A623,stroke:#333,stroke-width:1px,color:#fff
    style rightNode fill:#D0021B,stroke:#333,stroke-width:1px,color:#fff
    style meanNode fill:#9013FE,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px,color:#fff
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1: Define Resource Leveling and Resource Smoothening. `[KTU University Exam — May 2024]`

**Model Answer:**

**Resource Leveling** is a resource optimization technique in which the start and finish dates of project activities are adjusted to balance the workload of resources. The critical path *may change*, and the *project end date may be extended* when leveling is applied. It is used when shared or critical resources are available only at specific times or in limited quantities.

**Resource Smoothening** is a resource optimization technique used to optimize the utilization of resources *without changing the project completion date*. Activities are rescheduled only within their available float. The critical path remains unchanged.

**Key Difference Table:**

| Aspect | Leveling | Smoothening |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Project End Date | Can extend | Cannot extend |
| Critical Path | Can change | Does not change |
| Resource Constraint | Yes (hard constraint) | No (soft constraint) |

---

### Question 2: What is a Critical Path? How is it identified in a project network? `[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]`

**Model Answer:**

The **Critical Path** is the longest sequence of activities in a project network that determines the *minimum time required to complete the project*. It represents the chain of dependent tasks that have **zero total float (slack)**.

**Identification Procedure:**
1. Construct the Activity-on-Arrow (AOA) or Activity-on-Node (AON) network.
2. Perform a **Forward Pass** to compute ES and EF for each activity.
3. Perform a **Backward Pass** to compute LS and LF.
4. Compute **Total Float** for each activity using $TF = LS - ES$.
5. Activities with **TF = 0** form the **Critical Path**.
6. The sum of durations of critical activities equals the **minimum project duration**.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Module Internal Choice Questions (14 Marks Each)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU ESE Pattern:** Students must answer **one full question out of two choices**. Each question has sub-parts (a) carrying 7 marks and (b) carrying 7 marks. Attempt both sub-parts.

---

### **Question A (Choice 1) — 14 Marks** `[KTU University Exam — Dec 2024]`

#### (a) Construct the CPM network and identify the critical path. (7 Marks)

| Activity | Duration (Days) | Predecessors |
| :--- | :---: | :--- |
| P | 5 | — |
| Q | 8 | — |
| R | 6 | P |
| S | 4 | Q |
| T | 7 | R, S |
| U | 5 | T |
| V | 9 | U |

**Solution:**

**Step 1 — Forward Pass:**

$$\begin{aligned}
ES_P &= 0, \quad EF_P = 0 + 5 = 5 \\
ES_Q &= 0, \quad EF_Q = 0 + 8 = 8 \\
ES_R &= EF_P = 5, \quad EF_R = 5 + 6 = 11 \\
ES_S &= EF_Q = 8, \quad EF_S = 8 + 4 = 12 \\
ES_T &= \max(EF_R, EF_S) = \max(11, 12) = 12, \quad EF_T = 12 + 7 = 19 \\
ES_U &= EF_T = 19, \quad EF_U = 19 + 5 = 24 \\
ES_V &= EF_U = 24, \quad EF_V = 24 + 9 = 33
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 2 — Backward Pass:**

$$\begin{aligned}
LF_V &= 33, \quad LS_V = 33 - 9 = 24 \\
LF_U &= LS_V = 24, \quad LS_U = 24 - 5 = 19 \\
LF_T &= LS_U = 19, \quad LS_T = 19 - 7 = 12 \\
LF_S &= LS_T = 12, \quad LS_S = 12 - 4 = 8 \\
LF_R &= LS_T = 12, \quad LS_R = 12 - 6 = 6 \\
LF_Q &= LS_S = 8, \quad LS_Q = 8 - 8 = 0 \\
LF_P &= LS_R = 6, \quad LS_P = 6 - 5 = 1
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 3 — Total Float:**

| Activity | Duration | ES | EF | LS | LF | TF | Critical? |
| :--- | :---: | :---: | :---: | :---: | :---: | :---: | :--- |
| P | 5 | 0 | 5 | 1 | 6 | 1 | No |
| Q | 8 | 0 | 8 | 0 | 8 | 0 | ✅ Yes |
| R | 6 | 5 | 11 | 6 | 12 | 1 | No |
| S | 4 | 8 | 12 | 8 | 12 | 0 | ✅ Yes |
| T | 7 | 12 | 19 | 12 | 19 | 0 | ✅ Yes |
| U | 5 | 19 | 24 | 19 | 24 | 0 | ✅ Yes |
| V | 9 | 24 | 33 | 24 | 33 | 0 | ✅ Yes |

**Critical Path:** Q → S → T → U → V

**Project Duration:** $8 + 4 + 7 + 5 + 9 = 33$ days

---

#### (b) Explain the three types of floats in project scheduling. (7 Marks)

**Model Answer:**

In project network analysis, three types of float (slack) are computed to determine activity flexibility:

**1. Total Float (TF):**
The amount of time an activity can be delayed *without delaying the project completion date*. It is computed as:
$$TF = LS - ES = LF - EF$$

**2. Free Float (FF):**
The amount of time an activity can be delayed *without delaying the early start of any successor activity*. It is computed as:
$$FF = \min(ES_{\text{successors}}) - EF_{\text{current}}$$

**3. Independent Float (IF):**
The amount of time an activity can be delayed *assuming all predecessors are at their latest finish and all successors are at their earliest start*. It is computed as:
$$IF = \min(ES_{\text{successors}}) - \max(EF_{\text{predecessors}}) - D_{\text{current}}$$

**Key Relationships:**

| Float Type | Significance |
| :--- | :--- |
| **Total Float** | Affects overall project deadline |
| **Free Float** | Affects immediate successor activities only |
| **Independent Float** | Independent scheduling flexibility |

> [!TIP]
> **Remember:** Independent Float ≤ Free Float ≤ Total Float. Independent Float can be **negative**, while Free and Total Float are **non-negative**.

---

### **Question B (Choice 2) — 14 Marks** `[KTU University Exam — May 2024]`

#### (a) Differentiate between PERT and CPM. When would you prefer PERT over CPM in a software project? (7 Marks)

**Model Answer:**

| Parameter | PERT | CPM |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Full Form** | Program Evaluation Review Technique | Critical Path Method |
| **Orientation** | Event-oriented (Activity-on-Arrow) | Activity-oriented (Activity-on-Node) |
| **Time Estimates** | Three estimates (Optimistic, Most Likely, Pessimistic) | Single deterministic estimate |
| **Duration Type** | Probabilistic / Stochastic | Deterministic |
| **Best Suited For** | R&D, novel, uncertain projects | Construction, repetitive, well-defined projects |
| **Focus** | Time | Time-Cost Tradeoff |
| **Crashing Analysis** | Not typically performed | Cost slope and crashing possible |
| **Beta Distribution** | Uses beta distribution | Uses point estimates |

**When to prefer PERT over CPM in software projects:**

1. **Uncertain Task Durations:** When the project involves novel algorithms, R&D modules, or emerging technologies where duration cannot be estimated with certainty.
2. **R&D-Heavy Phases:** During proof-of-concept or prototype development.
3. **Probability of Completion:** When the management requires a *probability-based confidence level* for delivery deadlines.
4. **Lack of Historical Data:** When no prior similar project exists to extract benchmark durations.

For routine software maintenance, ERP customization, or well-understood module development, **CPM is preferred** due to its deterministic clarity and cost-optimization capability.

---

#### (b) A software project network has critical path activities with the following PERT estimates. Compute the expected project duration, variance, and the probability of completing in 24 weeks given scheduled duration of 24 weeks. (7 Marks)

| Critical Activity | $t_o$ | $t_m$ | $t_p$ |
| :--- | :---: | :---: | :---: |
| A | 4 | 8 | 18 |
| B | 2 | 5 | 14 |
| C | 6 | 10 | 22 |
| D | 3 | 7 | 17 |

**Solution:**

**Step 1 — Compute Expected Time for Each Activity:**

**Activity A:**
$$\begin{aligned}
t_e^A &= \frac{4 + 4(8) + 18}{6} = \frac{4 + 32 + 18}{6} = \frac{54}{6} = 9 \text{ weeks}
\end{aligned}$$

**Activity B:**
$$\begin{aligned}
t_e^B &= \frac{2 + 4(5) + 14}{6} = \frac{2 + 20 + 14}{6} = \frac{36}{6} = 6 \text{ weeks}
\end{aligned}$$

**Activity C:**
$$\begin{aligned}
t_e^C &= \frac{6 + 4(10) + 22}{6} = \frac{6 + 40 + 22}{6} = \frac{68}{6} \approx 11.33 \text{ weeks}
\end{aligned}$$

**Activity D:**
$$\begin{aligned}
t_e^D &= \frac{3 + 4(7) + 17}{6} = \frac{3 + 28 + 17}{6} = \frac{48}{6} = 8 \text{ weeks}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 2 — Expected Project Duration ($T_e$):**

$$\begin{aligned}
T_e &= t_e^A + t_e^B + t_e^C + t_e^D = 9 + 6 + 11.33 + 8 = 34.33 \text{ weeks}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 3 — Variance for Each Activity:**

$$\begin{aligned}
\sigma_A^2 &= \left(\frac{18 - 4}{6}\right)^2 = \left(\frac{14}{6}\right)^2 \approx 5.44 \\
\sigma_B^2 &= \left(\frac{14 - 2}{6}\right)^2 = \left(\frac{12}{6}\right)^2 = 4.00 \\
\sigma_C^2 &= \left(\frac{22 - 6}{6}\right)^2 = \left(\frac{16}{6}\right)^2 \approx 7.11 \\
\sigma_D^2 &= \left(\frac{17 - 3}{6}\right)^2 = \left(\frac{14}{6}\right)^2 \approx 5.44
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 4 — Project Variance and Standard Deviation:**

$$\begin{aligned}
\sigma_{\text{proj}}^2 &= 5.44 + 4.00 + 7.11 + 5.44 = 21.99 \\
\sigma_{\text{proj}} &= \sqrt{21.99} \approx 4.69 \text{ weeks}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 5 — Compute Z-score:**

$$\begin{aligned}
Z &= \frac{T_s - T_e}{\sigma_{\text{proj}}} = \frac{24 - 34.33}{4.69} = \frac{-10.33}{4.69} \approx -2.20
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 6 — Probability from Standard Normal Table:**

For $Z = -2.20$, the probability of completing within 24 weeks is approximately **1.39%**.

**Conclusion:** The probability of completing the project within 24 weeks is only **1.39%**, which is very low. Management should renegotiate the deadline or accelerate (crash) the critical activities.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes Where KTU Students Lose Marks:**
>
> 1. **Backwards Pass Direction Error:** Students often perform the backward pass in forward direction. Always remember — *Backward pass starts from the END node and moves toward the START node.*
>
> 2. **Forgetting the MAX/MIN in Merged Nodes:** When an activity has multiple predecessors, ES = **MAX** of all predecessor EFs. When multiple successors exist, LF = **MIN** of all successor LSs.
>
> 3. **Confusing Resource Leveling with Smoothening:** Examiners specifically test this distinction. *Leveling changes the critical path; Smoothening does not.*
>
> 4. **PERT Beta Distribution Misuse:** The expected time formula $t_e = \frac{t_o + 4t_m + t_p}{6}$ is valid **only** under the assumption of a beta distribution. Avoid using it for non-PERT contexts.
>
> 5. **Critical Path Identification via Float = 0 Only:** A common mistake is to assume the *longest individual path* without computing float. *Always compute LS and ES first, then identify TF = 0.*
>
> 6. **Variance Summation:** Project variance is the **sum of variances** along the critical path, *not the average*. Standard deviation is the **square root** of summed variance.
>
> 7. **Skipping Units:** Always write *days* or *weeks* after numerical answers. KTU evaluators deduct marks for missing units.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

> [!TIP]
> **Print this checklist before your KTU exam — it summarizes everything you must memorize.**

- [x] **CPM** is deterministic (single time estimate); **PERT** is probabilistic (three estimates).
- [x] **Forward Pass** computes **ES** and **EF**; **Backward Pass** computes **LF** and **LS**.
- [x] ES of an activity = **MAX** of all predecessor EFs.
- [x] LF of an activity = **MIN** of all successor LSs.
- [x] **Critical Path** = Path with **Total Float = 0** for all activities.
- [x] **Project Duration** = Sum of durations along the critical path.
- [x] **Total Float** = LS − ES = LF − EF.
- [x] **Free Float** = MIN(ES of successors) − EF of current activity.
- [x] **Independent Float** can be negative; Total/Free Float are non-negative.
- [x] PERT Expected Time: $t_e = \frac{t_o + 4t_m + t_p}{6}$.
- [x] PERT Variance: $\sigma^2 = \left(\frac{t_p - t_o}{6}\right)^2$.
- [x] **Project Variance** = Sum of critical-path activity variances.
- [x] **Project Std Dev** = Square root of project variance.
- [x] **Z-score Formula:** $Z = \frac{T_s - T_e}{\sigma_{\text{proj}}}$.
- [x] **Resource Leveling** may extend project deadline and change critical path.
- [x] **Resource Smoothening** does NOT extend deadline and does NOT change critical path.
- [x] **Gantt Charts** are visual bar-chart representations of project schedules.
- [x] **Milestones** have zero duration; **Activities** have positive duration.
- [x] **Crashing** reduces duration by adding resources; governed by *cost slope*.
- [x] **Fast-tracking** parallelizes activities that were originally sequential.
- [x] **Slack/Float** belongs to a path or activity; it is consumed when delayed.

---

> **End of Module 3 Notes — Identifying and Scheduling Resources**
> *Prepared under KTU-PREMIER-ENGINE V10 | Aligned with KTU 2024 Scheme (PEITT633)*

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Creating Critical Paths
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PEITT633 - SOFTWARE PROJECT MANAGEMENT > - **Module:** Module 3 > - **Topic:** Creating Critical Paths ## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition The **Critical Path Method (CPM)** is a deterministic, step-based project management technique used to identify the sequence of **network-dependent activities** that measure the **minimum project duration**. Formally defined by the KTU 2024 Scheme syllabus, the Critical Path is the **longest-duration path of logically connected activities** through a project network diagram, where the cumulative float on each constituent activity is mathematically equal to **zero days**. The mathematical foundation of CPM rests on the computation of four primary schedule parameters for every activity *i*: - **Early Start (ESᵢ)** — the earliest time an activity can commence. - **Early Finish (EFᵢ)** — the earliest time an activity can complete. - **Late Start (LSᵢ)** — the latest time an activity can commence without delaying the project. - **Late Finish (LFᵢ)** — the latest time an activity can complete without delaying the project. > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU 2024 Syllabus Mandate:** Students must be proficient in constructing network diagrams using both **Activity-on-Arrow (AOA)** and **Activity-on-Node (AON)** notations, computing forward and backward passes, and identifying critical paths with zero total float. Expect a **minimum 14-mark derivation problem** in End Semester Examinations. ### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition > [!NOTE] > **Real-World Analogy: The Mountain Hiking Expedition** > > Imagine a group of hikers planning to summit the highest peak in Kerala — **Anamudi**. There are three parallel trails starting from the base camp: Trail A (gentle, 5 hours), Trail B (moderate, 7 hours), and Trail C (steep, 9 hours). All trails merge at the summit. > > - The **base camp → summit** route is fixed. > - However, from the summit, they need to descend and return to the base. Different return paths exist. > - The **Critical Path** is the **slowest combination** of going up and coming back. The expedition can ONLY be considered "over" when the *last* hiker returns. If Trail C takes the longest to go up, it becomes the critical ascent path, because even if the others finish early, the group cannot declare success. > > In software projects: even if 90% of features are completed early, the project is NOT complete until the 10% on the critical path finishes. **Critical Path = the bottleneck of the project.** ### 1.3 Core Metrics in Critical Path Analysis | Metric | Symbol | Unit | Significance | |---|---|---|---| | Estimated Duration | **Dᵢ** | **days/weeks** | The expected time to complete activity *i* | | Early Start | **ESᵢ** | time units | When activity *i* can logically begin | | Early Finish | **EFᵢ** | time units | ESᵢ + Dᵢ | | Late Start | **LSᵢ** | time units | Latest permissible start without project delay | | Late Finish | **LFᵢ** | time units | LSᵢ + Dᵢ | | Total Float | **TFᵢ** | time units | LFᵢ − EFᵢ = LSᵢ − ESᵢ | | Free Float | **FFᵢ** | time units | ESⱼ − EFᵢ (where *j* is the immediate successor) | | Project Duration | **Tₚ** | time units | EF of the terminal node = max(EF of end events) | ## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ### 2.1 The Operational Logic of Critical Path Construction The creation of a critical path is a **four-stage pipeline**: 1. **Activity Decomposition (Work Breakdown):** Every project is decomposed into atomic activities using a **Work Breakdown Structure (WBS)**. Each activity is assigned a deterministic duration *Dᵢ*. 2. **Precedence / Dependency Mapping:** Logical relationships (Finish-to-Start, Start-to-Start, Finish-to-Finish, Start-to-Finish) are established. In the KTU syllabus, the **Finish-to-Start (FS)** relationship is the default. 3. **Network Diagramming:** Activities and events are plotted using either **AOA (Activity on Arrow)** or **AON (Activity on Node)**. In AOA, events (nodes) represent milestones; arrows carry the activity. In AON, nodes carry the activity; arrows represent dependencies. 4. **Critical Path Computation:** - **Forward Pass (Left → Right):** Computes ES and EF. - **Backward Pass (Right → Left):** Computes LS and LF. - **Float Analysis:** Activities with **TF = 0** form the critical path. ### 2.2 The Forward Pass Algorithm For every activity *i* with predecessor(s) *P*, the forward pass applies the **max-rule**: > [!IMPORTANT] > **Rule of Maximum Predecessor Finish (Forward Pass):** > ES of an activity = MAX(EF of all its immediate predecessors) > EF of an activity = ES + D This makes intuitive sense: an activity can only START when ALL its prerequisites are FINISHED, so the activity must wait for the *slowest* predecessor. ### 2.3 The Backward Pass Algorithm For every activity *i* with successor(s) *S*, the backward pass applies the **min-rule**: > [!IMPORTANT] > **Rule of Minimum Successor Start (Backward Pass):** > LF of an activity = MIN(LS of all its immediate successors) > LS of an activity = LF − D For the **terminal activities** (those with no successors), the LF is set equal to the project completion time, which is the **maximum EF across all end events**. ### 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet | # | Formula | Description | Application | |---|---|---|---| | 1 | $EF_i = ES_i + D_i$ | Early Finish calculation | Forward pass | | 2 | $ES_i = \max(EF_p)$ for all predecessors $p$ | Maximum rule for start | Forward pass | | 3 | $LS_i = LF_i - D_i$ | Late Start calculation | Backward pass | | 4 | $LF_i = \min(LS_s)$ for all successors $s$ | Minimum rule for finish | Backward pass | | 5 | $TF_i = LS_i - ES_i = LF_i - EF_i$ | Total Float | Float analysis | | 6 | $FF_i = \min(ES_s) - EF_i$ | Free Float | Float analysis | | 7 | $T_p = \max(EF_{\text{end events}})$ | Total Project Duration | Final network metric | | 8 | $IF_i = \min(TF_s)$ — Total Float of successors | Interfering Float | Advanced analysis | > [!NOTE] > **Engineering Utility:** In real-world software firms (e.g., TCS, Infosys, Cognizant operating out of Technopark Trivandrum), CPM is the bedrock of every Gantt chart generated in **Microsoft Project**, **Jira Advanced Roadmaps**, or **Primavera P6**. Knowing the critical path allows project managers to deploy "crashing" or "fast-tracking" techniques on the right activities to compress schedule without inflating cost. ### 2.5 Event Numbering and Dummy Activities In **AOA networks**, two problems arise: - **Two activities may share the same start and end events** (parallel activities with identical predecessors/successors). - **Solution:** Introduce **Dummy Activities** — zero-duration, zero-cost logical links represented as **dashed arrows**. Dummies carry no resource but transmit the logical dependency. > [!NOTE] > **Event Numbering Rule (Kahn's Process):** The head event number must always be **greater** than the tail event number. This is essential for identifying **dangling events** and ensures no cycles exist in the network. ## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ### 3.1 Exhaustive Worked Example: Forward and Backward Pass Consider a software project decomposed into **7 activities** with the following precedence and duration table: | Activity | Duration (days) | Predecessor(s) | |---|---|---| | A | 6 | — | | B | 4 | — | | C | 7 | A | | D | 3 | A | | E | 5 | B, C | | F | 6 | D | | G | 4 | E, F | **Step 1: Construct the AON Network (conceptually).** Nodes: A, B, C, D, E, F, G Edges: A→C, A→D, B→E, C→E, D→F, E→G, F→G **Step 2: FORWARD PASS — Compute ES and EF for each activity.** **Activity A (no predecessor):** $$ES_A = 0 \quad \text{(project kickoff)}$$ $$EF_A = ES_A + D_A = 0 + 6 = 6 \text{ days}$$ **Activity B (no predecessor):** $$ES_B = 0$$ $$EF_B = 0 + 4 = 4 \text{ days}$$ **Activity C (predecessor: A):** $$ES_C = \max(EF_A) = \max(6) = 6$$ $$EF_C = 6 + 7 = 13 \text{ days}$$ **Activity D (predecessor: A):** $$ES_D = \max(EF_A) = 6$$ $$EF_D = 6 + 3 = 9 \text{ days}$$ **Activity E (predecessors: B, C):** $$ES_E = \max(EF_B, EF_C) = \max(4, 13) = 13$$ $$EF_E = 13 + 5 = 18 \text{ days}$$ **Activity F (predecessor: D):** $$ES_F = \max(EF_D) = 9$$ $$EF_F = 9 + 6 = 15 \text{ days}$$ **Activity G (predecessors: E, F):** $$ES_G = \max(EF_E, EF_F) = \max(18, 15) = 18$$ $$EF_G = 18 + 4 = 22 \text{ days}$$ **Project Duration:** $$T_p = \max(EF_{\text{terminal activities}}) = EF_G = 22 \text{ days}$$ **Step 3: BACKWARD PASS — Compute LS and LF for each activity (right to left).** **Activity G (terminal):** $$LF_G = T_p = 22$$ $$LS_G = LF_G - D_G = 22 - 4 = 18 \text{ days}$$ **Activity F (successor: G):** $$LF_F = \min(LS_G) = 18$$ $$LS_F = 18 - 6 = 12 \text{ days}$$ **Activity E (successor: G):** $$LF_E = \min(LS_G) = 18$$ $$LS_E = 18 - 5 = 13 \text{ days}$$ **Activity D (successor: F):** $$LF_D = \min(LS_F) = 12$$ $$LS_D = 12 - 3 = 9 \text{ days}$$ **Activity C (successor: E):** $$LF_C = \min(LS_E) = 13$$ $$LS_C = 13 - 7 = 6 \text{ days}$$ **Activity B (successor: E):** $$LF_B = \min(LS_E) = 13$$ $$LS_B = 13 - 4 = 9 \text{ days}$$ **Activity A (successors: C, D):** $$LF_A = \min(LS_C, LS_D) = \min(6, 9) = 6$$ $$LS_A = 6 - 6 = 0 \text{ days}$$ **Step 4: FLOAT ANALYSIS — Identify Critical Activities.** For each activity, compute **Total Float**: $$TF_i = LS_i - ES_i = LF_i - EF_i$$ ### 3.2 Analytical Matrix: Complete Schedule Table | Activity | Duration (D) | ES | EF | LS | LF | TF | Critical? | |---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---| | **A** | 6 | 0 | 6 | 0 | 6 | 0 | ✅ **YES** | | B | 4 | 0 | 4 | 9 | 13 | 9 | ❌ No | | **C** | 7 | 6 | 13 | 6 | 13 | 0 | ✅ **YES** | | D | 3 | 6 | 9 | 9 | 12 | 3 | ❌ No | | **E** | 5 | 13 | 18 | 13 | 18 | 0 | ✅ **YES** | | F | 6 | 9 | 15 | 12 | 18 | 3 | ❌ No | | **G** | 4 | 18 | 22 | 18 | 22 | 0 | ✅ **YES** | **Step 5: CRITICAL PATH IDENTIFICATION.** Concatenate all activities where **TF = 0**, in chronological order of network traversal: $$\boxed{\text{Critical Path: A} \rightarrow \text{C} \rightarrow \text{E} \rightarrow \text{G}}$$ **Verification (path sum):** $$D_A + D_C + D_E + D_G = 6 + 7 + 5 + 4 = 22 \text{ days} = T_p \quad \checkmark$$ **Step 6: Free Float Computation (advanced, often asked in 14-mark questions).** For Activity B (successor E only): $$FF_B = \min(ES_E) - EF_B = 13 - 4 = 9 \text{ days}$$ For Activity D (successor F only): $$FF_D = \min(ES_F) - EF_D = 9 - 9 = 0 \text{ days}$$ For Activity F (successor G): $$FF_F = \min(ES_G) - EF_F = 18 - 15 = 3 \text{ days}$$ > [!IMPORTANT] > **Key Insight:** A critical activity **always** has both TF = 0 and FF = 0. However, an activity with FF = 0 need NOT be critical (e.g., Activity D above has FF = 0 but TF = 3). The reverse is not true. ### 3.3 Mathematical Justification: Why the Longest Path is Critical The critical path is mathematically the longest path because of a fundamental property of project networks: - A project is complete **only when the LAST activity finishes**. - The earliest the project can finish is the **maximum** of the EF of all terminal activities. - Any non-critical path has **positive float**, meaning it can be delayed without affecting $T_p$. - The critical path, having zero float, **cannot be delayed at all** — it dictates $T_p$. Formally, if $P_k$ is any path from start to end with activities $i_1, i_2, \dots, i_m$: $$\text{Path Duration}(P_k) = \sum_{j=1}^{m} D_{i_j}$$ $$T_p = \max_{k} \text{Path Duration}(P_k)$$ The critical path is the $P_k^*$ that achieves this maximum. ## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ### 4.1 AON Network Diagram with Computed Schedule Values ```mermaid graph LR start([Start]) --> nodeA["A
D=6
ES=0 EF=6
LS=0 LF=6
TF=0"] start --> nodeB["B
D=4
ES=0 EF=4
LS=9 LF=13
TF=9"] nodeA --> nodeC["C
D=7
ES=6 EF=13
LS=6 LF=13
TF=0"] nodeA --> nodeD["D
D=3
ES=6 EF=9
LS=9 LF=12
TF=3"] nodeB --> nodeE["E
D=5
ES=13 EF=18
LS=13 LF=18
TF=0"] nodeC --> nodeE nodeD --> nodeF["F
D=6
ES=9 EF=15
LS=12 LF=18

D=4
ES=18 EF=22
LS=18 LF=22
TF=0"] nodeF --> nodeG nodeG --> finish([End Tp=22 days]) style nodeA fill:#ff6b6b,stroke:#c92a2a,stroke-width:3px,color:#ffffff style nodeC fill:#ff6b6b,stroke:#c92a2a,stroke-width:3px,color:#ffffff style nodeE fill:#ff6b6b,stroke:#c92a2a,stroke-width:3px,color:#ffffff style nodeG fill:#ff6b6b,stroke:#c92a2a,stroke-width:3px,color:#ffffff style nodeB fill:#51cf66,stroke:#2f9e44,stroke-width:2px,color:#ffffff style nodeD fill:#51cf66,stroke:#2f9e44,stroke-width:2px,color:#ffffff style nodeF fill:#51cf66,stroke:#2f9e44,stroke-width:2px,color:#ffffff style start fill:#339af0,stroke:#1864ab,stroke-width:2px,color:#ffffff style finish fill:#339af0,stroke:#1864ab,stroke-width:2px,color:#ffffff ``` > **Legend:** 🔴 **Red nodes** = Critical Activities (TF=0). 🟢 **Green nodes** = Non-Critical (have float). 🔵 **Blue nodes** = Project boundary events. ### 4.2 AOA Network Diagram (Same Project with Dummy Activity) ```mermaid graph LR e1((1)) -- "A=6" --> e2((2)) e1((1)) -- "B=4" --> e3((3)) e2((2)) -- "C=7" --> e4((4)) e2((2)) -- "D=3" --> e5((5)) e3((3)) -. "Dummy=0" .-> e4((4)) e4((4)) -- "E=5" --> e6((6)) e5((5)) -- "F=6" --> e6((6)) e6((6)) -- "G=4" --> e7((7)) style e1 fill:#339af0,color:#ffffff style e7 fill:#339af0,color:#ffffff style e2 fill:#ffd43b,stroke:#fab005,stroke-width:2px style e3 fill:#ffd43b,stroke:#fab005,stroke-width:2px style e4 fill:#ffd43b,stroke:#fab005,stroke-width:2px style e5 fill:#ffd43b,stroke:#fab005,stroke-width:2px style e6 fill:#ffd43b,stroke:#fab005,stroke-width:2px ``` > **Note on Dashed Line:** The dashed arrow from Event 3 to Event 4 is the **dummy activity**. It is required because both C and E need B as a logical predecessor, but C also needs A. The dummy bridges the logical dependency without consuming time. ### 4.3 Block-Level Functional Architecture of CPM Computation ```mermaid flowchart TD A["WBS Input
Activity List + Durations"] --> B["Precedence
Matrix"] B --> C{"Network Type?"} C -->|"AOA"| D["AOA Diagram
+ Dummies"] C -->|"AON"| E["AON Diagram
Node-Edge Model"] D --> F["Event Numbering
Kahn's Rule"] E --> F F --> G["Forward Pass
Compute ES and EF"] G --> H["Backward Pass
Compute LS and LF"] H --> I["Float Analysis
TF and FF"] I --> J{"TF equals 0?"} J -->|"Yes"| K["Mark as
CRITICAL"] J -->|"No"| L["Non-Critical
Has Float"] K --> M["Construct
Critical Path"] L --> M M --> N["Compute Project
Duration Tp"] style A fill:#e7f5ff,stroke:#1971c2 style N fill:#d3f9d8,stroke:#2f9e44,stroke-width:2px style K fill:#ffe3e3,stroke:#c92a2a,stroke-width:2px style L fill:#fff3bf,stroke:#f08c00 ``` ## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each) **Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]** **Define the term "Critical Path" in a project network. Why is it called the "critical" path?** **Model Answer:** The Critical Path in a project network is the **longest-duration sequence of logically connected activities** that determines the **minimum time required to complete the project**. It is called "critical" because any delay in any of its constituent activities **directly delays the entire project completion date** — these activities have **zero total float (TF = 0)**. They require **continuous monitoring and resource priority allocation** by the project manager. --- **Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]** **Distinguish between Total Float and Free Float with suitable formulas.** **Model Answer:** | Aspect | Total Float (TF) | Free Float (FF) | |---|---|---| | **Formula** | $TF_i = LS_i - ES_i$ | $FF_i = \min(ES_{\text{successors}}) - EF_i$ | | **Meaning** | Time an activity can be delayed **without delaying the project** | Time an activity can be delayed **without delaying any successor's ES** | | **Project Impact** | Affects overall $T_p$ if consumed | Does NOT affect $T_p$ even if fully consumed | | **Critical Activities** | TF = 0 for critical | FF = 0 for critical, but FF = 0 alone doesn't imply critical | --- ### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Module Internal Choice) #### **Question A (14 Marks)** **[KTU University Exam – May 2024 / Model Paper 2024 Scheme]** **(a)** For a software development project, the following activities, durations, and dependencies are given: | Activity | Duration (weeks) | Predecessor(s) | |---|---|---| | A | 4 | — | | B | 6 | — | | C | 5 | A | | D | 3 | A, B | | E | 7 | B | | F | 4 | C | | G | 6 | D, E | | H | 5 | F, G | **(i)** Draw the network diagram using **AON** notation. **(5 marks)** **(ii)** Perform a **forward pass** and **backward pass** to determine ES, EF, LS, LF for every activity. **(5 marks)** **(iii)** Identify the **critical path** and compute the **project duration**. **(4 marks)** **Model Solution:** **(i) AON Network Diagram:** ``` A → C → F → ┐ ├→ H B → D → G → ┤ B → E → G → ┘ ``` **(ii) Forward Pass:** - $ES_A = 0, \quad EF_A = 0 + 4 = 4$ - $ES_B = 0, \quad EF_B = 0 + 6 = 6$ - $ES_C = \max(EF_A) = 4, \quad EF_C = 4 + 5 = 9$ - $ES_D = \max(EF_A, EF_B) = \max(4, 6) = 6, \quad EF_D = 6 + 3 = 9$ - $ES_E = \max(EF_B) = 6, \quad EF_E = 6 + 7 = 13$ - $ES_F = \max(EF_C) = 9, \quad EF_F = 9 + 4 = 13$ - $ES_G = \max(EF_D, EF_E) = \max(9, 13) = 13, \quad EF_G = 13 + 6 = 19$ - $ES_H = \max(EF_F, EF_G) = \max(13, 19) = 19, \quad EF_H = 19 + 5 = 24$ **Backward Pass:** - $LF_H = EF_H = 24, \quad LS_H = 24 - 5 = 19$ - $LF_G = \min(LS_H) = 19, \quad LS_G = 19 - 6 = 13$ - $LF_F = \min(LS_H) = 19, \quad LS_F = 19 - 4 = 15$ - $LF_E = \min(LS_G) = 13, \quad LS_E = 13 - 7 = 6$ - $LF_D = \min(LS_G) = 13, \quad LS_D = 13 - 3 = 10$ - $LF_C = \min(LS_F) = 15, \quad LS_C = 15 - 5 = 10$ - $LF_B = \min(LS_D, LS_E) = \min(10, 6) = 6, \quad LS_B = 6 - 6 = 0$ - $LF_A = \min(LS_C, LS_D) = \min(10, 10) = 10, \quad LS_A = 10 - 4 = 6$ **(iii) Float and Critical Path:** | Activity | D | ES | EF | LS | LF | TF | Critical? | |---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---| | A | 4 | 0 | 4 | 6 | 10 | 6 | ❌ | | B | 6 | 0 | 6 | 0 | 6 | 0 | ✅ | | C | 5 | 4 | 9 | 10 | 15 | 6 | ❌ | | D | 3 | 6 | 9 | 10 | 13 | 4 | ❌ | | E | 7 | 6 | 13 | 6 | 13 | 0 | ✅ | | F | 4 | 9 | 13 | 15 | 19 | 6 | ❌ | | G | 6 | 13 | 19 | 13 | 19 | 0 | ✅ | | H | 5 | 19 | 24 | 19 | 24 | 0 | ✅ | $$\boxed{\text{Critical Path: B} \rightarrow \text{E} \rightarrow \text{G} \rightarrow \text{H}, \quad T_p = 6 + 7 + 6 + 5 = 24 \text{ weeks}}$$ --- **(b)** Explain the concept of **dummy activities** in an AOA network with a suitable example. Why are they not needed in AON networks? **(7 marks)** **Model Solution:** A **dummy activity** is a logical connector represented by a **dashed arrow** in an AOA (Activity-on-Arrow) network. It has: - **Zero duration** (consumes no time) - **Zero cost** (consumes no resources) **Purpose:** Dummies are needed in AOA networks to represent **logical dependencies** between events when two activities have the same start and end events, OR when a precedence relationship exists that cannot be drawn with a real activity. **Example:** Suppose activities X, Y, and Z exist such that: - Y depends on X (FS) - Z also depends on X (FS) - Y and Z are parallel; Z also depends on Y for a review. In AOA, if we have parallel activities X→Y and X→Z both starting from event 1 and ending at event 2, we cannot draw two distinct solid arrows between the same two events. We need a **dummy activity** to disambiguate. **Why not needed in AON:** In AON, dependencies are represented by **arrows between activity nodes**. Each activity is a node, and the arrows are pure logic. There is no ambiguity because activities don't need to be on arrows — they ARE the nodes. Hence, no dummy activities are required. --- #### **Question B (14 Marks — Alternative Choice)** **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 / Model Paper]** **(a)** Explain the **forward pass** and **backward pass** algorithms in critical path computation. State the mathematical rules used. **(7 marks)** **Model Solution:** **Forward Pass (Left → Right):** Computes the **earliest** time an activity can start and finish. - **Rule 1 (Start Node):** For the first activity (no predecessor), $ES = 0$. - **Rule 2 (Maximum Predecessor):** For any other activity: $$ES_i = \max_{\forall p \in \text{pred}(i)} EF_p$$ - **Rule 3 (Finish):** $EF_i = ES_i + D_i$ **Backward Pass (Right → Left):** Computes the **latest** time an activity can start and finish without delaying the project. - **Rule 4 (End Node):** For terminal activities, $LF = T_p$ (the project duration). - **Rule 5 (Minimum Successor):** For any other activity: $$LF_i = \min_{\forall s \in \text{succ}(i)} LS_s$$ - **Rule 6 (Start):** $LS_i = LF_i - D_i$ **Example (briefly):** If Activity X has successors P and Q with LS = 10 and LS = 14, then $LF_X = \min(10, 14) = 10$. --- **(b)** The following table represents a software project. Construct an AOA network with dummies if needed, perform the analysis, and identify the critical path. **(7 marks)** | Activity | 1-2 | 2-3 | 2-4 | 3-4 | 3-5 | 4-5 | 5-6 | |---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---| | Duration | 5 | 3 | 6 | 0 (dummy) | 4 | 2 | 7 | **Model Solution:** The table directly gives us the AOA network structure (the dummy is already identified as 3-4 with D=0). | Activity | D | ES | EF | LS | LF | TF | |---|---|---|---|---|---|---| | 1-2 | 5 | 0 | 5 | 0 | 5 | 0 ✅ | | 2-3 | 3 | 5 | 8 | 7 | 10 | 2 | | 2-4 | 6 | 5 | 11 | 5 | 11 | 0 ✅ | | 3-4 | 0 | 8 | 8 | 11 | 11 | 3 | | 3-5 | 4 | 8 | 12 | 10 | 14 | 2 | | 4-5 | 2 | 11 | 13 | 12 | 14 | 1 | | 5-6 | 7 | 13 | 20 | 13 | 20 | 0 ✅ | **Forward Pass:** - $EF_{1-2} = 5$ - $ES_{2-3} = EF_{1-2} = 5, \quad EF_{2-3} = 8$ - $ES_{2-4} = 5, \quad EF_{2-4} = 11$ - $ES_{3-4} = EF_{2-3} = 8, \quad EF_{3-4} = 8$ - $ES_{3-5} = 8, \quad EF_{3-5} = 12$ - $ES_{4-5} = \max(EF_{2-4}, EF_{3-4}) = \max(11, 8) = 11, \quad EF_{4-5} = 13$ - $ES_{5-6} = \max(EF_{3-5}, EF_{4-5}) = \max(12, 13) = 13, \quad EF_{5-6} = 20$ **Backward Pass:** - $LF_{5-6} = 20, \quad LS_{5-6} = 13$ - $LF_{4-5} = LS_{5-6} = 13, \quad LS_{4-5} = 11$ - $LF_{3-5} = LS_{5-6} = 13, \quad LS_{3-5} = 9$ - $LF_{3-4} = LS_{4-5} = 11, \quad LS_{3-4} = 11$ - $LF_{2-4} = \min(LS_{4-5}, LS_{3-4}) = \min(11, 11) = 11, \quad LS_{2-4} = 5$ - $LF_{2-3} = \min(LS_{3-4}, LS_{3-5}) = \min(11, 9) = 9, \quad LS_{2-3} = 6$ - $LF_{1-2} = \min(LS_{2-3}, LS_{2-4}) = \min(6, 5) = 5, \quad LS_{1-2} = 0$ **Critical Path:** $$\boxed{\text{Critical Path: (1-2)} \rightarrow \text{(2-4)} \rightarrow \text{(4-5)} \rightarrow \text{(5-6)}}$$ **Verification:** $D_{1-2} + D_{2-4} + D_{4-5} + D_{5-6} = 5 + 6 + 2 + 7 = 20$ weeks $\checkmark$ --- > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:** > > 1. **MAX vs MIN confusion:** Students frequently invert the rules. In the **forward pass, use MAX** (you must wait for the SLOWEST predecessor). In the **backward pass, use MIN** (you must start BEFORE the EARLIEST successor). Mixing these up gives completely wrong critical paths. > > 2. **Forgetting terminal LF = Tₚ:** In the backward pass, students often forget to set $LF$ of the final activity equal to the project duration. This propagates errors into all subsequent backward calculations. > > 3. **AOA without dummies:** Drawing two parallel activities between the same two events WITHOUT a dummy is a **guaranteed 2-mark deduction** by KTU examiners. > > 4. **Cyclic networks:** Never draw a loop. If your network has a cycle, re-verify the precedence table. > > 5. **Not checking path sum:** Always verify that the sum of durations along the critical path equals $T_p$. If it does not, an error exists somewhere in your forward/backward pass. > > 6. **Mixing AOA and AON notations:** Pick ONE notation for the diagram. Don't mix dashed (dummy) arrows in an AON diagram. --- ### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist) > [!IMPORTANT] > **Last-Minute Revision Bullet Points — Print This!** - ✅ **Critical Path = Longest Path = Zero Float Path = Minimum Project Duration.** - ✅ **Forward Pass:** Uses the **MAX** rule. Computes **ES** and **EF**. - ✅ **Backward Pass:** Uses the **MIN** rule. Computes **LS** and **LF**. - ✅ **Formulas:** $EF = ES + D$, $LS = LF - D$, $TF = LS - ES = LF - EF$. - ✅ **Free Float:** $FF_i = \min(ES_{\text{successors}}) - EF_i$. Indicates float that does NOT delay successors. - ✅ **Dummy activities** = Zero duration, dashed arrow, used only in **AOA** networks. - ✅ **AON** networks do NOT need dummies — dependencies are node-to-node edges. - ✅ **Multiple Critical Paths** are possible if two paths have equal maximum duration. - ✅ **Kahn's Rule:** Head event number > Tail event number in AOA. - ✅ **Network Errors to avoid:** Dangling events (events with no successor), cyclic loops, missing dummies for parallel activities. - ✅ **Project Duration** $T_p = \max(EF_{\text{end events}})$. - ✅ **Critical activities cannot be crashed indefinitely** — there is a **crash limit** and a **crash cost** (Cost-Time Tradeoff). - ✅ **Crashing** = Adding resources to critical activities to reduce duration (usually increases cost). - ✅ **Fast-Tracking** = Executing activities in parallel that were originally sequential (increases risk). - ✅ **For KTU 14-mark questions:** Always show the schedule table with ES, EF, LS, LF, TF for **all activities**, not just the critical ones. - ✅ **Verification Step:** Sum of durations on the critical path MUST equal $T_p$ computed from the forward pass.

topics.title: Cost Schedule
topics.content:
<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEITT633 - SOFTWARE PROJECT MANAGEMENT
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Cost Schedule

# Cost Schedule — Foundational Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

In the KTU 2024 Scheme framework for **PEITT633 — Software Project Management**, the topic **Cost Schedule** refers to the integrated discipline of **planning, estimating, aggregating, and controlling the financial resources** (cost) and their **distribution over the project timeline** (schedule). It unifies two traditionally separate management dimensions — **Cost Management** and **Schedule Management** — into a single, time-phased budget plan that allows project managers to forecast cash-flow requirements and monitor project health through quantitative performance indicators.

According to the **PMBOK® Guide (7th Edition)** terminology adopted by KTU, the Cost Schedule includes:
