> **Definition:** *Cost Schedule is a time-phased budget that aggregates the estimated costs of work packages over the project schedule, providing a baseline (planned value) against which cost and schedule performance can be measured and controlled.*

The two principal sub-areas are:
1. **Cost Estimation & Budgeting** — determining *how much* the project will cost.
2. **Cost Aggregation & Scheduling** — determining *when* the cost will be incurred.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:**
> The Module 3 syllabus explicitly requires the learner to demonstrate the ability to:
> - Apply cost estimation techniques (analogous, parametric, bottom-up, three-point).
> - Construct a cost aggregation and S-curve.
> - Compute Earned Value Management (EVM) indicators such as **CV, SV, CPI, SPI, EAC, ETC, VAC, TCPI**.

---

## 1.2 Intuitive Analogy — "The Wedding Planner" View

Imagine you are planning a destination wedding with a total budget of **₹10,00,000** spread across 12 months. You don't pay everything upfront; instead, you create a **monthly spend plan**:

| Month | Planned Spend (₹) | Why? |
|---|---|---|
| Jan | 50,000 | Booking venue |
| Feb | 1,00,000 | Advance for catering |
| ... | ... | ... |
| Dec | 3,00,000 | Final payment |

This **monthly spend plan is the Cost Schedule**. It tells you:
- **How much** should be spent each month (Planned Value / BCWS).
- **How much** you *actually* spent (Actual Cost / ACWP).
- **How much work** you actually completed for that spend (Earned Value / BCWP).

If by March you were supposed to spend ₹3,00,000 (cumulative) but you have actually spent ₹4,00,000 and only completed work equivalent to ₹2,50,000 — you are **over budget AND behind schedule**. This is exactly what EVM metrics tell you in a software project.

> [!NOTE]
> **Plain English Summary:** Cost Schedule = "How much money, when?" — combined with "How much work have we actually done for that money?"

---

## 1.3 Key Terms and Standard Metrics (Mandatory Bold Highlights)

| Term | Expansion | Standard Metric Symbol |
|---|---|---|
| **PV** | Planned Value (Budgeted Cost of Work Scheduled) | **BCWS** |
| **EV** | Earned Value (Budgeted Cost of Work Performed) | **BCWP** |
| **AC** | Actual Cost (Actual Cost of Work Performed) | **ACWP** |
| **BAC** | Budget at Completion | **BAC** |
| **CV** | Cost Variance | **EV − AC** |
| **SV** | Schedule Variance | **EV − PV** |
| **CPI** | Cost Performance Index | **EV / AC** |
| **SPI** | Schedule Performance Index | **EV / PV** |
| **EAC** | Estimate at Completion | **BAC / CPI** |
| **ETC** | Estimate to Complete | **EAC − AC** |
| **VAC** | Variance at Completion | **BAC − EAC** |
| **TCPI** | To-Complete Performance Index | **(BAC − EV) / (BAC − AC)** |

The thresholds used by KTU board examiners:
- **CPI ≥ 1.0** → Under or on budget ✅
- **CPI < 1.0** → Over budget ❌
- **SPI ≥ 1.0** → Ahead or on schedule ✅
- **SPI < 1.0** → Behind schedule ❌

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

# Cost Schedule — Deep Theoretical Analysis

## 2.1 The Cost-Schedule Management Process Flow

The KTU 2024 model for managing project cost and schedule is an **iterative, four-stage closed-loop cycle**:

1. **Cost Estimation** → Produce initial cost estimates using estimation techniques.
2. **Cost Aggregation** → Roll up activity costs into work packages and then to the project control account level.
3. **Cost Scheduling (Time-Phased Budget)** → Distribute the aggregated costs over the project schedule calendar to produce the **cost baseline** (S-curve).
4. **Cost Control (EVM Monitoring)** → Compare actuals against the baseline and compute EVM metrics; feed corrective actions back into Stage 1.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why closed-loop?** Because software projects rarely execute exactly as planned. The feedback from EVM (Stage 4) triggers revised estimates (Stage 1), forming a continuous improvement loop — a core NEP 2020 outcome emphasizing **critical thinking and adaptive decision-making**.

---

## 2.2 Cost Estimation Techniques (Theory)

### 2.2.1 Analogous (Top-Down) Estimation
Uses historical data from similar, completed projects. Fast but low accuracy (**−25% to +75%**, per PMBOK). Best in **early** project phases when scope is vague.

### 2.2.2 Parametric Estimation
Uses statistical relationships between cost and one or more project parameters (e.g., **KLOC**, **Function Points**).

**Basic COCOMO formula (organic mode):**

$$E = a \cdot (\text{KLOC})^b$$

where **E** = effort in **person-months**, and **a, b** are mode-dependent constants.

### 2.2.3 Bottom-Up Estimation
Estimates cost of each work package, then aggregates upward. Most accurate (**−5% to +10%**) but time-consuming; suited for **detailed design** phase.

### 2.2.4 Three-Point Estimation (PERT Beta Distribution)

$$E = \frac{O + 4M + P}{6}$$

where **O** = Optimistic, **M** = Most Likely, **P** = Pessimistic estimate. Standard deviation:

$$\sigma = \frac{P - O}{6}$$

### 2.2.5 Reserve Analysis
Adds **management reserves** (for unknown unknowns) and **contingency reserves** (for known unknowns) to the cost baseline.

---

## 2.3 Cost Aggregation and the S-Curve

**Cost aggregation** is the process of summing the costs of work packages according to their WBS hierarchy up to the project level.

**Cost scheduling** then distributes this aggregated cost along the project timeline. When the cumulative cost is plotted against time, the resulting graph exhibits an **S-shape**:

- **Slow start** (requirements, design phase — few resources deployed).
- **Steep middle** (coding, integration — peak manpower).
- **Tail-off** (testing, deployment — resources ramp down).

This curve is the **Cost Baseline** and serves as the reference for EVM.

---

## 2.4 Earned Value Management (EVM) — The Heart of Cost-Schedule Control

EVM integrates **scope, schedule, and cost** into one framework. The three primary parameters form a triangle:

| Parameter | Question Answered |
|---|---|
| **PV (BCWS)** | "How much work *should* have been done by now, in terms of budget?" |
| **EV (BCWP)** | "How much work *has actually* been done, in terms of budget?" |
| **AC (ACWP)** | "How much money *has actually* been spent?" |

**Variances** measure deviation; **Indices** measure efficiency.

---

## 2.5 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Board Examiner's High-Yield Formula Table** — memorize before attempting any EVM numerical.

| # | Formula Name | Equation | Interpretation |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Cost Variance | $\text{CV} = \text{EV} - \text{AC}$ | Negative ⇒ Over budget |
| 2 | Schedule Variance | $\text{SV} = \text{EV} - \text{PV}$ | Negative ⇒ Behind schedule |
| 3 | Cost Performance Index | $\text{CPI} = \dfrac{\text{EV}}{\text{AC}}$ | $\vert \cdot \vert < 1$ ⇒ Inefficient |
| 4 | Schedule Performance Index | $\text{SPI} = \dfrac{\text{EV}}{\text{PV}}$ | $\vert \cdot \vert < 1$ ⇒ Slow progress |
| 5 | Estimate at Completion | $\text{EAC} = \dfrac{\text{BAC}}{\text{CPI}}$ | Forecasted total cost |
| 6 | Estimate to Complete | $\text{ETC} = \text{EAC} - \text{AC}$ | Money still needed |
| 7 | Variance at Completion | $\text{VAC} = \text{BAC} - \text{EAC}$ | Expected cost overrun/savings |
| 8 | To-Complete Performance Index | $\text{TCPI} = \dfrac{\text{BAC} - \text{EV}}{\text{BAC} - \text{AC}}$ | Required future efficiency |

> **Note on LaTeX in tables:** The vertical bar inside $\vert \cdot \vert$ is written using `\vert` (or `\mid`) to comply with markdown table syntax rules — never use the raw pipe `|` symbol inside a table cell.

---

## 2.6 Engineering Utility — Where Is This Used?

- **Software outsourcing contracts:** Fixed-price contracts use **EAC** to forecast final payout; clients track **CPI** to detect overruns early.
- **Agile/Scrum at scale (SAFe):** EVM principles are mapped to **burn-down/burn-up charts** in PI planning.
- **Defense/Aerospace software (DRDO, ISRO):** Mandated by quality standards (CMMI-DEV, ISO 9001) for milestone billing.
- **IT Service Management (ITIL):** Budget tracking for change releases and incident resolution projects.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->

# Step-by-Step Derivations and Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Exhaustive Derivation — Three-Point PERT Estimate

The PERT Beta Distribution technique assumes the optimistic and pessimistic estimates are **6 standard deviations** apart from the most-likely estimate. The mathematical justification:

Assume a triangular probability distribution with:
- Optimistic value $O$ at probability density zero (left edge),
- Most Likely value $M$ at the mode (peak),
- Pessimistic value $P$ at the right edge.

The expected value $E$ of a triangular distribution is given by:

$$E = \frac{O + M + P}{3}$$

PERT modifies this weighting. Empirical research showed that the **most-likely estimate is 4× more likely to occur** than either extreme. Therefore:

$$
\begin{aligned}
E_{\text{PERT}} &= \frac{1 \cdot O + 4 \cdot M + 1 \cdot P}{1 + 4 + 1} \\
&= \frac{O + 4M + P}{6}
\end{aligned}
$$

The variance of a triangular distribution is:

$$\sigma^2 = \frac{(P - O)^2}{36}$$

Hence, the standard deviation is:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\sigma &= \sqrt{\frac{(P - O)^2}{36}} \\
&= \frac{P - O}{6}
\end{aligned}
$$

The 95% confidence interval for the true estimate is then:

$$E \pm 2\sigma = \frac{O + 4M + P}{6} \pm \frac{P - O}{3}$$

### Worked Example (3 marks standard KTU short numerical)

> A software module has the following estimates: O = 4 person-weeks, M = 10 person-weeks, P = 22 person-weeks. Compute the expected duration and 95% confidence interval.

**Solution — Step 1:** Compute the expected value $E$.

$$
\begin{aligned}
E &= \frac{O + 4M + P}{6} \\
&= \frac{4 + 4(10) + 22}{6} \\
&= \frac{4 + 40 + 22}{6} \\
&= \frac{66}{6} \\
&= 11 \text{ person-weeks}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Solution — Step 2:** Compute the standard deviation $\sigma$.

$$
\begin{aligned}
\sigma &= \frac{P - O}{6} \\
&= \frac{22 - 4}{6} \\
&= \frac{18}{6} \\
&= 3 \text{ person-weeks}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Solution — Step 3:** 95% confidence interval.

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Lower bound} &= E - 2\sigma = 11 - 6 = 5 \text{ person-weeks} \\
\text{Upper bound} &= E + 2\sigma = 11 + 6 = 17 \text{ person-weeks}
\end{aligned}
$$

---

## 3.2 Exhaustive Derivation — EVM EAC Equation

The most commonly used EAC formula assumes that **the cost performance observed so far (CPI) will continue to apply** for the remaining work.

By definition:

$$\text{CPI} = \frac{\text{Earned Value}}{\text{Actual Cost}} = \frac{\text{EV}}{\text{AC}}$$

We want to forecast the **total cost of the project** (Estimate at Completion, EAC). The total cost can be split as:

$$\text{EAC} = \text{Actual Cost so far} + \text{Estimated cost to complete remaining work}$$

The remaining work is **BAC − EV** (budget remaining). If we expect future cost efficiency equal to current CPI, then the cost of remaining work equals the remaining budget divided by CPI:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{EAC} &= \text{AC} + \frac{\text{BAC} - \text{EV}}{\text{CPI}} \\
&= \text{AC} + \frac{\text{BAC} - \text{EV}}{\text{EV}/\text{AC}} \\
&= \text{AC} + \frac{(\text{BAC} - \text{EV}) \cdot \text{AC}}{\text{EV}} \\
&= \text{AC} \cdot \left( 1 + \frac{\text{BAC} - \text{EV}}{\text{EV}} \right) \\
&= \text{AC} \cdot \frac{\text{BAC}}{\text{EV}} \\
&= \frac{\text{AC} \cdot \text{BAC}}{\text{EV}} \\
&= \frac{\text{BAC}}{\text{EV}/\text{AC}} \\
&= \frac{\text{BAC}}{\text{CPI}}
\end{aligned}
$$

Hence the canonical form:

$$\boxed{\text{EAC} = \frac{\text{BAC}}{\text{CPI}}}$$

### Worked EVM Numerical (14-mark KTU standard problem)

> **Problem:** A software project has BAC = ₹ 2,00,000. At the end of month 4, status data are: PV = ₹ 80,000; EV = ₹ 70,000; AC = ₹ 90,000.
>
> Compute: (a) CV, SV, CPI, SPI, and project health status. (b) EAC, ETC, VAC, TCPI and write a 3-line interpretation suitable for a steering committee report.

#### Part (a) — Variances and Indices

**Step 1:** Cost Variance.

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{CV} &= \text{EV} - \text{AC} \\
&= 70{,}000 - 90{,}000 \\
&= -20{,}000 \text{ INR}
\end{aligned}
$$

Interpretation: Negative CV ⇒ **₹ 20,000 over budget**.

**Step 2:** Schedule Variance.

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{SV} &= \text{EV} - \text{PV} \\
&= 70{,}000 - 80{,}000 \\
&= -10{,}000 \text{ INR}
\end{aligned}
$$

Interpretation: Negative SV ⇒ **₹ 10,000 worth of work behind schedule**.

**Step 3:** Cost Performance Index.

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{CPI} &= \frac{\text{EV}}{\text{AC}} \\
&= \frac{70{,}000}{90{,}000} \\
&= 0.778 \text{ (approximately)}
\end{aligned}
$$

Interpretation: For every **₹ 1 spent**, only **₹ 0.778 of work** is completed. The project is **cost-inefficient**.

**Step 4:** Schedule Performance Index.

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{SPI} &= \frac{\text{EV}}{\text{PV}} \\
&= \frac{70{,}000}{80{,}000} \\
&= 0.875
\end{aligned}
$$

Interpretation: The project is progressing at only **87.5% of the planned rate** — **behind schedule**.

#### Part (b) — Forecasts

**Step 5:** Estimate at Completion.

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{EAC} &= \frac{\text{BAC}}{\text{CPI}} \\
&= \frac{2{,}00{,}000}{0.778} \\
&\approx 2{,}57{,}069 \text{ INR}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 6:** Estimate to Complete.

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{ETC} &= \text{EAC} - \text{AC} \\
&= 2{,}57{,}069 - 90{,}000 \\
&\approx 1{,}67{,}069 \text{ INR}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 7:** Variance at Completion.

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{VAC} &= \text{BAC} - \text{EAC} \\
&= 2{,}00{,}000 - 2{,}57{,}069 \\
&\approx -57{,}069 \text{ INR}
\end{aligned}
$$

Negative VAC ⇒ **Expected cost overrun of about ₹ 57,069**.

**Step 8:** To-Complete Performance Index.

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{TCPI} &= \frac{\text{BAC} - \text{EV}}{\text{BAC} - \text{AC}} \\
&= \frac{2{,}00{,}000 - 70{,}000}{2{,}00{,}000 - 90{,}000} \\
&= \frac{1{,}30{,}000}{1{,}10{,}000} \\
&\approx 1.182
\end{aligned}
$$

Since TCPI > 1.0, achieving the original BAC requires an **unrealistically high future efficiency** of **₹ 1.182 of work per ₹ 1 spent**. This is a strong **red flag** for management.

---

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Decision Rules

| Condition | Diagnosis | Recommended Action |
|---|---|---|
| CV ≥ 0 and SV ≥ 0 | **On track** ✅ | Continue monitoring |
| CV < 0 and SV < 0 | **Critical** ❌ | Re-baseline or invoke recovery plan |
| CV < 0 and SV ≥ 0 | Over budget, on time | Improve cost efficiency; check scope creep |
| CV ≥ 0 and SV < 0 | On budget, behind schedule | Add resources or reduce scope |
| CPI × SPI < 0.5 | Critical | Consider project cancellation |
| TCPI > 1.2 | Unrealistic | Re-baseline BAC |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

# Structural Diagrams and Schematics

## 4.1 Cost-Schedule Management Process Block Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Start of Project] --> B[Cost Estimation]
    B --> B1[Analogous]
    B --> B2[Parametric - COCOMO]
    B --> B3[Bottom-Up]
    B --> B4[Three-Point PERT]
    B1 --> C[Cost Aggregation]
    B2 --> C
    B3 --> C
    B4 --> C
    C --> D[Cost Scheduling - Time-Phased Budget]
    D --> E[Cost Baseline - S Curve]
    E --> F[Project Execution - Collecting PV, EV, AC]
    F --> G[Compute EVM Metrics]
    G --> G1[CV and CPI - Cost Health]
    G --> G2[SV and SPI - Schedule Health]
    G1 --> H{Performance Acceptable?}
    G2 --> H
    H -- Yes --> I[Continue Monitoring]
    H -- No --> J[Corrective Action]
    J --> K[Update Estimates]
    K --> B
    I --> L[Project Closure]
```

## 4.2 EVM Triangle — The Conceptual Model

```mermaid
graph LR
    PV[PV - BCWS - Planned Value] -->|Baseline| EV[EV - BCWP - Earned Value]
    AC[AC - ACWP - Actual Cost] -->|Performance| EV
    EV --> CV[CV - Cost Variance]
    EV --> SV[SV - Schedule Variance]
    EV --> CPI[CPI - Cost Performance Index]
    EV --> SPI[SPI - Schedule Performance Index]
    CPI --> EAC[EAC - Estimate at Completion]
    SPI --> EAC
    EAC --> ETC[ETC - Estimate to Complete]
    EAC --> VAC[VAC - Variance at Completion]
    EV --> TCPI[TCPI - To Complete Performance Index]
```

## 4.3 Typical S-Curve Cost Baseline

```mermaid
xychart-beta
    title "Cumulative Cost Baseline S-Curve"
    x-axis [M1, M2, M3, M4, M5, M6, M7, M8, M9, M10, M11, M12]
    y-axis "Cumulative Cost (INR Lakhs)" 0 --> 100
    line [2, 6, 14, 26, 42, 60, 76, 87, 93, 97, 99, 100]
```

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    Start[Month 1 - Slow Start] --> Mid[Months 3 to 9 - Peak Spend]
    Mid --> End[Months 10 to 12 - Tail Off]
    Start -.-> Slow[Requirements and Design]
    Mid -.-> Build[Coding and Integration]
    End -.-> Test[Testing and Deployment]
```

## 4.4 Decision Logic for EVM Indicators

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Start[Compute CPI and SPI] --> A{CPI greater than 1?}
    A -- Yes --> B[Under Budget]
    A -- No --> C[Over Budget]
    Start --> D{SPI greater than 1?}
    D -- Yes --> E[Ahead of Schedule]
    D -- No --> F[Behind Schedule]
    B --> G{Final Decision}
    C --> G
    E --> G
    F --> G
    G -- All Healthy --> H[Project Green]
    G -- Mixed --> I[Project Yellow - Take Corrective Action]
    G -- All Critical --> J[Project Red - Re-Baseline]
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->

# KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:**
> 1. **Sign convention trap:** In EVM, a **negative CV or SV means bad news** (over budget / behind schedule). Students often write "CV = −20,000 so project is healthy" — instant 1-mark deduction.
> 2. **Unit mismatch trap:** PV, EV, AC, BAC must all be in the **same currency unit**. Mixing thousands and lakhs causes cascading errors.
> 3. **COCOMO parameter trap:** Forgetting mode-specific constants (**a, b**) is the #1 reason students lose marks in estimation questions.
> 4. **SPI ≠ Time progress:** SPI is **schedule efficiency**, not elapsed time. Writing "SPI = 0.875 means we are 87.5% through the project" is incorrect.
> 5. **Three-point PERT trap:** The mean is **NOT** simply the arithmetic mean of O, M, P — the formula is **(O + 4M + P) / 6**. Examiners explicitly check this.

---

## Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks each)

### Q1. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]
**Define Cost Schedule. List any four cost estimation techniques used in software project management.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**Cost Schedule** is a time-phased budget plan that distributes the aggregated project cost over the project timeline, providing a baseline for monitoring both cost and schedule performance. *(1 mark)*

Four cost estimation techniques: *(2 marks — 0.5 each)*
1. **Analogous (Top-Down) Estimation** — uses historical data from similar past projects.
2. **Parametric Estimation** — uses statistical models such as COCOMO using KLOC or Function Points.
3. **Bottom-Up Estimation** — estimates each work package and aggregates upward.
4. **Three-Point (PERT) Estimation** — uses Optimistic, Most Likely, and Pessimistic values.

---

### Q2. [KTU University Exam — May 2024]
**Differentiate between Cost Variance (CV) and Schedule Variance (SV). What do negative values indicate?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Aspect | Cost Variance (CV) | Schedule Variance (SV) |
|---|---|---|
| Formula | EV − AC | EV − PV |
| Measures | Cost performance | Schedule performance |
| Negative Value Means | Over budget (spent more than work completed) | Behind schedule (less work done than planned) |
| Positive Value Means | Under budget | Ahead of schedule |

**Negative values** of CV and SV indicate **unfavourable** performance — the project is over budget and/or behind schedule. *(1 mark)*

---

## Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks each, Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks) — EVM Computation

**[KTU University Exam — Dec 2022, Model Paper KTU 2024 Scheme]**

A software project has a **Budget at Completion (BAC) = ₹ 15,00,000** scheduled over 10 months. At the end of **month 5**, the project manager collects the following status data:

- **PV (BCWS) = ₹ 6,00,000**
- **EV (BCWP) = ₹ 5,25,000**
- **AC (ACWP) = ₹ 6,50,000**

**(a)** Compute the Cost Variance (CV), Schedule Variance (SV), Cost Performance Index (CPI) and Schedule Performance Index (SPI). State the health of the project in terms of cost and schedule. *(7 marks)*

**(b)** Calculate EAC, ETC, VAC and TCPI. Write a **3-line interpretation** suitable for inclusion in a steering committee report. *(7 marks)*

---

#### Model Solution — Part (a)

**Step 1:** Compute CV.

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{CV} &= \text{EV} - \text{AC} \\
&= 5{,}25{,}000 - 6{,}50{,}000 \\
&= -1{,}25{,}000 \text{ INR}
\end{aligned}
$$

*(1 mark)*

**Step 2:** Compute SV.

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{SV} &= \text{EV} - \text{PV} \\
&= 5{,}25{,}000 - 6{,}00{,}000 \\
&= -75{,}000 \text{ INR}
\end{aligned}
$$

*(1 mark)*

**Step 3:** Compute CPI.

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{CPI} &= \frac{\text{EV}}{\text{AC}} \\
&= \frac{5{,}25{,}000}{6{,}50{,}000} \\
&= 0.808
\end{aligned}
$$

*(1 mark)*

**Step 4:** Compute SPI.

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{SPI} &= \frac{\text{EV}}{\text{PV}} \\
&= \frac{5{,}25{,}000}{6{,}00{,}000} \\
&= 0.875
\end{aligned}
$$

*(1 mark)*

**Step 5:** Project health interpretation. *(3 marks)*

- **CV = −1,25,000** is negative ⇒ the project is **over budget by ₹ 1,25,000**.
- **SV = −75,000** is negative ⇒ the project is **behind schedule by ₹ 75,000 worth of work**.
- **CPI = 0.808 < 1.0** ⇒ for every **₹ 1.00 spent**, only **₹ 0.808 of work** is being produced — **cost inefficient**.
- **SPI = 0.875 < 1.0** ⇒ work is progressing at only **87.5% of the planned rate** — **schedule slippage**.
- **Overall Health:** Project is in the **Critical (Red)** quadrant — corrective action is mandatory.

---

#### Model Solution — Part (b)

**Step 6:** Compute EAC.

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{EAC} &= \frac{\text{BAC}}{\text{CPI}} \\
&= \frac{15{,}00{,}000}{0.808} \\
&\approx 18{,}56{,}436 \text{ INR}
\end{aligned}
$$

*(1.5 marks)*

**Step 7:** Compute ETC.

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{ETC} &= \text{EAC} - \text{AC} \\
&= 18{,}56{,}436 - 6{,}50{,}000 \\
&\approx 12{,}06{,}436 \text{ INR}
\end{aligned}
$$

*(1.5 marks)*

**Step 8:** Compute VAC.

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{VAC} &= \text{BAC} - \text{EAC} \\
&= 15{,}00{,}000 - 18{,}56{,}436 \\
&\approx -3{,}56{,}436 \text{ INR}
\end{aligned}
$$

*(1.5 marks)*

**Step 9:** Compute TCPI.

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{TCPI} &= \frac{\text{BAC} - \text{EV}}{\text{BAC} - \text{AC}} \\
&= \frac{15{,}00{,}000 - 5{,}25{,}000}{15{,}00{,}000 - 6{,}50{,}000} \\
&= \frac{9{,}75{,}000}{8{,}50{,}000} \\
&\approx 1.147
\end{aligned}
$$

*(1.5 marks)*

**Step 10:** 3-line Steering Committee Interpretation. *(1 mark)*

1. The project is forecast to overrun its budget by approximately **₹ 3.56 lakhs** (VAC = −3,56,436).
2. To finish within the original BAC, future cost efficiency must reach **TCPI = 1.147**, which is **unrealistically high** and likely unachievable.
3. **Recommendation:** Initiate a formal change request to re-baseline the BAC, or implement scope reduction plus accelerated delivery plan within 30 days.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — Cost Estimation & Scheduling

**[KTU University Exam — May 2023, Model Paper KTU 2024 Scheme]**

**(a)** Explain the **three-point PERT estimation technique**. A software project has the following estimates for its modules: *(7 marks)*

| Module | Optimistic (O) | Most Likely (M) | Pessimistic (P) |
|---|---|---|---|
| Login | 3 | 5 | 13 |
| Payment | 4 | 8 | 18 |
| Reports | 2 | 4 | 12 |

Compute the **expected duration** and **standard deviation** for each module. Which module is the **most risky**?

**(b)** Construct the **cost baseline (S-curve)** for a project with the following monthly planned expenditure (in ₹ thousands): 20, 40, 80, 120, 140, 130, 100, 70, 50, 30, 20, 10. Calculate the **cumulative PV** at the end of months 3, 6, 9, and 12. Mention any **two properties** of the S-curve. *(7 marks)*

---

#### Model Solution — Part (a)

**Theory (2 marks):**
The **three-point PERT technique** uses three estimates for every activity: **Optimistic (O)**, **Most Likely (M)**, and **Pessimistic (P)**. The expected duration is computed using the weighted formula **E = (O + 4M + P) / 6**, giving four times the weight to the most likely value. The standard deviation is **σ = (P − O) / 6**.

**Step 1: Login module** *(1.5 marks)*

$$
\begin{aligned}
E_{\text{Login}} &= \frac{3 + 4(5) + 13}{6} = \frac{36}{6} = 6 \text{ days} \\
\sigma_{\text{Login}} &= \frac{13 - 3}{6} = \frac{10}{6} = 1.67 \text{ days}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2: Payment module** *(1.5 marks)*

$$
\begin{aligned}
E_{\text{Payment}} &= \frac{4 + 4(8) + 18}{6} = \frac{54}{6} = 9 \text{ days} \\
\sigma_{\text{Payment}} &= \frac{18 - 4}{6} = \frac{14}{6} = 2.33 \text{ days}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3: Reports module** *(1.5 marks)*

$$
\begin{aligned}
E_{\text{Reports}} &= \frac{2 + 4(4) + 12}{6} = \frac{30}{6} = 5 \text{ days} \\
\sigma_{\text{Reports}} &= \frac{12 - 2}{6} = \frac{10}{6} = 1.67 \text{ days}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4: Risk ranking** *(0.5 mark)*

The **Payment module** has the highest standard deviation (2.33 days) ⇒ it is the **most risky** module.

---

#### Model Solution — Part (b)

**Step 1: Cumulative PV calculation.** *(4 marks — 1 each)*

| Month | Planned (₹ '000) | Cumulative PV (₹ '000) |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | 20 | 20 |
| 2 | 40 | 60 |
| 3 | 80 | **140** |
| 4 | 120 | 260 |
| 5 | 140 | 400 |
| 6 | 130 | **530** |
| 7 | 100 | 630 |
| 8 | 70 | 700 |
| 9 | 50 | **750** |
| 10 | 30 | 780 |
| 11 | 20 | 800 |
| 12 | 10 | **810** |

**Step 2: Plot the S-curve** — cumulative cost (Y-axis) vs. time (X-axis). *(1 mark)*

The curve starts slow (month 1–2), rises steeply through months 3–6, peaks at month 5 (monthly spend ₹ 140 K), and tapers down through month 12 — producing the classical **S-shape**.

**Step 3: Two properties of the S-curve** *(2 marks — 1 each)*

1. The S-curve has an **inflection point near the project midpoint**, where cumulative spend transitions from convex (acceleration) to concave (deceleration).
2. The **slope at any point equals the planned monthly spend rate**; it is the derivative of the cumulative cost function.
3. (Bonus) The area under the S-curve (when drawn correctly) is not directly used; rather, the **curve itself is the cost baseline** for EVM measurement.

---

## Topic Recap and Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Cost Schedule**

- **Definition:** Cost Schedule = time-phased budget integrating cost and schedule.
- **Estimation Techniques:** Analogous, Parametric (COCOMO), Bottom-Up, Three-Point (PERT), Reserve Analysis.
- **Three-Point Formula:** $E = (O + 4M + P) / 6$;  $\sigma = (P - O) / 6$.
- **COCOMO Basic:** $E = a \cdot (\text{KLOC})^b$ with mode-specific constants.
- **EVM Core Parameters:** **PV (BCWS)**, **EV (BCWP)**, **AC (ACWP)**, **BAC**.
- **Variances:** $\text{CV} = \text{EV} - \text{AC}$ ;  $\text{SV} = \text{EV} - \text{PV}$.
- **Indices:** $\text{CPI} = \text{EV} / \text{AC}$ ;  $\text{SPI} = \text{EV} / \text{PV}$.
- **Forecasts:** $\text{EAC} = \text{BAC} / \text{CPI}$ ;  $\text{ETC} = \text{EAC} - \text{AC}$ ;  $\text{VAC} = \text{BAC} - \text{EAC}$.
- **Future Efficiency:** $\text{TCPI} = (\text{BAC} - \text{EV}) / (\text{BAC} - \text{AC})$.
- **S-Curve:** Slow start → steep middle → tail off; this is the **cost baseline**.
- **Sign Convention:** Negative CV/SV = bad; Positive CV/SV = good. CPI/SPI > 1 = good; < 1 = bad.
- **Decision Rule:** TCPI > 1.2 ⇒ re-baseline; CV < 0 AND SV < 0 ⇒ red zone.
- **KTU Favourite Numerical Format:** Always a 14-mark EVM problem with sub-parts (a) variances/indices and (b) forecasts/interpretation.
- **Examiner Hot-Trap Questions:** Sign convention, three-point formula misuse, mixing TCPI and CPI, missing units.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Creating the Framework
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEITT633 - SOFTWARE PROJECT MANAGEMENT
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Creating the Framework

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

In the context of Software Project Management (SPM), **Creating the Framework** refers to the systematic establishment of the underlying structure, processes, and controls that will guide a software project from inception to delivery. According to the standard KTU-referenced Software Engineering curriculum (aligned with Pressman's framework), the SPM framework is a *logical, decomposable, and measurable* architecture that defines **what work must be done, how it will be done, who will do it, when it will be done, and why it is being done**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Definition (Verbatim from Module 4):**
> *Creating the Framework is the activity that establishes the software project management process by defining scope, selecting a software process model, decomposing the work, identifying the task set, and integrating all management activities (estimation, scheduling, risk, quality) into a coherent, controllable system.*

The framework is built upon three foundational pillars:

| Pillar | Purpose | KTU Reference |
|---|---|---|
| **Scope Definition** | Establishes boundaries of the project | 4.1 |
| **Process Model Selection** | Chooses the lifecycle paradigm (Waterfall, Incremental, Evolutionary, Spiral, Agile) | 4.2 |
| **Management Process Decomposition** | Breaks the lifecycle into manageable *phases → activities → tasks* | 4.3 |

## 1.2 Intuitive Analogy — The "House Construction" Model

Imagine you are going to build a house. Before laying a single brick, you must:

1. **Decide the scope** — How many floors? 2BHK or 4BHK? (This is *Project Scope*.)
2. **Choose a building method** — Will you build floor-by-floor (Waterfall) or assemble pre-fabricated units (Incremental)? (This is *Process Model Selection*.)
3. **Create a Work Breakdown Structure (WBS)** — Divide the entire house into foundation, walls, roof, plumbing, electrical, and painting. (This is *Decomposition*.)
4. **Estimate cost, time, and effort** — Bricks, cement, labour days. (This is *Estimation*.)
5. **Schedule the work** — Foundation first, walls next, roof after. (This is *Project Scheduling*.)

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Intuition:** A software project is identical in principle. The "Creating the Framework" phase is the *architectural drawing* for the project. Without it, the project becomes a chaotic, unmanageable, and unmeasurable effort.

## 1.3 The W5HH Principle (Pressman's Foundational Framework)

The framework is built around Pressman's **W5HH Principle** — a set of seven questions that *every* software project manager must answer:

- **Why** — Is the system being developed? (Business justification)
- **What** — Will be done? (Deliverables and scope)
- **When** — Will it be done? (Schedule)
- **Who** — Will do it? (Team, stakeholders)
- **Where** — Are the organization, customer, and development locations?
- **How** — Will the work be performed technically and managerially? (Process, technology, methodology)
- **How much** — Will it cost, in effort, money, and resources?

> [!TIP]
> **KTU Exam Tip:** The W5HH Principle is a **guaranteed short-answer question** in KTU Module 4. Memorize all seven W's verbatim.

## 1.4 Why the Framework Matters — Three Driving Forces

A formal SPM framework is needed to address three intrinsic challenges of software:

1. **Invisibility** — Software is intangible; you cannot "see" progress like a building under construction.
2. **Complexity** — Software systems have exponential inter-dependencies.
3. **Conformity** — Software must conform to external constraints (human, organizational, hardware, regulations).

The framework provides the **visibility, tractability, and controllability** required to manage these forces.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Five-Step Process for Creating the Framework

The KTU 2024 Scheme expects students to articulate the framework creation as a **5-step logical process**. Below is the high-yield analytical breakdown:

### Step 1: Define the Scope of the Project (Project Scope Statement)

The **Scope** answers: *"What is the system doing, and what is it NOT doing?"*

A scope statement must include:
- **Functional Scope** — Features and capabilities
- **Performance Scope** — Speed, throughput, response time
- **Constraint Scope** — Hardware, OS, regulatory, budget limits
- **Exclusion List** — Explicitly stated "out-of-scope" items

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **100% Rule of Scope:** A scope statement is valid only if it covers **100%** of the work — no more, no less. Any feature above 100% is *scope creep* (a KTU high-frequency term).

### Step 2: Select the Appropriate Software Process Model

The **Process Model** is the abstraction of the development lifecycle. KTU lists the following major models:

| Model | Best Use Case | Risk Profile |
|---|---|---|
| **Waterfall** | Well-defined, stable requirements | High late-discovery risk |
| **Incremental** | Modular system, partial delivery needed | Integration risk |
| **Evolutionary (Prototyping)** | Unclear or changing requirements | Scope creep risk |
| **Spiral** | High-risk, mission-critical systems | Cost overhead risk |
| **Agile (Scrum/XP)** | Dynamic, customer-driven delivery | Coordination risk |

The framework ties the **process model** to the **management process** — a management activity exists for *every* process activity.

### Step 3: Decompose the Process (Process Decomposition)

The chosen process model is broken into a hierarchy:

$$\text{Process Model} \rightarrow \text{Phases} \rightarrow \text{Activities} \rightarrow \text{Tasks} \rightarrow \text{WBS Elements}$$

This decomposition yields the **Activity Set (A)** and the **Task Set (T)**.

### Step 4: Identify the Task Set (WBS Construction)

Each task is described using a **Task Template** with the following attributes (Pressman):

- **Task Description** (What is done?)
- **Predecessor Task(s)** (What must be done first?)
- **Owner / Role** (Who does it?)
- **Estimated Effort, Duration, Resources** (How much, how long?)
- **Deliverable** (Output)
- **Entry / Exit Criteria** (Quality gates)

> [!NOTE]
> **WBS Definition:** A *Work Breakdown Structure* is a deliverable-oriented hierarchical decomposition of the total scope of work to be carried out by the project team. The deepest level of WBS is called the **Work Package**.

### Step 5: Establish Management Integration

All project management activities (estimation, scheduling, risk, quality, configuration) are integrated. This is the **"closing the loop"** step where the framework becomes operational.

## 2.2 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

Although *Creating the Framework* is largely conceptual, it directly interfaces with effort estimation. The following table contains KTU's required formulas:

| # | Formula | Description | Unit / Domain |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $E = a \times \text{KLOC}^{b}$ | Basic COCOMO Effort Estimation | Person-Months |
| 2 | $D = c \times E^{d}$ | Basic COCOMO Development Time | Months |
| 3 | $E_{adj} = E \times \prod_{i=1}^{17} EAF_i$ | Adjusted Effort (with 17 cost drivers) | Person-Months |
| 4 | $\text{SS} = \frac{1 + 2 + \sum (1 \text{ to } 5)}{100} \times 100\%$ | WBS Validation: Sum of Sub-Scope percentages = 100% | Percentage |
| 5 | $\text{SPI} = \frac{EV}{PV}$ | Schedule Performance Index (framework monitoring) | Ratio (≥1.0 = good) |
| 6 | $\text{CPI} = \frac{EV}{AC}$ | Cost Performance Index (framework monitoring) | Ratio (≥1.0 = good) |
| 7 | $\text{CV} = EV - AC$ | Cost Variance (output of framework) | Currency |
| 8 | $\text{SV} = EV - PV$ | Schedule Variance (output of framework) | Currency |

> [!WARNING]
> **Markdown Safety Note:** All absolute values and divisions in formulas above use standard math notation. **No vertical pipe `|` characters are used** to prevent markdown table corruption.

## 2.3 Engineering Utility of the Framework

The framework serves three real-world engineering utilities:

1. **Risk Reduction** — Decomposition exposes risk at the task level where mitigation is feasible.
2. **Communication Vehicle** — Provides a shared mental model between developer, manager, and customer.
3. **Control Mechanism** — Enables Earned Value Management (EVM) through measurable Work Packages.

In industry, the framework aligns with **PMBOK's 5 Process Groups (Initiating, Planning, Executing, Monitoring & Controlling, Closing)** and is foundational for ISO 9001, CMMI Level 2-3 compliance.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Exhaustive Derivation: From Scope to WBS

Below is the **exhaustive, step-by-step analytical derivation** of the framework, written exactly as expected in a KTU 14-mark question.

### Step A — Start with the W5HH Question Set

The framework is built by answering the W5HH questions in sequence. Formally:

$$F = \text{Answer}(\text{Why}) \cup \text{Answer}(\text{What}) \cup \text{Answer}(\text{When}) \cup \text{Answer}(\text{Who}) \cup \text{Answer}(\text{Where}) \cup \text{Answer}(\text{How}) \cup \text{Answer}(\text{How Much})$$

where $F$ is the *Framework Artifact Set* and $\cup$ denotes the union of all derived artifacts (charter, scope, schedule, budget, etc.).

### Step B — Derive the Scope Statement $S$

Given a customer problem $P$, the scope is defined as:

$$S = \{\text{Functional Features}\} \cup \{\text{Performance Targets}\} \cup \{\text{Constraints}\} - \{\text{Exclusions}\}$$

The scope is validated by the **100% Rule**:

$$\sum_{i=1}^{n} \text{Feature}_{i} = 100\% \text{ of System Capability}$$

Any deviation indicates either *scope creep* (sum > 100%) or *scope gap* (sum < 100%).

### Step C — Select the Process Model $M$

The model $M$ is selected by maximizing a suitability function:

$$M^{*} = \arg\max_{M \in \mathcal{M}} \; U(M)$$

where $\mathcal{M} = \{\text{Waterfall, Incremental, Evolutionary, Spiral, Agile}\}$ and $U(M)$ is the suitability score based on requirements stability, risk, customer involvement, and team experience.

A KTU-typical decision mapping:

$$M = \begin{cases} \text{Waterfall} & \text{if } R_s \geq 0.8 \text{ AND } C_i \leq 0.2 \\ \text{Spiral} & \text{if } \text{Risk} \geq \text{Threshold}_{high} \\ \text{Agile} & \text{if } R_s \leq 0.4 \text{ AND } C_i \geq 0.7 \\ \text{Incremental} & \text{otherwise} \end{cases}$$

where $R_s$ = requirements stability index, $C_i$ = customer involvement ratio.

### Step D — Decompose into Activity Set $A$ and Task Set $T$

The process model $M$ is decomposed through a function $D(\cdot)$:

$$A = D_1(M) \quad \text{(Phases} \rightarrow \text{Activities)}$$

$$T = D_2(A) \quad \text{(Activities} \rightarrow \text{Tasks)}$$

$$W = D_3(T) \quad \text{(Tasks} \rightarrow \text{Work Packages)}$$

A concrete example for a Waterfall model on a 5-phase, 12-activity, 40-task project:

$$M_{\text{waterfall}} = \{\text{Req}, \text{Design}, \text{Code}, \text{Test}, \text{Maintain}\}$$

$$A = \{A_{11}, A_{12}, A_{21}, A_{22}, A_{23}, A_{31}, \ldots, A_{52}\}, \quad \vert A \vert = 12$$

$$T = \{T_{111}, T_{112}, \ldots, T_{52k}\}, \quad \vert T \vert = 40$$

$$W = \{W_{1}, W_{2}, \ldots, W_{40}\} \quad \text{(40 Work Packages)}$$

### Step E — Compute Total Effort Using COCOMO (Integrated Output)

Once WBS is built, total effort is computed by summing across all work packages, then adjusted using COCOMO cost drivers. For a 3-mode COCOMO:

$$E_{basic} = a \times \text{KLOC}^{b}$$

$$E_{adjusted} = E_{basic} \times \prod_{i=1}^{17} EAF_i$$

$$D = c \times E_{adjusted}^{d}$$

For a typical **Organic** mode (small team, familiar environment, simple application): $a = 2.4$, $b = 1.05$, $c = 2.5$, $d = 0.38$.

For a **Semidetached** mode: $a = 3.0$, $b = 1.12$, $c = 2.5$, $d = 0.35$.

For an **Embedded** mode (tight constraints, complex hardware): $a = 3.6$, $b = 1.20$, $c = 2.5$, $d = 0.32$.

**Worked Numerical Example (for KTU 14-mark problem):**

Suppose a project of **KLOC = 10**, in **Organic** mode, with all 17 cost drivers set to nominal (so $EAF = 1.0$):

$$E_{basic} = 2.4 \times 10^{1.05}$$

$$\log E_{basic} = \log 2.4 + 1.05 \times \log 10 = 0.3802 + 1.05 = 1.4302$$

$$E_{basic} = 10^{1.4302} = 26.92 \text{ Person-Months}$$

$$D = 2.5 \times (26.92)^{0.38} = 2.5 \times 3.81 = 9.52 \text{ Months}$$

This $D$ value then feeds back into the **Schedule** component of the W5HH framework, completing the integration loop.

## 3.2 Analytical Matrix: Linking Framework Steps to Outputs

| Framework Step | Input Artifact | Output Artifact | Verification Method |
|---|---|---|---|
| Step 1: Define Scope | Customer problem $P$ | Scope Statement $S$ | 100% Rule check |
| Step 2: Select Model | Scope $S$ | Selected Model $M$ | Suitability scoring |
| Step 3: Decompose Process | Model $M$ | Activity Set $A$ | Lifecycle mapping |
| Step 4: Build Task Set | Activities $A$ | Task Set $T$ + WBS $W$ | WBS validation |
| Step 5: Integrate Management | All artifacts | Final Framework $F$ | EVM baseline | 

> [!NOTE]
> **Memory Anchor for KTU Exam:** Memorize the 5 steps in this exact order — *Scope, Model, Decompose, Task, Integrate*. This is the most frequently asked structure in KTU's 14-mark ESE questions.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Block Diagram: The SPM Framework Creation Process

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A0[Start: Customer Need] --> A1[Step 1: Define Scope]
    A1 --> A2[Step 2: Select Process Model]
    A2 --> A3[Step 3: Decompose into Phases]
    A3 --> A4[Step 4: Identify Activities]
    A4 --> A5[Step 5: Build Task Set and WBS]
    A5 --> A6[Step 6: Estimate Effort and Cost]
    A6 --> A7[Step 7: Build Schedule]
    A7 --> A8[Step 8: Integrate Management Activities]
    A8 --> A9[Final SPM Framework]
    A8 --> A10[Risk Management]
    A8 --> A11[Quality Management]
    A8 --> A12[Configuration Management]
    A10 --> A9
    A11 --> A9
    A12 --> A9
```

## 4.2 Mermaid Hierarchy: WBS Decomposition Structure

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    L0[PROJECT: ECOMMERCE SYSTEM] --> L1A[1. REQUIREMENTS]
    L0 --> L1B[2. DESIGN]
    L0 --> L1C[3. IMPLEMENTATION]
    L0 --> L1D[4. TESTING]
    L0 --> L1E[5. DEPLOYMENT]
    L1A --> L2A1[1.1 Stakeholder Analysis]
    L1A --> L2A2[1.2 SRS Document]
    L1A --> L2A3[1.3 Use Case Models]
    L1B --> L2B1[2.1 High Level Design]
    L1B --> L2B2[2.2 Database Design]
    L1B --> L2B3[2.3 UI and UX Design]
    L1C --> L2C1[3.1 Frontend Coding]
    L1C --> L2C2[3.2 Backend Coding]
    L1C --> L2C3[3.3 API Integration]
    L1D --> L2D1[4.1 Unit Testing]
    L1D --> L2D2[4.2 Integration Testing]
    L1D --> L2D3[4.3 System and UAT]
    L1E --> L2E1[5.1 Server Setup]
    L1E --> L2E2[5.2 Go Live]
    L1E --> L2E3[5.3 Training]
    L2C1 --> L3C1A[Work Package 3.1.1]
    L2C1 --> L3C1B[Work Package 3.1.2]
    L2C2 --> L3C2A[Work Package 3.2.1]
    L2C2 --> L3C2B[Work Package 3.2.2]
```

## 4.3 Mermaid Diagram: W5HH Principle as a Decision Web

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    Q1[WHY: Business Justification] --> F[SPM FRAMEWORK]
    Q2[WHAT: Scope and Deliverables] --> F
    Q3[WHEN: Schedule and Milestones] --> F
    Q4[WHO: Team and Stakeholders] --> F
    Q5[WHERE: Locations and Platforms] --> F
    Q6[HOW: Process and Tools] --> F
    Q7[HOW MUCH: Cost, Effort, Resources] --> F
    F --> OUT1[Project Charter]
    F --> OUT2[WBS]
    F --> OUT3[Schedule]
    F --> OUT4[Budget]
```

## 4.4 Mermaid Diagram: The WBS Validation (100% Rule)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    V0[Validate WBS using 100% Rule] --> V1[List all Level 2 Sub-Scopes]
    V1 --> V2[Compute Sub-Scope Percentages]
    V2 --> V3{Does sum equal 100%?}
    V3 -- YES --> V4[FRAMEWORK VALID]
    V3 -- NO, sum greater than 100 --> V5[SCOPE CREEP DETECTED]
    V3 -- NO, sum less than 100 --> V6[SCOPE GAP DETECTED]
    V5 --> V7[Revise Scope Statement]
    V6 --> V7
    V7 --> V1
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Diagram Interpretation:** The WBS is the *spine* of the framework. The 100% rule is a hard contract — anything that fails it must trigger an immediate revision of the scope statement before any work begins.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

### Question 1: Define the term "Creating the Framework" in Software Project Management. Why is it important? `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

Creating the Framework is the foundational activity in software project management that establishes the overall structure, process, and controls of a project. It involves **defining the scope, selecting an appropriate software process model, decomposing the work into activities and tasks, identifying the task set using a Work Breakdown Structure (WBS), and integrating all management activities** (estimation, scheduling, risk, quality) into a coherent system.

**Importance (1.5 marks):** It provides visibility into an otherwise invisible process, enables measurement and control through work packages, supports communication among stakeholders, and forms the baseline for Earned Value Management. Without a framework, the project lacks structure, traceability, and the ability to detect deviations early.

**Answer Framing:** **Definition (1.5 marks) + Importance (1.5 marks).**

---

### Question 2: State and explain Pressman's W5HH Principle. `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

The **W5HH Principle** is Pressman's framework for capturing the *complete* information needed to manage a software project. It poses seven questions:

- **Why** is the system being developed? (Business case)
- **What** will be done? (Scope and deliverables)
- **When** will it be done? (Schedule and milestones)
- **Who** will do it? (Team, roles, stakeholders)
- **Where** are they located? (Geographic and organizational)
- **How** will the work be performed? (Technical and managerial methodology)
- **How much** will it cost? (Effort, money, resources)

Together, these seven questions cover all planning dimensions. A project manager who can answer all seven has a complete framework. **(0.4 marks per W + 0.2 marks for the summary statement = 3 marks)**

---

## 5.2 PART B — 14-Mark Module Questions with Internal Choice

> **KTU ESE Pattern:** Each question carries 14 marks, typically split as **(a) 7 marks** and **(b) 7 marks**. Module-level internal choice is mandatory.

---

### Question A: (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - May 2023]`

**(a)** Explain the five-step process for *Creating the Framework* in software project management. Discuss each step with a suitable example. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** What is a Work Breakdown Structure (WBS)? Explain the WBS construction with a real-world example for a 5-phase software project. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution for Question A(a) — 7 Marks

**Step 1: Define the Scope (1.5 marks)**

The scope defines the boundaries of the project. For example, for an *Online Examination System*, scope includes features like user registration, question bank, timer, result generation — and excludes features like payment gateway integration.

**Step 2: Select the Process Model (1.5 marks)**

Based on requirements stability and risk, a model is chosen. For a well-defined exam system, **Waterfall** is selected. For an unclear AI-proctored system, **Spiral** would be preferred.

**Step 3: Decompose the Process (1 mark)**

The selected model is broken into phases → activities. Waterfall = {Requirements, Design, Coding, Testing, Maintenance}.

**Step 4: Identify the Task Set / WBS (1.5 marks)**

Each activity is broken into tasks. For example, *Coding Phase* → {Frontend coding, Backend coding, Database scripting, API development}.

**Step 5: Integrate Management Activities (1.5 marks)**

Estimation, scheduling, risk, quality, and configuration plans are integrated with the task set. Effort estimation using COCOMO gives the baseline; scheduling produces the Gantt chart; risk plan identifies top risks; quality plan defines testing standards.

**Example Continuation:** Total effort is calculated as $E = a \times \text{KLOC}^b$, and adjusted using EAFs to produce the final EVM baseline.

---

#### Model Solution for Question A(b) — 7 Marks

**Definition of WBS (2 marks):** A Work Breakdown Structure is a deliverable-oriented hierarchical decomposition of the total scope of work. The lowest level is called the **Work Package**, which is short-duration, measurable, and assignable.

**WBS Construction Steps (3 marks):**
1. Identify the final deliverable(s) — e.g., the deployed exam system.
2. Decompose into major sections — modules, phases.
3. Continue decomposition until work packages are obtained (typically 8–80 hours of effort).
4. Assign WBS codes — e.g., 1.1, 1.2, 2.1.1.
5. Validate using the **100% Rule** — the sum of all sub-elements must equal 100% of the parent.

**Real-World Example (2 marks):** For a 5-phase project — Requirements (1), Design (2), Coding (3), Testing (4), Deployment (5) — each phase is broken into 3–5 sub-activities, and each sub-activity is further broken into 2–4 tasks. The WBS is validated by ensuring all tasks together account for 100% of the project scope.

---

### Question B: (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2022]`

**(a)** Discuss the importance of *Project Scope Statement* in the framework. What is *Scope Creep* and how can it be controlled? **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A software project of estimated 8 KLOC is to be developed using COCOMO (Organic mode). Calculate the basic effort, adjusted effort with 4 cost drivers (RELY=1.0, DATA=0.95, CPLX=1.0, TIME=1.1), and the development time. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution for Question B(a) — 7 Marks

**Importance of Project Scope Statement (3 marks):**
- Acts as the **written contract** between customer and developer.
- Serves as the **baseline** for all subsequent planning (estimation, scheduling, WBS).
- Provides a **reference for change control** — any change must be evaluated against the scope.
- Enables **scope validation** through the 100% rule.

**Scope Creep Definition (2 marks):** Scope creep is the *uncontrolled expansion* of project scope without corresponding adjustments to time, budget, and resources. It typically arises from poorly defined scope, weak change control, or weak customer engagement.

**Control Mechanisms (2 marks):**
1. Formal change control board (CCB) approval for all scope changes.
2. Rigorous WBS validation using the 100% rule.
3. Earned Value Analysis to detect variances early.
4. Strong configuration management and baselining.

---

#### Model Solution for Question B(b) — 7 Marks

Given: $\text{KLOC} = 8$, Organic mode ($a = 2.4$, $b = 1.05$, $c = 2.5$, $d = 0.38$).

**Step 1: Basic Effort (2.5 marks)**

$$E_{basic} = 2.4 \times 8^{1.05}$$

$$\log E_{basic} = \log 2.4 + 1.05 \times \log 8$$

$$\log 2.4 = 0.3802, \quad \log 8 = 0.9031$$

$$\log E_{basic} = 0.3802 + (1.05 \times 0.9031) = 0.3802 + 0.9482 = 1.3284$$

$$E_{basic} = 10^{1.3284} = 21.30 \text{ Person-Months}$$

**Step 2: Adjusted Effort (2.5 marks)**

$$EAF = 1.0 \times 0.95 \times 1.0 \times 1.1 = 1.045$$

$$E_{adj} = 21.30 \times 1.045 = 22.26 \text{ Person-Months}$$

**Step 3: Development Time (2 marks)**

$$D = 2.5 \times (22.26)^{0.38}$$

$$\log D = \log 2.5 + 0.38 \times \log 22.26 = 0.3979 + 0.38 \times 1.3474 = 0.3979 + 0.5120 = 0.9099$$

$$D = 10^{0.9099} = 8.13 \text{ Months}$$

**Final Answer:** $E_{adj} = 22.26$ Person-Months, $D = 8.13$ Months.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning

> [!WARNING]
> **Where Students Lose Marks in this Topic:**
> 1. **Forgetting the 5 steps in order** — Examiners expect *Scope → Model → Decompose → Task → Integrate* in that exact sequence. A shuffled answer loses 1–2 marks.
> 2. **Confusing WBS with Activity List** — WBS is *deliverable-oriented*; Activity List is *action-oriented*. Use the right term.
> 3. **Skipping the 100% Rule** — Any WBS question without the 100% rule is incomplete and loses 1 mark.
> 4. **Miscalculating $\log$ in COCOMO** — Always show $\log$ steps explicitly; do not jump from $8^{1.05}$ to a final answer.
> 5. **Not stating units** — Always write *Person-Months* and *Months*. Bare numbers lose 0.5 mark each.
> 6. **Ignoring EAF** — A COCOMO question without EAF adjustment is treated as an incomplete solution (loses 2–3 marks).

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Creating the Framework**

- ✅ Framework = foundation of the project; built *before* any code is written.
- ✅ Built upon Pressman's **W5HH Principle** (7 questions).
- ✅ 5-Step Process: **Scope → Model → Decompose → Task → Integrate**.
- ✅ **Scope** must satisfy the **100% Rule** (no creep, no gap).
- ✅ **Process Models** covered: Waterfall, Incremental, Evolutionary, Spiral, Agile.
- ✅ **Decomposition hierarchy:** Model → Phases → Activities → Tasks → Work Packages.
- ✅ **WBS** is deliverable-oriented; **Activity List** is action-oriented.
- ✅ **Task Template** includes: description, predecessors, owner, effort, duration, deliverable, entry/exit criteria.
- ✅ **COCOMO Basic:** $E = a \times \text{KLOC}^b$.
- ✅ **COCOMO Adjusted:** $E_{adj} = E \times \prod EAF_i$ (17 cost drivers).
- ✅ **Development Time:** $D = c \times E_{adj}^d$.
- ✅ **Organic mode:** $a=2.4, b=1.05, c=2.5, d=0.38$.
- ✅ **Semidetached mode:** $a=3.0, b=1.12, c=2.5, d=0.35$.
- ✅ **Embedded mode:** $a=3.6, b=1.20, c=2.5, d=0.32$.
- ✅ **EVM metrics:** SPI = EV/PV, CPI = EV/AC, CV = EV−AC, SV = EV−PV.
- ✅ **Three driving forces for framework:** invisibility, complexity, conformity of software.
- ✅ **Scope Creep** = uncontrolled scope expansion; controlled via CCB, EVM, and 100% rule.
- ✅ **Management Integration** = estimation + scheduling + risk + quality + configuration.
- ✅ **Final Output** of the framework = Project Charter, WBS, Schedule, Budget — ready for execution phase.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Collecting Data
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PEITT633 - SOFTWARE PROJECT MANAGEMENT > - **Module:** Module Module 4 > - **Topic:** Collecting Data # SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 What is "Collecting Data" in Software Project Management? In the context of **KTU 2024 Scheme – Software Project Management**, the topic *Collecting Data* refers to the **systematic, disciplined, and repeatable process of capturing, recording, validating, and storing measurements** related to a software project's **product, process, and resources**, so that they can be analysed to support estimation, scheduling, quality assurance, and process improvement. > [!IMPORTANT] > **Formal Definition (KTU-aligned):** > *"Data collection is the operational phase of the software measurement programme in which raw observations (size, effort, defects, duration, cost, etc.) are captured at well-defined checkpoints using standardized forms, tools, and procedures, with the explicit purpose of feeding the Goal–Question–Metric (GQM) model defined by Basili."* ### 1.2 The Three-Class View of Software Measurement Before data is collected, one must clearly distinguish between three closely related concepts that KTU examiners frequently interchange in exam questions: | Term | Definition | Example | |------|------------|---------| | **Measurement** | The act of obtaining a numerical value for an attribute by some empirical rule. | Counting the lines of code in a module. | | **Measure** | The numerical value itself obtained by a measurement. | `LOC = 1,250`. | | **Metric** | A mathematical/comparative function that combines two or more measures. | `Defect Density = Defects / KLOC`. | | **Indicator** | A metric that provides insight into a software project's status. | `Productivity = KLOC / Person-Month`. | > [!NOTE] > In KTU board papers, the question *"Differentiate between measure and metric"* carries **3 marks** and is a perennial favourite. Memorize the relationship **Measure → Metric → Indicator**. ### 1.3 Why Collect Data? — The Conceptual Analogy > [!TIP] > **Intuition – The Hospital Analogy:** > Imagine a doctor treating a patient. Without measuring temperature, blood pressure, heart-rate, and blood-sugar, the doctor cannot diagnose, prescribe, or evaluate recovery. Likewise, a **Project Manager without metrics is a doctor without instruments** — they will *guess* deadlines, costs, and quality rather than *know* them. > > Data collection is the **stethoscope, thermometer, and sphygmomanometer** of software engineering. Without it, **SPM** becomes superstition. ### 1.4 Goals of a Data Collection Programme A well-designed data collection programme must satisfy the **Five Ws** (and one H): 1. **Why** — Improve estimation accuracy and process maturity. 2. **What** — Identify which entities, attributes, and attributes' values are needed. 3. **When** — Define collection points (end-of-phase, end-of-task, weekly, etc.). 4. **Where** — Identify the source (developers, repositories, CASE tools). 5. **Who** — Assign clear responsibility for each data item. 6. **How** — Choose the form, tool, and storage medium. > [!WARNING] > **Common Pitfall:** KTU examiners frequently note that students treat data collection as a *one-time* activity. In reality, it is a **continuous, life-cycle-long** activity that starts at *project inception* and ends only at *project closure*. ### 1.5 The Conceptual Difference between Product, Process, and Project Data | Category | Question Answered | Examples | |----------|-------------------|----------| | **Product Data** | *What did we build?* | Size (LOC, FP), complexity, defects. | | **Process Data** | *How did we build it?* | Effort, duration, methods used. | | **Project Data** | *Are we on track?* | Cost variance, schedule slip, productivity. | ### 1.6 Classification by Lifecycle Phase Data collection is not an event; it is a **phase-straddling activity**: - **Requirements Phase:** Number of requirements, volatility index. - **Design Phase:** Number of modules, coupling/cohesion indices. - **Coding Phase:** LOC, Cyclomatic Complexity, code churn. - **Testing Phase:** Defects discovered, defect removal efficiency. - **Maintenance Phase:** Mean Time to Failure (MTTF), change requests. > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Board Tip:** Always mention that data collection must follow the **Operational Definition Principle** — every metric must have an unambiguous, written-down procedure for collection. If two engineers measuring the same thing get two different numbers, your data collection process has failed. # SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 The Goal–Question–Metric (GQM) Paradigm — Basili's Method The **GQM paradigm**, formulated by **Victor R. Basili** (University of Maryland), is the **gold standard** that KTU follows for defining what data to collect. It transforms an abstract organizational goal into measurable quantities. ### 2.1.1 The Three-Layer Hierarchy of GQM The GQM hierarchy is built in three rigorous steps: **Step 1 — Define the GOAL** - Specify the purpose of measurement in terms of: - **Object** (what is being measured — product, process, resource). - **Purpose** (why — assess, predict, control, improve). - **Focus** (quality attribute — reliability, maintainability, productivity). - **Viewpoint** (whose perspective — developer, manager, customer). - **Environment** (context — language, domain, team). **Step 2 — Derive QUESTIONS** - Each goal is refined into 2–8 questions whose answers trace back to the goal. - Each question must be answerable from data. **Step 3 — Define METRICS** - Each question is operationalized into one or more metrics. - Metrics must be objectively collectable. ### 2.1.2 Worked Example of a GQM Derivation > **Goal:** Improve the on-time delivery of the **Release-1** software from the perspective of the **Project Manager**. > > **Q1:** What is the current schedule slip across recent projects? > **Q2:** Which phase accounts for the maximum schedule slip? > **Q3:** What is the rework effort ratio per phase? > > **Metrics:** > - M1 (Q1): **Schedule Slip = (Actual Duration – Planned Duration) / Planned Duration × 100**. > - M2 (Q2): **Phase-wise duration variance**. > - M3 (Q3): **Rework Effort / Total Effort**. ## 2.2 Principles of Data Collection — The Six Pillars A robust data collection programme rests on six pillars: 1. **Operational Definition:** Each metric must be *objectively defined*. If two people can read the definition and obtain the same number, the definition is operational. 2. **Standardization:** Uniform forms, units, and naming conventions. 3. **Automation:** Wherever possible, capture data automatically from tools (Git, Jira, IDEs). 4. **Validation:** Range checks, sanity checks, and cross-validation at entry. 5. **Auditability:** Every data point must be traceable to its source and date. 6. **Confidentiality:** Anonymization of individual-level data to encourage honest reporting. ## 2.3 Data Collection Methods ### 2.3.1 Manual Data Collection - Uses **standardized forms** (paper or digital). - Data is filled in by developers, testers, or PMO staff. - Pros: Cheap, no tooling needed. Cons: Error-prone, slow, incomplete. ### 2.3.2 Automatic Data Collection - Uses **CASE tools, version control (Git), bug trackers (Jira, Bugzilla), CI/CD pipelines, IDE plugins**. - Pros: Reliable, real-time, large volumes. Cons: Tool integration overhead. ### 2.3.3 Hybrid Approach - Combines manual + automatic. **Industry-recommended.** ## 2.4 The Data Collection Form — Anatomy A KTU-grade data collection form must include: | Field | Purpose | |-------|---------| | Project ID & Name | Identification | | Module / Component | Granularity | | Phase / Activity | Lifecycle stage | | Date of Collection | Time-stamp | | Attribute & Unit | What is measured | | Value | The actual measurement | | Collector ID | Accountability | | Remarks | Context, deviations | ## 2.5 Data Validation Techniques - **Range Check:** Reject negative LOC, effort > 24h/day. - **Logical Check:** Effort > 0 only if the module has any change. - **Cross-Validation:** Compare reported hours with version-control commit timestamps. - **Outlier Detection:** Use **Z-score** or **IQR** to flag anomalies. ## 2.6 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet | # | Formula / Concept | Purpose | Unit | |---|-------------------|---------|------| | 1 | **LOC** = Σ Lines of code | Size measure | Lines | | 2 | **KLOC** = LOC / 1000 | Normalized size | KLOC | | 3 | **FP** = Σ (Unadjusted FPs) × VAF | Functional size | FP | | 4 | **VAF** = 0.65 + 0.01 × Σ(Fi) | Value Adjustment Factor | dimensionless (0.65 – 1.35) | | 5 | **Productivity** = KLOC / Effort | Process efficiency | KLOC / person-month | | 6 | **Defect Density** = Defects / KLOC | Quality indicator | Defects / KLOC | | 7 | **Effort (COCOMO)** = a × (KLOC)^b | Effort estimation | Person-months | | 8 | **DRE** = (Defects found pre-release) / (Defects found pre + post) | Removal efficiency | Ratio (0–1) | | 9 | **MTTF** = Total operating time / Failures | Reliability | Hours | | 10 | **Schedule Slip** = (Actual – Planned)/Planned × 100 | Schedule control | Percentage | | 11 | **Cost Performance Index (CPI)** = EV / AC | Earned value analysis | Ratio | | 12 | **Schedule Performance Index (SPI)** = EV / PV | Earned value analysis | Ratio | | 13 | **Z-score** = (X − μ) / σ | Outlier detection | σ units | | 14 | **Estimation Accuracy** = (Actual − Estimate)/Estimate × 100 | Estimation quality | Percentage | > [!NOTE] > **Strict KTU Notation Rule:** Always state the unit explicitly. Examiners allot **½ mark** for correct units in numerical problems. ## 2.7 Real-World Utility - **Industry:** Enables empirical estimation models (COCOMO II, SLIM, Function Point Analysis). - **CMMI Level 4/5:** Requires quantitative data to be available for process control. - **ISO/IEC 15939:** International standard for software measurement process. - **Agile/Lean:** Burn-down charts, velocity, and cycle time are all *derived from* collected data. # SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Derivation I: From Goal to Metric via GQM (Algebraic Mapping) The GQM process can be expressed as a structured mapping: Let $G$ denote a **Goal**, $Q$ denote a **Question**, and $M$ denote a **Metric**. The GQM decomposition establishes the following **set-theoretic relationship**: $$ \mathcal{M}(G) \;=\; \bigcup_{q \,\in\, Q(G)} \mathcal{M}(q) $$ **Explanation of each row in the derivation:** - $G$ is the top-level organizational intent. Example: *"Reduce defect leakage from system testing to UAT by 50 % within two releases."* - $Q(G)$ is the *question set* derived from $G$. Example: $Q(G) = \{q_1, q_2, q_3\}$. - $\mathcal{M}(q)$ is the *metric set* that operationalizes each question. Now expand for our specific example: $$ \mathcal{M}(q_1) = \left\{ \text{Defect Leakage Ratio (DLR)} \right\} $$ $$ \mathcal{M}(q_2) = \left\{ \text{Defects found in UAT} \right\} $$ $$ \mathcal{M}(q_3) = \left\{ \text{Defects found in System Test} \right\} $$ The final composite metric becomes: $$ \text{DLR} \;=\; \frac{\sum \text{Defects found in UAT}}{\sum \text{Defects found in System Test} + \sum \text{Defects found in UAT}} $$ **This step is critical because it shows that every metric must have a clear logical link back to a question, which links back to a goal.** KTU board examiners award full marks only when this traceability is explicit. ## 3.2 Derivation II: Function Point (FP) Calculation — A Step-by-Step Walkthrough This is a **14-mark standard KTU problem**. The procedure is rigid and must be memorized. ### Step 1: Identify the Function Types and Count Them The five FP components are: | Function Type | Description | |---------------|-------------| | **External Inputs (EI)** | Inputs that update internal data files. | | **External Outputs (EO)** | Outputs that produce information for the user. | | **External Inquiries (EQ)** | Inputs that retrieve data with minimal processing. | | **Internal Logical Files (ILF)** | Logical data maintained within the application boundary. | | **External Interface Files (EIF)** | Logical data referenced but maintained by another app. | ### Step 2: Assign Complexity Weights Using the standard **IFPUG** table: | Function Type | Low | Average | High | |---------------|-----|---------|------| | **EI** | 3 | 4 | 6 | | **EO** | 4 | 5 | 7 | | **EQ** | 3 | 4 | 6 | | **ILF** | 7 | 10 | 15 | | **EIF** | 5 | 7 | 10 | ### Step 3: Compute Unadjusted FP (UFP) $$ \text{UFP} \;=\; \sum_{f \in \text{FP types}} \left( \text{Low}_f \cdot N_{L,f} + \text{Avg}_f \cdot N_{A,f} + \text{High}_f \cdot N_{H,f} \right) $$ ### Step 4: Compute Value Adjustment Factor (VAF) The 14 General System Characteristics (GSCs) are rated on a scale of **0 to 5**: $$ \text{VAF} \;=\; 0.65 \;+\; 0.01 \cdot \sum_{i=1}^{14} F_i $$ ### Step 5: Compute Adjusted FP $$ \text{FP} \;=\; \text{UFP} \times \text{VAF} $$ ### Worked Numerical Example > **Problem:** A system has **10 EI (average)**, **5 EO (high)**, **3 EQ (low)**, **4 ILF (average)**, and **2 EIF (low)**. The 14 GSCs are rated as $\{4, 3, 2, 5, 1, 4, 3, 2, 4, 5, 3, 2, 4, 3\}$. Compute the **Adjusted Function Point**. **Solution — every transition is shown explicitly:** $$ \begin{aligned} \text{UFP} &= (EI_{avg} \times 10) + (EO_{high} \times 5) + (EQ_{low} \times 3) + (ILF_{avg} \times 4) + (EIF_{low} \times 2) \\ &= (4 \times 10) + (7 \times 5) + (3 \times 3) + (10 \times 4) + (5 \times 2) \\ &= 40 + 35 + 9 + 40 + 10 \\ &= 134 \end{aligned} $$ $$ \begin{aligned} \sum F_i &= 4+3+2+5+1+4+3+2+4+5+3+2+4+3 = 45 \\ \text{VAF} &= 0.65 + 0.01 \times 45 \\ &= 0.65 + 0.45 \\ &= 1.10 \end{aligned} $$ $$ \begin{aligned} \text{FP} &= 134 \times 1.10 \\ &= 147.4 \;\approx\; 147 \;\text{FP} \end{aligned} $$ > [!IMPORTANT] > KTU board answer key pattern: Always show all **5 steps** with explicit intermediate sums. Marks are awarded per step, not just for the final number. ## 3.3 Derivation III: Productivity, Defect Density, and DRE — Interlinked Measures These three are frequently clubbed together in a 7-mark sub-question. **Given** (sample data): - Total Effort = 24 person-months - Total LOC = 12,000 - Total Defects discovered pre-release = 96 - Total Defects discovered post-release = 24 $$ \begin{aligned} \text{KLOC} &= \frac{12{,}000}{1{,}000} = 12 \;\text{KLOC} \\ \text{Productivity} &= \frac{12}{24} = 0.5 \;\text{KLOC / person-month} \\ \text{Defect Density} &= \frac{96 + 24}{12} = 10 \;\text{defects / KLOC} \\ \text{DRE} &= \frac{96}{96 + 24} = 0.80 = 80\% \end{aligned} $$ > [!TIP] > **KTU Examiner's Insight:** When a question gives only pre-release defects, students often wrongly use total defects in the DRE formula. Always re-read: *DRE uses only defects found during the validation phases.* ## 3.4 Derivation IV: Outlier Detection Using Z-score The Z-score of an observation $x_i$ in a dataset is: $$ Z_i = \frac{x_i - \mu}{\sigma} $$ Where $\mu$ is the mean and $\sigma$ is the standard deviation. **Rule of thumb (KTU syllabus):** Observations with $|Z_i| > 3$ are statistically *extreme* outliers. **Worked Micro-Example:** Suppose weekly effort (hours) is: $\{38, 40, 42, 41, 39, 200\}$. $$ \begin{aligned} \mu &= \frac{38+40+42+41+39+200}{6} = \frac{400}{6} \approx 66.67 \\ \sigma &= \sqrt{\frac{\sum (x_i - \mu)^2}{n}} \approx 65.0 \end{aligned} $$ $$ Z_{200} = \frac{200 - 66.67}{65.0} \approx 2.05 $$ Interpretation: The 200-hour week is suspicious but **not yet a $3\sigma$ outlier**; it warrants investigation but is *not* automatically rejected. ## 3.5 Derivation V: Earned Value Metrics from Collected Data | Term | Formula | Meaning | |------|---------|---------| | Planned Value (PV) | $\sum \text{Planned cost up to time t}$ | Budgeted work. | | Earned Value (EV) | $\sum \text{Completed work's planned cost}$ | Actual progress valued at planned rates. | | Actual Cost (AC) | $\sum \text{Actual cost incurred up to time t}$ | Real money spent. | $$ \text{Cost Variance (CV)} = EV - AC $$ $$ \text{Schedule Variance (SV)} = EV - PV $$ $$ \text{CPI} = \frac{EV}{AC}, \qquad \text{SPI} = \frac{EV}{PV} $$ > [!NOTE] > **CPI > 1 ⇒ Under budget.** **SPI > 1 ⇒ Ahead of schedule.** This single rule carries **1 mark** in any KTU EV question. ## 3.6 Analytical Summary Matrix | # | Metric | Numerator | Denominator | Direction of "Good" | |---|--------|-----------|-------------|---------------------| | 1 | Productivity | KLOC | Effort | ↑ | | 2 | Defect Density | Defects | KLOC | ↓ | | 3 | DRE | Pre-release defects | All defects | ↑ | | 4 | CPI | EV | AC | ↑ | | 5 | SPI | EV | PV | ↑ | | 6 | Schedule Slip | Actual – Planned | Planned | ↓ | | 7 | MTTF | Operating time | Failures | ↑ | | 8 | Z-score | X − μ | σ | \vert Z \vert ≤ 3 | # SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 The Goal–Question–Metric (GQM) Hierarchy — Top-Down Decomposition The diagram below illustrates how an organizational **Goal** decomposes into measurable **Questions** and finally into **Metrics** that drive data collection. ```mermaid flowchart TD node1["Goal G1: Improve on-time delivery
Object: Project
Purpose: Control
Focus: Schedule
Viewpoint: Project Manager"] node2["Question Q1: What is the current schedule slip?"] node3["Question Q2: Which phase has the maximum slip?"] node4["Question Q3: What is the rework effort ratio?"] node5["Metric M1: Schedule Slip percentage"] node6["Metric M2: Phase-wise duration variance"] node7["Metric M3: Rework Effort / Total Effort"] node8["Data Collection: Planned vs Actual duration, Effort logs"] node1 --> node2 node1 --> node3 node1 --> node4 node2 --> node5 node3 --> node6 node4 --> node7 node5 --> node8 node6 --> node8 node7 --> node8 ``` ## 4.2 The End-to-End Data Collection Workflow This block diagram maps the **operational pipeline** of a data collection programme — from the moment data is conceived to the moment it informs a decision. ```mermaid flowchart LR stepA["Identify Goals GQM Top Level"] stepB["Derive Questions and Metrics"] stepC["Design Data Collection Forms"] stepD["Assign Roles and Schedule"] stepE["Manual OR Automatic Capture"] stepF["Validate Range Logical Outlier"] stepG["Store in Central Repository"] stepH["Analyse and Report Indicators"] stepI["Feedback to Estimation and Process Improvement"] stepA --> stepB stepB --> stepC stepC --> stepD stepD --> stepE stepE --> stepF stepF --> stepG stepG --> stepH stepH --> stepI stepI -.-> stepA ``` ## 4.3 Classification Tree of Data Collection Methods ```mermaid flowchart TD root["Data Collection Methods"] leaf1["Manual Forms"] leaf2["Automatic Tools"] leaf3["Hybrid Approach"] sub1["Paper Based"] sub2["Spreadsheet Based"] sub3["CASE Tools"] sub4["Version Control Git"] sub5["Bug Trackers Jira"] sub6["IDE Plugins"] sub7["CI CD Pipelines"] sub8["Manual Effort Logs Plus Auto Capture"] root --> leaf1 root --> leaf2 root --> leaf3 leaf1 --> sub1 leaf1 --> sub2 leaf2 --> sub3 leaf2 --> sub4 leaf2 --> sub5 leaf2 --> sub6 leaf2 --> sub7 leaf3 --> sub8 ``` ## 4.4 The Data Validation Flow — How a Single Data Point is Processed ```mermaid flowchart TD node1["Raw Data Captured"] node2["Range Check"] node3["Logical Consistency Check"] node4["Cross Source Validation"] node5["Outlier Z Score or IQR"] node6{"All Checks Passed?"} node7["Accept and Store"] node8["Flag for Review"] node9["Rejection Notification"] node1 --> node2 node2 --> node3 node3 --> node4 node4 --> node5 node5 --> node6 node6 --> node7 node6 --> node8 node8 --> node9 ``` ## 4.5 The Mapping of Metrics to Lifecycle Phases ```mermaid flowchart LR ph1["Requirements Phase"] ph2["Design Phase"] ph3["Coding Phase"] ph4["Testing Phase"] ph5["Maintenance Phase"] m1["Number of Requirements Volatility Index"] m2["Number of Modules Coupling Cohesion"] m3["LOC Cyclomatic Complexity Code Churn"] m4["Defects Discovered Test Coverage"] m5["MTTF Change Requests Defect Backlog"] ph1 --> m1 ph2 --> m2 ph3 --> m3 ph4 --> m4 ph5 --> m5 ``` ## 4.6 The Cost-of-Defect-Time Curve (Data Collection Justification) While a physical graph is not possible in Mermaid, the following block captures the **structural analogy** that data collection defends: ```mermaid flowchart LR nodeA["Requirements Defect"] nodeB["Design Defect"] nodeC["Code Defect"] nodeD["Test Defect"] nodeE["Post Release Defect"] costA["Cost approx 1X"] costB["Cost approx 5X"] costC["Cost approx 10X"] costD["Cost approx 50X"] costE["Cost approx 200X"] nodeA --> costA nodeB --> costB nodeC --> costC nodeD --> costD nodeE --> costE ``` > [!TIP] > Use this block diagram in your exam answers to **justify** why data must be collected *early and continuously*. Examiners award bonus marks for connecting data collection to cost-effectiveness. # SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap --- ## 📘 PART A — 3-Mark Short-Answer Questions ### Q1. `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]` **Define the Goal–Question–Metric (GQM) paradigm and explain how it guides data collection in software projects.** **Model Answer (3 Marks):** The **Goal–Question–Metric (GQM) paradigm**, proposed by **Victor Basili**, is a top-down measurement framework that defines measurement in three layers: 1. **Goal:** Specifies the purpose of measurement (object, purpose, focus, viewpoint, environment). 2. **Questions:** Refines the goal into a set of 2–8 answerable questions. 3. **Metrics:** Operationalizes each question into objectively collectable data. **How it guides data collection:** The GQM paradigm ensures that every piece of data collected is **traceable** to a business goal. It prevents *vanity metrics* and ensures **only relevant, decision-supporting data** is captured. For example, a goal to *"improve on-time delivery"* leads to the question *"what is the current schedule slip?"*, which leads to the metric *"schedule slip percentage"* — and *this* is what the team actually collects from project logs. *(Examiner's Note: Award 1 mark for each layer correctly explained.)* --- ### Q2. `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]` **Differentiate between a *Measure*, a *Metric*, and an *Indicator* in software engineering.** **Model Answer (3 Marks):** | Term | Definition | Example | |------|------------|---------| | **Measure** | Direct numerical value obtained by a measurement process. | LOC = 1,250; Effort = 24 person-months. | | **Metric** | A mathematical/comparative relationship derived from two or more measures. | Defect Density = Defects / KLOC. | | **Indicator** | A metric chosen to provide insight into a project's status; supports decision-making. | Defect Density > 5 ⇒ Quality Risk. | *(Examiner's Note: 1 mark for definition + 1 mark for example + 1 mark for distinction.)* --- ## 📗 PART B — 14-Mark Long-Answer Questions ### INTERNAL CHOICE PROVIDED — Answer ANY ONE full question. --- ### ✅ Question A (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2022]` **(a) Explain the GQM approach with a suitable example. Discuss how operational definitions ensure data consistency. (7 Marks)** **(b) A software project collects the following data over three iterations:** | Iteration | LOC (in KLOC) | Effort (Person-Months) | Pre-Release Defects | Post-Release Defects | |-----------|---------------|------------------------|----------------------|-----------------------| | 1 | 8 | 12 | 40 | 10 | | 2 | 12 | 16 | 50 | 6 | | 3 | 15 | 18 | 48 | 4 | **Compute for each iteration:** (i) Productivity (KLOC / Person-Month) (ii) Defect Density (defects / KLOC) (iii) Defect Removal Efficiency (DRE) **Also identify the iteration that performed best overall and justify.** --- #### MODEL SOLUTION **(a) GQM Approach + Operational Definitions (7 Marks)** **GQM (5 Marks):** - GQM is a **top-down, goal-oriented measurement paradigm** developed by **Basili & Weiss** at the University of Maryland. - It operates on three levels: - **Conceptual Level — Goal:** Defines purpose in terms of object, purpose, focus, viewpoint, environment. - **Operational Level — Question:** Refines goal into answerable questions. - **Quantitative Level — Metric:** Provides the numerical form of each question. **Example (any of the following is acceptable):** - **Goal:** Improve the maintainability of the billing module. - **Q1:** What is the cyclomatic complexity per function?
