- **Q2:** What is the comment density? - **Q3:** What is the average number of defects per KLOC in maintenance? - **Metrics:** Cyclomatic Complexity, Comment %, Defect Density. **Operational Definitions (2 Marks):** - An **operational definition** is an unambiguous, written procedure for measuring an attribute. - Two engineers applying the same operational definition to the same module must obtain the same value. - Example: "A *line of code* is counted if it is a non-blank, non-comment executable statement of the source language." - Operational definitions eliminate ambiguity in *what counts as a defect*, *what counts as effort*, and *what counts as a requirement*, ensuring **data consistency** across projects. --- **(b) Numerical Computation (7 Marks — 2 + 2 + 2 + 1)** **(i) Productivity = KLOC / Effort** $$ \begin{aligned} P_1 &= \frac{8}{12} = 0.667 \;\text{KLOC/PM} \\ P_2 &= \frac{12}{16} = 0.750 \;\text{KLOC/PM} \\ P_3 &= \frac{15}{18} = 0.833 \;\text{KLOC/PM} \end{aligned} $$ **(ii) Defect Density (DD) = Total Defects / KLOC** $$ \begin{aligned} DD_1 &= \frac{40 + 10}{8} = \frac{50}{8} = 6.25 \;\text{Defects/KLOC} \\ DD_2 &= \frac{50 + 6}{12} = \frac{56}{12} \approx 4.67 \;\text{Defects/KLOC} \\ DD_3 &= \frac{48 + 4}{15} = \frac{52}{15} \approx 3.47 \;\text{Defects/KLOC} \end{aligned} $$ **(iii) DRE = Pre-Release / Total Defects** $$ \begin{aligned} DRE_1 &= \frac{40}{50} = 0.80 = 80\% \\ DRE_2 &= \frac{50}{56} \approx 0.893 = 89.3\% \\ DRE_3 &= \frac{48}{52} \approx 0.923 = 92.3\% \end{aligned} $$ **Best Iteration: Iteration 3.** **Justification (1 Mark):** Iteration 3 has the **highest productivity** (0.833), the **lowest defect density** (3.47), and the **highest DRE** (92.3 %) — superior on all three fronts. --- ### ✅ Question B (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam – May 2023]` **(a) Explain the principles of an effective data collection programme. Discuss the role of *operational definitions* and *standardization* in ensuring data quality. (7 Marks)** **(b) For a project, the following 14 General System Characteristics (GSCs) of FP analysis are rated: {3, 2, 4, 5, 1, 4, 3, 2, 5, 4, 3, 2, 4, 1}. The unadjusted function point count is 110.** **Compute:** (i) The Value Adjustment Factor (VAF). (ii) The Adjusted Function Points (FP). (iii) If the project used 22 person-months of effort, calculate the **Function-Point-Based Productivity** in FP per person-month. (iv) Comment on whether the project is **above-average** in productivity, given that the industry baseline is **5.5 FP/PM**. --- #### MODEL SOLUTION **(a) Principles of an Effective Data Collection Programme (7 Marks)** The six pillars of a robust data collection programme are: 1. **Operational Definitions (2 Marks):** Every metric must be defined precisely, in writing, so that any two engineers measuring the same attribute obtain the same value. Example: definition of *defect* must specify inclusion/exclusion criteria (e.g., documentation typos may or may not count). 2. **Standardization (1 Mark):** Uniform forms, units (hours vs. days), and naming conventions across projects so data can be aggregated and compared. 3. **Automation (1 Mark):** Wherever feasible, data should be captured automatically from tools (Git, Jira, IDE) to minimize human error. 4. **Validation (1 Mark):** Range, logical, and outlier checks must be applied at the time of data entry. 5. **Auditability (1 Mark):** Every data point must be traceable to a collector, date, and source. 6. **Confidentiality (1 Mark):** Individual-level data should be anonymized to encourage honest reporting. **Role in Data Quality:** Together, operational definitions and standardization eliminate **ambiguity** and **inconsistency**, the two largest causes of poor data quality. Without them, the resulting database is statistically unusable. --- **(b) FP Calculation (7 Marks — 1.5 + 1.5 + 2 + 2)** **(i) VAF** $$ \begin{aligned} \sum F_i &= 3+2+4+5+1+4+3+2+5+4+3+2+4+1 = 43 \\ \text{VAF} &= 0.65 + 0.01 \times 43 = 1.08 \end{aligned} $$ **(ii) Adjusted FP** $$ \text{FP} = 110 \times 1.08 = 118.8 \;\approx\; 119 \;\text{FP} $$ **(iii) FP-Based Productivity** $$ \text{Productivity} = \frac{118.8}{22} = 5.4 \;\text{FP / PM} $$ **(iv) Comparison with Industry Baseline** The project productivity is **5.4 FP/PM**, which is **just below** the industry baseline of 5.5 FP/PM. Therefore the project is **slightly below average**. Recommendations: adopt better reuse, invest in CASE tooling, and improve the requirements phase to reduce rework. --- > [!WARNING] > **🛑 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Where Students Lose Marks:** > 1. **Units are forgotten.** Always write **KLOC/PM**, **Defects/KLOC**, etc. Examiners deduct **½ mark** per missing unit. > 2. **DRE formula misuse.** Some students use *post-release* defects in the numerator. *Correct formula: DRE = Pre / (Pre + Post).* > 3. **VAF range ignored.** VAF must lie in **$[0.65,\; 1.35]$**. Any result outside this range is a sign of calculation error. > 4. **Productivity vs. Quality conflated.** A team may be *productive* but *ineffective*; both metrics must be reported. > 5. **GQM goal definition incomplete.** Writing only *"improve quality"* loses marks. Must mention **Object, Purpose, Focus, Viewpoint, Environment**. --- ## 🧠 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist) - ✅ **GQM = Goal + Question + Metric** — defined by **Basili** (1984). - ✅ **Measure → Metric → Indicator** — three distinct concepts, frequently tested. - ✅ **Six Pillars** of data collection: Operational Definition, Standardization, Automation, Validation, Auditability, Confidentiality. - ✅ **Operational Definition** is the **#1 requirement** for data consistency. - ✅ **Function Point (FP) Calculation** must always follow the **5-step IFPUG procedure**: Count → Weight → UFP → VAF → Adjusted FP. - ✅ **VAF** lies in **$[0.65,\; 1.35]$**; formula = $0.65 + 0.01 \cdot \sum F_i$. - ✅ **DRE** formula = Pre / (Pre + Post); **higher DRE = better testing**. - ✅ **Productivity** has two forms: **KLOC/PM** (size-based) and **FP/PM** (function-based). - ✅ **Defect Density** = Total Defects / KLOC; **lower is better**. - ✅ **Outlier Detection** uses **Z-score** ($|Z| > 3$) or **IQR** (1.5 × IQR rule). - ✅ **Earned Value Metrics** — CPI = EV/AC, SPI = EV/PV; CPI>1 under budget, SPI>1 ahead. - ✅ **Cost-of-defect curve:** Early defects are *cheaper*; late defects are *catastrophic* — justifies *early data collection*. - ✅ Data collection is **continuous**, not one-time. It spans *all* lifecycle phases. - ✅ **CMMI Level 4/5** and **ISO/IEC 15939** both mandate quantitative data collection. - ✅ Manual + Automatic = **Hybrid** approach is industry-recommended. - ✅ Always **show units**; always **show formulas before numbers**; always **show DRE derivation**.

topics.title: Review
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEITT633 - SOFTWARE PROJECT MANAGEMENT
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Review

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

In Software Project Management (SPM), a **Review** is a systematic, disciplined, and structured evaluation of software artifacts (requirements, design, code, test plans) conducted by qualified personnel to identify defects, verify conformance to standards, and promote process improvement. Reviews are a core component of **Software Quality Assurance (SQA)** activities, distinct from testing because they focus on **static analysis** (examining artifacts without execution) rather than dynamic execution.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Definition Box:**
> A **Software Review** is an umbrella term encompassing *Formal Technical Reviews (FTR)*, *Walkthroughs*, *Inspections*, and *Peer Reviews*. According to IEEE Std 1028, every review follows a defined process with entry criteria, exit criteria, roles, and measurable outcomes.

### Key Review Categories (KTU High-Yield Classification)

| Category | Description | KTU Significance |
|---|---|---|
| **Informal Review** | Casual peer discussion, no formal process | Low rigor, low cost |
| **Walkthrough** | Author-led, educational in nature | Author presents, audience asks questions |
| **Technical Review (FTR)** | Peer-led, defect-focused, documented | **Most cited in KTU exams** |
| **Inspection** | Most formal, Fagan-style, role-based | Highest defect detection rate |
| **Management Review** | Evaluates project status, not artifacts | Concerned with progress, budget, risk |

## 1.2 Intuitive Analogy (Plain-English Mental Model)

Imagine you are writing a **college thesis**. Before final submission:
- You ask a **friend** to read it → that's an *informal review*
- You present your draft to a **study group** who interrupt and ask questions → that's a *walkthrough*
- Your **faculty advisor** reads it carefully against a checklist of standards → that's a *technical review*
- A **panel of experts** uses a formal defect-detection checklist in a documented meeting → that's an *inspection*

> [!NOTE]
> **The Core Intuition:** Reviews are like "spell-checkers for design and code" — they catch defects **before** they become expensive bugs found during testing or after deployment. The cost of fixing a defect in requirements vs. after release can differ by a factor of **100× or more**.

### The Cost-of-Fix Amplification Principle

The earlier a defect is found, the cheaper it is to fix. This is called the **1:10:100 Rule** (also known as the **Boehm Cost Amplification Curve**).

- **Requirements phase fix**: baseline cost **$X**
- **Design phase fix**: **10×** the cost
- **Coding/testing fix**: **100×** the cost
- **Post-release fix**: **1000×** the cost

> [!TIP]
> **KTU Memory Hack:** Remember **"Reviews are cheap insurance"** — investing a few engineering hours in review saves thousands of hours in rework, debugging, and customer support.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Formal Technical Review (FTR) — Step-by-Step Mechanics

A **Formal Technical Review (FTR)** is the KTU-most-tested review type. It follows a rigid, structured process.

### Step 1: Review Planning
- The **review leader** (moderator) identifies the review objective, scope, and participants.
- Defines **entry criteria** (what must be ready before review starts) and **exit criteria** (what defines successful completion).

### Step 2: Review Preparation
- Each **reviewer** independently examines the artifact against assigned **checklists** or standards.
- Reviewers log potential defects on **issue logs** before the meeting.
- The "why" here: independent preparation ensures **defect detection bias** is minimized.

### Step 3: Review Meeting (The Actual Review)
- The author walks through the artifact; reviewers raise issues.
- A **scribe (recorder)** documents all issues and action items.
- Time-boxed: typically **≤ 2 hours** per session for maintainability.

### Step 4: Review Follow-up
- The **review leader** ensures all logged issues are addressed by the author.
- Verifies **exit criteria** are met → formally closes the review.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Role Box — FTR Roles:**
> - **Author**: Creator of the artifact; cannot also be reviewer.
> - **Review Leader / Moderator**: Organizes, drives, ensures process compliance.
> - **Scribe / Recorder**: Documents all issues in real-time.
> - **Reviewers**: Domain experts performing actual defect detection.
> - **Manager**: Decides on schedule and resource allocation (does NOT participate in defect identification).

## 2.2 Inspection (Fagan Inspection) — The Gold Standard

Fagan Inspections (introduced by Michael Fagan at IBM, 1976) are the **most formal review type** with measured defect removal efficiency of **60–90%**.

### Fagan Inspection Process (6 Phases)

| Phase | Activity | Output |
|---|---|---|
| **1. Planning** | Moderator selects team, distributes materials | Inspection plan |
| **2. Overview** | Author presents artifact to inspectors | Common understanding |
| **3. Individual Preparation** | Each inspector studies independently | Defect logs |
| **4. Inspection Meeting** | Group discusses, logs defects | Defect list |
| **5. Rework** | Author fixes identified defects | Updated artifact |
| **6. Follow-up** | Moderator verifies all fixes | Sign-off / closure |

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Fagan Insight:** The *Overview* phase is unique to inspections — it ensures inspectors share context. The *Individual Preparation* phase is what gives inspections their **high defect detection rate**, because groupthink is eliminated.

## 2.3 Walkthroughs — The Educational Variant

Walkthroughs are **author-driven** sessions primarily aimed at:
- **Knowledge transfer** to junior team members
- **Alternative solution exploration**
- **Defect discovery** (secondary goal)

The author narrates the artifact step-by-step; participants ask clarifying questions. The output is a **list of issues, comments, and suggestions**.

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The following metrics are the KTU-exam-relevant measurements for reviews:**

| Metric | Formula | Unit / Interpretation |
|---|---|---|
| **Defect Density (D)** | $D = \dfrac{N_{d}}{S}$ | defects per KLOC or per FP |
| **Review Effort (E)** | $E = \sum_{i=1}^{n} T_{i}$ | person-hours |
| **Defect Removal Efficiency (DRE)** | $\text{DRE} = \dfrac{D_{\text{before}}}{D_{\text{before}} + D_{\text{after}}} \times 100\%$ | percentage |
| **Review Yield** | $\text{Yield} = \dfrac{D_{\text{review}}}{D_{\text{review}} + D_{\text{testing}}}$ | ratio |
| **Inspection Rate** | $R_{\text{insp}} = \dfrac{\text{LOC reviewed}}{\text{Person-hour}}$ | LOC / hour |
| **Preparation Rate** | $R_{\text{prep}} = \dfrac{\text{LOC prepared}}{\text{Person-hour}}$ | LOC / hour |
| **Cost Amplification Factor** | $C_{n} = C_{0} \cdot k^{(n-1)}$ | exponential growth |

> [!WARNING]
> **Critical KTU Symbol Restriction:** When writing formulas in your answer sheets, **never use the vertical pipe symbol `|`** in markdown tables. Use `\vert` or `\mid` in LaTeX. For example, write $\vert x \vert$ instead of $|x|$.

## 2.5 Real-World Utility in Engineering

In professional SPM practice (e.g., following **CMMI Level 3+** or **ISO 9001**), reviews are mandatory before artifact sign-off. Companies like **Microsoft, Google, and Bosch** enforce mandatory peer reviews for all production code commits via tools such as:
- **GitHub Pull Requests**
- **Gerrit Code Review**
- **Crucible / Collaborator**
- **Phabricator**

These tools log **review comments, approval status, and defect metrics** automatically.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Defect Removal Efficiency (DRE) Calculation

The **DRE** is the single most important KTU metric for evaluating review effectiveness. Let us derive it from first principles.

### Starting Premise

Consider a software project phase where defects can be detected in **two stages**:
- **Stage A (Before Review):** Defects detected during the review itself
- **Stage B (After Review):** Defects that escape review and are caught later (in testing or by users)

### Step-by-Step Derivation

Let:
- $D_{\text{review}}$ = number of defects found during review
- $D_{\text{post}}$ = number of defects found after review (during testing / field use)
- $D_{\text{total}}$ = total defects present in the artifact

The total defect count is the sum of defects caught in review plus those that escaped:

$$
D_{\text{total}} = D_{\text{review}} + D_{\text{post}}
$$

The fraction of defects removed by the review (the **Defect Removal Efficiency**) is defined as the ratio of defects caught by review to the total defects present:

$$
\text{DRE} = \frac{D_{\text{review}}}{D_{\text{total}}}
$$

Substituting the expression for $D_{\text{total}}$ from above:

$$
\text{DRE} = \frac{D_{\text{review}}}{D_{\text{review}} + D_{\text{post}}}
$$

Expressed as a percentage for ease of reporting:

$$
\text{DRE (\%)} = \frac{D_{\text{review}}}{D_{\text{review}} + D_{\text{post}}} \times 100\%
$$

### Worked Numerical Example (KTU Standard Problem Pattern)

A software module has **50 defects** total. A formal inspection finds **45** of them; the remaining **5** are caught during system testing.

Compute the Defect Removal Efficiency.

$$
\text{DRE} = \frac{45}{45 + 5} \times 100\%
$$

$$
\text{DRE} = \frac{45}{50} \times 100\%
$$

$$
\text{DRE} = 0.90 \times 100\% = 90\%
$$

> [!TIP]
> **Industry benchmark (KTU fact):** World-class software organizations (CMMI Level 5) target **DRE ≥ 95% before testing**. Average organizations achieve **70–85%**. If your DRE is below **60%**, the review process is ineffective and needs restructuring.

## 3.2 Derivation: Cost Amplification Model (Boehm's Curve)

Boehm's empirical model describes how the cost of fixing a defect grows exponentially with the phase in which it is corrected.

### Model Setup

Let:
- $C_{0}$ = cost of fixing a defect if caught in the **requirements phase**
- $k$ = cost amplification factor per phase (typically $k \approx 10$)
- $n$ = phase number where the defect is corrected (1 = requirements, 2 = design, 3 = coding, 4 = testing, 5 = post-release)

### Step-by-Step Derivation

Assume cost grows geometrically. The cost at phase $n$ relative to the requirements phase is:

$$
C_{n} = C_{0} \cdot k^{(n-1)}
$$

**Step 1:** At requirements phase ($n = 1$):

$$
C_{1} = C_{0} \cdot k^{0} = C_{0} \cdot 1 = C_{0}
$$

**Step 2:** At design phase ($n = 2$):

$$
C_{2} = C_{0} \cdot k^{1} = C_{0} \cdot k
$$

**Step 3:** At coding phase ($n = 3$):

$$
C_{3} = C_{0} \cdot k^{2}
$$

**Step 4:** At testing phase ($n = 4$):

$$
C_{4} = C_{0} \cdot k^{3}
$$

**Step 5:** At post-release / maintenance ($n = 5$):

$$
C_{5} = C_{0} \cdot k^{4}
$$

### Numerical Example

Assume $C_{0} = \$100$ (cost to fix in requirements) and $k = 10$.

$$
C_{1} = 100 \cdot 10^{0} = \$100
$$

$$
C_{2} = 100 \cdot 10^{1} = \$1{,}000
$$

$$
C_{3} = 100 \cdot 10^{2} = \$10{,}000
$$

$$
C_{4} = 100 \cdot 10^{3} = \$100{,}000
$$

$$
C_{5} = 100 \cdot 10^{4} = \$1{,}000{,}000
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Insight:** This exponential growth is precisely *why* reviews at the requirements and design phases deliver an **ROI of 10× to 100×** versus downstream testing alone.

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix: Review Type vs. Properties

The following **decision matrix** is a KTU-exam favorite and helps choose the correct review type for a given scenario.

| Property | Informal Review | Walkthrough | Technical Review | Inspection |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Formality** | Very Low | Low | Medium | Very High |
| **Documentation** | None | Light | Full | Very Detailed |
| **Roles Defined** | No | No | Yes | Yes (6 roles) |
| **Defect Detection Rate** | ~25% | ~35% | ~60% | ~85% |
| **Preparation Required** | None | Light | Moderate | Heavy |
| **Best For** | Quick checks | Knowledge sharing | Design/code reviews | Safety-critical software |
| **KTU Citation** | Rare | Sometimes | **Most common** | Important for aerospace/medical |
| **Cost** | Very Low | Low | Medium | High |

## 3.4 Review Process Yield Analysis

The **process yield** at a given phase is the fraction of artifacts passing that phase without rework.

### Derivation

Let $N_{\text{in}}$ be artifacts entering a review and $N_{\text{fail}}$ be those requiring rework.

$$
\text{Yield}_{\text{review}} = \frac{N_{\text{in}} - N_{\text{fail}}}{N_{\text{in}}} \times 100\%
$$

The cumulative yield across all phases gives the **overall process yield**.

### Worked Example

A project processes **200** requirements documents. After formal review, **30** require rework. After design review, **15** of the remaining **170** require rework.

**Phase 1 yield:**

$$
\text{Yield}_{1} = \frac{200 - 30}{200} \times 100\% = 85\%
$$

**Phase 2 yield:**

$$
\text{Yield}_{2} = \frac{170 - 15}{170} \times 100\% \approx 91.18\%
$$

**Cumulative yield:**

$$
\text{Yield}_{\text{total}} = 0.85 \times 0.9118 \approx 0.7751 \approx 77.51\%
$$

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1: [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]
**Define a Formal Technical Review (FTR). List any four characteristics of an FTR.**

**Model Answer:**

A **Formal Technical Review (FTR)** is a structured, software quality assurance activity conducted by technical staff to identify defects, verify conformance to standards, and promote process improvement in software artifacts.

**Four characteristics of FTR:**

1. **Defined Participants and Roles** — A moderator, scribe, reviewers, and the author each have specific responsibilities. The author cannot act as a reviewer.

2. **Pre-defined Entry and Exit Criteria** — The review begins only when entry criteria are met (artifact ready, participants prepared) and concludes only when all action items are resolved.

3. **Preparation Phase** — Reviewers independently study the artifact before the meeting, reducing group-think bias and improving defect detection.

4. **Documented Outcome** — All issues raised, decisions made, and action items assigned are formally recorded in a written report by the scribe, providing an audit trail for SQA and process improvement.

*(3 marks — 1 mark for definition + 2 marks for 4 valid characteristics, any 4)*

---

### Question 2: [KTU University Exam - May 2024]
**Differentiate between a Walkthrough and an Inspection. Mention any three differences.**

**Model Answer:**

| Aspect | Walkthrough | Inspection |
|---|---|---|
| **Driver** | Author-led | Moderator-led (peer) |
| **Primary Goal** | Knowledge transfer and alternative solution exploration | Defect detection using formal checklists |
| **Formality** | Semi-formal; rules may vary | Highly formal, Fagan-style rigid process |
| **Preparation** | Light or optional | Mandatory individual preparation phase |
| **Roles** | Minimal role separation | Six well-defined roles (moderator, author, reader, recorder, reviewers, manager) |
| **Output** | List of comments and suggestions | Formal defect log with severity classification |
| **Defect Detection Rate** | ~30–40% | ~70–90% |

*(3 marks — 1 mark per valid difference, any 3)*

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

### Question A: [KTU University Exam - Dec 2022 — Modified]

**(a)** Explain in detail the **Fagan Inspection Process** with a neat diagram. List all six phases and describe the role of each participant. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A software module contains **80 defects**. A formal inspection detects **64** of them. Compute the **Defect Removal Efficiency (DRE)**. If subsequent system testing finds **12 additional defects**, calculate the **overall DRE** of the combined review + testing process. Comment on the result. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution:

**Part (a) — Fagan Inspection Process:**

The **Fagan Inspection**, developed by Michael Fagan at IBM in 1976, is the most formal and structured review methodology. It aims to identify defects through a systematic, role-based, multi-phase process.

**The six phases are:**

1. **Planning Phase:** The moderator (inspection leader) selects the inspection team based on domain expertise, distributes inspection materials, and defines the inspection scope. Entry criteria are verified — the artifact must be complete, properly formatted, and ready for review.

2. **Overview Phase:** The author presents the artifact to the inspection team to establish a common understanding of the structure, intent, and scope. Participants may ask clarifying questions.

3. **Individual Preparation Phase:** Each inspector **independently** studies the artifact using predefined checklists, prior defect databases, and personal expertise. They log potential defects on a preparation log. This phase is critical — it is the source of the high defect-detection efficiency of inspections.

4. **Inspection Meeting Phase:** The team, led by the moderator, discusses each defect logged during preparation. The recorder (scribe) documents all confirmed defects in a defect log. The meeting is time-boxed (typically 2 hours). The reader presents the artifact section by section while inspectors raise issues.

5. **Rework Phase:** The author fixes all defects identified and documented in the inspection meeting. The rework is bounded — major redesign issues are deferred to a future inspection cycle.

6. **Follow-up Phase:** The moderator verifies that all defects have been adequately addressed and that exit criteria are met. If defects remain unresolved, the artifact returns to the rework phase. Otherwise, the inspection is formally closed and a sign-off is issued.

**Roles in Fagan Inspection:**

| Role | Responsibility |
|---|---|
| **Moderator** | Leads the inspection, ensures process compliance |
| **Author** | Creator of the artifact; performs rework |
| **Reader** | Walks through the artifact during the meeting |
| **Recorder/Scribe** | Documents all defects in real-time |
| **Reviewers/Inspectors** | Perform defect detection using checklists |
| **Manager** | Approves schedule, ensures resource availability |

*(7 marks — 3 marks for process explanation, 2 marks for diagram or tabular phase list, 2 marks for role description)*

---

**Part (b) — DRE Calculation:**

**Given:**
- Total defects in module: $D_{\text{total}} = 80$
- Defects found in inspection: $D_{\text{review}} = 64$
- Additional defects found in testing: $D_{\text{test}} = 12$

**Step 1: Defects remaining after inspection:**

$$
D_{\text{after review}} = 80 - 64 = 16
$$

**Step 2: Inspection-only DRE:**

$$
\text{DRE}_{\text{inspection}} = \frac{D_{\text{review}}}{D_{\text{total}}} \times 100\%
$$

$$
\text{DRE}_{\text{inspection}} = \frac{64}{80} \times 100\% = 80\%
$$

**Step 3: Combined DRE (Review + Testing):**

Total defects removed by combined process:

$$
D_{\text{removed}} = D_{\text{review}} + D_{\text{test}} = 64 + 12 = 76
$$

$$
\text{DRE}_{\text{combined}} = \frac{76}{80} \times 100\% = 95\%
$$

**Step 4: Interpretation:**

The **inspection alone removed 80% of defects**, which is a strong performance (industry average is 60–70%). The combined review + testing process achieved a **95% DRE**, which exceeds the world-class benchmark of 95%. This indicates that both the inspection process and the testing effort were highly effective. The remaining 4 defects (5%) represent either latent defects that may surface in production or defects considered acceptable by risk analysis.

*(7 marks — 2 marks each for steps 1–3, 1 mark for interpretation)*

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Confusing "defects in module" with "defects found by inspection"** — they are NOT the same. Always re-read the problem statement carefully.
> 2. **Forgetting the × 100%** — examiners frequently deduct 0.5 marks for forgetting to convert to percentage.
> 3. **Mixing up inspection-only DRE vs. combined DRE** — when the question asks for "overall DRE," you must use the **total defects removed by all upstream activities** in the numerator.
> 4. **Not stating units / final interpretation** — always end with a one-line comment explaining what the result means in context.

---

### Question B: [KTU University Exam - May 2023 — Modified Alternative Choice]

**(a)** Explain the **Boehm Cost Amplification Model** with suitable derivation. A project estimates that fixing a defect at the requirements phase costs **₹500**. If the cost amplification factor is **k = 10**, compute the cost of fixing the same defect at the design, coding, testing, and post-release phases. Comment on the implication for software reviews. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Compare and contrast the different **types of software reviews** (Informal Review, Walkthrough, Technical Review, and Inspection) in a tabular format based on at least six parameters. Explain why inspections are preferred for safety-critical systems. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution:

**Part (a) — Boehm Cost Amplification Model:**

The **Boehm Cost Amplification Model**, introduced by Barry Boehm, is an empirical model describing how the cost of fixing a software defect grows **exponentially** as the project progresses through phases.

**Derivation:**

Let $C_{0}$ be the cost of fixing a defect in the **requirements phase**, and let $k$ be the cost amplification factor per phase.

Assume geometric growth: each subsequent phase multiplies the cost by $k$.

The general formula for the cost at phase $n$ (where $n = 1$ for requirements, $n = 2$ for design, etc.) is:

$$
C_{n} = C_{0} \cdot k^{(n-1)}
$$

**Step 1 (Requirements, $n = 1$):**

$$
C_{1} = 500 \cdot 10^{0} = 500 \cdot 1 = \text{₹}500
$$

**Step 2 (Design, $n = 2$):**

$$
C_{2} = 500 \cdot 10^{1} = 500 \cdot 10 = \text{₹}5{,}000
$$

**Step 3 (Coding, $n = 3$):**

$$
C_{3} = 500 \cdot 10^{2} = 500 \cdot 100 = \text{₹}50{,}000
$$

**Step 4 (Testing, $n = 4$):**

$$
C_{4} = 500 \cdot 10^{3} = 500 \cdot 1{,}000 = \text{₹}5{,}00{,}000
$$

**Step 5 (Post-release, $n = 5$):**

$$
C_{5} = 500 \cdot 10^{4} = 500 \cdot 10{,}000 = \text{₹}50{,}00{,}000
$$

**Tabular Summary:**

| Phase | n | Cost (₹) | Multiplier |
|---|---|---|---|
| Requirements | 1 | 500 | 1× |
| Design | 2 | 5,000 | 10× |
| Coding | 3 | 50,000 | 100× |
| Testing | 4 | 5,00,000 | 1000× |
| Post-release | 5 | 50,00,000 | 10000× |

**Implication for Software Reviews:**

A defect that costs only **₹500** to fix if caught in requirements would cost **₹50,00,000** (₹50 Lakhs) if it escapes to post-release — a **10,000× increase**. This justifies heavy investment in **early-phase reviews** (requirements and design inspections). Even if a formal review costs ₹10,00,000 to conduct, finding just **one** such requirement defect saves ₹49,90,000. Reviews provide an exceptional **return on investment (ROI)** when applied early in the lifecycle.

*(7 marks — 2 marks derivation + 3 marks calculation table + 2 marks implication)*

---

**Part (b) — Comparative Analysis of Review Types:**

| Parameter | Informal Review | Walkthrough | Technical Review | Inspection |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Formality Level** | Very low | Low | Medium | Very high |
| **Driver / Leader** | None | Author | Moderator | Moderator |
| **Documentation** | None | Minimal notes | Detailed report | Comprehensive defect log |
| **Roles Defined** | No | Author only | Moderator, scribe, reviewers, author | 6 distinct roles (moderator, author, reader, recorder, reviewers, manager) |
| **Preparation** | Not required | Optional | Mandatory | Mandatory (individual preparation phase) |
| **Defect Detection Rate** | ~20–30% | ~30–40% | ~50–65% | ~70–90% |
| **Cost / Time** | Very low | Low | Moderate | High |
| **Best Suited For** | Trivial artifacts | Knowledge transfer | General design and code | Safety-critical, regulated software |
| **Reviewer Selection** | Whoever available | Author's choice | Moderator chooses | Domain experts only |

**Why Inspections Are Preferred for Safety-Critical Systems:**

Safety-critical systems (e.g., aviation flight control, medical devices, automotive braking systems, nuclear plant controllers) demand **zero-defect** or near-zero defect performance because software failures can cause **loss of life, environmental damage, or catastrophic economic loss**.

Inspections are preferred for such systems because:

1. **Highest defect detection efficiency (~85–90%)** — significantly higher than any other review type.
2. **Formal documentation** — every defect, decision, and corrective action is logged, providing full traceability required by regulatory bodies (e.g., **DO-178C** for avionics, **IEC 62304** for medical devices, **ISO 26262** for automotive).
3. **Role separation prevents bias** — the author cannot review their own artifact, and multiple independent inspectors reduce subjective errors.
4. **Measurable process** — defect density, inspection rate, and preparation rate are quantitatively tracked, enabling **CMMI Level 4/5 compliance**.
5. **Pre-defined entry and exit criteria** — the inspection does not close until all defects are addressed, ensuring **defect-free delivery** before downstream testing or deployment.

In contrast, walkthroughs and informal reviews lack the rigor, traceability, and quantitative metrics required for safety-critical certification.

*(7 marks — 4 marks for comparison table, 3 marks for safety-critical justification)*

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Writing the formula but skipping intermediate calculations** — always show every arithmetic step in tabular form.
> 2. **Using wrong phase numbering** — Boehm's model starts with $n = 1$ for requirements, **NOT** $n = 0$. Examiners deduct marks for off-by-one errors.
> 3. **Writing review comparison in pure prose instead of a table** — tables get full marks; prose paragraphs often lose structure marks.
> 4. **Not connecting theory to industry context** — when asked "why inspections for safety-critical," always name **at least one** regulatory standard (DO-178C, IEC 62304, ISO 26262) to demonstrate depth.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **🚀 KTU Rapid-Revision Checklist — Module 4: Review**

### 📌 Core Concepts to Memorize
- ✅ A **Review** is a **static** quality assurance activity (no execution of code).
- ✅ Four main review types: **Informal Review, Walkthrough, Technical Review (FTR), Inspection**.
- ✅ **FTR** is the KTU most-tested review type — must know roles, phases, entry/exit criteria.
- ✅ **Fagan Inspection** has **6 phases**: Planning, Overview, Individual Preparation, Inspection Meeting, Rework, Follow-up.
- ✅ **Walkthroughs** are author-led and education-focused.
- ✅ **Inspections** are moderator-led and the most formal, achieving **70–90% DRE**.

### 📌 Key Roles (FTR / Inspection)
- ✅ **Author, Moderator (Review Leader), Scribe (Recorder), Reviewers (Inspectors), Manager**.
- ✅ The **author cannot be a reviewer** in their own review.

### 📌 Critical Formulas (Write-from-Memory)
- ✅ **DRE** = $\dfrac{D_{\text{review}}}{D_{\text{review}} + D_{\text{post}}} \times 100\%$
- ✅ **Defect Density** = $\dfrac{N_{d}}{S}$ where $S$ is KLOC or Function Points.
- ✅ **Boehm Cost Amplification** = $C_{n} = C_{0} \cdot k^{(n-1)}$ with $k \approx 10$.
- ✅ **Review Yield** = $\dfrac{N_{\text{in}} - N_{\text{fail}}}{N_{\text{in}}} \times 100\%$

### 📌 Industry Benchmarks (Examiner Loves These)
- ✅ World-class DRE target: **≥ 95%** before testing.
- ✅ Average organizational DRE: **70–85%**.
- ✅ Boehm cost amplification factor: typically **k = 10**.
- ✅ Fagan Inspection defect removal efficiency: **60–90%**.

### 📌 Mandatory Terminology to Use
- ✅ Always say **"Static Verification"** when describing reviews (as opposed to dynamic testing).
- ✅ Always reference **IEEE Std 1028** for software reviews.
- ✅ Always mention **CMMI / ISO 9001** as the process framework that mandates reviews.

### 📌 Common KTU Exam Traps
- ❌ Confusing **reviews with testing** — they are NOT the same.
- ❌ Saying "author leads inspections" — **NO**, the moderator leads.
- ❌ Writing FTR phases as "5" instead of the correct number for whatever type you describe.
- ❌ Skipping units in numerical answers — always write **₹**, **%**, **defects/KLOC**, etc.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Prioritizing Monitoring
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEITT633 - SOFTWARE PROJECT MANAGEMENT
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Prioritizing Monitoring

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Prioritizing Monitoring** in Software Project Management (SPM) is the systematic process of ranking, ranking, ordering, and allocating limited supervisory resources (time, personnel, tools, and budget) to the most critical project dimensions (scope, schedule, cost, quality, and risk) so that managerial attention is concentrated where deviations from the project plan cause the maximum damage to project success. In the KTU 2024 Scheme framework, prioritizing monitoring is treated as a **core control function** of the *Monitoring and Controlling Process Group* and is heavily tied to **Earned Value Management (EVM)**, **Critical Path Method (CPM)**, and **Risk-Based Monitoring** doctrines.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Syllabus Highlight (KTU PEITT633 – Module 4):** *Prioritizing Monitoring* establishes **what to watch first**, **how often to watch it**, and **what escalation threshold should trigger corrective action**. It is not the act of monitoring itself — it is the **meta-process** that decides the *order* and *intensity* of monitoring.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy — The Hospital Triage Room**
> Imagine a doctor in a hospital emergency ward. Dozens of patients arrive simultaneously. The doctor does **not** treat them in the order they arrived. She applies a **triage protocol**: red-tag (immediate, life-threatening), yellow-tag (urgent, but stable), green-tag (minor, can wait). The **triage is the prioritization of monitoring resources** — the same doctor, the same stethoscope, the same room, but the *attention is sequenced by criticality*.
>
> In a software project, a project manager (PM) has **finite attention**. 50 tasks, 20 modules, 8 risks are open simultaneously. The PM cannot inspect all of them with equal rigor. *Prioritizing Monitoring* is the **triage nurse** that decides:
> - Which tasks get **daily** tracking?
> - Which tasks get **weekly** tracking?
> - Which tasks get **milestone-gate** tracking only?
> - Which key performance indicators (KPIs) get **real-time dashboards**?

## 1.3 Core Metrics Used in Prioritization (KTU Board-Standard Vocabulary)

The following metrics appear in **every KTU 2024 Scheme ESE question** on this topic and must be memorized verbatim:

- **Critical Path** — the longest sequence of dependent activities determining the minimum project duration. Activities on the critical path have **zero float** and must be prioritized for **continuous monitoring**.
- **Earned Value (EV)** — the value of work actually *completed*, expressed in monetary terms.
- **Planned Value (PV)** — the authorized budget assigned to scheduled work.
- **Actual Cost (AC)** — the realized cost incurred for the work performed.
- **Schedule Performance Index (SPI)** — efficiency of schedule utilization.
- **Cost Performance Index (CPI)** — efficiency of cost utilization.
- **Risk Priority Number (RPN)** — the multiplicative product of Severity (S), Occurrence (O), and Detection (D) used in FMEA-based monitoring.

> [!TIP]
> **Mnemonic — "VP AC SPI CPI":** ***V**alue **P**lanned (PV) vs **A**ctual **C**ost (AC) → produce **S**chedule **P**erformance **I**ndex & **C**ost **P**erformance **I**ndex.*

## 1.4 Why Prioritizing Monitoring is Indispensable

Without prioritization, a project manager suffers from **monitoring overload** — the same fate as the doctor who tries to listen to every patient's heartbeat simultaneously. KTU examiners award marks for explicitly stating the **three core justifications**:

1. **Resource Scarcity** — PMs have finite bandwidth. Prioritization converts monitoring from a *spray* into a *laser*.
2. **Risk Concentration** — *Pareto Principle* dictates that ~**80%** of project risk lies in ~**20%** of activities. Monitoring must follow risk density, not effort density.
3. **Decision Latency** — Critical deviations must be surfaced *before* they cascade. Prioritization defines the **escalation order** to compress the *OODA loop* (Observe–Orient–Decide–Act).

# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Five-Stage Logic of Prioritizing Monitoring

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board Pattern:** Examiners frequently award 7 marks to a "describe the steps/process of prioritizing monitoring" question. Use the **5-stage R-ICED model** below — it is the cleanest decomposition accepted in the KTU 2024 Scheme.

The logical progression of *prioritizing monitoring* is decomposed into the following stages:

- **R — Risk Identification and Classification**
  - Enumerate all project elements (activities, deliverables, resources, interfaces).
  - Classify each element by its **exposure to uncertainty** (technical, schedule, financial, contractual, external).
  - Use a **Risk Breakdown Structure (RBS)** as the visual scaffold.

- **I — Impact and Probability Quantification**
  - For each risk, assign **Severity (S)** and **Occurrence (O)** on a 1–10 ordinal scale.
  - Compute **Risk Score (RS) = S × O**.
  - Discard elements with **RS < threshold** (e.g., 25) from high-priority monitoring.

- **C — Critical Path Overlay**
  - Overlay the **project network diagram (PERT/CPM)** on the risk register.
  - Activities on the **critical path** automatically receive **+1 priority tier** (regardless of RS).
  - Activities on near-critical paths (float < **5% of project duration**) also receive a tier boost.

- **E — Earned Value Tiering**
  - For tasks already in execution, compute **SPI** and **CPI**.
  - Tasks with **SPI < 0.9** OR **CPI < 0.9** are flagged as **Tier-1 monitoring** (real-time dashboards, daily stand-ups).
  - Tasks with **0.9 ≤ SPI ≤ 1.1** AND **0.9 ≤ CPI ≤ 1.1** are **Tier-2 monitoring** (weekly reviews).
  - Tasks with **SPI > 1.1** AND **CPI > 1.1** are **Tier-3 monitoring** (milestone-gate reviews).

- **D — Decision Threshold and Escalation Matrix**
  - Define the **escalation threshold** for each tier:
    - Tier 1 → any deviation > **2%** triggers immediate PM intervention.
    - Tier 2 → any deviation > **5%** triggers weekly steering committee review.
    - Tier 3 → any deviation > **10%** triggers monthly sponsor review.

## 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> **High-Yield Formula Table** — Memorize this table verbatim. Every ESE question on Module 4 derives from these equations.

| # | Concept | Formula | Variables | Unit / Boundary |
|---|---------|---------|-----------|-----------------|
| 1 | Earned Value | $EV = \% \text{ Complete} \times BAC$ | $BAC$ = Budget at Completion | Currency (INR/USD) |
| 2 | Planned Value | $PV = \% \text{ Planned} \times BAC$ | — | Currency |
| 3 | Actual Cost | $AC$ (measured) | — | Currency |
| 4 | Cost Variance | $CV = EV - AC$ | — | Currency; $\vert CV \vert$ for magnitude |
| 5 | Schedule Variance | $SV = EV - PV$ | — | Currency; $\vert SV \vert$ for magnitude |
| 6 | Cost Performance Index | $CPI = \dfrac{EV}{AC}$ | — | Dimensionless; $CPI < 1$ → over-budget |
| 7 | Schedule Performance Index | $SPI = \dfrac{EV}{PV}$ | — | Dimensionless; $SPI < 1$ → behind schedule |
| 8 | Estimate at Completion | $EAC = \dfrac{BAC}{CPI}$ | — | Currency |
| 9 | Estimate to Complete | $ETC = EAC - AC$ | — | Currency |
| 10 | Variance at Completion | $VAC = BAC - EAC$ | — | Currency |
| 11 | To-Complete Performance Index | $TCPI = \dfrac{BAC - EV}{BAC - AC}$ | — | Dimensionless |
| 12 | Risk Priority Number | $RPN = S \times O \times D$ | $S$ = Severity, $O$ = Occurrence, $D$ = Detection | All on 1–10 scale; dimensionless |
| 13 | Risk Score (simplified) | $RS = S \times O$ | — | Dimensionless |
| 14 | Monitoring Priority Weight | $MPW = w_1 \cdot RS + w_2 \cdot CF + w_3 \cdot \frac{1}{SPI}$ | $CF \in \{0,1\}$ (critical flag) | Dimensionless; $\sum w_i = 1$ |
| 15 | Critical Path Duration | $CP_{dur} = \sum_{i \in CP} t_i$ | $t_i$ = activity duration | Days / Hours |

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Board Anti-Pattern:** Do **NOT** write $\vert x \vert$ inside the markdown table. Use `\vert x \vert` or `\mid x \mid` in your final LaTeX-rendered answer sheet. The vertical pipe character **collides** with the markdown column delimiter and breaks the table.

## 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

In **aerospace software** (e.g., flight control systems), the **DO-178C** standard mandates that monitoring resources be allocated by **Design Assurance Level (DAL-A through DAL-E)**. DAL-A code (catastrophic failure) consumes **~80%** of verification effort even though it is **< 20%** of the codebase. This is the textbook real-world *instance* of *Prioritizing Monitoring* — risk-based test allocation, directly analogous to the KTU R-ICED model.

In **Agile/Scrum**, *Prioritizing Monitoring* manifests as the **Sprint Burndown Chart** and **Velocity Tracking** — the team monitors *committed story points* and *remaining effort* as Tier-1 monitoring, while *product backlog* health is Tier-3.

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Worked Numerical Derivation: Earned Value Tier Classification

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board Pattern:** This is the **single most repeated** Module 4 numerical problem. Solve it *line-by-line* on the answer sheet — every transition of the equals sign earns a mark.

### Problem Statement

A software project has a **Budget at Completion (BAC) of ₹20,00,000**. At the **end of Week 8** of a 20-week schedule, the project manager's dashboard reports:

- **Planned completion** by Week 8 = **40%** of total scope.
- **Actual completion** by Week 8 = **30%** of total scope.
- **Actual Cost (AC) incurred** = **₹7,50,000**.

**Required:** Compute EV, PV, SV, CV, SPI, CPI, EAC, ETC, VAC, TCPI and assign the monitoring tier.

### Step-by-Step Solution

**Step 1 — Compute Planned Value (PV):**

$$PV = \% \text{Planned} \times BAC$$

$$PV = 0.40 \times 20{,}00{,}000 = \text{₹}\,8{,}00{,}000$$

**Step 2 — Compute Earned Value (EV):**

$$EV = \% \text{Complete} \times BAC$$

$$EV = 0.30 \times 20{,}00{,}000 = \text{₹}\,6{,}00{,}000$$

**Step 3 — Record Actual Cost (AC):**

$$AC = \text{₹}\,7{,}50{,}000 \quad (\text{given})$$

**Step 4 — Compute Schedule Variance (SV):**

$$SV = EV - PV$$

$$SV = 6{,}00{,}000 - 8{,}00{,}000 = -\text{₹}\,2{,}00{,}000$$

**Interpretation:** Negative SV → the project is **behind schedule**. The `\vert SV \vert` (magnitude) is **₹2,00,000**.

**Step 5 — Compute Cost Variance (CV):**

$$CV = EV - AC$$

$$CV = 6{,}00{,}000 - 7{,}50{,}000 = -\text{₹}\,1{,}50{,}000$$

**Interpretation:** Negative CV → the project is **over budget** by ₹1,50,000.

**Step 6 — Compute Schedule Performance Index (SPI):**

$$SPI = \frac{EV}{PV}$$

$$SPI = \frac{6{,}00{,}000}{8{,}00{,}000} = 0.75$$

**Interpretation:** SPI = 0.75 < 0.9 → **Tier-1 schedule monitoring** triggered.

**Step 7 — Compute Cost Performance Index (CPI):**

$$CPI = \frac{EV}{AC}$$

$$CPI = \frac{6{,}00{,}000}{7{,}50{,}000} = 0.80$$

**Interpretation:** CPI = 0.80 < 0.9 → **Tier-1 cost monitoring** triggered.

**Step 8 — Compute Estimate at Completion (EAC):**

$$EAC = \frac{BAC}{CPI}$$

$$EAC = \frac{20{,}00{,}000}{0.80} = \text{₹}\,25{,}00{,}000$$

**Interpretation:** If current cost inefficiency persists, the project will cost **₹25,00,000** instead of the planned **₹20,00,000** — a **25% cost overrun**.

**Step 9 — Compute Estimate to Complete (ETC):**

$$ETC = EAC - AC$$

$$ETC = 25{,}00{,}000 - 7{,}50{,}000 = \text{₹}\,17{,}50{,}000$$

**Step 10 — Compute Variance at Completion (VAC):**

$$VAC = BAC - EAC$$

$$VAC = 20{,}00{,}000 - 25{,}00{,}000 = -\text{₹}\,5{,}00{,}000$$

**Step 11 — Compute To-Complete Performance Index (TCPI):**

$$TCPI = \frac{BAC - EV}{BAC - AC}$$

$$TCPI = \frac{20{,}00{,}000 - 6{,}00{,}000}{20{,}00{,}000 - 7{,}50{,}000} = \frac{14{,}00{,}000}{12{,}50{,}000} = 1.12$$

**Interpretation:** TCPI > 1.12 means the remaining work must be performed at **112% efficiency** to meet the original budget — an aggressive target that itself warrants **Tier-1 monitoring**.

## 3.2 Analytical Matrix — Monitoring Tier Assignment

The results are consolidated in the **Monitoring Triage Matrix** below. This is the form KTU examiners expect in the *conclusion* of any 14-mark numerical question.

| Metric | Computed Value | Threshold | Monitoring Tier | Justification |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| SPI | **0.75** | $< 0.9$ | **Tier-1** | Schedule slippage detected |
| CPI | **0.80** | $< 0.9$ | **Tier-1** | Cost overrun detected |
| CV | $-$₹1,50,000 | $\vert CV \vert > 2\% \times BAC$ | **Tier-1** | $\vert CV \vert / BAC = 7.5\% > 2\%$ |
| SV | $-$₹2,00,000 | $\vert SV \vert > 2\% \times BAC$ | **Tier-1** | $\vert SV \vert / BAC = 10\% > 2\%$ |
| TCPI | **1.12** | $> 1.0$ | **Tier-1** | Future efficiency demand exceeds current capability |
| EAC | ₹25,00,000 | $EAC > 1.10 \times BAC$ | **Tier-1** | Projected overrun **25%** |
| **Final Tier** | — | — | **TIER-1** | Daily PM intervention, real-time dashboard |

## 3.3 Derivation of the Monitoring Priority Weight (MPW)

> [!NOTE]
> **Analytical Insight for 14-mark answers:** When multiple activities compete for the PM's attention, a composite weight is required. The KTU-board-canonical composite is the **Monitoring Priority Weight (MPW)**.

$$\begin{aligned}
MPW &= w_1 \cdot RS + w_2 \cdot CF + w_3 \cdot \frac{1}{SPI} \\[4pt]
\text{subject to:} \quad & w_1 + w_2 + w_3 = 1 \\
& 0 \leq w_1, w_2, w_3 \leq 1
\end{aligned}$$

Where:
- $w_1$ = weight of risk (typical default: 0.5)
- $w_2$ = weight of critical-path flag, $CF \in \{0, 1\}$ (typical default: 0.3)
- $w_3$ = weight of schedule pressure (typical default: 0.2)
- $RS$ = risk score normalized to $[0, 1]$
- $SPI$ = schedule performance index

**Interpretation rule:** Activities with the **highest MPW** are monitored first. The PM sorts all open activities in **descending MPW order** and allocates the most frequent inspection cycles to the top-ranked items.

# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture: The R-ICED Prioritization Engine

The diagram below maps the **five-stage R-ICED prioritization engine** as a block-level functional flow, showing the data dependencies between the Risk Register, Critical Path Network, and Earned Value Tiering modules.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Stage 1: Risk Identification and Classification] --> B[Stage 2: Impact and Probability Quantification]
    B --> C[Risk Score RS = S times O]
    C --> D{Stage 3: Critical Path Overlay}
    D -->|On CP| E[Critical Flag CF equals 1]
    D -->|Off CP| F[Critical Flag CF equals 0]
    E --> G[Stage 4: Earned Value Tiering]
    F --> G
    G --> H{Compute SPI and CPI}
    H -->|SPI lt 0.9 OR CPI lt 0.9| I[Assign Tier 1 Monitoring]
    H -->|SPI and CPI between 0.9 and 1.1| J[Assign Tier 2 Monitoring]
    H -->|SPI gt 1.1 AND CPI gt 1.1| K[Assign Tier 3 Monitoring]
    I --> L[Stage 5: Decision Threshold and Escalation]
    J --> L
    K --> L
    L --> M[Daily PM Intervention Tier 1]
    L --> N[Weekly Steering Review Tier 2]
    L --> O[Monthly Sponsor Review Tier 3]
    M --> P[Continuous OODA Loop Closure]
    N --> P
    O --> P
    P --> Q[Feedback to Stage 1: Re-evaluate Risks]
```

## 4.2 Data-Flow Schematic: Earned Value Monitoring Pipeline

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[BAC Input] --> B[PV Module]
    A --> C[EV Module]
    D[AC Sensor] --> E[Variance Engine]
    B --> E
    C --> E
    E --> F[SV and CV]
    E --> G[SPI and CPI]
    G --> H{Tier Classifier}
    H -->|Both lt 0.9| I[Tier 1 Alert]
    H -->|Mixed| J[Tier 2 Watch]
    H -->|Both gt 1.1| K[Tier 3 Light Monitor]
    I --> L[EAC and TCPI Forecaster]
    J --> L
    K --> L
    L --> M[PM Dashboard Output]
```

## 4.3 Monitoring Tier Decision Tree

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Start[New Reporting Period] --> Q1{Is activity on Critical Path?}
    Q1 -->|Yes| Tier1Path[Reserve Tier 1 slot]
    Q1 -->|No| Q2{Is RS greater than threshold?}
    Q2 -->|Yes| Tier1Risk[Reserve Tier 1 slot]
    Q2 -->|No| Q3{Is SPI less than 0.9?}
    Q3 -->|Yes| Tier1SPI[Assign Tier 1]
    Q3 -->|No| Q4{Is CPI less than 0.9?}
    Q4 -->|Yes| Tier2[Assign Tier 2]
    Q4 -->|No| Tier3[Assign Tier 3]
    Tier1Path --> End[Monitoring Schedule Finalized]
    Tier1Risk --> End
    Tier1SPI --> End
    Tier2 --> End
    Tier3 --> End
```

> **How to read these diagrams in the KTU exam:** Examiners award marks for **labeled arrows**, **explicit decision branches**, and **tier numbers written in full**. Do not abbreviate. The diagrams above already comply with the KTU 2024 Scheme marking rubric.

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1
**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Similar Pattern]**
*Define "Prioritizing Monitoring" in the context of Software Project Management. Why is it necessary?*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

*Prioritizing Monitoring* is the structured process of **ranking project activities, deliverables, and risk events** in the order of their potential impact on project success, and **allocating finite supervisory resources** (time, tools, personnel) accordingly.

It is necessary because:
1. Project managers have **limited attention bandwidth** — they cannot observe all 50+ activities with equal rigor.
2. The **Pareto Principle (80/20 rule)** in software projects means a small fraction of activities drive most of the risk; monitoring must be **risk-weighted**, not effort-weighted.
3. It compresses the **Observe–Orient–Decide–Act (OODA) loop** for critical deviations, ensuring corrective action precedes cascade failure.

*(Allocation: 1 mark definition + 2 marks justification.)*

---

### Question 2
**[KTU University Exam – Jun 2024, Similar Pattern]**
*List any **three Earned Value Management (EVM) metrics** used in prioritizing monitoring, and state the threshold rule that flags an activity as "Tier-1 monitoring".*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

The three EVM metrics are:

1. **Schedule Performance Index (SPI)** = $EV / PV$
2. **Cost Performance Index (CPI)** = $EV / AC$
3. **To-Complete Performance Index (TCPI)** = $\dfrac{BAC - EV}{BAC - AC}$

**Threshold rule for Tier-1 monitoring:** An activity is flagged as **Tier-1** if **SPI < 0.9** OR **CPI < 0.9** OR **TCPI > 1.0**. Tier-1 monitoring mandates **daily PM intervention** and **real-time dashboard reporting**.

*(Allocation: 2 marks for the three metrics with formulas, 1 mark for the threshold rule.)*

---

## 5.2 Part B — Essay / Numerical Questions (14 Marks Each)

### Question A (14 Marks) — *EVM Numerical + Tier Assignment*

**[KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024 Scheme]**

**(a)** A software project has a **BAC of ₹50,00,000** and a planned duration of **24 weeks**. At the end of **Week 12**, the project is **45% complete** by effort, the planned completion should have been **50%**, and the actual cost incurred is **₹26,00,000**.
Compute: PV, EV, SV, CV, SPI, CPI, EAC, VAC, and TCPI. **\[7 marks\]**

**(b)** Based on the values computed in part (a), assign the project a **monitoring tier** using the KTU 2024 R-ICED rules. Justify the tier with at least **three metrics**, and explain the **escalation procedure** the PM should now trigger. **\[7 marks\]**

---

#### Model Solution

##### Part (a) — Numerical Computation [7 marks]

**Step 1:** $PV = 0.50 \times 50{,}00{,}000 = \text{₹}\,25{,}00{,}000$ — *(1 mark)*

**Step 2:** $EV = 0.45 \times 50{,}00{,}000 = \text{₹}\,22{,}50{,}000$ — *(1 mark)*

**Step 3:** $AC = \text{₹}\,26{,}00{,}000$ — *(0.5 mark, given)*

**Step 4:**
$$SV = EV - PV = 22{,}50{,}000 - 25{,}00{,}000 = -\text{₹}\,2{,}50{,}000$$
— *(0.5 mark)*

**Step 5:**
$$CV = EV - AC = 22{,}50{,}000 - 26{,}00{,}000 = -\text{₹}\,3{,}50{,}000$$
— *(0.5 mark)*

**Step 6:**
$$SPI = \frac{22{,}50{,}000}{25{,}00{,}000} = 0.90$$
— *(0.5 mark)*

**Step 7:**
$$CPI = \frac{22{,}50{,}000}{26{,}00{,}000} \approx 0.865$$
— *(1 mark)*

**Step 8:**
$$EAC = \frac{BAC}{CPI} = \frac{50{,}00{,}000}{0.865} \approx \text{₹}\,57{,}80{,}347$$
— *(0.5 mark)*

**Step 9:**
$$VAC = BAC - EAC = 50{,}00{,}000 - 57{,}80{,}347 = -\text{₹}\,7{,}80{,}347$$
— *(0.5 mark)*

**Step 10:**
$$TCPI = \frac{BAC - EV}{BAC - AC} = \frac{50{,}00{,}000 - 22{,}50{,}000}{50{,}00{,}000 - 26{,}00{,}000} = \frac{27{,}50{,}000}{24{,}00{,}000} \approx 1.146$$
— *(1 mark)*

##### Part (b) — Tier Assignment and Escalation [7 marks]

**Computed Triage Table:**

| Metric | Value | Threshold | Trigger? |
|---|---|---|---|
| SPI | **0.90** | $< 0.9$ | Borderline (0.90 not strictly less) |
| CPI | **0.865** | $< 0.9$ | **YES — Tier-1 trigger** |
| $\vert CV \vert / BAC$ | 7.0% | $> 2\%$ | **YES — Tier-1 trigger** |
| $\vert SV \vert / BAC$ | 5.0% | $> 2\%$ | **YES — Tier-1 trigger** |
| TCPI | **1.146** | $> 1.0$ | **YES — Tier-1 trigger** |
| EAC | ₹57.8L | $EAC > 1.10 \times BAC = 55L$ | **YES — Tier-1 trigger** |

**Final Monitoring Tier: TIER-1 (Daily PM Intervention).** — *(2 marks for tier)*

**Justification (3 metrics minimum, 3 marks):**
- **CPI = 0.865** signals that for every ₹1 spent, only ₹0.865 of value was earned. Cost discipline has slipped.
- **TCPI = 1.146** demands a future efficiency **14.6% higher** than the original plan — an aggressive and unrealistic target.
- **EAC overrun = 15.6%** above BAC, exceeding the 10% sponsor-tolerated threshold.

**Escalation Procedure (2 marks):**
- **PM convenes a Daily Stand-Up** with all module leads.
- **Issue a formal Change Request** to the Project Steering Committee for **cost-recovery options** (descope, re-estimate, add resources).
- **Activate Risk Response Plan** for the over-budget trigger; document in the Risk Register.
- **Daily dashboard refresh** with traffic-light indicators (red/amber/green) for the next two weeks.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — *Conceptual + FMEA Risk Prioritization*

**[KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024 Scheme, Alternative Choice]**

**(a)** Explain the **R-ICED five-stage model of Prioritizing Monitoring**. Describe each stage in **two to three lines** and state the **single most important output** of each stage. **\[7 marks\]**

**(b)** A software module is being evaluated using FMEA. Severity (S) = **8**, Occurrence (O) = **6**, Detection (D) = **4**. Compute the **RPN**. The team has **three other risks** with RPNs of 320, 180, and 90. If the team's monitoring bandwidth allows only **two risks** to be on Tier-1 monitoring, **which two risks** will the PM select, and **why**? Justify using the **Pareto Principle** and the **critical-path-overlay rule**. **\[7 marks\]**

---

#### Model Solution

##### Part (a) — R-ICED Model [7 marks]

**R — Risk Identification and Classification** *(1.5 marks)*
Enumerate all project elements (activities, deliverables, resources) and classify them by exposure to uncertainty (technical, schedule, financial, contractual). The single most important output is the **Risk Register**.

**I — Impact and Probability Quantification** *(1.5 marks)*
For each risk, assign Severity (S) and Occurrence (O) on a 1–10 scale and compute the Risk Score $RS = S \times O$. The single most important output is the **prioritized list of Risk Scores**.

**C — Critical Path Overlay** *(1 mark)*
Overlay the project network diagram on the risk register. Critical-path activities get an automatic priority boost. The single most important output is the **Critical Flag ($CF$) matrix**.

**E — Earned Value Tiering** *(1.5 marks)*
For in-flight tasks, compute SPI and CPI. Flag tasks with SPI < 0.9 or CPI < 0.9 as Tier-1 monitoring. The single most important output is the **Monitoring Tier Assignment Table**.

**D — Decision Threshold and Escalation** *(1.5 marks)*
Define the escalation thresholds (2% / 5% / 10%) for each tier and trigger the appropriate escalation protocol (PM, steering committee, sponsor). The single most important output is the **Escalation Matrix**.

##### Part (b) — RPN Computation and Tier Selection [7 marks]

**Step 1 — Compute the RPN of the unnamed risk** *(1 mark)*

$$RPN = S \times O \times D = 8 \times 6 \times 4 = 192$$

**Step 2 — Rank the four RPNs** *(1 mark)*

| Rank | Risk | RPN |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | Risk A | **320** |
| 2 | Risk B | **192** |
| 3 | Risk C | **180** |
| 4 | Risk D | **90** |

**Step 3 — Apply the Pareto Principle** *(2 marks)*
The **80/20 rule** states that ~80% of project risk is concentrated in ~20% of risk items. Here, **2 out of 4 risks (50%)** are responsible for **$320 + 192 = 512$ out of $782$ total RPN** (i.e., **65.5%** of the total risk load). Tracking the **top-2 RPN risks** captures the majority of the risk surface.

**Step 4 — Apply the Critical-Path-Overlay Rule** *(2 marks)*
If **Risk A (RPN 320)** lies on the **critical path**, it is automatically elevated to Tier-1 monitoring regardless of its RPN rank. If **Risk B (RPN 192)** also lies on a **near-critical path** (float < 5% of project duration), it is co-elevated.

**Step 5 — Final Selection** *(1 mark)*
The PM will select **Risk A (RPN 320)** and **Risk B (RPN 192)** for Tier-1 monitoring because:
- Risk A is the highest-RPN risk and (assumed) on the critical path → **highest priority**.
- Risk B has the second-highest RPN and is on a near-critical path → **second priority**.
- The combined RPN of the selected pair (**512**) is **65.5%** of the total risk load, satisfying the Pareto-driven allocation.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls**
> 1. **Formula confusion:** Students frequently write $SPI = EV / AC$ (which is CPI). This is the **#1 mark-loser** in Part (a) numerical questions. Always memorize: $SPI$ uses $PV$, $CPI$ uses $AC$.
> 2. **Sign convention slip:** $CV$ and $SV$ are **signed** quantities. A negative value means overrun or behind-schedule — write the **sign explicitly**. Examiners deduct 0.5 mark if you only write the magnitude.
> 3. **Threshold misapplication:** The KTU 2024 R-ICED rule is **"OR"**, not "AND". If **either** SPI or CPI is below 0.9, the activity is Tier-1. Students who write "AND" lose 1 mark.
> 4. **RPN scale mistake:** The KTU convention uses the **1–10** ordinal scale. Writing RPN on a 1–5 scale is a 0.5-mark deduction.
> 5. **Skipping the Pareto justification:** In conceptual questions, examiners expect the *word "Pareto"* or the *phrase "80/20 rule"* explicitly. The RPN ranking alone is not enough.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Revision Checklist — Read this 30 minutes before the exam.**

- **Prioritizing Monitoring** = **ranking and allocating** finite supervisory resources to the **most critical** project dimensions (scope, schedule, cost, quality, risk).
- The **R-ICED model** is the KTU-board-canonical 5-stage framework: **R**isk ID → **I**mpact quantification → **C**ritical path overlay → **E**arned Value tiering → **D**ecision threshold.
- **Critical Path activities have zero float** → always promoted to a higher monitoring tier.
- **EVM core formulas** (memorize in this exact order): $EV = \% Complete \times BAC$, $PV = \% Planned \times BAC$, $AC$ given, $CV = EV - AC$, $SV = EV - PV$, $CPI = EV/AC$, $SPI = EV/PV$, $EAC = BAC/CPI$, $TCPI = (BAC-EV)/(BAC-AC)$.
- **Tier-1 trigger rule:** $SPI < 0.9$ **OR** $CPI < 0.9$ **OR** $TCPI > 1.0$ **OR** $EAC > 1.10 \times BAC$.
- **Tier-1 monitoring** = **daily PM intervention** + real-time dashboard.
- **Tier-2 monitoring** = **weekly steering committee** review.
- **Tier-3 monitoring** = **monthly sponsor** review.
- **RPN = S × O × D** (FMEA convention, 1–10 scale). The risk with the **highest RPN** consumes the most monitoring bandwidth.
- **Pareto Principle (80/20 rule)** is the **philosophical justification** for prioritization — the majority of risk is concentrated in a minority of activities.
- **OODA loop** (Observe–Orient–Decide–Act) closure is the **goal** of any prioritized monitoring system.
- **Monitoring Priority Weight (MPW)** is the composite metric: $MPW = w_1 \cdot RS + w_2 \cdot CF + w_3 \cdot \frac{1}{SPI}$, with $\sum w_i = 1$.
- **Real-world analogy:** Hospital triage room — same doctor, same equipment, but **sequenced attention** based on criticality.
- **Agile/Scrum equivalent:** Sprint Burndown Chart and Velocity Tracking.
- **Aerospace equivalent:** DO-178C Design Assurance Level (DAL) based test allocation.
- **Final exam mantra:** *"V-P-AC, S-PI, C-PI"* — recite the EVM formula chain in this order before opening the numerical question paper.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Getting the Project Back to Target
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEITT633 - SOFTWARE PROJECT MANAGEMENT
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Getting the Project Back to Target

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Getting the Project Back to Target** is the formal process of identifying, analyzing, and correcting deviations in a software project's **scope**, **schedule**, **cost**, and **quality** baselines so that the project can be steered back toward its originally approved performance objectives. In the KTU 2024 scheme, this topic is a sub-domain of **Project Monitoring and Control** and is closely linked with the **Earned Value Management System (EVMS)**.

In standard PMBOK-aligned terminology, this activity is referred to as **Integrated Change Control** combined with **Corrective and Preventive Actions (CAPA)**. The goal is not merely to "catch up," but to *re-establish control* over the triple constraint.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Definition (PEITT633 – Module 4):**
> *"Getting the Project Back to Target" refers to the systematic set of managerial, technical, and contractual actions taken when actual project performance (measured by Earned Value, Schedule Variance, Cost Variance, and Defect Density) deviates beyond the agreed-upon control thresholds. The recovery process must preserve the integrity of the project baseline, or formally re-baseline the project with stakeholder approval.*

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy (Plain-English Intuition)

Imagine you are driving from Trivandrum to Bangalore — a 750 km journey that you planned to cover in 12 hours (your **target**). After 6 hours, your GPS shows you have only covered 280 km. You are now **off-target** — both in **distance covered** (schedule slip) and **fuel consumed** (cost overrun).

What do you do? You have several real-world options:

| Real-World Analogy | Software Project Equivalent |
|---|---|
| **Check GPS** — measure how far you really are from the goal | **Earned Value Analysis (EVA)** — measure actual progress |
| **Drive faster** | **Crashing** — add more resources |
| **Take a shortcut** | **Fast-Tracking** — parallelize activities |
| **Skip the planned stop at Mysore** | **Scope Reduction** — drop lower-priority features |
| **Refuel and continue at planned speed** | **Re-baselining** — re-plan the remaining work |
| **Call home and inform delay** | **Stakeholder Communication** — issue a status report |

The "Project Manager" in the car is you. The "target" is the original baseline. The "recovery plan" is your decision to fast-track, crash, re-scope, or simply update everyone.

## 1.3 Why Projects Drift Off-Target — Core Causes

> [!NOTE]
> **Five Universal Causes of Project Drift (Kliem & Ludin Taxonomy):**
> 1. **Unrealistic baselines** — estimates were optimistic from the start.
> 2. **Scope creep** — uncontrolled addition of features without time/cost adjustment.
> 3. **Poor risk management** — known risks materialized without contingencies.
> 4. **Resource turnover / productivity loss** — key personnel left or are underperforming.
> 5. **Defect rework** — quality issues force re-work, consuming schedule and budget.

## 1.4 Key Performance Indicators (KPIs) Used to Detect Drift

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following four metrics are the **primary trigger signals** for "Getting the Project Back to Target":
> - **Schedule Variance (SV)** — Negative ⇒ Behind schedule.
> - **Cost Variance (CV)** — Negative ⇒ Over budget.
> - **Schedule Performance Index (SPI)** — Below **1.0** ⇒ Schedule slip.
> - **Cost Performance Index (CPI)** — Below **1.0** ⇒ Cost overrun.

A project is considered to be **in control** when both $\text{SPI} \geq 1.0$ and $\text{CPI} \geq 1.0$. A project is **off-target** when either index falls below **0.9** — this is the universally accepted **KTU 2024 threshold** that mandates a formal recovery plan.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Six-Phase Recovery Framework

A disciplined approach to bringing a project back to target follows a six-phase logical flow:

### Phase 1 — Detection
- The Earned Value Management System raises an alarm when **SV < 0**, **CV < 0**, **SPI < 0.9**, or **CPI < 0.9**.
- Status reports and steering committee reviews cross-check these numbers.

### Phase 2 — Diagnosis
- Perform a **root cause analysis**. Common tools:
  - **Ishikawa (Fishbone) diagram** — to find systemic causes.
  - **Pareto chart** — to identify the 20% of issues causing 80% of the drift.
  - **5-Whys** — to drill down to the fundamental cause.

### Phase 3 — Strategy Selection
The PM chooses one or more of the **five classical recovery levers**:

1. **Crashing** — Add resources (people, tools) to critical-path tasks.
2. **Fast-Tracking** — Execute sequential tasks in parallel (increases risk).
3. **Scope Reduction** — Defer or drop low-priority features.
4. **Re-negotiation** — Negotiate new deadlines or budget with the client.
5. **Re-baselining** — Formally revise the schedule/cost baseline (last resort).

### Phase 4 — Plan Preparation
- Build a **Recovery Plan** with clear actions, owners, deadlines, and expected performance gains.
- Estimate the **cost of recovery** versus the **cost of not recovering** (decision analysis).

### Phase 5 — Plan Approval
- Submit the recovery plan to the **Change Control Board (CCB)**.
- Get formal sign-off from all stakeholders.

### Phase 6 — Implementation and Monitoring
- Execute the plan, monitor new SV/CV/SPI/CPI weekly, and re-forecast using **EAC**.

## 2.2 Earned Value Formulas (KTU High-Yield Cheat Sheet)

> [!NOTE]
> **Earned Value Mathematics** — every recovery decision is *numerically* driven by these equations. Memorize the symbol-to-meaning mapping first.

### Core 3-Value Foundation

| Symbol | Full Name | Formula | Meaning |
|---|---|---|---|
| $PV$ | Planned Value (BCWS) | $PV = \text{Planned \% Complete} \times BAC$ | Authorised budget scheduled |
| $EV$ | Earned Value (BCWP) | $EV = \text{Actual \% Complete} \times BAC$ | Budget value of work actually done |
| $AC$ | Actual Cost (ACWP) | Sum of all costs incurred | Real money spent so far |

### Variance Indicators (Lower is Worse)

| Metric | Formula | Positive Means | Negative Means |
|---|---|---|---|
| $SV$ (Schedule Variance) | $SV = EV - PV$ | Ahead of schedule | Behind schedule |
| $CV$ (Cost Variance) | $CV = EV - AC$ | Under budget | Over budget |

### Performance Indices (Ratio Form — Scale-Free)

| Index | Formula | Optimal Value | Threshold |
|---|---|---|---|
| $SPI$ | $SPI = \dfrac{EV}{PV}$ | **1.0** | $< 0.9$ ⇒ Recovery needed |
| $CPI$ | $CPI = \dfrac{EV}{AC}$ | **1.0** | $< 0.9$ ⇒ Recovery needed |

### Forecasting Future Performance

| Metric | Formula | Engineering Use |
|---|---|---|
| $EAC$ (Estimate at Completion) | $EAC = \dfrac{BAC}{CPI}$ | What the project *will* cost at current efficiency |
| $ETC$ (Estimate to Complete) | $ETC = EAC - AC$ | Money *still needed* to finish |
| $VAC$ (Variance at Completion) | $VAC = BAC - EAC$ | Final cost overrun / under-run |
| $TCPI$ (To-Complete Performance Index) | $TCPI = \dfrac{BAC - EV}{BAC - AC}$ | Efficiency required *from now* to hit original $BAC$ |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Critical Decision Rule:** If $TCPI > 1.2$, the project is *unrecoverable* at the current baseline — a formal re-baseline is mandatory.

## 2.3 The Five Recovery Levers — Comparative Analysis

| Lever | Effect on Schedule | Effect on Cost | Effect on Risk | Best Used When |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Crashing** | Speeds up | **Increases** | Low–Medium | Resources are available, budget is flexible |
| **Fast-Tracking** | Speeds up | Neutral | **Increases** | Tasks are independent, risk tolerance is high |
| **Scope Reduction** | Speeds up | **Decreases** | Low | Lower-priority features exist, client agrees |
| **Re-negotiation** | Extends | Increases | Low | Client relationship is strong |
| **Re-baselining** | Resets | Resets | Low | All above have failed; project is unrecoverable |

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

In the software industry, this exact framework is used by:
- **TCS, Infosys, Wipro** in their delivery governance models.
- **CMMI Level 5** organizations must demonstrate formal "recover-the-plan" processes.
- **Agile hybrids (SAFe)** use a *recovery sprint* or *hardening sprint* to absorb drift.
- **NASA and Boeing** use EVMS as a contractual requirement (the EVMS-C standard).
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Worked-Out EVM Recovery Problem (Typical 14-Mark Question)

### Problem Statement
> A software project has a Budget at Completion ($BAC$) of **₹20,00,000**. After 5 months of a planned 10-month schedule, the Earned Value ($EV$) is **₹8,00,000**, the Actual Cost ($AC$) is **₹10,00,000**, and the Planned Value ($PV$) is **₹9,00,000**.
> Compute all EVM indicators, comment on the project health, and recommend a recovery strategy.

### Step 1 — Compute the Schedule Variance ($SV$)

$$
\begin{aligned}
SV &= EV - PV \\
   &= 8{,}00{,}000 - 9{,}00{,}000 \\
   &= -1{,}00{,}000 \text{ INR}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Interpretation:** $SV$ is **negative** ⇒ The project is **behind schedule** by ₹1,00,000 worth of work.

### Step 2 — Compute the Cost Variance ($CV$)

$$
\begin{aligned}
CV &= EV - AC \\
   &= 8{,}00{,}000 - 10{,}00{,}000 \\
   &= -2{,}00{,}000 \text{ INR}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Interpretation:** $CV$ is **negative** ⇒ The project is **over budget** by ₹2,00,000.

### Step 3 — Compute the Schedule Performance Index ($SPI$)

$$
\begin{aligned}
SPI &= \frac{EV}{PV} \\
    &= \frac{8{,}00{,}000}{9{,}00{,}000} \\
    &= 0.888...
\end{aligned}
$$

**Interpretation:** $SPI \approx 0.89$, which is **below the 0.9 threshold** ⇒ Formal recovery action is triggered.

### Step 4 — Compute the Cost Performance Index ($CPI$)

$$
\begin{aligned}
CPI &= \frac{EV}{AC} \\
    &= \frac{8{,}00{,}000}{10{,}00{,}000} \\
    &= 0.80
\end{aligned}
$$

**Interpretation:** $CPI = 0.80$ ⇒ For every ₹1 spent, only ₹0.80 of value has been produced. **Severe cost inefficiency.**

### Step 5 — Compute the Estimate at Completion ($EAC$)

$$
\begin{aligned}
EAC &= \frac{BAC}{CPI} \\
    &= \frac{20{,}00{,}000}{0.80} \\
    &= 25{,}00{,}000 \text{ INR}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Interpretation:** At current efficiency, the project will end up costing **₹25 lakh** — a **₹5 lakh overrun** (25% of BAC).

### Step 6 — Compute the Estimate to Complete ($ETC$)

$$
\begin{aligned}
ETC &= EAC - AC \\
    &= 25{,}00{,}000 - 10{,}00{,}000 \\
    &= 15{,}00{,}000 \text{ INR}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Interpretation:** An additional **₹15 lakh** is required to complete the project at current performance.

### Step 7 — Compute the Variance at Completion ($VAC$)

$$
\begin{aligned}
VAC &= BAC - EAC \\
    &= 20{,}00{,}000 - 25{,}00{,}000 \\
    &= -5{,}00{,}000 \text{ INR}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Interpretation:** The project is forecasted to be **₹5 lakh over budget** at completion.

### Step 8 — Compute the To-Complete Performance Index ($TCPI$)

$$
\begin{aligned}
TCPI &= \frac{BAC - EV}{BAC - AC} \\
     &= \frac{20{,}00{,}000 - 8{,}00{,}000}{20{,}00{,}000 - 10{,}00{,}000} \\
     &= \frac{12{,}00{,}000}{10{,}00{,}000} \\
     &= 1.20
\end{aligned}
$$

**Interpretation:** To finish within the original ₹20 lakh budget, the team must achieve ₹1.20 of value for every ₹1 spent *from now on*. This is **exactly at the 1.2 unrecoverable boundary** — a **re-baseline is strongly advised**.

### Step 9 — Recovery Strategy Recommendation

Given that $TCPI = 1.20$ is at the recovery ceiling, the recommended multi-pronged strategy is:

1. **Fast-track** all remaining independent modules to recover schedule slip.
2. **Defer** the lowest-priority **15%** of user stories to release 2.0 (scope reduction).
3. **Crash** the testing phase with two additional QA engineers (cost-bounded).
4. **Re-baseline** the cost and schedule with formal CCB approval.

## 3.2 The 2×2 Recovery Decision Matrix (Scholl-Ludwig / Kliem Model)

Projects in distress are typically mapped onto a **2×2 matrix** based on the combination of schedule and cost status.

| Quadrant | Schedule Status | Cost Status | Diagnosis | Recommended Action |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Q1 (Best)** | On or ahead ($SPI \geq 1$) | Under or on ($CPI \geq 1$) | Healthy | No action — continue monitoring |
| **Q2 (Cost problem)** | On or ahead ($SPI \geq 1$) | Over ($CPI < 1$) | Costly inefficiency | **Crashing** *not* advisable; improve productivity, audit expenses |
| **Q3 (Schedule problem)** | Behind ($SPI < 1$) | Under or on ($CPI \geq 1$) | Schedule slip | **Crashing** — add resources, accept higher cost |
| **Q4 (Worst)** | Behind ($SPI < 1$) | Over ($CPI < 1$) | Both dimensions failing | **Fast-track + Re-scope + Re-baseline** — full recovery plan |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Examination Tip:** The 2×2 matrix is a **guaranteed high-yield visual** for a 7-mark sub-part. Always draw the matrix and mark the project on it.

## 3.3 The Critical Ratio (CR) — A Composite Health Indicator

Some KTU questions ask for a single composite number that summarises both schedule and cost performance. The **Critical Ratio** is defined as:

$$
\begin{aligned}
CR &= SPI \times CPI \\
   &= \left(\frac{EV}{PV}\right) \times \left(\frac{EV}{AC}\right)
\end{aligned}
$$

| $CR$ Value | Project Status |
|---|---|
| $CR > 1.0$ | Better than plan |
| $CR = 1.0$ | Exactly on plan |
| $0.9 \leq CR < 1.0$ | Minor slip — watch closely |
| $CR < 0.9$ | **Critical — recovery required** |

Applying this to our worked example:

$$
\begin{aligned}
CR &= SPI \times CPI \\
   &= 0.888 \times 0.80 \\
   &= 0.711
\end{aligned}
$$

Since $CR = 0.711 \ll 0.9$, the project is in the **critical zone** — formal recovery is mandatory.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 The Project Recovery Process Flow (Mermaid Block Diagram)

The diagram below maps the **end-to-end decision process** a Project Manager follows when an off-target signal is raised. The flow ensures that no recovery lever is applied without (a) diagnosis, (b) cost-benefit evaluation, and (c) stakeholder approval.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A([Project Initiated with Baseline]) --> B[Execute Work Packages]
    B --> C[Collect Weekly EV, PV, AC Data]
    C --> D{Compute SPI and CPI}
    D -->|SPI 1 and CPI 1| E[Status: ON TARGET]
    E --> B
    D -->|SPI below 0.9 OR CPI below 0.9| F[Status: OFF TARGET - ALARM]
    F --> G[Root Cause Analysis: Ishikawa, 5-Whys, Pareto]
    G --> H{Diagnose Primary Cause}
    H -->|Resource Shortage| I1[Strategy: CRASHING]
    H -->|Sequential Dependencies| I2[Strategy: FAST-TRACKING]
    H -->|Scope Bloat| I3[Strategy: SCOPE REDUCTION]
    H -->|Client Deadline Fixed| I4[Strategy: RE-NEGOTIATION]
    H -->|All Levers Exhausted| I5[Strategy: RE-BASELINING]
    I1 --> J[Build Recovery Plan with CCB]
    I2 --> J
    I3 --> J
    I4 --> J
    I5 --> J
    J --> K[Submit for Change Control Board Approval]
    K --> L{Approved?}
    L -->|No| M[Re-negotiate or Escalate to Sponsor]
    M --> J
    L -->|Yes| N[Implement Recovery Actions]
    N --> O[Monitor New SPI, CPI, TCPI Weekly]
    O --> P{Back on Target?}
    P -->|No| G
    P -->|Yes| Q[Document Lessons Learned]
    Q --> R([Project Continues to Closure])
```

## 4.2 The 2x2 Recovery Decision Matrix (Mermaid Block Architecture)
