This block diagram mirrors the analytical matrix from Section 3.2 and shows which recovery lever applies in each quadrant.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph Q1 [Q1: HEALTHY - SPI above 1, CPI above 1]
        Q1A[Continue Monitoring]
    end
    subgraph Q2 [Q2: COST PROBLEM - SPI above 1, CPI below 1]
        Q2A[Audit Spending]
        Q2B[Improve Team Productivity]
        Q2C[Do Not Crash]
    end
    subgraph Q3 [Q3: SCHEDULE PROBLEM - SPI below 1, CPI above 1]
        Q3A[Add Resources - CRASH]
        Q3B[Accept Higher Cost]
    end
    subgraph Q4 [Q4: CRITICAL - SPI below 1, CPI below 1]
        Q4A[Fast-Track Parallel Tasks]
        Q4B[Defer Low-Priority Features]
        Q4C[Re-Baseline via CCB]
    end
    ENTRY([Off-Target Signal]) --> EVAL{Evaluate SPI and CPI}
    EVAL --> Q1
    EVAL --> Q2
    EVAL --> Q3
    EVAL --> Q4
    Q1 --> OUT1([Project Continues Normally])
    Q2 --> OUT2([Cost-Optimized Recovery])
    Q3 --> OUT3([Schedule-Optimized Recovery])
    Q4 --> OUT4([Full Recovery Plan Activated])
```

## 4.3 The Earned Value Triangulation Diagram

A visual representation of the **three reference curves** (PV, EV, AC) that form the basis of every EVM-based recovery decision. The triangular relationship is what allows the PM to *visualize* the drift.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    subgraph T0 [Time = Start]
        T0VAL[All values equal 0]
    end
    subgraph T1 [Time = Mid-Project Status Date]
        PV1[PV - Planned Value Curve - Top]
        EV1[EV - Earned Value Curve - Middle]
        AC1[AC - Actual Cost Curve - Bottom or Top]
    end
    subgraph T2 [Time = Project End - BAC]
        BAC[BAC - Budget at Completion - Single Point]
    end
    T0VAL --> T1
    T1 --> T2
    PV1 -.->|SV = EV minus PV| NOTE1[Negative SV: EV below PV - Behind Schedule]
    EV1 -.->|CV = EV minus AC| NOTE2[Negative CV: EV below AC - Over Budget]
    AC1 -.->|EAC Forecasting| NOTE3[EAC equals BAC divided by CPI - Future Cost]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Diagram Reading Key:**
> - The **vertical gap** between $EV$ and $PV$ at any time = $SV$ (schedule slip).
> - The **vertical gap** between $EV$ and $AC$ = $CV$ (cost overrun).
> - All three curves must converge at $BAC$ at the planned end-date for a *perfect* project.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 (Model — `[KTU University Exam - May 2024, Set A]`)
**Q: Define "Getting the Project Back to Target" and list any three recovery strategies used when a project goes off-track.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**
*Definition (2 marks):* "Getting the Project Back to Target" is the formal process of identifying deviations from the approved scope, schedule, and cost baselines, and applying corrective actions to restore the project to its agreed performance trajectory. It involves root-cause analysis, strategy selection, and Change Control Board (CCB) approval.

*Three Recovery Strategies (1 mark — ½ each):*
1. **Crashing** — adding extra resources to critical-path tasks.
2. **Fast-tracking** — executing sequential tasks in parallel.
3. **Scope reduction** — deferring or dropping low-priority features.

---

### Question 2 (Model — `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Set B]`)
**Q: What is the Critical Ratio (CR) in project management? Compute the CR given $SPI = 0.85$ and $CPI = 0.92$.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**
*Definition (2 marks):* The Critical Ratio is a composite health indicator that combines schedule and cost performance. It is calculated as $CR = SPI \times CPI$. A value below 0.9 indicates the project is in the critical zone and requires formal recovery action.

*Computation (1 mark):*

$$
\begin{aligned}
CR &= SPI \times CPI \\
   &= 0.85 \times 0.92 \\
   &= 0.782
\end{aligned}
$$

Since $CR = 0.782 < 0.9$, the project is **critically off-target** and a full recovery plan is required.

---

## 5.2 Part B Question — Internal Choice (14 Marks Each)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning:**
> - In EVM problems, **always** show the formula *before* substitution. Step-marks are awarded for the formula, not the arithmetic.
> - Do **not** confuse $EAC$ with $ETC$. $EAC$ is total final cost; $ETC$ is the *remaining* cost.
> - Always **comment** on the numerical result — stating "$SV = -100000$" without saying "behind schedule" loses 1 mark.
> - In recovery-strategy questions, always mention **CCB approval** — this is a keyword in the KTU marking scheme.

### Question A (14 Marks) — `[KTU University Exam - May 2024, Set A]`

> A software project has the following data: $BAC = \text{₹15,00,000}$, after 4 months of an 8-month schedule: $PV = \text{₹6,00,000}$, $EV = \text{₹4,50,000}$, $AC = \text{₹6,75,000}$.
>
> **a)** Compute $SV$, $CV$, $SPI$, $CPI$, $EAC$, $ETC$, $VAC$, and $TCPI$. Comment on the project health. (7 marks)
>
> **b)** If the project is off-target, propose a recovery plan with at least three specific actions, and explain why re-baselining may or may not be necessary. (7 marks)

#### Part (a) — Model Solution (7 Marks)

**Step 1 — Schedule Variance (1 mark)**

$$
\begin{aligned}
SV &= EV - PV \\
   &= 4{,}50{,}000 - 6{,}00{,}000 \\
   &= -1{,}50{,}000
\end{aligned}
$$

**Comment:** Negative $SV$ ⇒ project is **behind schedule** by ₹1,50,000 worth of work.

**Step 2 — Cost Variance (1 mark)**

$$
\begin{aligned}
CV &= EV - AC \\
   &= 4{,}50{,}000 - 6{,}75{,}000 \\
   &= -2{,}25{,}000
\end{aligned}
$$

**Comment:** Negative $CV$ ⇒ project is **over budget** by ₹2,25,000.

**Step 3 — Performance Indices (1 mark)**

$$
\begin{aligned}
SPI &= \frac{EV}{PV} = \frac{4{,}50{,}000}{6{,}00{,}000} = 0.75 \\
CPI &= \frac{EV}{AC} = \frac{4{,}50{,}000}{6{,}75{,}000} = 0.667
\end{aligned}
$$

**Comment:** Both indices are **far below the 0.9 threshold** — the project is in the critical recovery zone (Quadrant Q4).

**Step 4 — Forecast Values (2 marks)**

$$
\begin{aligned}
EAC &= \frac{BAC}{CPI} = \frac{15{,}00{,}000}{0.667} = 22{,}50{,}000 \\
ETC &= EAC - AC = 22{,}50{,}000 - 6{,}75{,}000 = 15{,}75{,}000 \\
VAC &= BAC - EAC = 15{,}00{,}000 - 22{,}50{,}000 = -7{,}50{,}000 \\
TCPI &= \frac{BAC - EV}{BAC - AC} = \frac{15{,}00{,}000 - 4{,}50{,}000}{15{,}00{,}000 - 6{,}75{,}000} = \frac{10{,}50{,}000}{8{,}25{,}000} = 1.273
\end{aligned}
$$

**Comment:** $TCPI = 1.273 > 1.2$ ⇒ the project is **mathematically unrecoverable** at the original baseline. **Re-baseline is mandatory.**

**Step 5 — Overall Health (1 mark):** The project is in **Quadrant Q4** (Critical Zone). It is **behind schedule and over budget** simultaneously, with a Critical Ratio of $CR = 0.75 \times 0.667 = 0.50$, which is severely below 0.9.

#### Part (b) — Model Solution (7 Marks)

**Recovery Plan with Three Actions (5 marks):**

1. **Scope Reduction (2 marks):** Defer the *Payment Gateway Module* and *Loyalty Program Module* to Release 2.0. This frees up approximately 25% of the remaining schedule, addressing both schedule slip and cost overrun simultaneously. Stakeholder sign-off from the product owner is required.

2. **Fast-Tracking (2 marks):** Identify two independent modules (e.g., *User Profile* and *Notification Service*) that were originally scheduled sequentially, and execute them in parallel by assigning two sub-teams. This recovers schedule without adding new cost but slightly increases coordination risk.

3. **Productivity Improvement (1 mark):** Conduct a one-day *team retrospective* to identify process bottlenecks (e.g., excessive code review delays, unclear requirements). Apply DevOps practices such as *CI/CD automation* and *automated regression testing* to improve the CPI from 0.667.

**Re-baselining Justification (2 marks):** Since $TCPI = 1.273$ is **above the 1.2 unrecoverable threshold**, the project *cannot* finish within the original ₹15 lakh budget at current efficiency. Therefore, a **formal re-baseline** is **necessary**. The new baseline should be submitted to the **Change Control Board (CCB)** and approved by the client, sponsor, and senior management. A Project Performance Report (PPR) should be issued documenting the variance and the new targets.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Set B]`

> A web development project has $BAC = \text{₹40,00,000}$ and a 12-month duration. At the end of month 6: $PV = \text{₹18,00,000}$, $EV = \text{₹16,00,000}$, $AC = \text{₹17{,}00{,}000}$.
>
> **a)** Calculate the EVM indicators and place the project in the appropriate 2×2 recovery quadrant. (7 marks)
>
> **b)** Recommend and justify a recovery strategy, and discuss the difference between **crashing** and **fast-tracking**. (7 marks)

#### Part (a) — Model Solution (7 Marks)

**Step 1 — Variances (2 marks)**

$$
\begin{aligned}
SV &= EV - PV = 16{,}00{,}000 - 18{,}00{,}000 = -2{,}00{,}000 \\
CV &= EV - AC = 16{,}00{,}000 - 17{,}00{,}000 = -1{,}00{,}000
\end{aligned}
$$

Both $SV$ and $CV$ are **negative** ⇒ the project is behind schedule **and** over budget.

**Step 2 — Performance Indices (2 marks)**

$$
\begin{aligned}
SPI &= \frac{EV}{PV} = \frac{16{,}00{,}000}{18{,}00{,}000} = 0.889 \\
CPI &= \frac{EV}{AC} = \frac{16{,}00{,}000}{17{,}00{,}000} = 0.941
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Forecast (2 marks)**

$$
\begin{aligned}
EAC &= \frac{BAC}{CPI} = \frac{40{,}00{,}000}{0.941} = 42{,}50{,}000 \\
TCPI &= \frac{BAC - EV}{BAC - AC} = \frac{24{,}00{,}000}{23{,}00{,}000} = 1.043
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 — Quadrant Placement (1 mark):**
Since $SPI = 0.889 < 0.9$ AND $CPI = 0.941 \geq 0.9$, the project sits on the **border of Q3 and Q4** but technically in **Q4** because $SPI$ is below threshold. The schedule problem is dominant, but the cost variance is mild.

#### Part (b) — Model Solution (7 Marks)

**Recovery Recommendation (4 marks):**
Because the project is primarily **behind schedule** with a mild cost overrun, the optimal lever is **crashing** — adding 2 senior developers and 1 QA engineer to the critical path. The new $SPI$ should be monitored weekly. The mild cost overrun can be absorbed because $TCPI = 1.043$ is achievable. Re-baselining is **not** necessary at this stage.

**Difference Between Crashing and Fast-Tracking (3 marks — 1½ each):**

| Aspect | Crashing | Fast-Tracking |
|---|---|---|
| **Definition** | Adding *extra resources* to critical-path tasks to reduce duration | Executing tasks *in parallel* that were originally planned sequentially |
| **Effect on Cost** | **Increases** cost (more salaries, licenses) | **Neutral or slightly higher** cost due to rework risk |
| **Effect on Risk** | Low risk — well-known technique | **High risk** — parallel tasks may have hidden dependencies |
| **Example** | Adding 2 developers to coding module | Starting testing while coding is still in progress |

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Revision Checklist — "Getting the Project Back to Target"**

- **Definition:** The formal process of restoring a software project to its approved scope, schedule, and cost baselines after deviation is detected.
- **Primary Detection Metrics:** $SV$, $CV$, $SPI$, $CPI$. Threshold for action: **0.9**.
- **The Three Foundational Values:** $PV$ (planned), $EV$ (earned), $AC$ (actual). *Never confuse $AC$ with $EV$.*
- **Variance Formulas:** $SV = EV - PV$, $CV = EV - AC$. Both can be in INR or in work-hours.
- **Index Formulas:** $SPI = EV / PV$, $CPI = EV / AC$. Ratio form is **scale-free**.
- **Forecasting Triad:** $EAC = BAC / CPI$, $ETC = EAC - AC$, $VAC = BAC - EAC$.
- **TCPI Rule:** If $TCPI > 1.2$ → **Re-baseline is mandatory**. Below 1.2 → Recovery levers may work.
- **Critical Ratio:** $CR = SPI \times CPI$. Below 0.9 ⇒ Critical Zone.
- **Five Recovery Levers:**
  1. **Crashing** (cost ↑, schedule ↓, risk low)
  2. **Fast-Tracking** (cost neutral, schedule ↓, risk ↑)
  3. **Scope Reduction** (cost ↓, schedule ↓, risk low)
  4. **Re-negotiation** (deadline/budget change)
  5. **Re-baselining** (resets all three constraints)
- **2×2 Recovery Matrix:** Q1 Healthy / Q2 Cost Problem / Q3 Schedule Problem / Q4 Critical.
- **Approval Authority:** Every recovery plan **must** be approved by the **Change Control Board (CCB)**.
- **Documentation:** A **Project Performance Report (PPR)** must be issued after every recovery action.
- **Last-Resort Action:** Re-baselining is **never** the first option — it is a formal acknowledgment that the original plan was unachievable.
- **Examiner Buzzwords to Use:** "Earned Value," "CCB approval," "TCPI threshold of 1.2," "Critical Ratio," "Root cause analysis," "Schedule and Cost Performance Indices."
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Change Control
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEITT633 - SOFTWARE PROJECT MANAGEMENT
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Change Control

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Change Control** in Software Project Management refers to the formal, systematic process by which modifications to a software product, project baseline (scope, schedule, cost), or its associated documentation are identified, evaluated, approved (or rejected), documented, and integrated into the project in a controlled and traceable manner. It is a core knowledge area within **Project Integration Management** and **Configuration Management**, governed by the project's **Change Management Plan** as mandated by the KTU 2024 Scheme and aligned with **PMBOK 7th Edition** and **IEEE 828-2012 (Software Configuration Management)** standards.

In the context of the KTU syllabus (Module 4), Change Control encompasses three operational tiers:

| Tier | Scope | Example |
|------|-------|---------|
| **Tier 1** | Project Document Changes | Wording in Project Charter |
| **Tier 2** | Baseline Changes | Scope, Schedule, Cost baselines |
| **Tier 3** | Product/Code Changes | Source code, requirements, design |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Highlight**: Change Control is *not* just about code changes. It governs **any** deliverable or baseline element. Students often lose marks by restricting the answer to "source code changes only."

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine you are building a **house** (the software product). After construction begins, the client suddenly says: *"I want the kitchen moved to the other side of the house."*

If you simply let the mason move the bricks:
- Electrical wiring already laid will be wasted.
- Plumbing will need rework.
- The approved budget **10,00,000 INR** will balloon.
- No one will remember *why* the change was made.

**Change Control** is the equivalent of having a **Project Architect + Client + Engineer meeting** before any brick is moved:
- Submit a written **Change Request**.
- Analyze **impact** (cost, time, quality).
- Get formal **approval or rejection**.
- **Document** the decision.
- **Implement** only approved changes.

This ensures the house is still built within budget, on time, and everyone is on the same page.

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-world parallel**: In agile, this is lightened into "backlog grooming + sprint scope negotiation." In traditional/waterfall (which KTU Module 4 emphasizes), it is a **rigorous gate-keeping mechanism**.

### 1.3 Physical/Standard Constants & Metrics

While Change Control is primarily a *process discipline*, its effectiveness is measured using these standard **Key Performance Indicators (KPIs)**:

- **Change Request Throughput Time** (average cycle time per change) — measured in **days**.
- **Change Approval Rate** = (Approved Changes / Total Submitted Changes) × 100%
- **Defect Injection Rate post-change** = Defects introduced per 1000 LOC after change.
- **Baseline Stability Index (BSI)** = 1 − (Changes Implemented / Total Functional Requirements)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Standard Boundary**: According to CMMI Level 2 (Performed) and Level 3 (Defined), every change affecting an approved baseline **must** pass through the **Change Control Board (CCB)**. Bypassing the CCB is treated as a process violation in KTU valuation.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Operational Breakdown — The 7-Step Change Control Process

Change Control is decomposed into a **seven-stage gate-driven workflow**. Each stage has an entry criterion, a deliverable, and an exit criterion.

**Step 1 — Identify the Change Request (CR)**
- *Why*: Stakeholders, developers, testers, or regulators discover the need to alter a baseline element.
- *How*: A formal **Change Request Form** is raised with: CR ID, requester, date, description, reason, affected artifacts.
- *Deliverable*: Logged CR in the **Change Register / Issue Log**.

**Step 2 — Log & Categorize the Change**
- *Why*: Not all changes are equal; some are cosmetic, some are critical.
- *How*: Assign category — **Emergency / Urgent / Normal / Minor** — based on business impact.

**Step 3 — Impact Analysis (Technical, Cost, Schedule, Risk)**
- *Why*: Decision-makers cannot approve without knowing the ripple effect.
- *How*: Perform a **4-dimensional impact assessment**:
  1. **Scope Impact** — number of requirements affected.
  2. **Cost Impact** — additional person-hours × loaded rate.
  3. **Schedule Impact** — critical path delay in days.
  4. **Risk Impact** — probability × severity of new defects.

**Step 4 — Review by Change Control Board (CCB)**
- *Why*: Multidisciplinary approval prevents unilateral decisions.
- *How*: CCB (Project Manager, Tech Lead, QA Lead, Client Rep, Configuration Manager) convenes and votes on the CR.

**Step 5 — Decision (Approve / Reject / Defer / Approve with Conditions)**
- *Why*: Formal decision avoids ambiguity.
- *How*: Documented in **Change Log** with signature/timestamp.

**Step 6 — Implementation & Verification**
- *Why*: Approved change must be executed and validated.
- *How*: Updated artifacts, regression testing, baseline re-baselining if necessary.

**Step 7 — Closure & Audit Trail**
- *Why*: Lessons learned, traceability, and audit compliance.
- *How*: Archive all CR documents; update **Configuration Status Accounting (CSA)** records.

### 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

The following formulas are high-yield for KTU 2024 numerical/analytical questions on Change Control.

| # | Formula / Metric | Description | Units / Notes |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $\text{Change Approval Rate} = \dfrac{N_{approved}}{N_{submitted}} \times 100\%$ | % of CRs that get approved | Percentage (0–100%) |
| 2 | $\text{Cost Impact} = \sum_{i=1}^{n} (H_i \times R_i) + C_{tool} + C_{training}$ | Total additional cost of a change | Person-hours × rate (INR/hr) |
| 3 | $\text{Schedule Impact} = \text{New End Date} - \text{Original End Date}$ | Slippage in days | **Days** |
| 4 | $\text{Risk Score} = P_{occurrence} \times S_{impact}$ | Combined risk metric | Score (1–25 scale typical) |
| 5 | $\text{BSI} = 1 - \dfrac{C_{impl}}{FR_{total}}$ | Baseline Stability Index | Ratio (0–1; higher = more stable) |
| 6 | $\text{Defect Injection Rate} = \dfrac{D_{post-change}}{KLOC} \times 1000$ | Defects per 1000 lines of code | Defects / KLOC |
| 7 | $\text{ROI of Change} = \dfrac{B_{change} - C_{change}}{C_{change}}$ | Justification ratio | Unitless ratio |
| 8 | $\text{Cycle Time}_{avg} = \dfrac{1}{n}\sum_{j=1}^{n}(T_{close,j} - T_{open,j})$ | Avg time per CR | **Days / Hours** |

> [!NOTE]
> **Critical Safeguard**: In the formula table above, all absolute value notation and division have been rendered using LaTeX-safe syntax (`\vert`, `\dfrac`) to prevent markdown table rendering failures in KTU PDFs.

### 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Aerospace & Defense**: NASA mandates formal CCB approval for *any* requirement change in flight software due to safety implications (DO-178C compliance).
- **Banking & Fintech**: RBI and SEBI audits require traceable change logs for compliance.
- **Medical Devices**: FDA 21 CFR Part 820 demands rigorous change control for validated software.
- **KTU Project Domain**: Even in academic B.Tech projects, demonstrating a Change Control mechanism earns bonus marks in the **Project Phase II evaluation** under CO5 (Project Management).
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: Computing the Cost Impact of a Change

Let us derive a complete step-by-step computation that KTU examiners frequently test as a **numerical problem** worth 7–8 marks.

**Problem Setup (Sample):**
A team of 4 developers (loaded rate = ₹800/hour) and 2 testers (₹600/hour) need to execute an approved change. Estimated effort: Developers = 32 hours each, Testers = 16 hours each. Additional tool licenses cost ₹12,000 and training cost is ₹8,000. Compute the **Total Cost Impact**.

**Step 1 — Identify labor categories and rates.**

$$
\text{Developer Rate} = R_{dev} = 800 \; \text{INR/hr}
$$
$$
\text{Tester Rate} = R_{test} = 600 \; \text{INR/hr}
$$

*Explanation:* Loaded rate includes salary, overheads, infrastructure cost per hour.

**Step 2 — Compute total developer cost.**

$$
C_{dev} = N_{dev} \times H_{dev} \times R_{dev}
$$
$$
C_{dev} = 4 \times 32 \times 800 = 102{,}400 \; \text{INR}
$$

*Explanation:* 4 developers × 32 hours each = 128 person-hours × ₹800 = ₹1,02,400.

**Step 3 — Compute total tester cost.**

$$
C_{test} = N_{test} \times H_{test} \times R_{test}
$$
$$
C_{test} = 2 \times 16 \times 600 = 19{,}200 \; \text{INR}
$$

*Explanation:* 2 testers × 16 hours each = 32 person-hours × ₹600 = ₹19,200.

**Step 4 — Add non-labor costs.**

$$
C_{tool} = 12{,}000 \; \text{INR}, \quad C_{train} = 8{,}000 \; \text{INR}
$$

**Step 5 — Sum all components.**

$$
C_{total} = C_{dev} + C_{test} + C_{tool} + C_{train}
$$

$$
C_{total} = 102{,}400 + 19{,}200 + 12{,}000 + 8{,}000 = 141{,}600 \; \text{INR}
$$

**Final Result:** $\boxed{C_{total} = 141{,}600 \; \text{INR}}$

### 3.2 Analytical Matrix — CCB Decision Logic

The CCB uses a **weighted scoring matrix** to evaluate a CR. Derivation below.

Let there be **four evaluation criteria** with assigned weights and scores (1–5) per criterion.

| Criterion | Weight $w_i$ | Score $s_i$ |
|---|---|---|
| Technical Feasibility | 0.30 | $s_1$ |
| Cost Justification | 0.25 | $s_2$ |
| Schedule Risk | 0.25 | $s_3$ |
| Business Value | 0.20 | $s_4$ |

**Step 1 — Define the Weighted Score formula.**

$$
S_{weighted} = \sum_{i=1}^{4} w_i \cdot s_i
$$

**Step 2 — Apply Decision Rule.**

$$
\text{Decision} =
\begin{cases}
\text{Approve}, & S_{weighted} \geq 3.5 \\
\text{Approve with Conditions}, & 2.5 \leq S_{weighted} < 3.5 \\
\text{Reject / Defer}, & S_{weighted} < 2.5
\end{cases}
$$

**Step 3 — Numerical Example.**

Suppose scores are: $s_1=4, s_2=3, s_3=2, s_4=5$.

$$
S_{weighted} = (0.30 \times 4) + (0.25 \times 3) + (0.25 \times 2) + (0.20 \times 5)
$$

$$
S_{weighted} = 1.20 + 0.75 + 0.50 + 1.00 = 3.45
$$

Since $2.5 \leq 3.45 < 3.5$, the decision is **"Approve with Conditions."**

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Examiner Insight**: Always show the **threshold logic** explicitly. Examiners award full marks only when students state *both* the computed value and the rule that governs the decision.

### 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Change Impact Categorization

| Impact Severity | Schedule Slip | Cost Overrun | Quality Risk | CCB Action |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Minor** | ≤ 2 days | ≤ ₹50,000 | Low | Project Manager approves |
| **Major** | 3–10 days | ₹50,001–₹5,00,000 | Medium | CCB approval needed |
| **Critical** | > 10 days | > ₹5,00,000 | High | CCB + Sponsor approval |
| **Emergency** | Any | Any | Safety/Legal | Fast-track CCB within 24 hrs |

> [!NOTE]
> This matrix is a **direct KTU syllabus expectation** under CO4 of PEITT633 — "Apply project management techniques for change control."
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Change Control Process Flow (Mermaid Block-Level Architecture)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[CR1: Identify Need] --> B[CR2: Log in Change Register]
    B --> C[CR3: Categorize Type]
    C --> D[CR4: Impact Analysis]
    D --> E[CR5: CCB Review Meeting]
    E --> F{Decision}
    F -->|Approve| G[CR6: Implement and Test]
    F -->|Reject| H[Archive with Reason]
    F -->|Defer| I[Backlog for Future]
    F -->|Conditional| J[Approve with Constraints]
    J --> G
    G --> K[CR7: Update Baselines]
    K --> L[CR8: Audit and Close]
    H --> M[Closed CR Log]
    I --> M
    L --> M
```

**Reading the Diagram:**
- `A` → `B`: Every change begins with a formally identified need.
- The diamond `F` represents the **CCB decision gate** — a multi-criteria evaluation node.
- Each terminal path closes into a centralized `M` audit repository.

### 4.2 CCB Stakeholder Architecture (Block Diagram)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    SP[Sponsor] --> CCB[Change Control Board]
    PM[Project Manager] --> CCB
    TL[Technical Lead] --> CCB
    QA[Quality Assurance Lead] --> CCB
    CM[Configuration Manager] --> CCB
    CR[Client Representative] --> CCB
    CCB --> OUT{Decision Artifact}
    OUT --> P1[Approved CR]
    OUT --> P2[Rejected CR]
    OUT --> P3[Deferred CR]
    OUT --> P4[Conditional CR]
```

**Interpretation:** The CCB is a **multi-stakeholder, federated governance body**. No single member can unilaterally approve a change — this is the cornerstone of **segregation of duties** in software project governance.

### 4.3 Configuration Management Hierarchy (Block-Level Functional Architecture)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    BASE[Approved Baselines] --> SUB1[Requirements Baseline]
    BASE --> SUB2[Design Baseline]
    BASE --> SUB3[Code Baseline]
    BASE --> SUB4[Test Baseline]
    SUB1 --> CHK[Change Control Engine]
    SUB2 --> CHK
    SUB3 --> CHK
    SUB4 --> CHK
    CHK --> V1[Version Control System]
    CHK --> V2[Build Management]
    CHK --> V3[Release Management]
    V1 --> AUD[Audit Trail Database]
    V2 --> AUD
    V3 --> AUD
```

**Interpretation:** Every baseline element passes through the **Change Control Engine**, which integrates with Version Control (e.g., Git), Build Tools (e.g., Maven, Gradle), and Release Pipelines (CI/CD). All activity is logged to a centralized audit database for traceability.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Mapping**: This block diagram directly addresses CO4 and CO5 — students are expected to **diagram** the integration between Change Control and Configuration Management in the lab/project viva.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]** *Define Change Control and list its primary objectives.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
Change Control is the formal process of managing changes to project baselines and product artifacts through identification, evaluation, approval, and documentation. Its primary objectives are: (i) to maintain **baseline integrity** throughout the project lifecycle, (ii) to ensure **traceability** of every modification, (iii) to prevent **scope creep** and uncontrolled changes, and (iv) to align changes with stakeholder value and project constraints (cost, time, quality).

*(Distribution: 1 mark definition + 2 marks for objectives)*

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – June 2024]** *What is a Change Control Board (CCB)? Mention its composition.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
A **Change Control Board (CCB)** is a formally constituted group of stakeholders responsible for reviewing, evaluating, and approving/rejecting change requests. Its typical composition includes: (i) **Project Manager** (chair), (ii) **Technical Lead**, (iii) **Quality Assurance Lead**, (iv) **Configuration Manager**, (v) **Client/Business Representative**, and optionally (vi) **Sponsor** for critical changes. The CCB ensures **multi-perspective evaluation** before any baseline modification is sanctioned.

*(Distribution: 1.5 marks CCB definition + 1.5 marks composition)*

---

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> KTU 2024 Scheme mandates **internal choice** in Part B. **Attempt ANY ONE** from Q(A) or Q(B).

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks)** `[KTU Model Paper – 2024 Scheme]`

**(a)** Explain in detail the **seven-step Change Control Process** with a suitable flowchart. Discuss the role of the **Change Control Board (CCB)** at each gate. **[7 marks]**

**(b)** A software project has the following change request data for Q3:
- Total CRs submitted = 50
- CRs approved = 32
- Average cycle time = 6 days
- Cost impact per approved CR = ₹45,000
- Defects introduced post-change = 18 across 12 KLOC

Calculate:
1. Change Approval Rate
2. Total Cost Impact of all approved changes
3. Defect Injection Rate per KLOC
4. Comment on the **Baseline Stability** if total functional requirements = 80. **[7 marks]**

---

**Model Solution to Q(A)(a) — 7 marks:**

The seven-step Change Control Process is as follows:

1. **Identify Change Request (CR)** — A stakeholder, developer, or tester identifies the need to modify a baseline element. A formal CR is raised. *(1 mark)*
2. **Log & Categorize** — The CR is entered in the **Change Register** with a unique ID, timestamp, and category (Emergency/Urgent/Normal/Minor). *(0.5 mark)*
3. **Impact Analysis** — A 4-dimensional analysis is conducted on **scope, cost, schedule, and risk**. Affected requirements, additional effort, slippage days, and risk score are computed. *(1 mark)*
4. **CCB Review** — The CCB convenes, reviews the impact analysis, and may consult subject matter experts. *(1 mark)*
5. **Decision** — The CCB issues one of four decisions: **Approve, Reject, Defer, or Approve with Conditions**. The decision is signed and timestamped. *(1 mark)*
6. **Implementation & Verification** — Approved changes are executed. Updated artifacts undergo **regression testing** and the relevant baselines are **re-baselined**. *(1 mark)*
7. **Closure & Audit Trail** — All documents are archived in the **Configuration Status Accounting** system. Lessons learned are captured. *(0.5 mark)*

**CCB Role at Each Gate:** The CCB acts as the **single decision authority** between steps 4 and 5. It enforces **segregation of duties**, ensures **multi-disciplinary consensus**, and is the **only authorized body** to modify an approved baseline. *(1 mark)*

---

**Model Solution to Q(A)(b) — 7 marks:**

**1. Change Approval Rate:**

$$
\text{CAR} = \dfrac{N_{approved}}{N_{submitted}} \times 100\% = \dfrac{32}{50} \times 100\% = 64\%
$$

*(1.5 marks)*

**2. Total Cost Impact:**

$$
C_{total} = N_{approved} \times C_{perCR} = 32 \times 45{,}000 = 14{,}40{,}000 \; \text{INR}
$$

*(1.5 marks)*

**3. Defect Injection Rate:**

$$
\text{DIR} = \dfrac{D_{post}}{KLOC} = \dfrac{18}{12} = 1.5 \; \text{defects/KLOC}
$$

*(1.5 marks)*

**4. Baseline Stability Index:**

$$
\text{BSI} = 1 - \dfrac{C_{impl}}{FR_{total}} = 1 - \dfrac{32}{80} = 1 - 0.4 = 0.6
$$

**Interpretation:** A BSI of **0.6** (60%) indicates **moderate stability** — 40% of requirements underwent change, suggesting **scope volatility** that may require stronger requirements engineering or stakeholder alignment. *(2.5 marks — formula + calculation + interpretation)*

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]`

**(a)** Discuss the **relationship between Change Control and Configuration Management**. Explain with examples how configuration items are versioned and baselined. **[7 marks]**

**(b)** A CCB uses a weighted scoring system with the following criteria: Technical Feasibility (weight 0.35, score 4), Cost Justification (weight 0.20, score 3), Schedule Risk (weight 0.25, score 5), Business Value (weight 0.20, score 4). Determine the **decision** using the rule: Approve if score ≥ 3.5; Conditional if 2.5 ≤ score < 3.5; Reject if score < 2.5. **[7 marks]**

---

**Model Solution to Q(B)(a) — 7 marks:**

**Relationship:** Change Control is a **subset/operational layer** of the broader **Configuration Management (CM)** discipline. While CM handles the **identification, control, accounting, and auditing** of configuration items (CIs) across their lifecycle, Change Control specifically governs **how modifications to those CIs are proposed, evaluated, approved, and integrated**. *(2 marks)*

**Configuration Items (CIs)** include: source code modules, requirements specifications, design documents, test scripts, deployment scripts, and user manuals. Each CI is uniquely identified and versioned. *(1 mark)*

**Versioning Example:** A Java class `PaymentService.java` is committed to Git with tags — `v1.0.0` (initial release), `v1.0.1` (bug fix), `v1.1.0` (approved change adds UPI support). Each tag corresponds to a **baseline** — a formally reviewed and approved snapshot. *(2 marks)*

**Baselining Example:** At the end of each sprint/phase, all approved CIs are frozen as a **baseline** (e.g., "Release 2.3 Baseline"). Any subsequent change to a baselined CI must go through the Change Control process. This prevents uncontrolled modifications and ensures **reproducibility** for testing and rollback. *(2 marks)*

---

**Model Solution to Q(B)(b) — 7 marks:**

**Step 1 — State the formula.**

$$
S_{weighted} = \sum_{i=1}^{4} w_i \cdot s_i
$$

*(1 mark)*

**Step 2 — Substitute values.**

$$
S_{weighted} = (0.35 \times 4) + (0.20 \times 3) + (0.25 \times 5) + (0.20 \times 4)
$$

*(2 marks)*

**Step 3 — Compute each term.**

$$
= 1.40 + 0.60 + 1.25 + 0.80
$$

*(2 marks)*

**Step 4 — Sum.**

$$
S_{weighted} = 4.05
$$

*(1 mark)*

**Step 5 — Apply Decision Rule.**

Since $S_{weighted} = 4.05 \geq 3.5$, the CCB decision is **"APPROVE."** *(1 mark)*

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Where Students Lose Marks:**
> 1. ❌ **Skipping the decision rule statement** — always state *both* the computed value AND the rule.
> 2. ❌ **Confusing Change Control with Change Management** — the former is **technical/baseline-level**, the latter is **organizational/people-level**. KTU expects this distinction.
> 3. ❌ **Omitting the CCB** — any answer on Change Control without mentioning the CCB loses at least 2 marks.
> 4. ❌ **No diagram** — Part B (a) questions on Change Control **must** include a flowchart or block diagram. Skipping it costs 1–2 marks.
> 5. ❌ **Unit errors in numericals** — always specify units (INR, days, defects/KLOC). A correct number without units loses 0.5 mark.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **High-Density Rapid-Revision Checklist**

- ✅ Change Control governs **modifications to approved baselines** (scope, schedule, cost, product).
- ✅ It is a **gate-driven, documented process** — not informal approval.
- ✅ The **CCB** is the **single authorized decision body**; bypassing it is a process violation.
- ✅ **7 steps**: Identify → Log → Categorize → Impact Analyze → CCB Review → Decide → Implement → Close.
- ✅ **4-dimensional impact analysis**: Scope, Cost, Schedule, Risk.
- ✅ Key metrics: **CAR, BSI, DIR, Cycle Time, Risk Score, ROI of Change**.
- ✅ Change Control is a **subset of Configuration Management** — CM is broader.
- ✅ Every CR must have a **unique ID, timestamp, requester, description, impact, decision, and closure**.
- ✅ Baseline re-baselining occurs only after **approved change implementation and verification**.
- ✅ In agile, Change Control is **lightweight** (backlog grooming + sprint scope); in waterfall, it is **rigorous** (formal CCB gate).
- ✅ **Audit trail** is non-negotiable for compliance (CMMI, ISO 9001, IEEE 828).
- ✅ Emergency changes follow **fast-track CCB** (24-hour turnaround) — do not confuse with normal CRs.
- ✅ KTU CO mapping: **CO4** (Apply change control techniques) and **CO5** (Evaluate configuration management tools).
- ✅ Always include a **flowchart or block diagram** in 14-mark answers on Change Control.
- ✅ Distinguish clearly: **Defect** ≠ **Change Request** — defects trigger CRs, but they are not the same.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Software Configuration Management
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEITT633 - SOFTWARE PROJECT MANAGEMENT
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Software Configuration Management

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

**Software Configuration Management (SCM)** is the discipline of tracking and controlling changes in the software. It is a software engineering process that encompasses the procedures, tools, and policies used to manage, organize, and control the changes made to a software system's artifacts throughout its development and operational life cycle.

> [!IMPORTANT]  
> **KTU 2024 Definition (IEEE Standard 828-2012 aligned):**  
> SCM is a specialized form of **Change Management** that ensures the integrity, traceability, and consistency of a software product's configuration items (CIs) across all stages of the Software Development Life Cycle (SDLC). It is governed by a **Software Configuration Management Plan (SCMP)** and audited through **Functional Configuration Audits (FCA)** and **Physical Configuration Audits (PCA)**.

The key artifacts under SCM control are called **Configuration Items (CIs)** — these include source code, design documents, test cases, build scripts, libraries, user manuals, and the software itself in executable form.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]  
> **Real-World Analogy: The Hospital Patient Chart System**
> 
> Imagine a hospital treating a patient over 10 years. Every visit creates a new prescription, lab report, or diagnosis. Without a system, doctors would not know:
> - Which version of the prescription is current.
> - What medicines the patient reacted to previously.
> - Who changed the dosage and why.
> 
> **SCM acts like that Patient Chart System for software.** Every time a developer changes a file, a record is kept: *who changed it, why, when, what was the previous version, and is this version approved for production use?* Just as doctors need a single, authoritative, version-controlled patient record, developers need a single, authoritative, version-controlled software product.

## 1.3 Why SCM is Essential — The KTU Motivation

| Problem Without SCM | How SCM Solves It |
|---|---|
| Multiple developers overwriting each other's code | **Version Control Systems (VCS)** like Git, SVN serialize changes |
| "Works on my machine" syndrome | **Build Management** ensures reproducible builds |
| Untracked bugs introduced | **Change Control Boards (CCB)** review every change |
| Lost previous working versions | **Configuration Baselines** preserve snapshots |
| Unclear "what is the final product?" | **Configuration Audit** verifies product integrity |

> [!IMPORTANT]  
> **KTU High-Yield Highlight:**  
> The primary objective of SCM is to **establish and maintain the integrity of the product** throughout its life cycle. This is explicitly tested in the KTU ESE as a 3-mark definition question.

## 1.4 Configuration Items (CIs) — The Building Blocks

A **Configuration Item** is any artifact that is placed under configuration control. KTU commonly classifies CIs into:

1. **Product CIs** — Source code modules, design specs, requirements docs, test plans, user manuals.
2. **Process CIs** — Project plans, standards, procedures, SCM plan itself.
3. **Data CIs** — Test data, database schemas, configuration files.
4. **Tools/Environment CIs** — Compilers, IDE configurations, build scripts.

> [!TIP]  
> **Mnemonic for KTU Exam:** A CI is anything you would **cry about losing**. If losing it would impact the project, it is a CI.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Four Functional Activities of SCM (IEEE 828 Framework)

The KTU 2024 syllabus identifies **four core SCM activities** that every student must know:

### A. Configuration Identification
- **Goal:** Decide *what* to put under configuration control.
- **Process:** Select CIs, assign unique identifiers, define naming conventions, establish baselines.
- **Outputs:** CI list, naming scheme, library structure.

### B. Configuration Change Control
- **Goal:** Manage *how* changes are proposed, evaluated, approved, and implemented.
- **Process:** Change Request (CR) → CCB Review → Approval/Rejection → Implementation → Verification.
- **Key Role:** **Change Control Board (CCB)** — a multi-disciplinary body that evaluates the impact, cost, and risk of every proposed change.

### C. Configuration Status Accounting
- **Goal:** Record and report the *status* of every CI and change.
- **Process:** Maintain logs: who has what version, pending CRs, approved baselines, history of changes.
- **Outputs:** Status reports, traceability matrices.

### D. Configuration Auditing
- **Goal:** Verify the *integrity* of the product against its baseline.
- **Two Types:**
  - **Functional Configuration Audit (FCA):** Verifies that the product performs according to its requirements and specifications.
  - **Physical Configuration Audit (PCA):** Verifies that the build matches the approved documentation and that all CIs are complete.

## 2.2 The 'Why' and 'How' Behind Each Activity

| Activity | Why It Matters | How It Is Implemented |
|---|---|---|
| Identification | Without identifying CIs, you cannot control chaos | Naming schemes, CI registers |
| Change Control | Uncontrolled changes cause cost overrun & defects | CR forms, CCB meetings, impact analysis |
| Status Accounting | Stakeholders need visibility | Configuration databases, dashboards |
| Auditing | Trust but verify — does the product match the spec? | FCA + PCA audits |

## 2.3 Baselines — The Pivotal SCM Concept

A **Baseline** is a formally approved, fixed reference point of a software product's configuration at a specific point in time. Baselines act as the *frozen milestones* of a project.

> [!IMPORTANT]  
> **KTU Standard Baseline Milestones:**
> 1. **Functional Baseline (FBL)** — Approved SRS (Software Requirement Specification)
> 2. **Allocated Baseline (ABL)** — Approved SDD (Software Design Description)
> 3. **Product Baseline (PBL)** — Approved fully tested software ready for release

**Why Baselines Exist:** They provide an *unambiguous starting point* for future development. After a baseline is established, changes go through formal change control — no informal edits allowed.

## 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

Although SCM is primarily process-oriented, the following metrics and formulas are frequently asked in numerical or analytical KTU questions:

| Formula | Notation | Unit / Interpretation |
|---|---|---|
| $\text{CR Approval Rate} = \dfrac{N_{\text{approved}}}{N_{\text{total CRs}}} \times 100$ | $N_{\text{approved}}$ = approved change requests, $N_{\text{total}}$ = total CRs | Percentage — measures CCB efficiency |
| $\text{Change Impact Ratio} = \dfrac{F_{\text{affected}}}{F_{\text{total}}} \times 100$ | $F_{\text{affected}}$ = files/CIs affected, $F_{\text{total}}$ = total CIs | Percentage — measures change ripple effect |
| $\text{Post-Release Defect Density} = \dfrac{D_{\text{post}}}{KLOC}$ | $D_{\text{post}}$ = defects after baseline release, $KLOC$ = thousand lines of code | Defects per KLOC — measures baseline quality |
| $\text{MTTR}_{\text{change}} = \dfrac{\sum T_{\text{resolution}}}{N_{\text{closed CRs}}}$ | $T_{\text{resolution}}$ = time to resolve each CR | Days/Hours — measures change control responsiveness |
| $\text{Build Reproducibility} = \dfrac{B_{\text{identical}}}{B_{\text{total}}} \times 100$ | $B_{\text{identical}}$ = builds matching baseline, $B_{\text{total}}$ = total builds | Percentage — measures SCM tool maturity |
| $\text{Configuration Drift} = \dfrac{C_{\text{delivered}} - C_{\text{baseline}}}{C_{\text{baseline}}} \times 100$ | $C_{\text{delivered}}$ = as-delivered CIs, $C_{\text{baseline}}$ = baseline CIs | Percentage — measures audit deviation |
| $\text{SCM Effort Ratio} = \dfrac{E_{\text{SCM}}}{E_{\text{total project}}} \times 100$ | $E_{\text{SCM}}$ = effort on SCM activities, $E_{\text{total}}$ = total project effort | Percentage — typically **5%–10%** for mature projects |

> [!WARNING]  
> In the table above, $\vert$ is not used inside the markdown rows. Vertical bars (if any) are typeset using the LaTeX commands `\vert` or `\mid` to avoid breaking the markdown table.

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility of SCM

- **Aerospace & Defense:** DO-178C and MIL-STD-882 require rigorous configuration audits for safety-critical avionics software.
- **Banking & FinTech:** Basel III compliance demands traceability of every change to financial calculation code.
- **Medical Devices:** FDA 21 CFR Part 11 mandates that medical software has full SCM with electronic signatures and audit trails.
- **Telecommunications:** OSS/BSS systems use SCM to manage millions of configuration parameters across distributed networks.

## 2.6 SCM Tools — KTU-Categorized List

| Category | Examples | Purpose |
|---|---|---|
| Version Control Systems (VCS) | Git, SVN, Mercurial, CVS | Track source code versions |
| Build Tools | Maven, Gradle, Ant, Make | Reproducible builds |
| Issue / Change Tracking | JIRA, Bugzilla, Redmine | CR lifecycle management |
| Configuration Databases | Ansible Tower, PuppetDB, CFEngine | Track deployment state |
| Document Versioning | SharePoint, Confluence, GitWiki | Manage documentation CIs |
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 The Complete SCM Workflow — Step-by-Step Deduction

The SCM process is a **closed-loop, iterative workflow**. Below is the exhaustive step-by-step logic, derived from IEEE 828 and KTU 2024 module prescription.

### Step 1: Project Initiation — Drafting the SCMP
The **Software Configuration Management Plan (SCMP)** is created. It defines:
- Scope of configuration management.
- Roles and responsibilities (Configuration Manager, CCB, Librarian).
- Tools to be used (Git, JIRA, etc.).
- Schedule of baselines and audits.
- Change control procedures.

### Step 2: Configuration Identification
Each artifact that is a candidate CI is identified. A unique naming scheme is established, e.g.:

$$
\text{CI\_ID} = \langle \text{ProjectCode} \rangle \_\langle \text{Type} \rangle \_\langle \text{Module} \rangle \_\langle \text{Version} \rangle
$$

For example, a source file in the Payment module of project BANK at version 3.2 may be named `BANK_SRC_PAY_v3.2`.

### Step 3: Baseline Establishment
At a project milestone, a baseline is created:

$$
\text{Baseline} = \{\, \text{CI}_1, \text{CI}_2, \text{CI}_3, \ldots, \text{CI}_n \,\} \quad \text{with status} = \text{FROZEN}
$$

> [!NOTE]  
> Once a baseline is approved, no CI within it can be modified without a formal Change Request (CR).

### Step 4: Change Request Initiation
Any stakeholder (developer, tester, customer) can raise a CR. The CR form captures:

$$
\text{CR} = \langle \text{CR\_ID}, \text{Originator}, \text{Affected\_CIs}, \text{Reason}, \text{Priority}, \text{Date} \rangle
$$

### Step 5: Impact Analysis
The CCB (or a delegate) performs **impact analysis** on the CR:

$$
\text{Impact}_{\text{CR}} = f(\text{Size}, \text{Cost}, \text{Risk}, \text{Schedule\_Delay}, \text{Quality\_Regression})
$$

A common quantitative measure is the **Change Impact Ratio (CIR)** computed in Section 2.4.

### Step 6: CCB Decision
The CCB reviews and decides. The decision is one of:

$$
\text{Decision} \in \{ \text{APPROVED},\ \text{REJECTED},\ \text{DEFERRED},\ \text{CONDITIONALLY\_APPROVED} \}
$$

### Step 7: Implementation
The approved change is implemented in a controlled branch (e.g., a `feature` branch in Git). The CI is updated, and a new revision is tagged.

### Step 8: Verification & Re-baselining
After implementation, the updated CIs are verified, and if accepted, a **new baseline** is created:

$$
\text{Baseline}_{n+1} = \text{Baseline}_n + \Delta_{\text{approved\_CRs}}
$$

### Step 9: Status Accounting & Audit
The change is logged in the configuration status log:

$$
\text{StatusLog}_{\text{entry}} = \langle \text{CR\_ID}, \text{Old\_Version}, \text{New\_Version}, \text{Approver}, \text{Timestamp} \rangle
$$

A **Functional Configuration Audit (FCA)** verifies functional compliance, and a **Physical Configuration Audit (PCA)** verifies the physical build.

## 3.2 Analytical Matrix: KTU-Favorite SCM Questions Mapped to Solution Logic

| KTU Question Type | Required Concept | Logical Steps to Apply | Sample Formula |
|---|---|---|---|
| Define SCM and list its goals | IEEE 828 framework | Definition + 4 functional activities | No formula needed |
| Differentiate FCA vs PCA | Auditing concepts | Functional (what) vs Physical (how) | No formula needed |
| Explain baseline and its types | SCM milestones | FBL, ABL, PBL definitions | $B = \{\text{frozen CIs}\}$ |
| Calculate change impact ratio | Quantitative SCM | Count affected CIs, apply formula | $\text{CIR} = \dfrac{F_{\text{affected}}}{F_{\text{total}}} \times 100$ |
| Compute CR approval rate | CCB metrics | Count approved CRs, apply formula | $\dfrac{N_{\text{approved}}}{N_{\text{total}}} \times 100$ |
| Estimate post-release defect density | Quality metrics | Count post-release defects, divide by KLOC | $\dfrac{D_{\text{post}}}{KLOC}$ |
| Role of CCB | Change control | Composition, responsibilities, decision flow | Decision set |
| Version control branching model | Tools & techniques | Centralized (SVN) vs Distributed (Git) | No formula needed |
| SCMP contents | Planning | Roles, schedule, tools, procedures | No formula needed |

## 3.3 Worked Numerical Example (KTU-Style 7-Mark Application)

> [!NOTE]  
> **Problem:** A software project has **480 total Configuration Items**. During a maintenance cycle, **54 CRs were raised**, of which **42 were approved** by the CCB. The approved changes affected **96 CIs**. Post-release, **18 defects** were reported in a module of **3 KLOC**. Calculate: (a) CR Approval Rate, (b) Change Impact Ratio, (c) Post-Release Defect Density.

### Solution (Step-by-Step):

**(a) CR Approval Rate:**

$$
\text{CR Approval Rate} = \dfrac{N_{\text{approved}}}{N_{\text{total CRs}}} \times 100
$$

$$
= \dfrac{42}{54} \times 100
$$

$$
= 0.7777 \times 100 = 77.78\%
$$

**(b) Change Impact Ratio:**

$$
\text{CIR} = \dfrac{F_{\text{affected}}}{F_{\text{total}}} \times 100
$$

$$
= \dfrac{96}{480} \times 100
$$

$$
= 0.20 \times 100 = 20.00\%
$$

**(c) Post-Release Defect Density:**

$$
\text{DD}_{\text{post}} = \dfrac{D_{\text{post}}}{KLOC}
$$

$$
= \dfrac{18}{3} = 6.00 \;\text{defects/KLOC}
$$

### Interpretation Matrix:

| Metric | Value | KTU Acceptable Benchmark |
|---|---|---|
| CR Approval Rate | 77.78% | **70%–85%** (mature CCB) — **PASS** |
| Change Impact Ratio | 20.00% | **< 25%** (low ripple effect) — **PASS** |
| Defect Density | 6.00/KLOC | **Industry Avg: 5–10** (typical) — **WITHIN RANGE** |

> [!TIP]  
> **Always show units in your final answer.** KTU examiners explicitly deduct marks for missing units in numerical problems.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Diagram 1: The SCM Closed-Loop Process Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Project Initiation] --> B[Draft SCMP]
    B --> C[Identify Configuration Items]
    C --> D[Establish Baselines FBL ABL PBL]
    D --> E[Change Request Raised]
    E --> F{CCB Impact Analysis}
    F -->|Approved| G[Implement Change in Branch]
    F -->|Rejected| H[Log Rejection and Close]
    F -->|Deferred| I[Add to Future Release Backlog]
    G --> J[Verification and Testing]
    J --> K{Tests Pass?}
    K -->|Yes| L[New Baseline Created]
    K -->|No| M[Rework and Retest]
    M --> J
    L --> N[Update Status Accounting Log]
    N --> O[Perform FCA and PCA Audit]
    O --> P{Audit Pass?}
    P -->|Yes| Q[Release to Production]
    P -->|No| G
    Q --> R[Post Release Monitoring]
    R --> E
    H --> N
    I --> N
```

## 4.2 Mermaid Diagram 2: Configuration Item Hierarchy and Library Structure

```mermaid
graph TB
    root[Software Product Baseline]
    root --> s1[Source Code CIs]
    root --> s2[Design Document CIs]
    root --> s3[Test Artifact CIs]
    root --> s4[Build and Deployment CIs]
    root --> s5[Documentation CIs]
    root --> s6[Environment and Tool CIs]

    s1 --> s1a[Module A source files]
    s1 --> s1b[Module B source files]
    s1 --> s1c[Module C source files]

    s2 --> s2a[SRS Document]
    s2 --> s2b[SDD Document]

    s3 --> s3a[Test Plans]
    s3 --> s3b[Test Cases]
    s3 --> s3c[Test Data Sets]

    s4 --> s4a[Build Scripts]
    s4 --> s4b[Makefiles]
    s4 --> s4c[Container Configs]

    s5 --> s5a[User Manuals]
    s5 --> s5b[Release Notes]

    s6 --> s6a[Compiler Versions]
    s6 --> s6b[OS Patches]
    s6 --> s6c[Dependency Libraries]
```

## 4.3 Mermaid Diagram 3: Change Control Board Decision Flow

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Change Request Submitted] --> B[Configuration Manager Triage]
    B --> C[Initial Feasibility Check]
    C -->|Feasible| D[Detailed Impact Analysis]
    C -->|Not Feasible| E[Reject and Inform Originator]
    D --> F[CCB Meeting Convened]
    F --> G{Decision Matrix}
    G -->|Cost Low Risk Low| H[Approve CR]
    G -->|Cost High or Risk High| I[Escalate to Senior Management]
    G -->|Schedule Conflict| J[Defer to Next Release]
    H --> K[Schedule Implementation]
    I --> L[Senior Management Review]
    L -->|Approve| K
    L -->|Reject| E
    J --> M[Add to Backlog]
    K --> N[Implement and Verify]
    N --> O[Close CR and Update Baseline]
```

## 4.4 Mermaid Diagram 4: Branching Strategy in Distributed VCS

```mermaid
gitGraph
    commit id: "Initial Commit on Main"
    commit id: "Baseline v1.0 FBL"
    branch dev
    checkout dev
    commit id: "Feature A in Progress"
    commit id: "Feature A Complete"
    checkout main
    merge dev id: "Merge Feature A to Main"
    commit id: "Baseline v1.1 ABL"
    branch hotfix
    checkout hotfix
    commit id: "Critical Bug Fix"
    checkout main
    merge hotfix id: "Merge Hotfix"
    commit id: "Baseline v1.2 PBL"
```

> [!NOTE]  
> **Reading the Git Graph:** The diagram shows a typical GitFlow strategy with `main`, `develop`, and `hotfix` branches. Each merge point represents a **baseline creation** in SCM terminology. KTU often asks: *"What is the SCM equivalent of a Git merge commit?"* — Answer: A **re-baselining event**.

## 4.5 Mermaid Diagram 5: SCM Roles and Responsibilities

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    sponsor[Project Sponsor] --> cc[Configuration Control Board CCB]
    cc --> cm[Configuration Manager]
    cm --> librarian[Configuration Librarian]
    cm --> dev[Development Team Lead]
    cm --> qa[QA Team Lead]
    cm --> ba[Build and Release Engineer]
    cc --> auditor[Configuration Auditor]
    auditor --> fca[FCA Auditor]
    auditor --> pca[PCA Auditor]
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## PART A: Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1: Define Software Configuration Management. List its four functional activities. `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

**Definition (1 Mark):**  
Software Configuration Management (SCM) is the discipline of tracking and controlling changes in the software. It is a set of procedures, tools, and policies used to manage, organize, and control the changes made to the software's configuration items (CIs) throughout the SDLC, ensuring product integrity, traceability, and consistency.

**Four Functional Activities (2 Marks — 0.5 each):**
1. **Configuration Identification** — Identifying and naming the configuration items.
2. **Configuration Change Control** — Managing the change request lifecycle via the CCB.
3. **Configuration Status Accounting** — Recording and reporting the status of all CIs and changes.
4. **Configuration Auditing** — Verifying product integrity via FCA and PCA.

> [!TIP]  
> **Exam Tip:** A common mistake is listing only **three** activities (forgetting Auditing). The IEEE 828 framework clearly mandates **four**. KTU examiners specifically test this.

---

### Question 2: Differentiate between Functional Configuration Audit (FCA) and Physical Configuration Audit (PCA). `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

| Aspect | FCA | PCA |
|---|---|---|
| **Focus** | Verifies *what* the product does | Verifies *how* the product is built |
| **Question Answered** | Does it meet the requirements? | Does it match the approved documentation? |
| **Verifies** | Functional performance vs. SRS/SDD | Physical completeness, build scripts, CI list |
| **When Performed** | Before formal acceptance testing | Before final release/delivery |
| **Output** | Functional compliance certificate | As-built configuration record |

(Any 3 valid points fetch full 3 marks.)

> [!WARNING]  
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout:**  
> Students often write that FCA and PCA are the *same* or interchange them. They are **complementary** — FCA answers "Does it work correctly?" and PCA answers "Is it built correctly?". Conflating them costs 1.5 marks.

---

## PART B: Long-Answer Questions with Internal Choice (14 Marks Each)

> [!IMPORTANT]  
> **KTU ESE Rule:** Part B questions are 14 marks with internal choice between **Question A** and **Question B**. Students answer **EITHER A OR B**. Each question has sub-parts (a) 7 marks and (b) 7 marks.

---

### Question A (14 Marks): Full SCM Process and Metrics

#### Part (a) — 7 Marks: Explain the SCM process in detail. Discuss the role of the Change Control Board (CCB) and the concept of baselines. `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**Model Answer:**

**1. Introduction to SCM Process (2 Marks):**  
The SCM process is an iterative, closed-loop workflow that begins with project initiation and continues through the entire software life cycle. It is governed by the **Software Configuration Management Plan (SCMP)**, which is a living document outlining the SCM strategy, tools, roles, and procedures.

**2. Steps of the SCM Process (3 Marks — 0.5 each):**
1. **Configuration Identification:** Identify and name all CIs (code, docs, tests, build scripts).
2. **Baseline Establishment:** Freeze CIs at milestones (FBL → ABL → PBL).
3. **Change Request Initiation:** Stakeholders raise CRs via the change management system.
4. **Impact Analysis & CCB Review:** CCB evaluates cost, risk, and ripple effect.
5. **Implementation:** Approved changes are made in controlled branches.
6. **Verification & Re-baselining:** New baselines replace old ones after successful testing.
7. **Status Accounting & Audit:** Logs are updated; FCA and PCA confirm integrity.

**3. Role of the Change Control Board (1 Mark):**  
The CCB is a multi-disciplinary body comprising the Configuration Manager, Project Manager, senior developers, QA lead, and customer representative. It reviews each CR, assesses impact, and decides whether to **approve, reject, defer, or conditionally approve** the change. The CCB is the *gatekeeper* of the baseline.

**4. Baselines (1 Mark):**  
A baseline is a formally approved, frozen snapshot of CIs at a milestone. Common types:
- **Functional Baseline (FBL):** Approved SRS.
- **Allocated Baseline (ABL):** Approved design.
- **Product Baseline (PBL):** Approved, fully tested product.

> [!WARNING]  
> **Examiner's Pitfall Callout:**  
> Students frequently write that baselines can be *informally* changed for minor bugs. **This is incorrect.** Any change to a baselined CI **must** go through a formal CR — no exceptions. Marks lost here: **1 mark**.

---

#### Part (b) — 7 Marks: A project has 600 Configuration Items. During a release, 75 CRs were raised, of which 60 were approved. These approved changes affected 120 CIs. Post-release, 24 defects were reported in 4 KLOC of code. Calculate: (i) CR Approval Rate, (ii) Change Impact Ratio, (iii) Post-Release Defect Density. Interpret each result against industry benchmarks. `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**Model Answer:**

**Given Data:**
- $F_{\text{total}} = 600$ CIs
- $N_{\text{total CRs}} = 75$
- $N_{\text{approved}} = 60$
- $F_{\text{affected}} = 120$ CIs
- $D_{\text{post}} = 24$ defects
- $KLOC = 4$

**Calculation (i) — CR Approval Rate:**

$$
\text{CR Approval Rate} = \dfrac{60}{75} \times 100 = 80.00\%
$$

**Calculation (ii) — Change Impact Ratio:**

$$
\text{CIR} = \dfrac{120}{600} \times 100 = 20.00\%
$$

**Calculation (iii) — Post-Release Defect Density:**

$$
\text{DD}_{\text{post}} = \dfrac{24}{4} = 6.00\;\text{defects/KLOC}
$$

**Interpretation Matrix (3 marks):**

| Metric | Computed | Industry Benchmark | Verdict |
|---|---|---|---|
| CR Approval Rate | 80.00% | 70%–85% (mature CCB) | Healthy — CCB is not too lenient or strict |
| Change Impact Ratio | 20.00% | < 25% (low ripple) | Low ripple — good CI modularity |
| Defect Density | 6.00/KLOC | 5–10 (typical) | Within normal range |

> [!WARNING]  
> **Examiner's Pitfall Callout:**  
> In part (b), students often forget the **interpretation** component and lose 1–2 marks. KTU requires the answer to *interpret* the result, not just *compute* it. Always add a 1-line interpretation after each numeric result.

---

### Question B (14 Marks): SCM Tools, Auditing, and Advanced Concepts (Internal Choice Alternative)

#### Part (a) — 7 Marks: Explain the Software Configuration Management Plan (SCMP). Discuss the various SCM tools used in industry and their categorization. `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**Model Answer:**

**1. SCMP — Definition (1 Mark):**  
The Software Configuration Management Plan is a formal document that defines how SCM will be conducted in a project. It is a deliverable of the project planning phase and is referenced throughout the SDLC.

**2. Contents of SCMP (3 Marks):**
- **Introduction:** Scope, goals, references.
- **SCM Management:** Roles (CM, Librarian, CCB), responsibilities.
- **SCM Activities:** Identification, change control, status accounting, auditing procedures.
- **SCM Schedule:** When baselines will be established; audit calendar.
- **SCM Resources:** Tools, libraries, repositories.
- **Maintenance:** How the SCMP itself will be kept current.

**3. SCM Tools — Categorization (3 Marks):**

| Category | Examples | Purpose |
|---|---|---|
| Version Control Systems | Git, SVN, Mercurial | Track code changes |
| Build Tools | Maven, Gradle, Ant | Reproducible builds |
| Issue/CR Tracking | JIRA, Bugzilla | Manage CR lifecycle |
| Documentation Versioning | Confluence, SharePoint | Manage doc CIs |
| Config Databases | Ansible Tower, Puppet | Track deployment state |

> [!WARNING]  
> **Examiner's Pitfall Callout:**  
> Students often omit **document versioning tools** and list only Git. KTU expects a *holistic* tool landscape including docs and build tools. Forgetting non-code CIs costs 1 mark.

---

#### Part (b) — 7 Marks: Explain configuration auditing in detail. Describe the Software Engineering Institute (SEI) CMMI levels as they relate to SCM maturity. `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**Model Answer:**

**1. Configuration Auditing — Definition (1 Mark):**  
Configuration auditing is the systematic review of software CIs to verify their integrity, completeness, and compliance with the approved baseline, standards, and contractual requirements.

**2. Types of Audits (3 Marks):**
- **Functional Configuration Audit (FCA):** Verifies the *as-built* product performs per the *as-specified* requirements. It confirms the *behavior* is correct.
- **Physical Configuration Audit (PCA):** Verifies the *as-built* product matches the *as-designed* configuration. It confirms the *build* is correct and complete.
- **In-Process Audits:** Periodic reviews during development to catch drift early.
- **Software Quality Audit:** Verifies adherence to coding standards, documentation standards, etc.

**3. CMMI Maturity Levels (3 Marks — 0.6 each):**

| Level | Name | SCM Process Maturity |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | Initial | Ad hoc, chaotic SCM; no formal CCB |
| 2 | Managed | Basic SCM; baselines and CRs exist but inconsistently |
| 3 | Defined | Organization-wide SCMP; CCB is mature |
| 4 | Quantitatively Managed | Metrics-driven; defect density tracked |
| 5 | Optimizing | Continuous process improvement via SCM data |

> [!WARNING]  
> **Examiner's Pitfall Callout:**  
> Many students write CMMI levels as "Level 1 = worst" without explaining the **process implication** of each level. KTU expects *what SCM looks like* at each level. Saying only the name fetches 0.3 marks; the **process description** fetches full marks.

---

## KTU Topic Recap: Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

> [!NOTE]  
> Use this checklist as your last-day revision before the KTU ESE. Tick off each item as you revise.

- [ ] **SCM Definition:** Track + control changes; ensure product integrity throughout SDLC.
- [ ] **Four SCM Activities:** Identification, Change Control, Status Accounting, Auditing.
- [ ] **Configuration Item (CI):** Any artifact under configuration control — code, docs, tests, build scripts.
- [ ] **SCMP:** The plan that governs all SCM activities in a project.
- [ ] **CCB:** Change Control Board — gatekeeper of the baseline; approves/rejects CRs.
- [ ] **Baselines:** FBL (SRS), ABL (Design), PBL (Tested product) — all FROZEN once approved.
- [ ] **Change Request (CR):** Formal mechanism to request a change; must go through CCB.
- [ ] **FCA vs PCA:** FCA = Does it work? PCA = Is it built right?
- [ ] **Version Control:** Centralized (SVN) vs Distributed (Git); Git is the industry standard.
- [ ] **Key Formulas:** CR Approval Rate, Change Impact Ratio, Defect Density, MTTR for change.
- [ ] **Industry Benchmarks:** CR Approval Rate 70–85%, CIR < 25%, Defect Density 5–10/KLOC.
- [ ] **CMMI Levels:** 5 levels, each describing progressively mature SCM.
- [ ] **Real-World Standards:** IEEE 828, DO-178C, FDA 21 CFR Part 11, MIL-STD-882.
- [ ] **Tools to Remember:** Git, SVN, JIRA, Maven, Confluence, Ansible Tower.
- [ ] **Diagram to Draw:** The closed-loop SCM workflow with CCB in the center.

> [!IMPORTANT]  
> **Final KTU Exam Day Tip:** Always start a 14-mark question with a **2–3 line introduction** defining the core concept, then proceed with sub-parts. Examiners award 0.5–1 mark for a strong introduction, even if the rest is incomplete. End with an **interpretation table** wherever numerics are involved.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Managing Contracts
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEITT633 - SOFTWARE PROJECT MANAGEMENT
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Managing Contracts

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Contract Management** in the context of software project management is the systematic process of managing legally binding agreements between two or more parties (typically the buyer/client and the seller/vendor) that govern the acquisition of software products, services, or solutions. According to the **PMBOK (Project Management Body of Knowledge)** framework, contract management encompasses the activities required to administer contracts, communicate with sellers, and manage risks, changes, and disputes throughout the contract lifecycle.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight (Module 4 – Managing Contracts):**
> A software contract is a legally enforceable agreement that defines the **Statement of Work (SOW)**, **deliverables**, **acceptance criteria**, **payment terms**, **intellectual property rights**, **confidentiality (NDA)**, **warranties**, **liability clauses**, and **dispute resolution mechanisms** between the client organization and the software development vendor.

The three primary parties involved in contract management are:

| Role | Responsibility |
|---|---|
| **Buyer / Acquirer** | Initiates the contract, defines requirements, accepts deliverables |
| **Seller / Vendor** | Executes the work, delivers software, provides warranties |
| **Contract Manager** | Monitors compliance, resolves disputes, manages changes |

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy: The House Construction Contract**
> Imagine you want to build a house. You sign a contract with a builder. The contract specifies *what* the builder will build (3 bedrooms, 2 bathrooms), *when* they will finish (6 months), *how much* you will pay (₹50 Lakhs in 3 installments), and *what happens* if the builder delays (penalty of ₹5,000/day). During construction, the builder might ask for *change orders* (e.g., "Add a balcony for ₹2 Lakhs extra"). At the end, an *inspector* checks if the house meets the agreed standards before final payment.
>
> **Software contract management works exactly the same way.** The "house" is the software, the "builder" is the IT vendor, and the "inspector" is the acceptance testing team. The contract manager acts like a project supervisor who ensures both parties honor their commitments.

## 1.3 Why Contracts Are Critical in Software Projects

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Software is an **intangible product**, which makes contracts especially important. Unlike physical goods where defects are visible, software defects can hide for years. Hence, contracts must explicitly cover:
> - **Source code escrow** (in case the vendor goes bankrupt)
> - **Service Level Agreements (SLAs)** (e.g., 99.9% uptime)
> - **Acceptance testing criteria** (measurable, objective standards)
> - **Maintenance and support** post-delivery
> - **Data protection / GDPR compliance**

## 1.4 The Contract Management Lifecycle (Overview)

The lifecycle consists of **six phases** that align with the project lifecycle:

1. **Planning** – Deciding what to procure and the contract type
2. **Solicitation** – Drafting RFP/RFQ and inviting vendors
3. **Evaluation** – Comparing bids, negotiating, selecting vendor
4. **Award** – Signing the formal contract
5. **Administration** – Monitoring performance, managing changes, handling disputes
6. **Closure** – Final acceptance, payment, and post-project review

These phases will be elaborated with diagrams in Section 4.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Types of Software Contracts (The Big Four)

Software contracts can be classified based on **risk allocation** between buyer and seller. The four most important types in the KTU syllabus are:

### 2.1.1 Fixed-Price (FP) Contract
- **Also called:** Lump Sum Contract
- **Risk:** Vendor bears cost-overrun risk
- **Suitability:** Well-defined requirements
- **Formula:**
$$\text{Total Contract Value (FP)} = \text{Base Price} + \text{Contingency Markup}$$
- **Variants:** Firm Fixed Price (FFP), Fixed Price Incentive Fee (FPIF)

### 2.1.2 Time and Materials (T&M) Contract
- **Risk:** Buyer bears cost-overrun risk
- **Suitability:** Evolving scope, small projects, staff augmentation
- **Formula:**
$$\text{Total Cost (T\&M)} = (\text{Hourly Rate} \times \text{Hours Worked}) + \text{Materials Cost} + \text{Markup}$$

### 2.1.3 Cost-Reimbursable (CR) Contract
- **Risk:** Buyer bears nearly all cost risk
- **Suitability:** High uncertainty, R&D projects
- **Variants:**
  - **Cost Plus Fixed Fee (CPFF):** Fee is fixed regardless of cost
  - **Cost Plus Incentive Fee (CPIF):** Fee varies with cost performance
  - **Cost Plus Percentage of Cost (CPPC):** Discouraged by PMBOK due to zero vendor incentive to control costs

### 2.1.4 Unit Price Contract
- **Used for:** Repetitive work, e.g., per-user licensing, per-transaction pricing
- **Formula:**
$$\text{Total Cost} = \sum_{i=1}^{n} (\text{Unit Price}_i \times \text{Quantity}_i)$$

## 2.2 Key Components of a Software Contract

A legally sound software contract contains the following clauses. Understanding these is **critical for KTU 14-mark questions**:

| Clause | Purpose | KTU Importance |
|---|---|---|
| **Statement of Work (SOW)** | Defines scope, deliverables, milestones | High |
| **Service Level Agreement (SLA)** | Performance metrics (uptime, response time) | High |
| **Acceptance Criteria** | Objective standards for sign-off | High |
| **Payment Terms** | Schedule (e.g., 20-30-40-10 milestones) | High |
| **Intellectual Property (IP)** | Ownership of code, designs, patents | High |
| **Confidentiality / NDA** | Protection of trade secrets | Medium |
| **Warranty** | Defect liability period (e.g., 90 days) | Medium |
| **Limitation of Liability** | Caps vendor's financial exposure | Medium |
| **Termination Clause** | Conditions to end contract early | High |
| **Dispute Resolution** | Arbitration, jurisdiction, mediation | High |
| **Force Majeure** | Excuses non-performance due to disasters | Low |

## 2.3 The Contract Administration Process

Contract administration is the day-to-day management after the contract is signed. It involves:

1. **Performance Monitoring** – Tracking vendor's KPIs (defect density, schedule variance)
2. **Change Control** – Processing change requests through a formal Change Control Board (CCB)
3. **Payment Processing** – Verifying invoices against deliverables before releasing payment
4. **Issue Resolution** – Handling disputes, escalations, and corrective actions
5. **Records Management** – Maintaining correspondence, approvals, and audit trails
6. **Risk Monitoring** – Tracking contract risks (vendor financial health, key personnel attrition)

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following table contains **all key formulas** required for KTU numerical problems on Managing Contracts. Memorize these.

| Formula | Description | Use Case |
|---|---|---|
| $C_{FP} = B + M$ | Fixed-Price Total Cost = Base + Markup | FP contracts |
| $C_{T\&M} = r \times h + M_{cost} + m$ | T&M Cost = Rate $\times$ Hours + Materials + Markup | T&M contracts |
| $C_{CPFF} = A_{cost} + F_{fixed}$ | CPFF = Actual Cost + Fixed Fee | Cost-plus fixed fee |
| $C_{CPIF} = A_{cost} + F_{base} + I(P - A_{target})$ | CPIF with incentive | Cost-plus incentive |
| $P_{final} = P_{contract} + \Delta_{change}$ | Revised price after change orders | Change management |
| $\text{Penalty} = R_{daily} \times D_{delay}$ | Delay penalty calculation | SLA breaches |
| $\text{Retention Amount} = T_{value} \times r_{retention}$ | Money withheld until final acceptance | Risk mitigation |
| $C_{unit} = \sum_{i=1}^{n} P_i \times Q_i$ | Total cost in unit price contract | Licensing/transaction contracts |
| $\text{Buy/Sell Ratio} = \dfrac{N_w - 1}{N_w + 1}$ | Winner's curse estimation (auction theory) | Vendor selection |
| $\text{Contract Value} = \sum_{k=1}^{n} \dfrac{PMT_k}{(1+r)^k}$ | NPV of contract payments | Multi-year contracts |

Where:
- $B$ = Base estimated cost
- $M$ = Markup/contingency
- $r$ = Hourly rate
- $h$ = Hours worked
- $A_{cost}$ = Actual cost incurred
- $F$ = Fee (fixed or incentive)
- $I$ = Incentive sharing ratio
- $P$ = Actual final cost
- $A_{target}$ = Target cost
- $N_w$ = Number of bidders

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

Contract management is the **backbone of outsourced software development**, which accounts for over **USD 1.5 trillion** in global IT spending. Mastery of contract types allows project managers to:
- **Allocate risk optimally** between client and vendor
- **Negotiate favorable terms** (e.g., shared-savings clauses in cloud migration)
- **Avoid litigation** by clearly defining acceptance criteria
- **Ensure compliance** with regulations like GDPR, HIPAA, and India's IT Act 2000

> [!NOTE]
> In the KTU 2024 scheme, the **Course Outcome (CO)** mapped to this topic is **CO4: Apply contract management principles to manage software procurement and vendor relationships.** Bloom's Taxonomy level: **Apply / Analyze**.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Worked Example 1: Cost-Plus Incentive Fee (CPIF) Calculation

> [!NOTE]
> **Problem Statement:**
> A software vendor signs a CPIF contract with a target cost of **₹20,00,000**. The target fee is **₹3,00,000**. The incentive sharing ratio is **80/20** (80% buyer / 20% vendor). The final actual cost comes out to be **₹22,00,000**. Calculate the final fee paid to the vendor and the total cost to the buyer.

### Step-by-Step Solution:

**Step 1: Identify the given parameters.**

Let:
- $A_{target} = ₹20,00,000$ (Target cost)
- $F_{base} = ₹3,00,000$ (Base/target fee)
- $I_{vendor} = 0.20$ (Vendor's share of savings/losses)
- $I_{buyer} = 0.80$ (Buyer's share)
- $A_{cost} = ₹22,00,000$ (Actual final cost)

**Step 2: Calculate the cost overrun (or underrun).**

$$\Delta = A_{cost} - A_{target}$$

Substituting the values:

$$\Delta = 22,00,000 - 20,00,000 = 2,00,000$$

Since $\Delta > 0$, there is a **cost overrun** of ₹2,00,000.

**Step 3: Calculate the incentive adjustment.**

The vendor's incentive is the vendor's share of the cost underrun (or loss, in case of overrun):

$$\text{Incentive} = I_{vendor} \times (-\Delta)$$

$$\text{Incentive} = 0.20 \times (-2,00,000) = -40,000$$

The negative sign indicates a **fee reduction** (penalty) because the cost exceeded the target.

**Step 4: Calculate the final fee.**

$$F_{final} = F_{base} + \text{Incentive}$$

$$F_{final} = 3,00,000 + (-40,000) = 2,60,000$$

**Step 5: Calculate the total cost to the buyer.**

$$C_{total} = A_{cost} + F_{final}$$

$$C_{total} = 22,00,000 + 2,60,000 = 24,60,000$$

**Final Answer:**

- **Final Fee to Vendor:** ₹2,60,000
- **Total Cost to Buyer:** ₹24,60,000
- **Cost Overrun Shared:** Buyer paid ₹40,000 less than they would have under a CPFF contract, thanks to the incentive clause.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Insight:** In a CPIF contract, both parties share the **pain of overruns** and the **gain of underruns**. This incentivizes the vendor to control costs because they keep 20% of any savings.

---

## 3.2 Worked Example 2: Fixed-Price Contract with Penalty Clause

> [!NOTE]
> **Problem Statement:**
> A client signs a Fixed-Price contract worth **₹15,00,000** with a software vendor for delivery in **6 months**. The contract has a penalty clause of **₹5,000 per day** for delays beyond the agreed date, capped at **10% of the contract value**. The vendor delivered the software **25 days late**. Calculate: (a) The total penalty, (b) The final amount paid to the vendor, (c) Verify if the penalty cap is triggered.

### Step-by-Step Solution:

**Step 1: Identify the given parameters.**

Let:
- $P_{contract} = ₹15,00,000$ (Contract value)
- $R_{daily} = ₹5,000$ (Daily penalty rate)
- $D_{delay} = 25$ days (Actual delay)
- $\text{Cap} = 10\% \text{ of } P_{contract}$

**Step 2: Calculate the maximum allowable penalty (the cap).**

$$\text{Max Penalty} = 0.10 \times 15,00,000 = 1,50,000$$

**Step 3: Calculate the uncapped penalty based on actual delay.**

$$\text{Penalty}_{raw} = R_{daily} \times D_{delay}$$

$$\text{Penalty}_{raw} = 5,000 \times 25 = 1,25,000$$

**Step 4: Compare the raw penalty with the cap and apply the minimum.**

$$\text{Final Penalty} = \min(\text{Penalty}_{raw}, \text{Max Penalty})$$

$$\text{Final Penalty} = \min(1,25,000, \ 1,50,000) = 1,25,000$$

Since $1,25,000 < 1,50,000$, the **cap is NOT triggered**.

**Step 5: Calculate the final amount paid to the vendor.**

$$P_{final} = P_{contract} - \text{Final Penalty}$$

$$P_{final} = 15,00,000 - 1,25,000 = 13,75,000$$

**Final Answer:**

- **(a) Total Penalty:** ₹1,25,000
- **(b) Final Payment to Vendor:** ₹13,75,000
